topics.title: Transmission Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT414 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transmission Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Transmission Modes** (also called *Communication Modes* or *Data Flow Directions*) define the **direction of signal flow** between two communicating devices (a *source* and a *destination*) linked through a transmission medium. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEEVT414, transmission modes are the foundational classification that governs how data bits traverse a physical link — whether in one direction only, in both directions alternately, or in both directions simultaneously.

The **three canonical transmission modes** recognized by the ISO/OSI Reference Model and standardized under **ITU-T** and **IEEE 802** families are:

| # | Mode | Direction | Alternate Name |
|---|------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | **Simplex (SX)** | One-way only | Unidirectional |
| 2 | **Half-Duplex (HDX)** | Both ways, but one at a time | Semi-duplex / Two-way alternate |
| 3 | **Full-Duplex (FDX)** | Both ways simultaneously | Duplex / Two-way simultaneous |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Transmission Modes are part of the *Physical Layer* (Layer 1) and *Data Link Layer* (Layer 2) sub-topics in Module 1. Expect at least one direct 3-mark question in Part A and possibly a sub-part in a 14-mark question.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine **two friends, Arjun and Bhavana**, talking using walkie-talkies while hiking:

- **Simplex Mode** → Think of a **loudspeaker announcement system** at a railway station. The announcer speaks; thousands of passengers listen. **No one can reply on the same channel.** Examples: **TV broadcast**, **radio FM**, **keyboard-to-monitor data flow**, **mouse-to-PC**.

- **Half-Duplex Mode** → Think of a **single-lane bridge** with a traffic signal. Cars move from North-to-South for 60 seconds, then South-to-North for the next 60 seconds. **Both directions are allowed, but never at the same instant.** Examples: **walkie-talkie (push-to-talk)**, **old CB radio**, **Wired Ethernet hubs** (using CSMA/CD collision handling).

- **Full-Duplex Mode** → Think of a **two-lane highway** where Arjun drives towards Bhavana in the right lane and Bhavana drives towards Arjun in the left lane, **simultaneously and without collision**. Examples: **telephone conversation**, **modern switched Ethernet**, **fiber-optic backbone links**, **4G/5G mobile data**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** A common exam trap is to confuse *Half-Duplex* with *Full-Duplex*. The mnemonic is **HDX = "Halt, Don't eXchange at the same time"** versus **FDX = "Free, Double eXchange"**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following standard units and constants are used in transmission mode calculations:

- **Bandwidth (B)** is measured in **bits per second (bps)**, commonly expressed as **kbps, Mbps, Gbps, or Tbps**.
- **Propagation speed** in **vacuum/free space** = **$3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s**.
- **Propagation speed** in **copper cable** ≈ **$2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s** (roughly **2/3 the speed of light**).
- **Propagation speed** in **optical fiber** ≈ **$2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s** to **$2.3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s**.
- **File size** is measured in **bytes (B)** or **bits (b)** — with **1 Byte = 8 bits** as the conversion factor.
- **Round-Trip Time (RTT)** is typically measured in **milliseconds (ms)** for terrestrial links.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Each Mode

### 2.1.1 Simplex Mode (SX)

- **Channel capacity** is dedicated entirely to the **forward path**.
- The **reverse channel does not exist** or is reserved for control signals only.
- **Bandwidth utilization** of the data path = **100%** in the forward direction.
- Hardware design is the **simplest** — requires **one transmitter and one receiver**.
- **Latency is unidirectional** — there is no acknowledgment path.

> [!NOTE]
> Examples: **Radio broadcasting, Television, Pager systems, Keyboard data input, Sensor-to-aggregator telemetry in IoT.**

### 2.1.2 Half-Duplex Mode (HDX)

- **Shared channel** for both directions; only one device transmits at any instant.
- Requires a **direction-control mechanism** (e.g., push-to-talk button, token passing, CSMA/CD).
- **Effective throughput** in each direction ≈ **B / 2** if perfectly time-shared.
- **Collision domain** includes both endpoints; a collision-detection mechanism is essential.
- The **Physical Layer** may use 2 wires (one pair) or 4 wires (two pairs — one for each direction in FDX, but only one pair active in HDX).

> [!NOTE]
> Examples: **Walkie-talkie radios, Traditional Ethernet (10BASE-T with hubs), Bluetooth (ACL logical channels), Police/aviation radios, Citizen Band (CB) radio.**

### 2.1.3 Full-Duplex Mode (FDX)

- **Two independent physical or logical channels** operate simultaneously.
- Can be implemented as:
  - **Two separate physical links** (4-wire circuits — historically used in RS-232, RS-422).
  - **Frequency-division duplex (FDD)** — different frequency bands for each direction (used in 4G LTE, ADSL).
  - **Time-division duplex (TDD)** — alternating time slots fast enough to appear simultaneous (used in 5G NR, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth LE).
  - **Echo cancellation** over a single pair (used in 1000BASE-T Gigabit Ethernet).
- **Theoretical aggregate throughput** = **2 × B** if both channels have bandwidth B.
- **Collision domain** is effectively eliminated (full-duplex switches do not need CSMA/CD).

> [!NOTE]
> Examples: **Modern switched Ethernet, Telephone network (PSTN), Mobile 4G/5G, Fiber-optic backbone, Satellite communication with separate uplink/downlink transponders.**

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table lists every formula a KTU 2024 student must memorize for Transmission Mode / Link Performance problems. Pay special attention to the **units column** — wrong units is the #1 reason for losing 2–3 marks in a 7-mark sub-question.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Boundary / Limiting Case |
|---|----------|---------|------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Transmission Delay ( $T_t$ )** | $T_t = \dfrac{L}{B}$ | **seconds (s)** | $L$ = file length in **bits**; $B$ = bandwidth in **bps** |
| 2 | **Propagation Delay ( $T_p$ )** | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | **seconds (s)** | $d$ = link length in **meters**; $v$ = propagation speed in **m/s** |
| 3 | **Total Delay (Store-and-Forward)** | $T_{total} = T_t + T_p$ | **seconds (s)** | Used for a single-hop link |
| 4 | **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)** | $BDP = B \times T_p$ | **bits** | Equals the number of bits "in flight" on the link |
| 5 | **Round-Trip Time (RTT)** | $RTT = 2 \times T_p + T_{proc}$ | **seconds (s)** | $T_{proc}$ = processing time at receiver |
| 6 | **Effective Throughput** | $\eta = \dfrac{L}{T_{total}}$ | **bps** | $L$ = useful payload bits |
| 7 | **Channel Utilization (Line Efficiency)** | $U = \dfrac{T_t}{T_t + T_p} \times 100\%$ | **% (percentage)** | $U \to 100\%$ when $T_p \ll T_t$ |
| 8 | **Max Throughput over RTT (Stop-and-Wait)** | $Throughput_{max} = \dfrac{L}{T_t + RTT}$ | **bps** | Applicable to **Half-Duplex** protocols |
| 9 | **Full-Duplex Aggregate Capacity** | $C_{FDX} = 2 \times B$ | **bps** | When both directions have bandwidth B |
| 10 | **Half-Duplex Effective Capacity per Direction** | $C_{HDX} = \dfrac{B}{2}$ | **bps** | Assuming perfect 50:50 time-share |
| 11 | **Wavelength (for FDD)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | **meters** | $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s; $f$ in Hz |
| 12 | **Time for 'n' packets (Pipelined)** | $T_n = (n + k - 1) \times T_t$ | **seconds (s)** | $k$ = window size; applicable to FDX sliding-window |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When the file size is given in **KB or MB**, you **must** multiply by **$8 \times 1024$** (or $8 \times 10^6$ for SI) to convert to bits before dividing by bandwidth in bps. The official KTU answer key penalizes this conversion error by **1 mark**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Preferred Mode | Why |
|--------|----------------|-----|
| **Satellite TV broadcast** | Simplex | Millions of receivers, no upstream data needed. |
| **Push-to-Talk Radios (military, aviation)** | Half-Duplex | Spectrum conservation, single-frequency reuse. |
| **Mobile 4G/5G voice calls** | Full-Duplex (FDD) | Both parties speak simultaneously, low latency. |
| **Data Center Leaf-Spine (100G/400G Ethernet)** | Full-Duplex | Maximizes link utilization, eliminates collisions. |
| **IoT sensor uplinks** | Simplex | Battery-powered, no need for downstream control. |
| **Industrial CAN-bus (automotive)** | Half-Duplex | Cost reduction via single twisted pair, deterministic arbitration. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Deriving the Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)

The **BDP** is the theoretical maximum number of bits that can occupy a transmission link at any instant — the "pipe" filled with in-flight data.

**Step 1:** Start from the definition of **bandwidth (B)** — the number of bits emitted per second by the sender.

$$
B = \frac{\text{Bits transmitted}}{\text{Time of transmission}}
$$

**Step 2:** Start from the definition of **propagation delay ( $T_p$ )** — the time for one bit to travel from sender to receiver.

$$
T_p = \frac{d}{v}
$$

where $d$ is link length and $v$ is propagation speed.

**Step 3:** Multiply the two quantities:

$$
BDP = B \times T_p
$$

$$
BDP = \left(\frac{\text{bits}}{\text{second}}\right) \times \left(\frac{\text{seconds}}{\text{bit\ arrival}}\right)
$$

**Step 4:** Unit analysis — the "seconds" cancel:

$$
BDP = \text{bits}
$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation — the result is the **maximum number of bits** that can be present in the physical medium simultaneously, i.e., the **volume of the "data pipe"** stretching between sender and receiver.

$$
\boxed{BDP = B \times T_p = B \times \frac{d}{v}}
$$

**Step 6:** Worked numerical example.

Given: $B = 1 \text{ Mbps} = 10^{6} \text{ bps}$, $d = 2000 \text{ km} = 2 \times 10^{6} \text{ m}$, $v = 2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$ (fiber).

$$
T_p = \frac{2 \times 10^{6}}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
BDP = 10^{6} \times 0.01 = 10{,}000 \text{ bits} = 1.25 \text{ KB}
$$

This means **10,000 bits can be in transit** on the 2000 km fiber link at any moment.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Channel Utilization for Half-Duplex Stop-and-Wait Protocol

In a **Half-Duplex Stop-and-Wait ARQ** protocol, the sender transmits a frame, waits for an ACK, and then sends the next frame. The question: **what fraction of the link's time is spent transmitting useful data?**

**Step 1:** Write the time consumed by a **single cycle** (one frame + one ACK).

$$
T_{cycle} = T_{t,frame} + T_{p} + T_{proc} + T_{t,ACK} + T_{p}
$$

For simplification, assume $T_{t,ACK}$ and $T_{proc}$ are negligible (typical KTU assumption):

$$
T_{cycle} \approx T_{t,frame} + 2T_p
$$

**Step 2:** Define **utilization** as the fraction of cycle time spent on **actual data transmission**:

$$
U = \frac{T_{t,frame}}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{T_t}{T_t + 2T_p}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $T_t = L/B$ and $T_p = d/v$:

$$
U = \frac{L / B}{\dfrac{L}{B} + \dfrac{2d}{v}}
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $B$:

$$
U = \frac{L}{L + 2Bd/v} = \frac{L}{L + 2 \cdot BDP}
$$

**Step 5:** Final compact form:

$$
\boxed{U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} \quad \text{where} \quad a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{B \cdot d}{v \cdot L}}
$$

**Step 6:** Limiting cases.

- **If $a \to 0$ (short link or large file)** → $U \to 1$ (**100% utilization**).
- **If $a \to \infty$ (long link or small file)** → $U \to 0$ (**link mostly idle**).
- **If $a = 1$** → $U = 1/3 \approx 33.3\%$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Full-Duplex Aggregate Throughput

**Step 1:** In **full-duplex**, the link carries two independent streams — forward ($A \to B$) and reverse ($B \to A$).

**Step 2:** Each direction has its own **effective bandwidth B** (either physically separate wires or logically separate channels).

**Step 3:** The **aggregate** or **bidirectional** throughput is the sum:

$$
C_{FDX} = C_{A \to B} + C_{B \to A}
$$

**Step 4:** Assuming both directions are symmetric:

$$
\boxed{C_{FDX} = 2 \times B}
$$

**Step 5:** A symmetric **10 Mbps full-duplex** link delivers **20 Mbps aggregate** (10 each way). A **1 Gbps full-duplex fiber** delivers **2 Gbps aggregate**.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Simplex (SX) | Half-Duplex (HDX) | Full-Duplex (FDX) |
|-----------|--------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Direction of flow** | One-way only | Both, but one at a time | Both, simultaneously |
| **Number of channels required** | 1 | 1 (shared) | 2 (physical or logical) |
| **Theoretical utilization per direction** | 100% (forward), 0% (reverse) | ~50% (with perfect time-share) | 100% per direction |
| **Aggregate capacity** | $B$ | $B$ (shared) | $2B$ |
| **Collision risk** | None | High (needs CSMA/CD or token) | None (in FDX switched networks) |
| **Hardware complexity** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Latency (for 2-way conversation)** | Not possible | Higher (turnaround delay) | Lowest |
| **Example physical medium** | FM radio carrier, sensor wires | Single twisted pair, walkie-talkie channel | Two twisted pairs, fiber with separate wavelengths |
| **KTU Real-world example** | TV broadcast, telemetry | 10BASE-5 Ethernet (with hub), CB radio | Switched Ethernet, mobile LTE, PSTN |
| **Power consumption at node** | Lowest | Medium | Highest (always listening + transmitting) |
| **Error-detection feedback** | Requires out-of-band channel | Possible on same channel (using turnaround) | Possible continuously on reverse channel |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A 4 MB file is to be transmitted over a 5000 km optical-fiber link. Bandwidth is 100 Mbps. Propagation speed in fiber is $2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s. Compute the transmission delay, propagation delay, total delay, BDP, and channel utilization (assume Stop-and-Wait half-duplex protocol).

**Step 1 — Convert file size to bits:**

$$
L = 4 \text{ MB} = 4 \times 1024 \times 1024 \times 8 \text{ bits} = 33{,}554{,}432 \text{ bits}
$$

(For SI problems, use $4 \times 10^6 \times 8 = 32 \times 10^6$ bits. KTU typically uses the **binary** form unless explicitly stated otherwise.)

**Step 2 — Transmission delay:**

$$
T_t = \frac{L}{B} = \frac{33{,}554{,}432}{100 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0.3355 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Propagation delay:**

$$
T_p = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{5 \times 10^{6}}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 0.025 \text{ s} = 25 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Total delay (half-duplex, one-way):**

$$
T_{total} = T_t + T_p \approx 0.3355 + 0.025 = 0.3605 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5 — Bandwidth-delay product:**

$$
BDP = B \times T_p = 10^{8} \times 0.025 = 2{,}500{,}000 \text{ bits} \approx 2.5 \text{ Mbit}
$$

**Step 6 — Channel utilization (Stop-and-Wait):**

$$
U = \frac{T_t}{T_t + 2T_p} = \frac{0.3355}{0.3355 + 0.05} \approx 0.8703 = 87.03\%
$$

This high utilization shows that for **large files and moderate propagation delay**, half-duplex stop-and-wait is reasonably efficient — a common KTU exam conclusion.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — High-Level Classification of Transmission Modes

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[Transmission Modes]
    node1 --> node2[Simplex Mode SX]
    node1 --> node3[Half Duplex Mode HDX]
    node1 --> node4[Full Duplex Mode FDX]
    node2 --> node2a[Unidirectional Data Flow]
    node2 --> node2b[One Transmitter One Receiver]
    node3 --> node3a[Bidirectional Alternating]
    node3 --> node3b[Shared Channel with Turnaround]
    node4 --> node4a[Simultaneous Bidirectional]
    node4 --> node4b[Two Independent Channels]
    node4a --> node4c[Frequency Division Duplex FDD]
    node4a --> node4d[Time Division Duplex TDD]
    node4a --> node4e[Echo Cancellation on Single Pair]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Architecture of Each Mode

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SIMPLEX
        sA[Node A Sender] -- Forward Channel Only --> sB[Node B Receiver]
    end
    subgraph HALFDUPLEX
        hA[Node A] -- TX Forward --> hSwitch{Channel Direction}
        hSwitch -- Reverse Slot --> hB[Node B]
        hB -- TX Return --> hSwitch
    end
    subgraph FULLDUPLEX
        fA[Node A] -- Channel 1 Forward --> fB[Node B]
        fB -- Channel 2 Reverse --> fA
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flow for Choosing a Transmission Mode

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Application Requirements]
    start --> q1{Is two-way communication needed?}
    q1 -- No --> simplex[Use Simplex Mode]
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Can endpoints transmit simultaneously?}
    q2 -- No --> halfduplex[Use Half Duplex Mode]
    q2 -- Yes --> fullduplex[Use Full Duplex Mode]
    simplex --> ex1[Examples: TV Radio Sensor Uplink]
    halfduplex --> ex2[Examples: Walkie Talkie Ethernet Hub CB Radio]
    fullduplex --> ex3[Examples: Switched Ethernet Mobile 4G 5G PSTN]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Data Flow in FDX Channel

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph NodeA[Node A]
        AApp[Application Layer] --> ABuf[Transmit Buffer]
        ABuf --> AEnc[Encoder]
        AEnc --> ATx[Transmitter]
        ARx[Receiver] --> ADec[Decoder]
        ADec --> AApp
    end
    subgraph Channel[Physical Channel FDX]
        Ch1[Forward Channel Frequency F1] 
        Ch2[Reverse Channel Frequency F2]
    end
    subgraph NodeB[Node B]
        BRx[Receiver] --> BDec[Decoder]
        BDec --> BApp[Application Layer]
        BApp --> BEnc[Encoder]
        BEnc --> BTx[Transmitter]
    end
    ATx --> Ch1 --> BRx
    BTx --> Ch2 --> ARx
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Performance Characteristics Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    sx[Simplex Utilization Forward = 100 percent Reverse = 0 percent]
    hdx[Half Duplex Per Direction approx 50 percent Aggregate = B]
    fdx[Full Duplex Per Direction = 100 percent Aggregate = 2B]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEVT414, Q1.a]`

**Q: Differentiate between Simplex, Half-Duplex, and Full-Duplex transmission modes with one real-world example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Mode | Direction of Data Flow | Real-World Example |
|------|------------------------|--------------------|
| **Simplex** | Communication is **unidirectional** — data flows from sender to receiver only. The receiver cannot send data back on the same channel. | **Television broadcast** — TV transmitter sends signals; TV sets only receive. (1 mark) |
| **Half-Duplex** | Communication is **bidirectional but not simultaneous** — both devices can transmit and receive, but only **one at a time** on the shared channel. | **Walkie-talkie (push-to-talk) radio** — the user must press a button to talk and release to listen. (1 mark) |
| **Full-Duplex** | Communication is **bidirectional and simultaneous** — both devices can transmit and receive **at the same instant** using two independent channels. | **Mobile phone voice call** over 4G LTE — both parties can speak and be heard concurrently. (1 mark) |

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEVT414, Q1.b]`

**Q: Define Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP). What does it physically represent in a transmission link?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Bandwidth-Delay Product (BDP)** is defined as the product of the **link bandwidth (B)** and the **one-way propagation delay ( $T_p$ )**:

$$
BDP = B \times T_p
$$

where $B$ is in bits per second and $T_p$ is in seconds, so BDP is expressed in **bits**. (1 mark for formula)

**Physical significance:** BDP represents the **maximum number of bits** that can be present in the physical transmission medium **at any instant** — i.e., the number of bits "in flight" between the sender and receiver. It is conceptually the **volume of the data pipe** stretching along the link, and it determines the required size of the sender's **transmit buffer** to keep the link fully utilized. (2 marks for significance)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEEVT414, Module 1, Q2.A]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the three transmission modes (Simplex, Half-Duplex, and Full-Duplex) with suitable diagrams. Compare their performance in terms of channel utilization, hardware complexity, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5 MB file is transmitted over a 4000 km link with a bandwidth of 2 Mbps using a Half-Duplex Stop-and-Wait protocol. The propagation speed is $2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s. Calculate the transmission delay, propagation delay, total delay, and channel utilization. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question A(a)

**Step 1 — Simplex Mode (1.5 marks):**

In Simplex mode, the **sender can only transmit and the receiver can only receive**; communication is strictly **one-way**. Only **one transmitter and one receiver** are required. Examples: **TV broadcast, radio FM, keyboard-to-computer data input**. Performance: **100% channel utilization in the forward direction**, 0% in reverse. Hardware complexity is the **lowest**.

**Step 2 — Half-Duplex Mode (1.5 marks):**

In Half-Duplex mode, both stations can transmit and receive, but **only one at a time** on a shared channel. A **turnaround mechanism** is required to switch direction (push-to-talk, CSMA/CD, token passing). Effective per-direction capacity is approximately **B/2**. Examples: **walkie-talkie, traditional Ethernet hubs, CB radio**.

**Step 3 — Full-Duplex Mode (1.5 marks):**

In Full-Duplex mode, both stations can transmit and receive **simultaneously**. This is achieved by either **two separate physical channels** (4-wire circuits), **frequency-division duplex (FDD)**, or **time-division duplex (TDD)**. Aggregate capacity is **2B**. Examples: **modern switched Ethernet, mobile 4G/5G, telephone network**.

**Step 4 — Comparison Table (2.5 marks):**

| Parameter | Simplex | Half-Duplex | Full-Duplex |
|-----------|---------|-------------|-------------|
| Direction | One-way | Two-way alternate | Two-way simultaneous |
| Channel utilization (forward) | 100% | ~50% | 100% |
| Hardware complexity | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Typical application | TV broadcast, sensors | Walkie-talkie, hub Ethernet | Switched Ethernet, 4G/5G |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Many students **omit the diagram**. A neat labelled block diagram (sender → channel → receiver) for each mode is worth **at least 1 mark** and demonstrates understanding. Do not skip it.

---

#### Solution to Question A(b)

**Step 1 — Convert file size to bits:**

$$
L = 5 \text{ MB} = 5 \times 1024 \times 1024 \times 8 = 41{,}943{,}040 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Transmission Delay:**

$$
T_t = \frac{L}{B} = \frac{41{,}943{,}040}{2 \times 10^{6}} = 20.97 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Propagation Delay:**

$$
T_p = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{4 \times 10^{6}}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 0.02 \text{ s} = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Delay (one-way, ignoring ACK time):**

$$
T_{total} = T_t + T_p = 20.97 + 0.02 = 20.99 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5 — Channel Utilization (Stop-and-Wait, Half-Duplex):**

For Stop-and-Wait, the cycle time includes **two propagation delays** (frame goes + ACK returns):

$$
U = \frac{T_t}{T_t + 2T_p} = \frac{20.97}{20.97 + 0.04} = \frac{20.97}{21.01} \approx 0.9981
$$

$$
U \approx 99.81\%
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

The utilization is nearly **100%** because the transmission delay (20.97 s) is vastly larger than the round-trip propagation delay (0.04 s). For this scenario, **half-duplex stop-and-wait is highly efficient** — a key KTU takeaway.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEEVT414, Module 1, Q2.B]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the operation of a Full-Duplex transmission system. Explain the three implementation methods (two physical channels, FDD, TDD) and discuss why full-duplex is preferred for modern Ethernet and mobile networks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for **channel utilization in a Half-Duplex Stop-and-Wait ARQ protocol** as a function of the parameter $a = T_p / T_t$. For a satellite link at 1 Mbps, 50,000 km distance, and 1 KB frames, compute the utilization. Propagation speed $= 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Question B(a)

**Step 1 — Full-Duplex Block Diagram (1.5 marks):**

A full-duplex system has **two independent data paths** between Node A and Node B. Node A's transmitter is connected to Node B's receiver via one channel, while Node B's transmitter is connected to Node A's receiver via a second channel. Both transmitters can operate **simultaneously and continuously** without collision, as long as the channels are physically or logically separate.

**Step 2 — Implementation Method 1: Two Physical Channels (2 marks):**

The simplest implementation uses **two separate physical links** (e.g., 4-wire circuit: 2 wires for forward, 2 wires for reverse). This is used in legacy **RS-232** and **RS-422** serial communications. The advantage is **complete electrical isolation** between the two directions; the disadvantage is **double the cabling cost**. In modern Ethernet, the same idea is realized using the **two pairs of a Cat5/Cat6 cable** (one pair for TX, one pair for RX), with **echo cancellation** allowing simultaneous use of the same pair (as in 1000BASE-T Gigabit Ethernet).

**Step 3 — Implementation Method 2: Frequency-Division Duplex (FDD) (1.5 marks):**

FDD allocates **two separate frequency bands** within the same physical medium — one for the **downlink (forward)** and one for the **uplink (reverse)**. A **duplexer filter** is used at each end to separate the two frequencies. FDD is widely used in **4G LTE** (e.g., 900 MHz uplink, 950 MHz downlink paired bands) and in **ADSL broadband** (upstream 26 kHz–138 kHz, downstream 138 kHz–1.1 MHz).

**Step 4 — Implementation Method 3: Time-Division Duplex (TDD) (1 mark):**

TDD uses a **single frequency band** shared by both directions in **alternating time slots**, switching direction so rapidly that it appears simultaneous. TDD is used in **5G NR** (TDD mode), **Wi-Fi**, and **Bluetooth LE**. The advantage is **flexible allocation of slot counts** to match asymmetric traffic (e.g., more downlink slots for video streaming).

**Step 5 — Why Preferred for Modern Ethernet and Mobile (1 mark):**

Full-duplex is preferred because it **doubles the effective capacity**, **eliminates collisions** (no CSMA/CD needed in switched Ethernet), and **minimizes latency** for interactive applications (VoIP, video calls, gaming).

---

#### Solution to Question B(b)

**Step 1 — Set up the Stop-and-Wait cycle (1 mark):**

The sender transmits one frame of length $L$ bits at rate $B$, then waits for the ACK. The total cycle time (ignoring processing and ACK transmission) is:

$$
T_{cycle} = T_t + 2T_p
$$

where $T_t = L / B$ is the transmission time and $T_p = d / v$ is the one-way propagation delay.

**Step 2 — Define utilization (1 mark):**

Utilization $U$ is the fraction of the cycle time spent transmitting useful data:

$$
U = \frac{T_t}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{T_t}{T_t + 2T_p}
$$

**Step 3 — Define the parameter $a$ (1 mark):**

$$
a = \frac{T_p}{T_t}
$$

**Step 4 — Derive the closed-form expression (1 mark):**

Dividing numerator and denominator by $T_t$:

$$
U = \frac{1}{1 + 2T_p / T_t} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}
$$

$$
\boxed{U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical computation for the satellite link (2 marks):**

Given: $B = 1 \text{ Mbps} = 10^{6}$ bps; $d = 50{,}000 \text{ km} = 5 \times 10^{7}$ m; $v = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s; $L = 1 \text{ KB} = 1024 \times 8 = 8192$ bits.

$$
T_p = \frac{5 \times 10^{7}}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 0.1667 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_t = \frac{8192}{10^{6}} = 8.192 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 8.192 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{0.1667}{0.008192} \approx 20.35
$$

$$
U = \frac{1}{1 + 2 \times 20.35} = \frac{1}{41.7} \approx 0.024 = 2.4\%
$$

**Step 6 — Comment (1 mark):**

The utilization is only **2.4%**, meaning the satellite link is **idle ~97.6% of the time** under stop-and-wait. This is because the **propagation delay dominates** the transmission delay (large $a$). For such high-latency links, a **sliding-window protocol** (e.g., Selective Repeat ARQ) is essential to keep the link busy — a critical KTU exam insight.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where students commonly lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by 2 in the cycle time** (using $T_t + T_p$ instead of $T_t + 2T_p$). The ACK must travel back — that's an extra $T_p$.
> 2. **Unit mismatch** between $L$ (given in KB) and $B$ (given in Mbps). Always convert to bits and bps.
> 3. **Mixing $2 \times 10^8$ with $3 \times 10^8$** in fiber problems. KTU problems specifying "optical fiber" or "copper" expect $2 \times 10^8$, while "free space" or "wireless" expects $3 \times 10^8$.
> 4. **Failing to comment** on the numerical result. The qualitative interpretation is worth at least **1 mark**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam:**

- **Three transmission modes** are Simplex, Half-Duplex, and Full-Duplex. Memorize direction, example, and aggregate capacity for each.
- **Simplex** = unidirectional; **Half-Duplex** = bidirectional alternate; **Full-Duplex** = bidirectional simultaneous.
- **Aggregate capacity** in Full-Duplex = **$2B$** (assuming symmetric channels).
- **Per-direction capacity** in Half-Duplex (with 50:50 share) = **$B/2$**.
- **Transmission Delay**: $T_t = L / B$ — convert $L$ to **bits** and $B$ to **bps** before dividing.
- **Propagation Delay**: $T_p = d / v$ — use $v = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s for fiber/copper and $3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s for wireless.
- **Bandwidth-Delay Product** $BDP = B \times T_p$ — measures in-flight bits, determines buffer size.
- **Channel Utilization for Stop-and-Wait (HDX)**: $U = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$ where $a = T_p / T_t$.
- **When $a$ is small** (large $L$, short link) → $U \to 1$ → HDX Stop-and-Wait is efficient.
- **When $a$ is large** (small $L$, long link like satellite) → $U \to 0$ → need sliding-window protocol in FDX.
- **FDD** uses **different frequencies** for each direction; **TDD** uses **alternating time slots** on the same frequency.
- **Modern Ethernet switches** operate in **Full-Duplex** by default — no CSMA/CD collision handling required.
- **Walkie-talkie, CB radio, Ethernet hubs** → **Half-Duplex** examples.
- **TV, radio, keyboard, sensor** → **Simplex** examples.
- **4G/5G mobile, telephone PSTN, switched Ethernet** → **Full-Duplex** examples.
- **CSMA/CD** is a **Half-Duplex** Media Access Control protocol used in legacy Ethernet.
- **A single physical fiber** can carry **thousands of FDX channels** using **WDM (Wavelength-Division Multiplexing)** — a Full-Duplex enhancement.
- **For KTU 14-mark questions**, always include: (i) neat diagram, (ii) comparison table, (iii) numerical with units, (iv) qualitative comment.
- **For KTU 3-mark questions**, one crisp sentence per mode plus one real-world example each is the safest structure.
- **Echo cancellation** (used in 1000BASE-T and modern modems) allows Full-Duplex over a **single wire pair** by subtracting the locally transmitted signal from the received one.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Asynchronous
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT414 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Asynchronous

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Asynchronous Transmission** is a *data communication technique* in which **each character (or small data unit) is transmitted independently**, with its own framing bits, and the time interval between successive characters is **arbitrary and not synchronized** by a shared clock signal between the transmitter and receiver.

In asynchronous transmission, the receiver does not know in advance *when* a character will arrive. Therefore, every character is "re-synchronized" at the receiver by means of a **Start bit**, followed by the data bits, an optional **Parity bit**, and one or more **Stop bits**. The idle state of the line is logically **1 (MARK)**.

Formally, if a character has $n$ data bits, the total frame size is:

$$ L_{\text{frame}} = 1\ (\text{Start}) + n\ (\text{Data}) + 1\ (\text{Parity, optional}) + 1, 1.5, \text{ or } 2\ (\text{Stop bits}) $$

For the standard 10-bit frame ($n = 8$, no parity, 1 stop bit):

$$ L_{\text{frame}} = 1 + 8 + 1 = 10\ \text{bits/character} $$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Postman and the Letterbox"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine sending letters to a friend through a postman. You do **not** know exactly *when* your friend is standing at the letterbox. So, every time you send a letter:
> 1. You **ring a bell** (this is the **Start bit** — it tells the receiver "wake up, data is coming").
> 2. You hand over the letter content (**data bits**).
> 3. Optionally, you whisper a **checksum word** (the **parity bit**) so the receiver can verify nothing got smudged.
> 4. You **ring a closing bell** (the **Stop bit** — "transmission of this character is done, you may sleep again").
>
> Between two letters, your friend goes back to waiting (the line returns to **idle/MARK = 1**). The next letter can arrive *anytime* — that's why it's called **a-synchronous** (not synchronized in time).

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are **standardized** and frequently used in KTU numerical problems:

| Term | Symbol | Standard Value |
|------|--------|----------------|
| Idle Line State | MARK | Logic **1** |
| Start Bit | SB | Always Logic **0** |
| Data Bits | $n$ | Typically **5, 6, 7, or 8** (commonly 8) |
| Parity Bit | $P$ | **None, Even, or Odd** |
| Stop Bit(s) | STP | **1, 1.5, or 2** bit-periods |
| Bit Duration | $T_b$ | $\frac{1}{R_b}$ where $R_b$ is bit rate (bps) |
| Bit Rate | $R_b$ | **bits per second (bps)** |
| Baud Rate | $R_s$ | **symbols per second** (for binary, $R_b = R_s$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Hook for Exam:** *Asynchronous = "Self-Framed Characters"*. Every character carries its own synchronization — start bit + data + stop bit. No shared clock needed.

## 1.4 Why Asynchronous Transmission is Used in Engineering

- **Simplicity of hardware** — only a UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter) is needed.
- **No dedicated clock line** — saves wiring in long-distance serial links.
- **Ideal for low-speed, bursty traffic** such as keyboard input, mouse data, legacy serial ports (RS-232), GPS modules, and embedded microcontroller debug ports.
- **Tolerance to irregular gaps** between characters makes it robust for human-generated or event-driven data.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Asynchronous Transmission Works

The transmission pipeline can be broken into the following logical steps:

1. **Idle State:** The communication line sits in the **MARK state (logic 1)**. The receiver continuously samples this state, waiting for a transition.
2. **Start Bit Detection:** The transmitter pulls the line to **SPACE (logic 0)** for exactly **one bit-period $T_b$**. The receiver detects this **1 → 0 transition** and instantly knows a character is beginning. It resets its local bit-counter.
3. **Data Sampling:** The receiver waits for **1.5 $T_b$** from the falling edge, then samples the center of each subsequent data bit. This mid-bit sampling minimizes the effect of clock drift.
4. **Parity Check (Optional):** If parity is enabled, the receiver computes parity on the received data bits and compares it with the incoming parity bit. Mismatch → framing/parity error flag is raised.
5. **Stop Bit Verification:** The receiver expects the line to return to **MARK (logic 1)** for at least the stop-bit duration. If it does not, a **framing error** is reported.
6. **Return to Idle:** The line returns to MARK and the receiver re-enters its "listening" state, ready for the next character.

## 2.2 Bit Rate, Baud Rate & Efficiency

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For binary asynchronous transmission, **1 bit = 1 symbol**, so the **bit rate numerically equals the baud rate**. The difference emerges in the *effective* throughput.

### Effective Throughput / Efficiency

The **efficiency $\eta$** of asynchronous transmission is the ratio of useful data bits to the total bits transmitted per character:

$$ \eta = \frac{n}{1 + n + P + S} $$

where $S$ is the number of stop bits (1, 1.5, or 2).

**Example values (commonly asked in KTU):**

| Frame Format | $n$ | $P$ | $S$ | Total Bits | $\eta$ |
|--------------|-----|-----|-----|------------|--------|
| 8-N-1 (most common) | 8 | 0 | 1 | 10 | **80%** |
| 7-E-1 | 7 | 1 | 1 | 10 | **70%** |
| 7-O-2 | 7 | 1 | 2 | 11 | **~63.6%** |
| 5-N-1.5 | 5 | 0 | 1.5 | 8 | **62.5%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** A common exam trap is to forget that the **Start bit always counts as 1 bit**, regardless of $P$ or $S$. Total bits = $1 + n + P + S$.

## 2.3 Receiver Clock Drift Constraint

Because there is no shared clock, the receiver must resample each bit using its **local clock**. The maximum tolerable clock drift between transmitter and receiver is constrained by the frame duration.

For a frame of $N$ total bits and a local clock tolerance of $\pm \delta$ (fraction), the drift over the frame must not exceed half a bit period:

$$ \delta \leq \frac{1}{2N} $$

**Worked intuition:** A 10-bit frame requires the receiver clock to be accurate to within **±5%** of the transmitter's clock. If $N$ is small (e.g., 5-bit data), the tolerance tightens to **±10%**, but short frames are still robust.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is your **exam-day reference** for all asynchronous transmission numericals.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Bit duration | $T_b = \dfrac{1}{R_b}$ | seconds (s) |
| 2 | Frame length | $L = 1 + n + P + S$ | bits |
| 3 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{n}{L}$ | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 4 | Throughput | $T = R_b \times \eta$ | bits per second (bps) |
| 5 | Time to send a file | $t = \dfrac{\text{File size} \times 8}{T}$ | seconds |
| 6 | Clock tolerance | $\delta \leq \dfrac{1}{2L}$ | fraction (e.g., 0.05 = 5%) |
| 7 | Characters per second | $C = \dfrac{R_b}{L}$ | chars/s |
| 8 | Effective data rate | $R_{\text{eff}} = R_b \times \dfrac{n}{L}$ | bps |
| 9 | Parity check (even) | $P = d_0 \oplus d_1 \oplus \dots \oplus d_{n-1}$ | XOR of data bits |
| 10 | Overhead fraction | $\text{OH} = 1 - \eta = \dfrac{1+P+S}{L}$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** The same $\eta$ formula governs throughput calculations in UART-based IoT devices, Bluetooth SPP (Serial Port Profile) links, and legacy modem standards. Engineers use it to size buffers and estimate battery life in low-power serial sensors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Efficiency of Asynchronous Transmission

**Given:**
- Data bits per character: $n$
- Parity bits: $P \in \{0, 1\}$
- Stop bits: $S$ (typically 1 or 2)
- Start bits: always 1

**Step 1 — Total bits in a frame.**
Each frame carries exactly **one start bit**, $n$ data bits, $P$ parity bits, and $S$ stop bits. Adding all of them:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{frame}} &= \text{Start} + \text{Data} + \text{Parity} + \text{Stop} \\[4pt]
&= 1 + n + P + S
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Useful information bits.**
Out of the $L_{\text{frame}}$ bits transmitted, only $n$ bits carry the *actual user data*. Parity, start, and stop bits are **overhead**.

**Step 3 — Define efficiency.**
Efficiency $\eta$ is the ratio of useful bits to total bits transmitted:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{\text{Useful bits}}{\text{Total bits}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{n}{1 + n + P + S}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check for 8-N-1 (8 data, no parity, 1 stop):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{8}{1 + 8 + 0 + 1} = \frac{8}{10} = 0.80 = 80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the **80%** figure widely cited for the most common UART frame.

## 3.2 Derivation — Receiver Clock Tolerance

**Given:** Frame length $L_{\text{frame}}$ bits, both transmitter and receiver clocks derived from oscillators with relative tolerance $\delta$.

**Step 1 — Bit-period error.**
If transmitter's bit period is $T_b$, receiver's measured bit period is $T_b(1 \pm \delta)$. The accumulated error after $L$ bits is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t &= L \cdot T_b \cdot \delta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Nyquist-style sampling requirement.**
To guarantee sampling inside the correct bit cell, the drift must not exceed **half a bit period**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t &\leq \frac{T_b}{2} \\[4pt]
L \cdot T_b \cdot \delta &\leq \frac{T_b}{2} \\[4pt]
\delta &\leq \frac{1}{2L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation for $L = 10$ (8-N-1 frame):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &\leq \frac{1}{2 \times 10} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05 = 5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

So the receiver's clock can be off by **at most 5%** from the transmitter's clock. This is a very relaxed requirement, which is why cheap UART chips work reliably with low-cost ceramic resonators.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Time to Transmit a File (Typical KTU Numerical)

**Problem:** A serial port uses 8-bit data, no parity, 1 stop bit, operating at **9600 bps**. Calculate the time required to transmit a **50 KB** ASCII file.

**Step 1 — Compute frame length.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 1 + n + P + S = 1 + 8 + 0 + 1 = 10\ \text{bits/character}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute efficiency.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{n}{L} = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute effective throughput.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= R_b \times \eta = 9600 \times 0.8 = 7680\ \text{bps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute total bits in the file.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{File size in bits} &= 50\ \text{KB} \times 1024\ \text{bytes/KB} \times 8\ \text{bits/byte} \\[4pt]
&= 409\,600\ \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute transmission time.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{\text{Total bits}}{T} = \frac{409\,600}{7680} \\[4pt]
&= 53.33\ \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Approximately **53.33 seconds** to transmit the file. (Note: if 1000 is used instead of 1024 for KB, the answer becomes ~52.08 s — KTU typically accepts both with the assumption stated.)

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Asynchronous vs. Synchronous Transmission

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison is **high-yield for KTU 14-mark questions** on data transmission methods.

| Parameter | Asynchronous | Synchronous |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| Clocking | **No shared clock**; per-character resync | **Shared clock** or clock-recovery from data |
| Frame size | **1 character** (typically 8–11 bits) | **Large block / frame** (hundreds–thousands of bits) |
| Overhead | **High** (≥ 20% for 8-N-1) | **Low** (typically a few % preamble + CRC) |
| Hardware complexity | **Low** (simple UART) | **High** (PLL, clock recovery) |
| Speed suitability | **Low-to-medium** (≤ ~115 kbps typical) | **Medium-to-very-high** (kbps to Gbps) |
| Error detection | Optional parity per character | CRC over the whole block |
| Use cases | Keyboards, mice, GPS, RS-232, debug consoles | Ethernet, USB, PCIe, SATA, HDLC |
| Idle line state | MARK (1) | Continuous stream (no idle in synchronous) |
| Latency | **Low per character**, good for interactive | Higher per block, good for bulk transfer |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Asynchronous Frame Structure (Visual Anatomy)

The diagram below shows the **anatomy of a standard 8-N-1 UART frame**, the most commonly tested configuration in KTU Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["IDLE  MARK  1"] --> B["START BIT  0"]
    B --> C["D0"]
    C --> D["D1"]
    D --> E["D2"]
    E --> F["D3"]
    F --> G["D4"]
    G --> H["D5"]
    H --> I["D6"]
    I --> J["D7"]
    J --> K["STOP BIT  1"]
    K --> L["IDLE  MARK  1"]

    style A fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#c0392b,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#27ae60,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#27ae60,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#27ae60,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#27ae60,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#27ae60,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#27ae60,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    style I fill:#27ae60,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#27ae60,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> - **Red cell** = Start bit (always 0).
> - **Green cells** = 8 data bits (LSB-first in UART convention).
> - **Dark cells** = Stop bit + idle return to 1.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Asynchronous Link

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TX["TRANSMITTER SIDE"]
        TX1["Parallel Data Source"] --> TX2["UART TX Engine"]
        TX2 --> TX3["Start Bit Generator"]
        TX3 --> TX4["Data Serializer LSB First"]
        TX4 --> TX5["Optional Parity Generator"]
        TX5 --> TX6["Stop Bit Appender"]
        TX6 --> TX7["Line Driver RS232"]
    end

    TX7 -->|"Serial bits 1 by 1"| CH["TRANSMISSION CHANNEL"]

    subgraph RX["RECEIVER SIDE"]
        CH --> RX1["Line Receiver RS232"]
        RX1 --> RX2["Start Bit Detector 1 to 0 Edge"]
        RX2 --> RX3["Local Bit Counter and Mid Bit Sampler"]
        RX3 --> RX4["Data Deserializer"]
        RX4 --> RX5["Parity Checker"]
        RX5 --> RX6["Stop Bit Validator"]
        RX6 --> RX7["Parallel Data Output"]
    end

    style TX fill:#e8f4f8,stroke:#1f3a5f
    style RX fill:#fdf2e9,stroke:#c0392b
    style CH fill:#fef9e7,stroke:#7d6608
```

## 4.3 Timing Diagram — Start-Bit-Triggered Resynchronization

```mermaid
gantt
    title Asynchronous Sampling Timeline (Conceptual)
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s

    section Line State
    IDLE MARK              :a1, 0, 5
    Start Bit Logic 0      :a2, 5, 1
    D0                     :a3, 6, 1
    D1                     :a4, 7, 1
    D2                     :a5, 8, 1
    D3                     :a6, 9, 1
    D4                     :a7, 10, 1
    D5                     :a8, 11, 1
    D6                     :a9, 12, 1
    D7                     :a10, 13, 1
    Stop Bit Logic 1       :a11, 14, 1

    section Receiver Sampling
    Edge Detect            :crit, b1, 5, 1
    Sample Mid D0          :crit, b2, 6, 1
    Sample Mid D1          :crit, b3, 7, 1
    Sample Mid D2          :crit, b4, 8, 1
    Sample Mid D3          :crit, b5, 9, 1
    Sample Mid D4          :crit, b6, 10, 1
    Sample Mid D5          :crit, b7, 11, 1
    Sample Mid D6          :crit, b8, 12, 1
    Sample Mid D7          :crit, b9, 13, 1
    Verify Stop            :crit, b10, 14, 1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The receiver samples **at the midpoint** of each bit (not the edge) to maximize noise margin. The local bit counter is **reset on every start bit**, which is why a long idle gap does not corrupt the next character.

## 4.4 Decision Flow — How a UART Receiver Validates a Character

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Power ON  Start Listening"] --> B{"Line MARK  1"}
    B -- "Yes  continue waiting" --> B
    B -- "No  detected 1 to 0 transition" --> C["Start bit confirmed  reset local counter"]
    C --> D["Wait 1.5 bit periods"]
    D --> E["Sample bit 0  LSB"]
    E --> F["Sample remaining 7 bits  MSB last"]
    F --> G{"Parity enabled"}
    G -- "No" --> I
    G -- "Yes" --> H["Check parity bit matches computed"]
    H -- "Mismatch" --> X1["Raise PARITY ERROR flag"]
    H -- "Match" --> I["Verify line returns to MARK for stop period"]
    I -- "Line stays low" --> X2["Raise FRAMING ERROR flag"]
    I -- "Line returns to MARK" --> Y["CHARACTER ACCEPTED  Output to parallel bus"]
    Y --> B

    style A fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style Y fill:#27ae60,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style X1 fill:#c0392b,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    style X2 fill:#c0392b,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
Define *Asynchronous Transmission*. What is the role of the **start bit** and **stop bit** in this mode of data communication?

**Model Answer:**

> **Asynchronous Transmission** is a method of serial data communication in which each character is transmitted independently with its own framing bits, and the time gap between two successive characters is not fixed or synchronized by a shared clock.

**Role of Start bit:**
- It is always a **logic 0** (SPACE).
- It signals the **beginning of a new character** to the receiver.
- The receiver detects the **1 → 0 transition** on the line (which was previously idle in MARK = 1) and synchronizes its local bit counter to this edge.
- It essentially "wakes up" the receiver.

**Role of Stop bit(s):**
- It is always a **logic 1** (MARK).
- It signals the **end of the current character**.
- It guarantees that the line returns to the idle state before the next character begins.
- It gives the receiver a minimum time window to process the received byte.

> [!NOTE]
> A 8-N-1 frame uses **1 start bit + 8 data bits + 1 stop bit = 10 bits per character**.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **asynchronous serial data format** for transmitting the ASCII character **'K'** (binary `1001011`) using **8 data bits, no parity, and 1 stop bit**.

**Model Answer:**

ASCII 'K' = `1001011` (7 bits). To send 8 data bits, we **left-pad with a 0** → `01001011`. The LSB is sent first in UART convention, so the transmission order on the line is:

`1 1 0 1 0 0 1 0` (D0 → D7)

| Bit Position | Function | Value |
|--------------|----------|-------|
| Start | Start bit | **0** |
| D0 | LSB | 1 |
| D1 | — | 1 |
| D2 | — | 0 |
| D3 | — | 1 |
| D4 | — | 0 |
| D5 | — | 0 |
| D6 | — | 1 |
| D7 | MSB | 0 |
| Stop | Stop bit | **1** |

```
Line:  ___      _ _   _   _ _   _      ___
        \____/_X_X\_X___X_X___X______/
        IDLE ST  D0 D1 D2 D3 D4 D5 D6 D7 STP IDLE
```

> The line is **idle (MARK = 1)** before start, drops to **0 for the start bit**, transmits 8 data bits **LSB first**, then returns to **1 for the stop bit** and stays idle.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation pattern**, Module 1 Part B questions offer an **internal choice** between two questions. We provide both options.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Asynchronous Transmission Deep Dive**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

#### (a) Explain the working of Asynchronous Serial Transmission with a neat block diagram. Discuss the function of UART. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Asynchronous serial transmission is a *character-oriented* data transmission technique where each character is transmitted as an independent unit framed by start and stop bits. The transmitter and receiver operate using their **own local clocks**, and resynchronization happens at the start of every character.

**Block Diagram (described in text):**

The transmitter side consists of:
1. **Parallel Data Source** — supplies the character to be sent (e.g., CPU data bus).
2. **UART Transmit Engine** — accepts the parallel byte and adds framing.
3. **Shift Register** — converts parallel byte into a serial bit stream.
4. **Line Driver** — converts TTL/CMOS logic levels to RS-232 voltage levels (e.g., ±12 V).

The receiver side consists of:
1. **Line Receiver** — converts RS-232 levels back to TTL/CMOS.
2. **Start Bit Detector** — monitors the line for a 1 → 0 transition.
3. **Bit Counter & Sampler** — samples each bit at its midpoint using a local clock running at $16\times$ the bit rate (oversampling for accuracy).
4. **Shift Register** — deserializes the incoming bits into a parallel byte.
5. **Parity Checker & Stop Bit Validator** — perform error checks.
6. **Parallel Data Output** — delivers the byte to the CPU/data bus.

**Function of UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter):**
- UART is the **hardware block** that implements asynchronous serial communication.
- It performs **parallel-to-serial conversion** on transmit and **serial-to-parallel conversion** on receive.
- It **automatically inserts/removes** start, stop, and parity bits.
- It supports **configurable parameters**: baud rate, data bits, parity type, stop bits.
- It uses a **baud-rate generator** (typically a divisor of the system clock) to derive the sampling clock.
- Common ICs: 8250, 16550 (PC UARTs); built-in UART modules in microcontrollers (e.g., 8051, ARM Cortex-M).

**Operation flow:** When the CPU writes a byte to the UART transmit register, the UART:
1. Adds the start bit.
2. Optionally computes and adds parity.
3. Shifts out the bits serially at the programmed baud rate.
4. Adds the stop bit(s).
5. Raises a "transmit complete" interrupt/flag for the CPU.

The reverse happens on the receive side.

---

#### (b) A system uses asynchronous transmission with **7 data bits, even parity, and 1 stop bit**, operating at **4800 bps**.
1. Compute the **frame efficiency**.
2. Compute the **time taken to transmit 1000 characters**.
3. What is the **maximum tolerable clock drift** between transmitter and receiver? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Frame Efficiency:**

Given: $n = 7$, $P = 1$ (even parity), $S = 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 1 + n + P + S \\[4pt]
&= 1 + 7 + 1 + 1 = 10\ \text{bits/character}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{n}{L} = \frac{7}{10} = 0.70 = 70\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part 2 — Time to transmit 1000 characters:**

Total bits sent:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total bits} &= 1000 \times 10 = 10\,000\ \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

Time to transmit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{\text{Total bits}}{R_b} = \frac{10\,000}{4800} \\[4pt]
&= 2.0833\ \text{seconds} \approx 2.08\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part 3 — Maximum clock drift:**

For a frame of $L = 10$ bits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{\max} &= \frac{1}{2L} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10} = \frac{1}{20} \\[4pt]
&= 0.05 = 5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

The receiver's clock may differ from the transmitter's by **at most ±5%**.

**Final Answers:**
- Efficiency = **70%**
- Time = **2.083 s**
- Clock tolerance = **±5%**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

#### (a) Compare **Asynchronous and Synchronous Transmission** in detail. Mention at least **six points** of comparison. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

| # | Parameter | Asynchronous | Synchronous |
|---|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Clocking** | No shared clock; each character is self-framed | Shared clock line or clock-recovered from data stream |
| 2 | **Data Unit** | Single character (typically 5–8 bits) | Large block of characters (frame) |
| 3 | **Framing** | Start bit + data + parity + stop bit | Preamble + data + CRC + trailer |
| 4 | **Overhead** | High (20–40% for short frames) | Low (typically < 5%) |
| 5 | **Speed** | Low to medium (up to ~115 kbps typical) | Medium to very high (Mbps to Gbps) |
| 6 | **Hardware** | Simple UART | Complex — needs PLL, clock-recovery |
| 7 | **Error Detection** | Optional simple parity per character | Strong CRC over the entire block |
| 8 | **Latency** | Very low per character (good for interactive I/O) | Higher per block (good for bulk transfer) |
| 9 | **Cost** | Low | Higher |
| 10 | **Examples** | RS-232, keyboard, mouse, GPS NMEA | Ethernet, USB, SATA, HDLC, PCIe |

**Conclusion:** Asynchronous transmission is preferred for **low-speed, bursty, character-oriented** data (e.g., user terminals, sensors), while synchronous transmission dominates in **high-speed, block-oriented** communication (e.g., LANs, storage, video).

---

#### (b) A 1200 bps asynchronous link uses **8 data bits, no parity, and 2 stop bits**. Calculate:
1. The **number of bits transmitted per character**.
2. The **transmission efficiency**.
3. The **effective data rate** in characters per second.
4. The **time to transmit a 200 KB file** (1 KB = 1024 bytes). **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1 — Bits per character:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 1 + n + P + S = 1 + 8 + 0 + 2 = 11\ \text{bits/character}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part 2 — Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{n}{L} = \frac{8}{11} \approx 0.7273 = 72.73\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part 3 — Effective data rate in characters per second:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{R_b}{L} = \frac{1200}{11} \approx 109.09\ \text{characters/second}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part 4 — Time to transmit 200 KB file:**

Total bits in file:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bits} &= 200 \times 1024 \times 8 = 1\,638\,400\ \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

Effective data throughput:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{eff}} &= R_b \times \eta = 1200 \times 0.7273 = 872.73\ \text{bps}
\end{aligned}
$$

Transmission time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{\text{Bits}}{R_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{1\,638\,400}{872.73} \\[4pt]
&\approx 1877.4\ \text{seconds} \approx 31.29\ \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- Bits/character = **11**
- Efficiency = **72.73%**
- Characters/second ≈ **109.09**
- Transmission time ≈ **1877.4 s (≈ 31.3 min)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Start bit:** Many students compute frame size as $n + P + S$, missing the mandatory **1 start bit**. This loses **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing Baud Rate with Bit Rate:** For binary signaling they are equal numerically, but the examiner wants you to *state* they are equal and not just assume it.
> 3. **Using 1000 instead of 1024 for KB:** Always write the assumption explicitly. If you use 1000, the final answer differs by ~2.4%.
> 4. **Misapplying the clock-tolerance formula:** It is $\delta \leq \frac{1}{2L}$ — not $\frac{1}{L}$ and not $\frac{T_b}{2}$ alone. Watch the units.
> 5. **Parity direction (even vs odd):** Even parity means total 1s (including parity) is even; odd means odd. A parity-mismatch diagram can score full marks only if the convention is stated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Asynchronous Transmission (Module 1)**

- **Asynchronous = "Start–Stop" transmission.** No shared clock between Tx and Rx.
- **Idle line = MARK (logic 1).** Start bit = SPACE (logic 0). Stop bit(s) = MARK (logic 1).
- **Standard frame size** for 8-N-1 is **10 bits/character**: 1 start + 8 data + 0 parity + 1 stop.
- **Efficiency formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{n}{1 + n + P + S}$. For 8-N-1, $\eta = 80\%$.
- **Throughput = Bit rate × Efficiency**, i.e., $T = R_b \cdot \eta$.
- **Clock tolerance** between Tx and Rx: $\delta \leq \dfrac{1}{2L}$. For 10-bit frames, **±5%** is acceptable.
- **Bit rate = Baud rate** for binary asynchronous links (each bit = 1 symbol).
- **Receiver samples at mid-bit**, not at the edge — to maximize noise margin and tolerate clock drift.
- **UART** is the dedicated hardware block that performs framing, serialization, and oversampling.
- **Asynchronous is ideal for low-speed, bursty, character-oriented traffic** (keyboards, mice, GPS, debug consoles, RS-232 sensors).
- **Synchronous transmission** is preferred for **high-speed bulk transfer**; uses shared clock or clock recovery with low overhead.
- **Parity bit** is a simple single-bit error-detection mechanism; CRC is far more powerful but used in synchronous protocols.
- **Common exam numbers:** 9600 bps, 8-N-1, $\eta = 80\%$, 10 bits/char, 960 chars/s, 5% clock tolerance.
- **Key contrasts to memorize:** Overhead (high vs low), hardware (simple vs complex), block size (1 char vs many), clock (none vs shared), examples (RS-232 vs Ethernet).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchronous
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT414 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Synchronous

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Data Communication** (Module 1, Unit: *Data Transmission Modes & Encoding*), **Synchronous Transmission** is defined as a data transfer method in which a continuous, time-staggered stream of **data bits** (organized into structured units called **frames** or **blocks**) is transmitted between the source and destination under the control of a **shared timing clock** (or a tightly synchronized clock-recovery mechanism derived from the bit stream itself).

Unlike asynchronous transmission, the sender and receiver do **not** rely on start/stop bits for every character. Instead, they agree in advance (handshake) on:
- **Bit duration** ($T_b = 1 / R_b$)
- **Block length** (typically hundreds to thousands of bits)
- **Framing / synchronization pattern** (e.g., preamble, flag bytes, SYNC characters)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight (CO1 – Apply):** Synchronous transmission is the **dominant mode in modern high-speed networks** (Ethernet, SONET/SDH, Wi-Fi, LTE, 5G NR) because it eliminates the **20–30% overhead** of start/stop bits present in asynchronous transmission.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Boundary Values for KTU Problems:**
> - Asynchronous overhead per character: **2 bits (1 start + 1 stop)** → ~20% for 8-bit data
> - Synchronous efficiency: typically **> 95%** (only flag/CRC overhead)
> - Bit rate unit: **bits per second (bps / bit/s)**
> - Bit duration unit: **seconds (s)**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two soldiers marching in step on a parade ground:

| Mode | Analogy | Behavior |
|------|---------|----------|
| **Asynchronous** | Two runners passing a baton independently; the receiver says "go" before each character. | Each character is "wrapped" with start/stop bits (≈ 2 extra bits per 8 bits of data). Simple, but inefficient. |
| **Synchronous** | Two soldiers in a drill squad marching to a single **drum beat (shared clock)**. | Both ends share a fixed rhythm. Long messages flow in **one steady stream** with a small **"boundary marker"** at the start and end of each block. |

> A **drummer beating a steady tempo** = the **shared clock signal**.
> A **squad of 100 soldiers** moving together = a **single synchronous frame** of many bits.
> A **flag/sync word** at the front of the row = the **frame delimiter**.

This is why synchronous transmission is used wherever **speed, efficiency, and bulk transfer** matter — exactly what KTU Module 1 expects you to recognize.

---

## 1.3 Where Synchronous Transmission Fits in the KTU Data Communication Stack

$$\underbrace{\text{Application}}_{\text{User Data}} \;\rightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{Encoding}}_{\text{NRZ, Manchester, 8B/10B}} \;\rightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{Framing}}_{\text{Flags / SYNC patterns}} \;\rightarrow\; \underbrace{\text{Transmit}}_{\text{Continuous stream}}$$

The **timing recovery** happens at the **Physical Layer** (clock embedded in bit transitions — e.g., Manchester encoding guarantees at least one transition per bit), while **framing and block synchronization** happen at the **Data Link Layer**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

Synchronous transmission operates through a deterministic sequence. Below is the **canonical KTU valuation flow** examiners expect in 14-mark answers.

- **Step 1 — Clock Establishment (Bit Synchronization)**
  The transmitter and receiver lock to a common bit-timing reference. Three practical methods:
  1. **Dedicated clock line** (parallel transmission, e.g., old I²S, SPI).
  2. **Clock-recovery from data** using **PLL (Phase-Locked Loop)** on edge transitions.
  3. **Self-clocking line codes** — *Manchester* and *Differential Manchester* (IEEE 802.3 legacy, 10 Mbps Ethernet).

- **Step 2 — Block / Frame Assembly**
  The transmitter collects data into frames of fixed size. A typical synchronous frame:
  $$\text{Frame} = \underbrace{\text{Flag}}_{8 \text{ bits}} \;||\; \underbrace{\text{Header}}_{\text{Addr + Ctrl}} \;||\; \underbrace{\text{Data Payload}}_{L \text{ bits}} \;||\; \underbrace{\text{CRC}}_{n \text{ bits}} \;||\; \underbrace{\text{Flag}}_{8 \text{ bits}}$$

- **Step 3 — Continuous Streaming**
  Frames are sent **back-to-back** with **no idle bits** between them. Some protocols (HDLC, SDLC) insert a `0` after five consecutive `1`s (**bit stuffing**) to prevent flag-byte imitation.

- **Step 4 — Receiver Re-synchronization (Block / Character Sync)**
  The receiver scans incoming bits for the known **flag pattern** (e.g., `01111110` in HDLC). Once detected, it knows the byte boundary and can read the rest of the frame.

- **Step 5 — Acknowledgment / Retransmission (Optional, Layer-2 protocol dependent)**
  Higher-layer protocols (e.g., HDLC, PPP) may add **ARQ (Automatic Repeat reQuest)** logic on top.

---

## 2.2 Why Synchronous? The "Why" Behind Each Design Choice

| Design Choice | Engineering Reason | Real-World Use |
|---------------|--------------------|----------------|
| Shared clock / self-clocking | Avoids drift on long-distance high-bit-rate links | SONET, SDH, Optical fiber |
| Large frames | Reduces protocol overhead per useful data bit | 1500-byte Ethernet MTU |
| Flag bytes at boundaries | Allows receiver to re-locate bit/byte sync after noise burst | HDLC, PPP over leased lines |
| Bit stuffing (HDLC) | Guarantees flag pattern uniqueness regardless of payload | All HDLC-derived protocols |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|------------------|------------|----------------|
| 1 | Bit duration | $T_b = \dfrac{1}{R_b}$ | seconds (s) |
| 2 | Frame transmission time | $T_f = \dfrac{L_f}{R_b}$ | seconds; $L_f$ = frame length in bits |
| 3 | Data rate (useful info) | $R_{\text{info}} = R_b \times \eta$ | bps; $\eta$ = efficiency |
| 4 | Synchronous efficiency (typical) | $\eta = \dfrac{L_{\text{data}}}{L_{\text{frame}}} \approx 0.95$–$0.99$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Asynchronous efficiency (per char) | $\eta_{\text{async}} = \dfrac{n}{n+2}$ for $n$-bit data | e.g., $8/10 = 0.8$ |
| 6 | Number of frames to send a file | $N_f = \left\lceil \dfrac{F}{L_{\text{data}}} \right\rceil$ | $F$ = file size in bits |
| 7 | Total transmission time (sync) | $T_{\text{total}} = N_f \times T_f$ | seconds |
| 8 | Throughput (sync) | $S = \dfrac{F}{T_{\text{total}} + T_{\text{prop}}}$ | bps; includes propagation |
| 9 | Bandwidth–delay product | $BD = R_b \times T_{\text{prop}}$ | bits in the pipe |
| 10 | PLL lock range (qualitative) | $f_{\text{carrier}} \pm \Delta f$ | Hz; governs synchronous recovery |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiners love comparing** $\eta_{\text{sync}}$ vs $\eta_{\text{async}}$ using formulas 4 and 5. Memorize both.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications backbone:** SONET/SDH (e.g., **STM-1 = 155.52 Mbps**, **STM-64 = 10 Gbps**) is purely synchronous.
- **Cellular:** 4G LTE and 5G NR are **synchronous OFDMA** systems requiring tight sub-frame alignment.
- **Enterprise storage:** Fibre Channel, SATA, PCIe — all synchronous.
- **Industrial control:** PROFIBUS, EtherCAT — synchronous for real-time determinism.

This is *why* Module 1 spends an entire unit on it: the **modern Internet cannot exist without synchronous transmission**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical — A Typical KTU 14-Mark Calculation

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):**
> A file of size **$F = 2$ MB** is to be transmitted over a synchronous link of bit rate $R_b = 1$ Mbps. Each synchronous frame carries $L_{\text{data}} = 8000$ bits of payload, plus 200 bits of overhead (flags + header + CRC). Compute:
> (a) Frame efficiency, (b) Number of frames required, (c) Total transmission time, (d) Compare efficiency with an 8-bit asynchronous scheme that uses 1 start + 1 stop bit per character.

### 3.1.1 Given Data (in canonical KTU form)

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 2 \text{ MB} = 2 \times 2^{20} \text{ bytes} = 2^{21} \text{ bytes} = 8 \times 2^{20} \text{ bits} = 8\,388\,608 \text{ bits} \\
R_b &= 1 \text{ Mbps} = 10^{6} \text{ bits/sec} \\
L_{\text{data}} &= 8000 \text{ bits} \\
L_{\text{OH}} &= 200 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Part (a) — Frame Efficiency $\eta$

Total frame size:
$$
L_f = L_{\text{data}} + L_{\text{OH}} = 8000 + 200 = 8200 \text{ bits}
$$

Efficiency:
$$
\eta = \frac{L_{\text{data}}}{L_f} = \frac{8000}{8200} = 0.9756 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \eta \approx 97.56\%
$$

> **Logic:** Synchronous efficiency is the *fraction of frame bits carrying useful user data*. A value of $\sim 97.56\%$ confirms the "high-efficiency" claim in the textbook.

### 3.1.3 Part (b) — Number of Frames $N_f$

$$
N_f = \left\lceil \frac{F}{L_{\text{data}}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{8\,388\,608}{8000} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 1048.576 \right\rceil = 1049 \text{ frames}
$$

> **Logic:** You cannot send a *fraction* of a frame. The ceiling function enforces whole-frame delivery. 1048 full frames cover $1048 \times 8000 = 8\,384\,000$ bits; the last frame carries the remaining $4608$ bits.

### 3.1.4 Part (c) — Total Transmission Time $T_{\text{total}}$

Time per full frame:
$$
T_f = \frac{L_f}{R_b} = \frac{8200}{10^{6}} = 8.2 \text{ ms}
$$

Total time:
$$
T_{\text{total}} = N_f \times T_f = 1049 \times 8.2 \text{ ms} = 8601.8 \text{ ms} \approx 8.602 \text{ s}
$$

> **Logic:** Because the last frame is partially full, an *exact* time is $T_f \times (N_f - 1) + T_{\text{last}}$, but for KTU valuation the simplified $N_f \times T_f$ is accepted when overhead is small (within **2% tolerance** — a standard examiner allowance).

### 3.1.5 Part (d) — Comparison with Asynchronous (8-bit, 1 start + 1 stop)

Asynchronous character size:
$$
n_{\text{async}} = 8 + 1_{\text{start}} + 1_{\text{stop}} = 10 \text{ bits/char}
$$

Asynchronous efficiency:
$$
\eta_{\text{async}} = \frac{8}{10} = 0.80 = 80\%
$$

Difference in efficiency:
$$
\Delta\eta = \eta_{\text{sync}} - \eta_{\text{async}} = 0.9756 - 0.80 = 0.1756 \;\;(\approx 17.56\% \text{ better})
$$

Equivalent asynchronous time (assuming the same physical bit rate of 1 Mbps):
$$
T_{\text{async}} = \frac{F \times \tfrac{10}{8}}{R_b} = \frac{8\,388\,608 \times 1.25}{10^{6}} \approx 10.486 \text{ s}
$$

> **Logic:** Asynchronous requires **1.25×** more bit-times for the same user payload. Over a 2 MB file, that is **~1.88 seconds wasted on start/stop overhead alone** — a classic KTU conclusion.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Decision Aid for "Sync vs Async"

| Criterion | Synchronous | Asynchronous |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| Overhead per user data unit | Very low (frame-level) | High (2 bits per character) |
| Typical efficiency | **95% – 99%** | 70% – 80% |
| Speed suitability | **High (Mbps → Tbps)** | Low (≤ 115.2 kbps historically) |
| Hardware complexity | **High (PLL, framing logic)** | Low (simple UART) |
| Best for | Bulk data, networks, telecom | Keyboard, mouse, legacy serial ports |
| Error detection | Yes (CRC at frame end) | Optional (parity) |
| Cost | **Expensive** | **Cheap** |
| Examples | Ethernet, SONET, HDLC, LTE | RS-232, UART, old teletypes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Synchronous Frame Structure (HDLC-style)

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the canonical synchronous frame format and the **bit-stuffing mechanism** that guarantees a unique flag.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FLAG 01111110] --> B[ADDRESS 8 bits]
    B --> C[CONTROL 8 bits]
    C --> D[PAYLOAD Variable]
    D --> E[FCS CRC 16 or 32 bits]
    E --> F[FLAG 01111110]

    subgraph BIT_STUFFING[Bit Stuffing Rule]
        G[Sender inserts 0 after five consecutive 1s]
        H[Receiver removes 0 after five consecutive 1s]
    end

    A -.contains.-> G
    F -.contains.-> G
    G -.reversed by.-> H
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Synchronous Link

This diagram maps the **functional interactions** between transmitter, channel, and receiver in a synchronous system, replacing what would otherwise be a complex physical-layer schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TX[Transmitter Side]
        APP[Application Data Source] --> FRAMER[Framer Block Assembler]
        CLK1[Local Clock Oscillator] --> PLL1[PLL Multiplier]
        FRAMER --> ENC[Line Encoder NRZ or Manchester]
        PLL1 --> ENC
    end

    subgraph CH[Physical Channel]
        ENC --> CHANNEL[Copper or Fiber or Wireless]
    end

    subgraph RX[Receiver Side]
        CHANNEL --> DEC[Line Decoder]
        DEC --> CDR[Clock Data Recovery PLL]
        CDR -->|Recovered Clock| DEC
        DEC --> DEFRAMER[Deframer and CRC Checker]
        DEFRAMER --> APPOUT[Application Data Sink]
    end

    style TX fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366
    style RX fill:#fff3e6,stroke:#663300
    style CH fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#333333
```

> **Reading the diagram:**
> - The **PLL Multiplier** at the transmitter and the **Clock Data Recovery (CDR) PLL** at the receiver together guarantee **bit synchronization** without a dedicated clock wire.
> - The **Line Encoder** is the *self-clocking* component (e.g., Manchester encoding embeds the clock into the data stream).
> - The **Framer / Deframer** pair implements **block synchronization** (locating the start of each frame using flag patterns).

## 4.3 Timing Comparison — Asynchronous vs Synchronous (Waveform Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ASYNC[Asynchronous Stream 8 bit char]
        S1[Idle] --> ST1[Start 0]
        ST1 --> D1[D0 D1 D2 D3 D4 D5 D6 D7]
        D1 --> SP1[Stop 1]
        SP1 --> ID1[Idle Gap]
    end

    subgraph SYNC[Synchronous Stream Continuous]
        F1[Flag] --> H1[Header]
        H1 --> P1[Payload 1000 bits]
        P1 --> C1[CRC]
        C1 --> F2[Flag Next Frame Starts Immediately]
    end

    style ASYNC fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000
    style SYNC fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
```

> The key visible difference: **no idle gaps** and **no per-character start/stop** in the synchronous stream — only frame-boundary flags.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023 (Model)]
**Define synchronous transmission. Why is it preferred over asynchronous transmission in modern high-speed networks?**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~60–80 words):**
- **1 mark — Definition:** Synchronous transmission is a data transfer mode in which bits are sent as a **continuous, timed stream** under a **shared clock** (or clock-recovered from the data), grouped into **frames** of fixed/known size.
- **1 mark — Reason 1:** It eliminates the **per-character start/stop bit overhead**, raising efficiency to **> 95%**.
- **1 mark — Reason 2:** A shared/recovered clock supports **high bit rates (Mbps–Tbps)** without timing drift, making it suitable for Ethernet, SONET, LTE, and 5G.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 (Model)]
**List any three differences between synchronous and asynchronous transmission.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, table format preferred):**

| Sl. | Synchronous | Asynchronous |
|-----|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | Continuous stream; no idle gaps | Character-by-character with start/stop bits |
| 2 | High efficiency (≈ 95–99%) | Low efficiency (≈ 70–80%) |
| 3 | Used in high-speed networks (Ethernet, SONET) | Used in low-speed legacy ports (RS-232, UART) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module-Internal-Choice Questions

> **Module 1, Question Pair: Both carry 14 marks. Answer ANY ONE.**

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 pattern]*

**A.** Synchronous transmission uses a structured frame and a shared clock. With reference to a **synchronous link**:
**(a)** Draw and explain the **typical synchronous frame format** (HDLC-style). State the purpose of each field. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A 4 MB file is transmitted synchronously at $R_b = 2$ Mbps. Each frame carries $L_{\text{data}} = 12\,000$ bits of payload and **240 bits of overhead**. Compute the **frame efficiency**, the **number of frames**, and the **total transmission time**. Compare the total time with the **asynchronous 8-bit character mode** (1 start + 1 stop bit). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Frame Format & Field Purposes (7 marks)**

The HDLC-style synchronous frame has the structure:

$$
\text{Frame} \;=\; \underbrace{\text{Flag}}_{8} \;||\; \underbrace{\text{Address}}_{8} \;||\; \underbrace{\text{Control}}_{8} \;||\; \underbrace{\text{Information}}_{L} \;||\; \underbrace{\text{FCS}}_{\text{16 or 32}} \;||\; \underbrace{\text{Flag}}_{8}
$$

| Field | Size | Purpose |
|-------|------|---------|
| **Opening Flag** | 8 bits (`01111110`) | Marks frame start; also used for sync |
| **Address** | 8 bits (extendable) | Identifies source/destination secondary station |
| **Control** | 8 bits | Specifies frame type (I-frame, S-frame, U-frame) |
| **Information** | Variable | Carries user payload (may be 0 in supervisory frames) |
| **FCS** | 16 or 32 bits | **Frame Check Sequence** — CRC for error detection |
| **Closing Flag** | 8 bits | Marks frame end; may serve as opening flag of next frame |

**Bit stuffing** rule: sender inserts a `0` after every five consecutive `1`s to guarantee the flag pattern is never imitated inside the payload. The receiver reverses this. **(2 marks for stuffing explanation)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

Given:
$$
F = 4 \text{ MB} = 4 \times 2^{20} \text{ bytes} = 33\,554\,432 \text{ bits}
$$
$$
R_b = 2 \text{ Mbps} = 2 \times 10^{6} \text{ bps}, \quad L_{\text{data}} = 12\,000, \quad L_{\text{OH}} = 240
$$

**Step 1 — Frame size & efficiency (2 marks):**
$$
L_f = 12\,240 \text{ bits}, \qquad \eta = \frac{12\,000}{12\,240} = 0.9804 \approx 98.04\%
$$

**Step 2 — Number of frames (2 marks):**
$$
N_f = \left\lceil \frac{33\,554\,432}{12\,000} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 2796.20 \right\rceil = 2797 \text{ frames}
$$

**Step 3 — Total synchronous transmission time (2 marks):**
$$
T_f = \frac{12\,240}{2 \times 10^{6}} = 6.12 \text{ ms}
$$
$$
