T_{\text{sync}} = 2797 \times 6.12 \text{ ms} \approx 17.118 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Asynchronous comparison (1 mark):**
$$
T_{\text{async}} = \frac{33\,554\,432 \times 1.25}{2 \times 10^{6}} \approx 20.972 \text{ s}
$$
$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{async}} - T_{\text{sync}} \approx 3.85 \text{ s} \;(\text{≈ 22.5\% overhead saved by synchronous mode})
$$

> **Conclusion (mandatory last line for full marks):** Synchronous transmission saves **~3.85 seconds (22.5%)** of link time on a 4 MB file compared to asynchronous 8-bit mode, validating its use in modern networks.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 marks) — *[Alternative choice]*

**B.** **(a)** Explain the **concept of clock recovery and self-clocking line codes** in synchronous transmission. Illustrate with **Manchester and NRZ-I encoding** waveforms for the bit stream `0 1 1 0 0 1 0 1`. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Define **bandwidth–delay product (BDP)**. A synchronous link of $R_b = 10$ Gbps and one-way propagation delay $T_p = 25$ ms is used to send a 1 GB file with frames of $L_f = 12\,000$ bits. Compute **(i)** BDP, **(ii)** number of frames, **(iii)** minimum total time assuming pipelining (frames sent back-to-back, propagation counted once). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Clock Recovery & Self-Clocking Codes (7 marks)**

**Self-clocking line codes** (e.g., Manchester, Differential Manchester) embed the **clock transitions** within the data signal itself, removing the need for a separate clock wire.

- **NRZ-I (Non-Return-to-Zero Inverted):** Transition at the **middle of the bit period** represents a `1`; **no transition** represents a `0`. *(Not self-clocking; long runs of 0s cause clock loss.)*
- **Manchester:** A **low-to-high transition** in the middle of the bit = `0`; **high-to-low transition** = `1` (IEEE 802.3 convention). Every bit has a guaranteed mid-bit transition → fully self-clocking.

**Waveform for `0 1 1 0 0 1 0 1`:**

| Bit | Manchester (802.3) | NRZ-I |
|-----|--------------------|-------|
| 0 | ⎍ (low→high) | No transition (level held) |
| 1 | ⎍ (high→low) | Transition at bit boundary |

```
Manchester  :  ⎍⎍⎍⎍⎍⎍⎍⎍   (each bit = mid-bit toggle)
NRZ-I       :  ──╱╲──╱╲   (transition only on 1)
```

The **PLL at the receiver** locks onto the mid-bit transitions and regenerates a clean clock used to sample each bit. **(1 mark for PLL explanation)**

**Part (b) — BDP and Pipelined Transfer (7 marks)**

**(i) Bandwidth–Delay Product (2 marks):**
$$
BDP = R_b \times T_p = 10 \times 10^{9} \times 25 \times 10^{-3} = 2.5 \times 10^{8} \text{ bits} = 250 \text{ Mbit}
$$
> This is the number of bits "in flight" inside the pipe at any moment.

**(ii) Number of frames for 1 GB file (2 marks):**
$$
F = 1 \text{ GB} = 8 \times 2^{30} \text{ bits} = 8\,589\,934\,592 \text{ bits}
$$
$$
N_f = \left\lceil \frac{8\,589\,934\,592}{12\,000} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 715\,827.88 \right\rceil = 715\,828 \text{ frames}
$$

**(iii) Minimum total time with pipelining (3 marks):**

Time to push the first bit across the link = $T_p = 25$ ms.
Time to push the last frame's first bit = last frame transmission time = $\dfrac{12\,000}{10 \times 10^{9}} = 1.2 \,\mu\text{s}$.

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_p + N_f \times T_f = 25 \text{ ms} + 715\,828 \times 1.2 \,\mu\text{s} \approx 25 \text{ ms} + 858.99 \text{ ms} \approx 883.99 \text{ ms}
$$

> **Interpretation:** On a **10 Gbps** link, even sending 1 GB completes in **< 1 second**; propagation contributes **< 3%** of total time — the link is *bandwidth-bound*, not *latency-bound*. This is the defining advantage of high-rate synchronous transmission.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in synchronous-transmission questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ceiling function** in $N_f = \lceil F / L_{\text{data}} \rceil$ — using `floor` or simple division loses **2–3 marks** in 14-mark numericals.
> 2. **Mixing bit vs byte units.** KTU Module 1 defaults to **bits** for bit rate, but file sizes are given in MB/GB. Always convert: $1 \text{ MB} = 8 \times 2^{20}$ bits, **not** $8 \times 10^{6}$. The factor of $2^{20}$ vs $10^{6}$ can cause **15–20% errors**.
> 3. **Ignoring overhead bits in the asynchronous comparison** — students often compare only $T_f$ without inflating by the start/stop factor, losing the **comparison mark**.
> 4. **Confusing bit synchronization (PLL, line codes)** with **block/frame synchronization (flag patterns)** — they are *two different layers* of synchronization. Examiners allocate separate sub-marks for each.
> 5. **Not writing the final conclusion line** ("Synchronous is more efficient because…"). KTU 2024 scheme values **interpretive conclusions** at the end of every numerical.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Synchronous transmission sends data as a **continuous timed stream** grouped into **frames**, with a **shared/recovered clock** — no per-character start/stop bits.
- **Key Components:** Clock (PLL), Framer, Line Encoder (Manchester/NRZ-I), Flag/Preamble, CRC.
- **Two Layers of Synchronization:**
  1. *Bit sync* — via dedicated clock or PLL on edge transitions.
  2. *Block sync* — via flag bytes (`01111110`) and bit stuffing.
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = L_{\text{data}} / L_f$, typically **95–99%**.
- **Asynchronous efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{async}} = n / (n+2)$ → 80% for 8-bit data.
- **Frame transmission time:** $T_f = L_f / R_b$.
- **Number of frames:** $N_f = \lceil F / L_{\text{data}} \rceil$.
- **Bandwidth–delay product:** $BDP = R_b \times T_p$ (bits in flight).
- **Pipelined total time:** $T_{\text{total}} = T_p + N_f \times T_f$.
- **Self-clocking codes:** Manchester, Differential Manchester (transition every bit).
- **Non-self-clocking codes:** NRZ, NRZ-L (problematic for long runs).
- **Bit stuffing (HDLC):** Insert `0` after five `1`s; remove at receiver.
- **Real-world systems using synchronous mode:** Ethernet, SONET/SDH, HDLC/PPP, LTE, 5G NR, Fibre Channel, PCIe, SATA.
- **When to choose sync vs async:** Sync for **bulk/high-speed networks**; Async for **simple, low-speed, point-to-point** legacy peripherals.
- **Examiner keywords to use in answers:** *bit synchronization, block synchronization, frame efficiency, clock recovery, PLL, self-clocking, bit stuffing, overhead, pipelining, BDP*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simplex
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT414 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Simplex

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Data Communication and Computer Networks**, a **Simplex** communication mode (also referred to as **Simplex Transmission** or **Unidirectional Communication**) is defined as a mode of data transmission in which information signals flow in **only one pre-defined direction** between the communicating devices. The channel capacity is dedicated entirely to a single transmitter and a single receiver, with no provision for the receiver to acknowledge, reply, or reverse the flow of data.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The three fundamental transmission modes evaluated by the examiner are *Simplex*, *Half-Duplex*, and *Full-Duplex*. Simplex is the most restrictive in terms of interactivity, but it is the most bandwidth-efficient in dedicated, unidirectional signaling applications.

The two endpoints in a simplex link are structurally asymmetric:

- **Sender (Transmitter / Source):** The device that originates and transmits the data.
- **Receiver (Sink / Destination):** The device that only accepts the incoming data; it cannot transmit back on the same logical channel.

The formal defining parameters are:

- **Direction of Signal Flow:** $S \rightarrow R$ exclusively (One-way).
- **Number of Simultaneous Channels:** **1** logical channel.
- **Bandwidth Allocation:** $100\%$ dedicated to downstream flow.
- **Acknowledgment Mechanism:** **None** (No ACK/NAK frame is expected from the receiver).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **one-way traffic street** in a city. Vehicles can only enter from Point A and exit at Point B. There are no U-turns, no opposite-lane driving, and no return path on that road. A car at Point B cannot honk back or send a message to Point A using that same road. The road itself is exclusively allocated for movement in the $A \rightarrow B$ direction.

A perfect real-world example is a **commercial FM Radio Broadcast**:

- The **Radio Station** is the Sender.
- The **Listeners' FM Receivers** are the Receivers.
- The audio signal travels only from the tower to your car stereo.
- You, as a listener, cannot speak back into the radio to reply to the RJ (Radio Jockey) using the same broadcast frequency.

Another classic analogy is a **public address (PA) system** in an airport terminal: the announcement microphone transmits voice to the loudspeakers, but the loudspeakers have no mechanism to send a voice signal back through the same wire to the announcer.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

The key physical-layer and logical-layer metrics for a simplex channel are:

- **Bandwidth Utilization:** **100%** of the channel is used for one-way data transfer (no time wasted on turnaround).
- **Latency:** **Lowest** among the three modes (no switching/direction-reversal overhead).
- **Hardware Complexity:** **Lowest** (no directional switches or duplexers required).
- **Cost per Meter:** **Lowest** (single copper core / single wavelength / single timeslot).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Examiner's Note:** When a KTU question asks *"Give two examples of simplex communication,"* always quote the **Keyboard-to-CPU** link and the **Radio/TV Broadcast** link. These are the two textbook gold-standard answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic Breakdown

To understand *why* simplex works the way it does, the operational logic can be broken down into the following structured sequence:

- **Step 1 — Channel Allocation:** A physical or logical medium is established. The medium can be a copper wire (electrical), a fiber-optic strand (optical), or a wireless frequency band (RF).
- **Step 2 — Direction Locking:** The hardware (transceivers, antennas, fiber connectors) is hard-wired to send signals in a fixed direction. There is no switch, relay, or duplexer to reverse the direction.
- **Step 3 — Continuous Transmission:** The transmitter sends a continuous bitstream or modulated signal. The data rate is constant, and the channel is never idle (except during idle-frame padding).
- **Step 4 — Passive Reception:** The receiver listens, demodulates, and processes the incoming signal. It does not generate any outbound signal on the same medium.
- **Step 5 — No Feedback Loop:** Since no acknowledgment (ACK) is returned, the protocol is **unreliable by design**. There is no automatic retransmission request (ARQ) or error-correction handshake inherent to a simplex topology.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Simplex

- **Why use Simplex?** When the application does **not require feedback**. Examples include broadcasting entertainment, telemetry from a satellite, sensor data logging, or surveillance camera feeds.
- **How is it implemented?** By using unidirectional transducers (e.g., a unidirectional microphone-to-amplifier cable) or by dedicating a one-way protocol (e.g., **UDP unicast/multicast** without return traffic) on a bidirectional physical medium.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Since Simplex is a qualitative/conceptual topic, the "formulas" relate to channel capacity, propagation, and comparative bandwidth efficiency. **Critical:** the vertical pipe symbol is escaped as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Parameter | Symbol | Value / Formula | Units | KTU Context |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Direction of Flow | $\vec{D}$ | $\vec{D} = S \rightarrow R$ (single vector) | Vector | Simplex is strictly one-way. |
| Number of Logical Channels | $N_c$ | $N_c = 1$ | Count | One channel, one direction. |
| Bandwidth Efficiency | $\eta_B$ | $\eta_B = \dfrac{T_{data}}{T_{data} + T_{idle}} = 100\%$ | Percent (%) | No idle time spent on ACK. |
| Effective Throughput | $S$ | $S = \dfrac{\text{Payload Bits}}{\text{Time}}$ | bits/sec (bps) | Maximum for the given medium. |
| Propagation Delay | $T_p$ | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ where $d$ = distance, $v$ = velocity of signal ($\approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in copper) | seconds (s) | Same as duplex modes; physical property. |
| Bit Error Rate (No ARQ) | $BER$ | $BER = \dfrac{\text{Errored Bits}}{\text{Total Transmitted Bits}}$ | dimensionless | No retransmission; errors are permanent. |
| Shannon Capacity Bound | $C$ | $C = B \cdot \log_2 \left( 1 + \dfrac{S}{N} \right)$ | bits/sec (bps) | Theoretical upper bound on simplex link. |
| Channel Capacity Allocation | $C_{alloc}$ | $C_{alloc} = C \cdot 100\%$ (downstream only) | bits/sec (bps) | No upstream/upward share needed. |
| Comparison Condition | $\vert$ | $\text{Simplex} = \text{HalfDuplex} \cdot 0.5$ in terms of bidirectional throughput | Ratio | Half-duplex shares time; simplex does not. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Do not confuse Simplex with **Simplex Method** (used in Linear Programming in Operations Research). In Computer Networks (PEEVT414), Simplex **always** means *unidirectional communication mode*.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Television Broadcasting (Terrestrial & Satellite):** The TV tower transmits; the set-top box at home only receives. There is no return path on the broadcast frequency.
- **Public Address (PA) Systems:** Used in railway stations, airports, and stadiums. Announcer $\rightarrow$ Speaker.
- **Keyboard / Mouse to CPU:** A USB keyboard sends keystrokes to the CPU. The CPU does not transmit back to the keyboard on that same HID endpoint.
- **Telemetry from Satellites / Drones:** Data is beamed down to a ground station; no uplink on the same telemetry channel.
- **CCTV Surveillance Cameras:** The camera is a transmitter; the DVR/monitor is a receiver.
- **Pager Networks (Legacy):** A paging base station sent alphanumeric messages to small handheld pagers; the pagers had no transmit capability.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Breakdown of Throughput Efficiency

Since this topic does not require a heavy algebraic derivation, the analytical matrix below walks through the **comparative throughput derivation** for Simplex, Half-Duplex, and Full-Duplex channels of equal raw bandwidth $B$.

Let the **raw channel bitrate** be $R$ bits per second.

For a **Simplex** channel, the entire bitrate is dedicated to one direction. There is no turnaround delay $T_{turn}$ and no reverse channel.

$$
R_{simplex} = R \cdot 1.0 = R
$$

For a **Half-Duplex** channel, the channel is shared in time. If a fraction $f$ of the time is used by Station A and $(1-f)$ by Station B, the *bidirectional aggregate* throughput is still $R$, but the *per-direction* peak is less than $R$.

$$
R_{half-duplex, A} = R \cdot f \leq R
$$

$$
R_{half-duplex, B} = R \cdot (1 - f) \leq R
$$

For a **Full-Duplex** channel (using two physical channels or echo cancellation), the bidirectional capacity effectively doubles.

$$
R_{full-duplex, A} = R
$$

$$
R_{full-duplex, B} = R
$$

$$
R_{full-duplex, total} = R + R = 2R
$$

**Logical comparison step:**

$$
R_{simplex} \;\vert\; R_{half-duplex, max} \;\vert\; R_{full-duplex, max} \;\Rightarrow\; R \;\vert\; R \;\vert\; 2R
$$

**Engineering Conclusion:**

- A simplex link provides $R$ bps downstream but **0 bps upstream**.
- A full-duplex link provides $R$ bps in **both** directions.
- Therefore, simplex is **half as efficient** as full-duplex when measured in total bidirectional capacity, but it is the **lowest-cost, lowest-latency** solution for one-way applications.

## 3.2 Shannon Channel Capacity Derivation (Applied to Simplex)

The Shannon-Hartley theorem bounds the maximum error-free data rate over a noisy channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$.

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2 \left( 1 + \frac{S}{N} \right)
$$

Where:
- $C$ is the channel capacity in **bits per second (bps)**.
- $B$ is the bandwidth of the simplex channel in **Hertz (Hz)**.
- $S/N$ is the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** (dimensionless, often expressed in dB as $10 \cdot \log_{10}(S/N)$).

**Step-by-step logical evaluation:**

- **Step 1 — Bandwidth:** A simplex TV broadcast channel occupies $B = 6$ MHz.
- **Step 2 — SNR:** A typical terrestrial broadcast has $S/N \approx 1000$ (30 dB).
- **Step 3 — Capacity:** Compute the bitrate ceiling.

$$
C = 6 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2 \left( 1 + 1000 \right)
$$

$$
C = 6 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2 (1001)
$$

$$
\log_2 (1001) \approx 9.967
$$

$$
C \approx 6 \times 10^{6} \cdot 9.967 \approx 5.98 \times 10^{7} \text{ bps} \approx 59.8 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Logical conclusion:** This is the theoretical upper bound for that simplex link. Real-world systems (like DVB-T) achieve a fraction of this, but the formula is the same. The formula applies identically to a simplex link because Shannon's theorem is **direction-agnostic** — it bounds the rate in whichever direction the signal is flowing.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Simplex vs. Half-Duplex vs. Full-Duplex

| Property | Simplex | Half-Duplex | Full-Duplex |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Direction | $S \rightarrow R$ only | $S \leftrightarrow R$ (one at a time) | $S \leftrightarrow R$ (simultaneous) |
| Number of Channels | 1 | 1 (shared) | 2 (or 1 with echo cancellation) |
| Sender/Receiver | Fixed | Alternating | Both can send & receive |
| Acknowledgment | Not possible | Possible (after turnaround) | Possible (always) |
| Bandwidth Utilization | $100\%$ downstream | Shared in time | $100\%$ in both directions |
| Latency | Lowest | Higher (turnaround delay) | Lowest (no switching) |
| Hardware Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
| Example | Radio, TV, Keyboard | Walkie-Talkie | Telephone, Full-Duplex Ethernet |
| Reliability | Lowest (no ACK) | Moderate | Highest |
| Use Case | Broadcast, Telemetry | Push-to-Talk, Legacy Radio | Modern Data Networks |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Simplex Communication Flow

The diagram below illustrates the strict unidirectional data flow in a simplex link between a Sender and a Receiver. Notice that the arrow is unidirectional and there is no return path on the channel.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[SENDER TRANSMITTER] -->|Unidirectional Data Flow Only| B[COMMUNICATION CHANNEL]
    B -->|Signal Propagates One Way| C[RECEIVER DESTINATION]
    
    A -.->|NO RETURN PATH| X[Channel Guarded Against Reverse Traffic]
    C -.->|CANNOT TRANSMIT BACK| X
    
    style A fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style B fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style C fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style X fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of Simplex Link

The following schematic represents the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of a typical simplex telemetry/broadcast system, mapping the signal transformations from source to sink.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Information Source Audio or Video or Sensor Data] --> S2[Source Encoder Compression and Formatting]
    S2 --> S3[Channel Encoder Adds FEC and Sync Bits]
    S3 --> S4[Modulator Maps Bits to Analog Waveform]
    S4 --> S5[Transmitter Antenna or Laser or Copper Line]
    S5 -->|Unidirectional Medium| S6[Propagation Channel Air or Fiber or Wire]
    S6 --> S7[Receiver Antenna or Photodiode or Line Input]
    S7 --> S8[Demodulator Recovers Baseband Bits]
    S8 --> S9[Channel Decoder Corrects Errors Using FEC Only]
    S9 --> S10[Source Decoder Decompresses to Original Data]
    S10 --> S11[Information Sink Monitor or Speaker or Storage]
    
    style S1 fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S5 fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S6 fill:#e377c2,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S7 fill:#7f7f7f,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S11 fill:#bcbd22,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparative Topology Diagram

This diagram contrasts the three communication modes side by side, highlighting the directional flow patterns of each.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Simplex_Mode [SIMPLEX MODE]
        D1[Station A Sender] ==> E1[Station B Receiver]
        E1 -. No Return .-> D1
    end
    
    subgraph HalfDuplex_Mode [HALF DUPLEX MODE]
        D2[Station A] <==> E2[Station B]
        D2 -. Alternating Direction .-> E2
    end
    
    subgraph FullDuplex_Mode [FULL DUPLEX MODE]
        D3[Station A] <=> E3[Station B]
        D3 -. Simultaneous Bidirectional .-> E3
    end
    
    style D1 fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style E1 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style D2 fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style E2 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style D3 fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style E3 fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Simplex mode of communication. Give two real-world examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Simplex communication is a unidirectional mode of data transmission in which signals can travel **only in one direction** between two devices. The sender can only transmit, and the receiver can only receive, with no provision for a return path on the same channel.

**Example 1 (1 Mark):** **Television / Radio Broadcasting** — The TV/radio station transmits audio-video signals to the receivers at homes, but the home receivers cannot send any signal back to the broadcast tower on the same frequency.

**Example 2 (1 Mark):** **Keyboard to Computer** — A computer keyboard transmits keystroke data to the CPU through a USB cable, but the CPU does not transmit any data back to the keyboard on the same HID endpoint.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse *Simplex* with the *Simplex Method* of Linear Programming. In Computer Networks, Simplex **always** refers to unidirectional communication. Writing anything about Linear Programming will result in **zero marks**.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Simplex, Half-Duplex and Full-Duplex modes of communication in terms of direction and channel usage.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Mode | Direction of Flow | Number of Channels | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Simplex** | One direction only ($S \rightarrow R$) | 1 | Radio broadcast, Keyboard |
| **Half-Duplex** | Both directions, but **one at a time** | 1 (shared in time) | Walkie-Talkie |
| **Full-Duplex** | Both directions **simultaneously** | 2 (or 1 with echo cancellation) | Telephone, Modern Ethernet |

*(1 Mark for correct direction classification, 1 Mark for channel usage, 1 Mark for valid examples.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the three modes of data communication (Simplex, Half-Duplex, Full-Duplex) with suitable diagrams and examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A simplex broadcast channel has a bandwidth of **4 MHz** and a signal-to-noise ratio of **31**. Calculate the maximum theoretical data rate (channel capacity) achievable on this link using the Shannon-Hartley theorem. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks:

**1. Simplex Mode (2 Marks):**
Simplex communication is **unidirectional**. Data flows from sender to receiver only, with no return path. The entire channel bandwidth is dedicated to a single direction.
- *Diagram:* Sender $\rightarrow$ Channel $\rightarrow$ Receiver (single arrow).
- *Examples:* TV broadcast, FM radio, Keyboard-to-CPU, CCTV cameras.

**2. Half-Duplex Mode (2 Marks):**
Half-duplex communication is **bidirectional but not simultaneous**. Both stations can transmit and receive, but only one can transmit at a time. The channel is shared in time.
- *Diagram:* Sender $\leftrightarrow$ Receiver (double-headed arrow, but with a switch/timing constraint).
- *Examples:* Walkie-Talkie, CB Radio, old Ethernet hubs.

**3. Full-Duplex Mode (2 Marks):**
Full-duplex communication is **bidirectional and simultaneous**. Both stations can transmit and receive at the same time using two independent channels or echo-cancellation on a single channel.
- *Diagram:* Sender $\Leftrightarrow$ Receiver (two parallel arrows).
- *Examples:* Modern telephone networks, Full-Duplex Ethernet switches, mobile phone calls.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** Full-duplex is the most efficient; simplex is the simplest and cheapest. The choice depends on the application's interactivity requirement.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks:

**Given:**
- Bandwidth $B = 4$ MHz $= 4 \times 10^{6}$ Hz
- Signal-to-Noise Ratio $\dfrac{S}{N} = 31$

**To Find:** Maximum theoretical channel capacity $C$.

**Formula (Shannon-Hartley Theorem):**

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2 \left( 1 + \frac{S}{N} \right)
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the given values:**

$$
C = 4 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2 \left( 1 + 31 \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the inner term:**

$$
1 + 31 = 32
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm:**

$$
\log_2 (32) = \log_2 (2^{5}) = 5
$$

**Step 4 — Final calculation:**

$$
C = 4 \times 10^{6} \cdot 5 = 20 \times 10^{6} \text{ bps} = 20 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Final Answer (1 Mark):** The maximum theoretical data rate on the simplex link is **$C = 20$ Mbps**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Q-A:** Students commonly forget to convert MHz to Hz before substituting into the formula. Writing $C = 4 \cdot \log_2(32) = 20$ (without units) is a **1-mark deduction**. Always show the unit conversions explicitly: $4 \text{ MHz} = 4 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$.

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** What is Simplex transmission? List its key characteristics. Compare it with Full-Duplex transmission in terms of bandwidth efficiency, cost, and reliability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A simplex telemetry link operates at a bandwidth of **2 MHz** with an SNR of **15**. Using the Nyquist formula, calculate the maximum data rate if the channel uses **4 signal levels**. Verify your answer using the Shannon-Hartley theorem. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks:

**Definition of Simplex (2 Marks):**
Simplex transmission is a **one-way communication mode** in which data flows from the sender (transmitter) to the receiver (destination) along a single direction. The receiver has no mechanism to send any data, control, or acknowledgment back to the sender on the same channel.

**Key Characteristics (2 Marks):**
1. **Unidirectional:** Data flows in one fixed direction only.
2. **Fixed Roles:** One device is permanently the sender; the other is permanently the receiver.
3. **No Acknowledgment:** No ACK/NAK frames are exchanged.
4. **100% Bandwidth Utilization:** Entire channel is dedicated to downstream flow.
5. **Lowest Latency:** No switching or turnaround overhead.
6. **Lowest Cost:** Single-channel hardware, no duplexer required.

**Comparison Table with Full-Duplex (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Simplex | Full-Duplex |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bandwidth Efficiency** | $100\%$ dedicated to one direction | $100\%$ in both directions (effectively doubles capacity) |
| **Cost** | Lowest (single channel) | Highest (two channels or echo-canceling DSP) |
| **Reliability** | Lowest (no ACK, no ARQ) | Highest (ACK supported, retransmission possible) |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks:

**Given:**
- Bandwidth $B = 2$ MHz $= 2 \times 10^{6}$ Hz
- Signal-to-Noise Ratio $\dfrac{S}{N} = 15$
- Number of signal levels $L = 4$

**To Find:** Maximum data rate using Nyquist, then verify with Shannon.

**Step 1 — Nyquist Formula (Noiseless Channel):**

$$
C_{Nyquist} = 2 \cdot B \cdot \log_2 (L)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
C_{Nyquist} = 2 \cdot (2 \times 10^{6}) \cdot \log_2 (4)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm:**

$$
\log_2 (4) = \log_2 (2^{2}) = 2
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the result:**

$$
C_{Nyquist} = 2 \cdot 2 \times 10^{6} \cdot 2 = 8 \times 10^{6} \text{ bps} = 8 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 5 — Shannon-Hartley Verification (Noisy Channel):**

$$
C_{Shannon} = B \cdot \log_2 \left( 1 + \frac{S}{N} \right)
$$

$$
C_{Shannon} = 2 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2 (1 + 15) = 2 \times 10^{6} \cdot \log_2 (16)
$$

$$
\log_2 (16) = \log_2 (2^{4}) = 4
$$

$$
C_{Shannon} = 2 \times 10^{6} \cdot 4 = 8 \times 10^{6} \text{ bps} = 8 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion (1 Mark):**

$$
C_{Nyquist} = C_{Shannon} = 8 \text{ Mbps}
$$

Both formulas yield the same result because the given $L=4$ and $S/N=15$ are consistent (the signal levels and SNR have been chosen to make the noiseless Nyquist limit equal to the noisy Shannon limit). In practice, $C_{Shannon}$ is the **absolute upper bound** that no real system can exceed, and Nyquist's formula gives the achievable rate only under ideal noiseless conditions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Q-B:** A common student mistake is to use **only the Shannon formula** when the question explicitly asks for **Nyquist first, then verify with Shannon**. Skipping the Nyquist calculation costs **3 marks**. Always solve in the order the question prescribes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Simplex is a **unidirectional**, one-way-only communication mode.
- **Direction Lock:** $S \rightarrow R$ exclusively; the receiver **cannot reply** on the same channel.
- **Bandwidth Use:** $100\%$ downstream, $0\%$ upstream.
- **Channel Count:** Only **1** logical channel is needed.
- **Feedback:** **No ACK**, **no NAK**, **no ARQ** possible inherently.
- **Cost:** **Lowest** (single channel, no duplexer).
- **Latency:** **Lowest** (no turnaround switching).
- **Reliability:** **Lowest** (errors are uncorrected at the link layer in pure simplex).
- **Classic Examples:** TV/Radio broadcast, Keyboard-to-CPU, PA systems, CCTV, Satellite telemetry, Pagers.
- **Contrast:** Simplex $\neq$ Simplex Method (LP/OR). In CN, it always means **transmission mode**.
- **Formulas to Memorize:**
    - Shannon: $C = B \cdot \log_2 \left( 1 + \dfrac{S}{N} \right)$
    - Nyquist: $C = 2B \cdot \log_2 (L)$
- **Examiner Heuristic:** When asked for "examples," always quote **TV broadcast** and **Keyboard-to-Computer**. When asked for "characteristics," always mention **direction, no ACK, and 100% bandwidth**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Half Duplex
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT414 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Half Duplex

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Half Duplex** is a bidirectional communication mode in which data transmission can occur in **both directions** between two communicating devices, but **not simultaneously**. At any given instant of time, the communication channel is reserved exclusively for one direction of data flow, while the opposite direction remains idle. The direction of transmission must be explicitly **toggled** using a control mechanism (such as a Push-To-Talk button, a token, or a media-access protocol) before the other station can begin transmitting.

In the OSI Physical Layer taxonomy, Half Duplex is classified under the category of **"Two-Way Alternating Communication"**, sitting between **Simplex** (one-way only) and **Full Duplex** (two-way simultaneous).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"Half Duplex communication is a two-way communication mode in which both stations can transmit and receive, but not at the same time. Only one device transmits while the other receives, and the roles alternate using a control signal or protocol."*

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The Walkie-Talkie

Imagine two forest rangers, **Ranger A** and **Ranger B**, communicating using a pair of **walkie-talkies** in a dense jungle. The walkie-talkie has a single **Push-To-Talk (PTT)** button on its side.

- When Ranger A presses the PTT button, **only Ranger A's voice** is transmitted through the channel. Ranger B can only listen.
- When Ranger A releases the PTT button, the channel becomes silent and free.
- Now Ranger B presses his PTT button, and only then can he speak. Ranger A listens.
- Both parties can talk, but **only one person speaks at a time**. This is the essence of Half Duplex.

> [!NOTE]
> **Another Everyday Analogy:** A two-lane road bridge that is only **one-lane wide**. Cars can cross in **both directions** (so it's not Simplex), but they must take **turns** based on a traffic signal (so it's not Full Duplex). This is Half Duplex.

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition | Standard Value / Notation |
|------|------------|---------------------------|
| **Channel Capacity** | Maximum data rate of the medium | Measured in **bits per second (bps)** |
| **Direction Toggle Time (T_toggle)** | Time to switch the line direction | Typically **microseconds to milliseconds** |
| **Effective Throughput** | Useful data transferred per unit time | $\eta \leq 0.5 \times C$ in idealized cases |
| **Channel Utilization (U)** | Fraction of time the channel carries useful data | Expressed as a **percentage (%)** |
| **Guard Time (T_g)** | Idle time between direction reversals | Prevents signal collision/echo |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Exam Highlight:** In Half Duplex, the *aggregate channel capacity* is **not doubled** like in Full Duplex, because both directions **share** the same physical medium. This is a high-frequency KTU board question.

## 1.4 Context in Computer Networks (Where Half Duplex is Used)

| Application / Standard | Usage of Half Duplex |
|-----------------------|----------------------|
| **Traditional Ethernet (10BASE5, 10BASE2)** | Coaxial bus topology — CSMA/CD arbitration |
| **Walkie-Talkies / Two-Way Radios** | Push-To-Talk (PTT) handheld devices |
| **CB Radio (Citizens Band)** | Citizen Band radio communication |
| **Half-Duplex Modems** | Early dial-up modems with echo suppression |
| **Wi-Fi (Legacy 802.11)** | Original 802.11 used CSMA/CA — half duplex over air |
| **Satellite Communication (VSAT)** | Some VSAT links use half duplex for cost reasons |
| **Intercom Systems** | Office and building intercoms |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Communication Modes — A Comparative Breakdown

To truly *understand* Half Duplex, it must be placed in contrast with its siblings.

### Mode 1 — Simplex (One-Way)
- **Direction:** Unidirectional (A → B only).
- **Examples:** Television broadcast, radio FM, keyboard-to-CPU data lines, public address (PA) systems.
- **Channel use:** The reverse channel does **not exist** or is unused.
- **Capacity:** All bandwidth is dedicated to one direction, so **C = full link bandwidth**.

### Mode 2 — Half Duplex (Two-Way Alternating) ⭐
- **Direction:** Bidirectional, but **mutually exclusive in time**.
- **Examples:** Walkie-talkies, legacy Ethernet bus, CB radio.
- **Channel use:** The full bandwidth is available to the active transmitter.
- **Capacity per direction:** At any instant, the active sender gets **C = full link bandwidth**, but over time each direction gets roughly **C/2** on average.

### Mode 3 — Full Duplex (Two-Way Simultaneous)
- **Direction:** Bidirectional, **simultaneously**.
- **Examples:** Modern telephone networks, full-duplex Ethernet (100BASE-TX, 1 Gbps+), fiber optic links.
- **Channel use:** Achieved via **separate channels** (two wire pairs) or **echo cancellation**.
- **Capacity:** Both directions get **C = full link bandwidth** simultaneously → aggregate = **2C**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Memory Trick for Exams:**
> - **Simplex** = One-lane one-way road (only forward).
> - **Half Duplex** = One-lane two-way road with a traffic light (alternating).
> - **Full Duplex** = Two-lane divided highway (always both ways).

## 2.2 Operational Mechanics — How Half Duplex Works (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Idle State:** Both stations are connected to a shared channel. Neither is transmitting. The channel is in **IDLE** state.
2. **Request to Transmit:** Station A wants to send data. It checks if the channel is free (Carrier Sense in CSMA/CD or CSMA/CA). If free, it proceeds.
3. **Transmit Phase:** Station A sends a data frame. Station B is in **receive-only** mode and cannot transmit during this window.
4. **Guard Time (T_g):** A small idle period is inserted after the last bit to allow for **propagation delay** and **hardware switching latency**. This prevents "echo" and overlap.
5. **Direction Toggle:** The channel is now logically "flipped." Control is transferred to Station B (either via a token, a PTT release, or a contention-based mechanism).
6. **Acknowledge / Reverse Transmit:** Station B either sends an ACK or its own data frame. Station A now listens.
7. **Repeat:** The cycle continues for as long as both sides have data to exchange.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Hidden Inefficiency:** In Half Duplex, the *aggregate* end-to-end throughput between two stations is **less than Full Duplex**, because every direction reversal incurs a *guard time* and a *contention delay* (especially in CSMA/CD networks).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | **Aggregate Capacity (Full Duplex)** $= 2 \times C$ | Both directions get full C | bps |
| 2 | **Effective Capacity per Direction (Half Duplex, ideal)** $= C/2$ | Time-shared between two stations | bps |
| 3 | **Actual Half-Duplex Throughput** $\eta = \dfrac{T_{data}}{T_{data} + T_{toggle} + T_{guard}}$ | Useful data time over total time | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 4 | **CSMA/CD Throughput (1-persistent, Half Duplex)** $S = \dfrac{G \cdot e^{-aG}}{G(1+2a) + e^{-aG}}$ | Classical Ethernet efficiency formula | dimensionless |
| 5 | **Channel Utilization (Half Duplex, One Station Active)** $U = \dfrac{T_{frame}}{T_{frame} + T_{prop} \times 2}$ | Worst-case busy tone for collision detection | dimensionless |
| 6 | **Propagation Delay** $T_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | Distance / propagation speed | seconds |
| 7 | **Transmission Delay** $T_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Frame length / link rate | seconds |
| 8 | **Total Round-Trip Time** $RTT = 2 \times T_{prop}$ | For half-duplex acknowledgment | seconds |
| 9 | **Number of Direction Reversals (Token Ring, n stations)** $= n$ per token rotation | Each station gets one transmit window | integer |
| 10 | **Token Hold Time (THT)** | Maximum time a station may transmit before releasing the token | seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Safety Note:** In the formula table above, absolute-value bars are written as `\vert` (not `\|`) to keep the markdown table parser intact. This is required for proper rendering on the KTU digital learning platform.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cost-Sensitive Deployments:** Half-duplex radios and modems are **50% cheaper** than full-duplex equivalents because they use a **single channel** and **one antenna/transceiver** instead of two. This is why walkie-talkies, marine radios, and amateur (HAM) radios overwhelmingly use half duplex.
- **Power Efficiency:** Mobile and battery-powered devices save power because the transmitter is active only **50% of the time** on average.
- **Spectrum Conservation:** In licensed radio bands (e.g., aviation, military, marine VHF), half duplex allows **frequency reuse** — a single frequency serves multiple users who take turns.
- **Legacy Compatibility:** Many industrial control systems (SCADA, Modbus over RS-485) still operate in half duplex for **wiring simplicity** — only **2 wires** (instead of 4 for full duplex).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Throughput of a Half-Duplex Link

**Problem Statement:** Two stations, A and B, share a half-duplex link of raw capacity $C$ bps. Station A transmits a frame of length $L$ bits to B, and B immediately sends an acknowledgment (ACK) of length $L_{ack}$ bits back to A. There is a **guard time** $T_g$ between the two transmissions and a **direction toggle overhead** $T_{toggle}$. The one-way propagation delay is $T_{prop}$. Find the **effective one-way throughput**.

### Step 1 — Write the Total Time for One Complete Round-Trip Cycle

The total cycle includes: A's transmission, A→B propagation, guard/toggle, B's ACK, B→A propagation.

$$
T_{cycle} = \underbrace{\frac{L}{R}}_{T_{tx,A}} + T_{prop} + T_{toggle} + T_{g} + \underbrace{\frac{L_{ack}}{R}}_{T_{tx,B}} + T_{prop}
$$

### Step 2 — Combine Like Terms

$$
T_{cycle} = \frac{L + L_{ack}}{R} + 2 \cdot T_{prop} + T_{toggle} + T_{g}
$$

### Step 3 — Define the Useful Data Transferred in One Cycle

Only the **data frame of $L$ bits** from A is considered "useful payload" for the A→B throughput calculation (ACK is overhead).

$$
D_{useful} = L \;\; \text{bits}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Effective Throughput

$$
\eta_{A \to B} = \frac{D_{useful}}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{L}{\dfrac{L + L_{ack}}{R} + 2 \cdot T_{prop} + T_{toggle} + T_{g}}
$$

### Step 5 — Simplification in the Limiting Case

Assume $L \gg L_{ack}$, and $T_{prop}$, $T_{toggle}$, $T_{g}$ are negligible compared to $\frac{L}{R}$ (long frames, short link):

$$
\eta_{A \to B} \approx \frac{L}{L/R} = R = C
$$

This means the **instantaneous** rate can equal the full link capacity $C$, but the **average** rate over time (counting both directions) is only $C/2$.

### Step 6 — Average Long-Term Throughput (Both Directions Combined)

$$
\eta_{avg} = \frac{L + L_{ack}}{T_{cycle}} \approx \frac{L}{L/R} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{C}{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even in the ideal, overhead-free limit, a perfectly symmetric half-duplex link can deliver, on average, **at most 50% of the raw channel capacity to each direction** — confirming the textbook ceiling of $C/2$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Given:**
- Link rate $R = 1$ Mbps $= 10^6$ bps
- Frame length $L = 1000$ bits
- ACK length $L_{ack} = 100$ bits
- Propagation delay $T_{prop} = 10$ μs (one way)
- Toggle overhead $T_{toggle} = 5$ μs
- Guard time $T_g = 2$ μs

**Find:** Effective throughput from A to B.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute each term:**

$$
\frac{L + L_{ack}}{R} = \frac{1000 + 100}{10^6} = \frac{1100}{10^6} = 1.1 \text{ ms} = 1100 \text{ μs}
$$

$$
2 \cdot T_{prop} = 2 \times 10 = 20 \text{ μs}
$$

$$
T_{toggle} + T_{g} = 5 + 2 = 7 \text{ μs}
$$

**Step 2 — Total cycle time:**

$$
T_{cycle} = 1100 + 20 + 7 = 1127 \text{ μs}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective throughput:**

$$
\eta_{A \to B} = \frac{1000 \text{ bits}}{1127 \text{ μs}} = \frac{1000}{1127 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 887,311 \text{ bps} \approx 887.3 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison:**

- **Raw link rate:** 1 Mbps
- **Achievable (half duplex, with overheads):** ~887 kbps
- **Loss due to overhead:** $1000 - 887.3 = 112.7$ kbps lost to ACKs, propagation, and toggling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always show **units** at every step. Examiners specifically look for unit consistency (μs vs ms, bits vs kbits). Many students lose **1–2 marks** for unit mistakes.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Half Duplex vs. Simplex vs. Full Duplex

| Performance Metric | **Simplex** | **Half Duplex** ⭐ | **Full Duplex** |
|--------------------|------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Direction of data** | One-way only | Both ways (alternating) | Both ways (simultaneous) |
| **Aggregate channel capacity** | $C$ (only one side) | $C$ (shared) | $2C$ (one $C$ per side) |
| **Instantaneous per-direction rate** | $C$ | $C$ (active side only) | $C$ per side |
| **Average per-direction rate (symmetric, ideal)** | 0 (other side) | $C/2$ | $C$ |
| **Hardware complexity** | **Lowest** (1 transmitter or 1 receiver) | **Medium** (transceiver, not simultaneous) | **Highest** (2 channels or echo canceller) |
| **Wiring requirement (electrical)** | 2 wires | 2 wires | 4 wires (or 2 + echo cancellation) |
| **Cost** | **Lowest** | **Medium** | **Highest** |
| **Latency (one-way data + ACK)** | Cannot ACK (one-way) | High (must toggle) | Low (parallel) |
| **Power consumption** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Example** | TV broadcast, PA system | Walkie-talkie, 10BASE2 Ethernet | Telephone, 100BASE-TX |
| **Channel efficiency (ideal symmetric case)** | 100% for one direction | 50% per direction | 100% per direction |
| **Susceptibility to echo / collision** | None | Medium (toggle race) | High (requires echo cancellation) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many students write *"Half Duplex is half the speed of Full Duplex."* This is **wrong in the instantaneous sense**. Half Duplex can deliver the **full raw rate $C$** to the currently-transmitting station; it is only the **average long-term rate** that is halved. Examiners love this trick question.

---

## 3.4 Mathematical Justification: Why $C/2$ is the Ceiling

Consider a half-duplex channel of capacity $C$ bps, alternately used by stations A and B for durations $T_A$ and $T_B$ respectively in one period $T = T_A + T_B$.

**Total data delivered:**

$$
D_{total} = C \cdot T_A + C \cdot T_B = C(T_A + T_B) = C \cdot T
$$

**Data delivered to A (when A is the source):**

$$
D_A = C \cdot T_A
$$

**Data delivered to B:**

$$
D_B = C \cdot T_B
$$

**Average rate to A over the full period:**

$$
R_A = \frac{C \cdot T_A}{T_A + T_B}
$$

In the **symmetric case** $T_A = T_B$:

$$
R_A = \frac{C \cdot T_A}{2 T_A} = \frac{C}{2}
$$

This rigorously proves the **$C/2$ per-direction ceiling** for symmetric half-duplex operation. ∎

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Three Communication Modes

This Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **internal architecture** of devices for each communication mode, mapping the signal paths and isolation logic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SimplexBlock["SIMPLEX DEVICE ARCHITECTURE"]
        direction TB
        srcA["Data Source A"]
        txA["Transmitter A"]
        channelA["Unidirectional Channel"]
        rxB["Receiver B"]
        sinkB["Data Sink B"]
        srcA --> txA --> channelA --> rxB --> sinkB
    end

    subgraph HalfDuplexBlock["HALF DUPLEX DEVICE ARCHITECTURE"]
        direction TB
        app1["Application Layer Data"]
        mux1["Direction MUX / Switch Logic"]
        trx1["Shared Transceiver"]
        chan1["Shared Two-Way Channel"]
        trx2["Shared Transceiver"]
        mux2["Direction MUX / Switch Logic"]
        app2["Application Layer Data"]
        app1 --> mux1 --> trx1 --> chan1 --> trx2 --> mux2 --> app2
        ctrl1["PTT / Token Controller"] -.-> mux1
        ctrl2["PTT / Token Controller"] -.-> mux2
    end

    subgraph FullDuplexBlock["FULL DUPLEX DEVICE ARCHITECTURE"]
        direction TB
        srcF1["Source F1"]
        txF1["Transmitter F1"]
        chanF1["Channel Path 1"]
        rxF2["Receiver F2"]
        snkF2["Sink F2"]
        srcF1 --> txF1 --> chanF1 --> rxF2 --> snkF2
        srcF2["Source F2"]
        txF2["Transmitter F2"]
        chanF2["Channel Path 2"]
        rxF1["Receiver F1"]
        snkF1["Sink F1"]
        srcF2 --> txF2 --> chanF2 --> rxF1 --> snkF1
    end
```

### Interpretation of the Diagram

- **Simplex:** Notice that the channel is **one-directional**. There is no return path.
- **Half Duplex:** The two stations share **one transceiver** and **one channel**, controlled by a **direction switch** (PTT button or protocol token). The dotted line represents the control signal that flips the MUX.
- **Full Duplex:** Two **independent channels** exist, so the transmitter of one and the receiver of the other can operate **simultaneously**.

---

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Half Duplex Communication Timeline

The following Mermaid sequence diagram shows the **temporal sequence** of events in a typical half-duplex data exchange between Station A and Station B.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Station A
    participant CH as Shared Channel
    participant B as Station B

    Note over A,B: Channel is IDLE
    A->>A: Carrier Sense (CSMA check)
    A->>CH: Press PTT / Acquire Token
    Note over A,CH: Direction = A to B
    A->>CH: Transmit DATA Frame (L bits)
    CH->>B: Deliver Frame after T_prop
    Note over CH: Guard Time T_g elapses
    A->>CH: Release PTT / Return Token
    Note over A,CH: Direction toggle
    B->>CH: Press PTT / Acquire Token
    Note over B,CH: Direction = B to A
    B->>CH: Transmit ACK Frame (L_ack bits)
    CH->>A: Deliver ACK after T_prop
    A->>A: Receive ACK, cycle complete
    Note over A,B: Channel returns to IDLE
```

### Reading the Diagram

1. Station A senses the channel (idle).
2. A transmits its data frame.
3. A small **guard time** passes to let signals settle.
4. A releases control; B acquires it.
5. B transmits the ACK.
6. Cycle restarts.

---

## 4.3 Channel Sharing State Diagram — Half Duplex FSM

This finite-state machine captures the **logical states** of a half-duplex channel.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE --> A_TO_B : A requests and gets channel
    A_TO_B --> GUARD : A finishes frame
    GUARD --> B_TO_A : Toggle signal issued
    B_TO_A --> GUARD2 : B finishes frame
    GUARD2 --> A_TO_B : Toggle signal issued
    A_TO_B --> IDLE : No more data from A
    B_TO_A --> IDLE : No more data from B
    IDLE --> [*]
```

### State Meanings

| State | Meaning |
|-------|---------|
| **IDLE** | Channel is free; both stations silent |
| **A_TO_B** | Station A is transmitting, B is receiving |
| **B_TO_A** | Station B is transmitting, A is receiving |
| **GUARD / GUARD2** | Small dead time between direction flips |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Half Duplex communication. How does it differ from Simplex? `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Half Duplex is a two-way communication mode in which both stations can transmit and receive, but **not at the same time**. The channel is shared between the two directions, and only one station transmits at a given instant while the other receives. The direction of transmission is alternated using a control mechanism (e.g., a Push-To-Talk button or a token).
>
> **Difference from Simplex (1 mark):** In **Simplex** communication, data flows in **only one direction** (e.g., television broadcast) and the reverse channel does not exist. In **Half Duplex**, although the channel is **bidirectional**, both directions **cannot be active simultaneously** — they take turns. Simplex is permanently one-way; Half Duplex is two-way but *time-shared*.

---

### Question 2: List any three real-world examples of Half Duplex communication systems. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Walkie-Talkies / Two-Way Radios** — Use Push-To-Talk (PTT) buttons to alternate between transmit and receive. (1 mark)
2. **Citizens Band (CB) Radio** — Truckers and hobbyists use single-channel radios requiring PTT. (1 mark)
3. **Legacy Ethernet (10BASE2 / 10BASE5)** — Coaxial bus Ethernet used CSMA/CD, which is a half-duplex contention protocol. (1 mark)
>
> *(Other acceptable answers: Half-duplex modems, intercom systems, satellite VSAT links, Wi-Fi CSMA/CA, RS-485 Modbus.)*

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** Explain in detail the three modes of data communication: Simplex, Half Duplex, and Full Duplex. Compare them in terms of direction of data flow, channel capacity, hardware cost, and provide one real-world example for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A half-duplex link operates at $R = 2$ Mbps. Station A sends a data frame of $L = 2000$ bits, and Station B replies with an ACK of $L_{ack} = 200$ bits. The one-way propagation delay is $T_{prop} = 25$ μs, the direction toggle time is $T_{toggle} = 10$ μs, and the guard time is $T_g = 5$ μs. Calculate:
> (i) Total cycle time for one data-ACK exchange. (ii) Effective throughput from A to B. (iii) The percentage of channel capacity that is "wasted" due to overhead. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Data communication between two devices can occur in three fundamental modes defined by the direction and simultaneity of data flow. These are Simplex, Half Duplex, and Full Duplex.

**Simplex Mode (2 marks):**
- **Direction of data flow:** Strictly **unidirectional** — one device (the transmitter) can only send, and the other (the receiver) can only receive. The reverse path does not exist.
- **Channel capacity:** The entire bandwidth $C$ is dedicated to one direction.
- **Hardware cost:** Lowest — only a transmitter at one end and a receiver at the other.
- **Example:** Television broadcast (TV transmitter → TV sets), FM radio, public address systems.

**Half Duplex Mode (2 marks):**
- **Direction of data flow:** **Bidirectional** but **mutually exclusive in time**. Both devices have transceivers, but only one transmits at a time.
- **Channel capacity:** The full link rate $C$ is available to the *active* transmitter, but the **average per-direction throughput** is $C/2$ in symmetric operation.
- **Hardware cost:** Medium — both ends need a transceiver and a direction-control mechanism.
- **Example:** Walkie-talkies, CB radio, legacy Ethernet (10BASE2 with CSMA/CD).

**Full Duplex Mode (2 marks):**
- **Direction of data flow:** **Bidirectional and simultaneous** — both devices can transmit and receive at the same time.
- **Channel capacity:** Aggregate $= 2C$, with each direction getting $C$ continuously.
- **Hardware cost:** Highest — requires either two physical channels (4-wire cabling) or sophisticated **echo-cancellation** circuitry.
- **Example:** Modern switched Ethernet (100BASE-TX, 1 Gbps), standard telephone networks.

> **[End of (a) Answer — 7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $R = 2 \times 10^6$ bps
- $L = 2000$ bits, $L_{ack} = 200$ bits
- $T_{prop} = 25$ μs (one way)
- $T_{toggle} = 10$ μs, $T_g = 5$ μs

**(i) Total Cycle Time (3 marks):**

$$
T_{cycle} = \frac{L + L_{ack}}{R} + 2 \cdot T_{prop} + T_{toggle} + T_{g}
$$

Compute each term:

$$
\frac{L + L_{ack}}{R} = \frac{2000 + 200}{2 \times 10^6} = \frac{2200}{2 \times 10^6} = 1100 \text{ μs}
$$

$$
2 \cdot T_{prop} = 2 \times 25 = 50 \text{ μs}
$$

$$
T_{toggle} + T_{g} = 10 + 5 = 15 \text{ μs}
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_{cycle} = 1100 + 50 + 15 = \boxed{1165 \text{ μs}}
$$

**(ii) Effective Throughput from A to B (2 marks):**

$$
\eta_{A \to B} = \frac{L}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{2000 \text{ bits}}{1165 \text{ μs}} = \frac{2000}{1165 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\eta_{A \to B} \approx 1{,}716{,}738 \text{ bps} \approx \boxed{1.717 \text{ Mbps}}
$$

**(iii) Percentage Wasted (2 marks):**

Wasted bits per cycle $= L_{ack} = 200$ bits of pure ACK overhead, plus the time spent in toggle, guard, and propagation.

Ideal throughput (no overhead) would be $R = 2$ Mbps. Actual is 1.717 Mbps.

$$
\text{Waste\%} = \frac{R - \eta_{A \to B}}{R} \times 100 = \frac{2{,}000{,}000 - 1{,}716{,}738}{2{,}000{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Waste\%} = \frac{283{,}262}{2{,}000{,}000} \times 100 \approx \boxed{14.16\%}
$$

> **[End of (b) Answer — 7 marks]**

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the working of Half Duplex communication. Discuss the role of CSMA/CD in traditional Ethernet as an example of half-duplex access. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A half-duplex satellite link has the following parameters: bandwidth $B = 500$ kHz, signal-to-noise ratio $S/N = 1000$ (linear), and the link is used alternately by two ground stations. Using the Shannon-Hartley theorem, calculate: (i) The maximum theoretical capacity $C$ of the channel. (ii) The maximum per-direction throughput when the link is used in symmetric half-duplex mode. (iii) Comment on how the result would change if the link were upgraded to full duplex. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Diagram (2 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.2 Sequence Diagram — a block diagram showing Station A, the shared channel with a PTT/toggle switch, and Station B should be drawn.)*

**Working Explanation (3 marks):**
In Half Duplex communication, two stations A and B share a single physical channel equipped with a **direction-control switch** (Push-To-Talk in radios, or a media-access protocol in networks). When A wishes to send, it asserts control of the channel — B switches to receive-only mode. A transmits its frame; upon completion, A releases control. After a small guard time, B acquires the channel and transmits in the reverse direction. The roles alternate as long as there is data to exchange.

**CSMA/CD in Traditional Ethernet (2 marks):**
Classical Ethernet (10BASE5, 10BASE2) used a **bus topology** with a single coaxial cable shared by all stations. This cable carried signals in **both directions** but **only one station could transmit at a time** — making it inherently **half duplex**. To coordinate access, Ethernet used **Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection (CSMA/CD)**: a station first *listens* to the channel (Carrier Sense), and if idle, begins transmitting. If two stations transmit simultaneously, their signals *collide*; both detect the collision (Collision Detection), abort transmission, wait a random back-off time, and retry. The CSMA/CD arbitration is what makes this half-duplex shared-medium access possible.

> **[End of (a) Answer — 7 marks]**

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $B = 500$ kHz $= 5 \times 10^5$ Hz
- $S/N = 1000$ (linear, dimensionless)

**Shannon-Hartley Theorem:**

$$
C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + S/N) \;\; \text{bits/second}
$$

**(i) Maximum Theoretical Capacity (3 marks):**

$$
C = 5 \times 10^5 \cdot \log_2(1 + 1000)
$$

$$
\log_2(1001) = \frac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} \approx \frac{3.0004}{0.30103} \approx 9.967 \text{ bits/Hz}
$$

$$
C = 5 \times 10^5 \times 9.967 \approx \boxed{4.98 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} \approx 4.98 \text{ Mbps}}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Per-Direction Throughput in Half Duplex (2 marks):**

In symmetric half-duplex operation, the time-averaged per-direction throughput ceiling is $C/2$:

$$
R_{per\,dir} = \frac{C}{2} = \frac{4.98}{2} \approx \boxed{2.49 \text{ Mbps}}
$$

**(iii) Full-Duplex Upgrade Comment (2 marks):**

If the link were upgraded to **Full Duplex**, both directions could transmit **simultaneously**. The aggregate throughput would become $2C \approx 9.96$ Mbps, and each direction would independently get the full $C \approx 4.98$ Mbps — **doubling** the per-direction performance compared to half duplex. This would require either a second physical channel (e.g., a second transponder or frequency band) or an **echo-cancellation** system on the existing channel, both of which increase the cost and complexity of the satellite ground segment.

> **[End of (b) Answer — 7 marks]**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–3 Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing "Half Duplex" with "Slow Communication":** Half Duplex is NOT slow; it is *time-shared*. The instantaneous rate equals the full link rate $C$. The *average per-direction throughput* is $C/2$. Examiners will deduct marks if you imply Half Duplex is intrinsically slower.
> 2. **Forgetting Units in Numerical Answers:** Always write μs, ms, kbps, or Mbps explicitly. A correct number with **wrong or missing units** = **0.5–1 mark deducted**.
> 3. **Mis-stating the Simplex Definition:** Simplex is **permanently** one-way. Half Duplex is **temporarily** one-way. Do not interchange these.
> 4. **Omitting the Guard Time** in cycle-time calculations. The total cycle is $T_{tx} + T_{prop} + T_{toggle} + T_g + T_{tx,ack} + T_{prop}$. Many students miss $T_g$ and $T_{toggle}$, leading to inflated throughput values.
> 5. **Writing "Half Duplex = Half Speed":** This is a *guaranteed* mark-deduction phrase. The correct phrasing is *"Half Duplex shares one channel between two directions in time, yielding an average per-direction rate of $C/2$."*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Half Duplex = Two-way ALTERNATING communication.** Both stations can transmit, but only one at a time.
- ✅ Direction is controlled by a **PTT button, a token, or a CSMA/CD-like media-access protocol**.
- ✅ The three modes in increasing capability: **Simplex → Half Duplex → Full Duplex**.
- ✅ **Instantaneous rate** of the active transmitter = full $C$. **Average per-direction rate** $\approx C/2$ in symmetric case.
- ✅ **Aggregate capacity** of Half Duplex = $C$ (NOT $2C$). Full Duplex = $2C$.
- ✅ **Hardware cost** is medium — one transceiver and one channel per station, plus a direction-control switch.
- ✅ **Classic examples:** walkie-talkies, CB radio, legacy 10BASE2/10BASE5 Ethernet, CSMA/CA Wi-Fi, RS-485 Modbus, marine VHF radio.
- ✅ **Total cycle time** for a data + ACK exchange: $T_{cycle} = (L + L_{ack})/R + 2T_{prop} + T_{toggle} + T_g$.
- ✅ **Effective throughput:** $\eta = L / T_{cycle}$.
- ✅ **Wired links:** half duplex needs **2 wires**; full duplex needs **4 wires** (or 2 + echo cancellation).
- ✅ Half duplex is preferred in **cost-sensitive, power-constrained, or spectrum-limited** applications.
- ✅ The $C/2$ ceiling is a **mathematical upper bound**, not just an engineering rule of thumb — it is provable from time-sharing algebra.
- ✅ **Key formulas** to memorize: $T_{prop} = d/v$, $T_{trans} = L/R$, $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$, and the half-duplex $\eta = L / T_{cycle}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Full Duplex Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT414 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Full Duplex Communication**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Full Duplex Communication (FDC)** is a bidirectional data transmission mode in which **two connected devices can transmit and receive data simultaneously over the same communication channel or link**, without temporal or directional conflict between the signals.

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, full duplex is classified as a **simultaneous two-way communication mode** belonging to the family of *Logical Transmission Modes* (alongside Simplex and Half Duplex). The key defining constraint is **temporal concurrency**: the forward channel (A → B) and the reverse channel (B → A) are active at the exact same instant $t$, allowing parallel information flow.

Mathematically, the **Channel Capacity** $C_{FD}$ in full duplex can be expressed as:

$$
C_{FD} = C_{forward} + C_{reverse}
$$

where $C_{forward}$ and $C_{reverse}$ are the bit-rates of the two independent logical sub-channels established over the physical medium.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Mobile Phone Call:**
> When you speak on a mobile phone, both you and the person on the other end can talk **at the same time**. Your voice is converted, transmitted over the network, and reproduced on the other side while their voice is simultaneously travelling back to you. There is no "press-to-talk" button and no "over" handover — the medium carries signals in both directions *in parallel*. This is **Full Duplex Communication** in action.

A simpler classroom analogy: imagine a **two-lane road** where one lane carries traffic from City A to City B, and the other lane carries traffic from City B to City A — *concurrently*. No vehicle has to wait for an oncoming vehicle to clear before proceeding.

Contrast this with:
- **Simplex** → A one-lane road (one-way only, like a TV broadcast or a keyboard → computer).
- **Half Duplex** → A single narrow lane where cars must take turns (like a walkie-talkie — "over").

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> - **Effective Data Rate:** $R_{eff} = 2 \times R_{link}$ (for symmetric full duplex)
> - **Channel Separation:** Achieved using **Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)**, **Time Division Duplexing (TDD)**, or **separate physical pairs** (e.g., twisted-pair Tx and Rx lines).
> - **Reference Standard:** IEEE **802.3x** standard defines full-duplex operation for Ethernet.
> - **Typical Link Speeds:** **100 Mbps**, **1 Gbps**, **10 Gbps** full-duplex Ethernet.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How Full Duplex Works

Full duplex operation requires the physical and data-link layers to be engineered so that **transmission and reception are decoupled**. This can be done in three primary ways:

- **Physical Separation (Most Common in Wired Networks)**
  - Two separate copper pairs (one for Tx, one for Rx) in an Ethernet cable.
  - Example: **10BASE-T / 100BASE-TX** uses Pair 2 (pins 1 & 2) for transmit and Pair 3 (pins 3 & 6) for receive.

- **Frequency Division (Used in Wireless & Broadband)**
  - Different carrier frequencies allocate non-overlapping bands to forward and reverse traffic.
  - Example: **ADSL** uses upstream (25 kHz – 138 kHz) and downstream (138 kHz – 1.1 MHz) bands.

- **Time Division with Rapid Switching (TDD)**
  - The same frequency is shared, but time slots alternate so fast that users perceive simultaneous flow.
  - Example: Some **5G NR** and **LTE-TDD** deployments.

### 2.2 Why Full Duplex — The Engineering Rationale

- **Eliminates Collision Domain:** In half-duplex Ethernet using CSMA/CD, devices must detect and back off on collisions. Full duplex **removes CSMA/CD entirely**, doubling the theoretical throughput.
- **Maximizes Channel Utilization:** Both directions are continuously active — no idle "wait" time.
- **Deterministic Latency:** Useful for real-time protocols (VoIP, video conferencing, industrial control).
- **Higher Aggregate Throughput:** A 1 Gbps full-duplex link yields an aggregate of **2 Gbps** (1 Gbps each way).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telephone Networks (PSTN / VoIP):** Natural human conversation requires full duplex.
- **Data Centers:** Server-to-switch full-duplex 10/25/40/100 Gbps links.
- **Full-Duplex Ethernet Switches:** Every port can send and receive at line rate simultaneously, eliminating head-of-line blocking at the port level.
- **Fiber Optics (WDM):** Different wavelengths carry traffic in opposite directions on a single fiber strand.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below contains the complete, KTU-exam-relevant formula set for this topic. Use the **\vert** notation (never the bare pipe symbol) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Channel Capacity (Full Duplex) | $C_{FD} = C_{fwd} + C_{rev}$ | bits/second (bps) | For symmetric links: $C_{FD} = 2R$ |
| 2 | Effective Aggregate Throughput | $T_{agg} = 2 \cdot R_{link}$ | bps | Doubles a half-duplex link of the same physical rate |
| 3 | Time to Transmit $N$ bits | $t = \dfrac{N}{R}$ | seconds | $R$ is the per-direction rate |
| 4 | Bidirectional File Transfer Time | $t_{FD} = \dfrac{\max(F_1, F_2)}{R}$ | seconds | Files of sizes $F_1$ (A→B) and $F_2$ (B→A) |
| 5 | Half-Duplex Equivalent (same task) | $t_{HD} = \dfrac{F_1 + F_2}{R}$ | seconds | Sequential, one direction at a time |
| 6 | Speed-up Factor | $S = \dfrac{t_{HD}}{t_{FD}} = \dfrac{F_1 + F_2}{\max(F_1, F_2)}$ | dimensionless | Always $\geq 1$ |
| 7 | Utilization Efficiency | $\eta_{FD} = 100\%$ (both directions) | percent | No idle time on either sub-channel |
| 8 | Signal-to-Noise (Shannon, per direction) | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | bps | $B$ in Hz, applied independently to each direction |

### 2.5 Key Boundary Conditions & Assumptions

- The link must be a **point-to-point dedicated connection** (no shared medium with CSMA/CD).
- The **physical medium** must support independent forward/reverse propagation (separate pairs, wavelengths, or TDD slots).
- For **symmetric** full duplex, $C_{fwd} = C_{rev} = R$. Asymmetric variants (e.g., ADSL) have $C_{fwd} \neq C_{rev}$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Aggregate Throughput of a Full-Duplex Link

**Problem Setup:** A full-duplex Ethernet link has a per-direction physical bit rate of $R = 1~\text{Gbps}$. Derive the aggregate throughput when the link is fully utilized in both directions simultaneously.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Define per-direction capacity.} \quad & C_{fwd} = R = 1~\text{Gbps}, \quad C_{rev} = R = 1~\text{Gbps} \\
\\
\text{Step 2: Apply full-duplex capacity definition.} \quad & C_{FD} = C_{fwd} + C_{rev} \\
\\
\text{Step 3: Substitute the values.} \quad & C_{FD} = 1~\text{Gbps} + 1~\text{Gbps} \\
\\
\text{Step 4: Compute the final aggregate.} \quad & C_{FD} = 2~\text{Gbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A "1 Gbps full-duplex" link actually delivers **2 Gbps of total user data** (1 Gbps in each direction). This is why vendors often advertise "full-duplex" capability as a *feature multiplier* on a base link speed.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bidirectional File Transfer Time

**Problem Setup:** Host A must send a file of size $F_1 = 800~\text{MB}$ to Host B, while B simultaneously sends a file of size $F_2 = 200~\text{MB}$ back to A. The link rate per direction is $R = 100~\text{Mbps}$. Compute the total time required under (i) Half Duplex, and (ii) Full Duplex operation.

**Given:**
- $F_1 = 800~\text{MB} = 800 \times 8~\text{Mb} = 6400~\text{Mb}$
- $F_2 = 200~\text{MB} = 200 \times 8~\text{Mb} = 1600~\text{Mb}$
- $R = 100~\text{Mbps}$

#### Case (i) — Half Duplex

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: In half duplex, only one direction transmits at a time.} \quad & t_{HD} = \frac{F_1}{R} + \frac{F_2}{R} \\
\\
\text{Step 2: Combine the fractions.} \quad & t_{HD} = \frac{F_1 + F_2}{R} \\
\\
\text{Step 3: Substitute the numerical values.} \quad & t_{HD} = \frac{6400 + 1600}{100} \\
\\
\text{Step 4: Compute the sum and divide.} \quad & t_{HD} = \frac{8000}{100} = 80~\text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Case (ii) — Full Duplex

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: In full duplex, both directions transmit simultaneously.} \quad & t_{FD} = \max\left(\frac{F_1}{R}, \frac{F_2}{R}\right) \\
\\
\text{Step 2: Evaluate each direction's time independently.} \quad & t_{A \to B} = \frac{6400}{100} = 64~\text{s}, \quad t_{B \to A} = \frac{1600}{100} = 16~\text{s} \\
\\
\text{Step 3: Take the maximum (the slower direction dictates finish time).} \quad & t_{FD} = \max(64, 16) = 64~\text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Speed-up Factor

$$
\begin{aligned}
S = \frac{t_{HD}}{t_{FD}} = \frac{80}{64} = 1.25
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Full duplex completes the same bidirectional transfer in **64 s** instead of **80 s** — a **25% improvement**. The savings grow larger when the two file sizes are comparable (worst case: $F_1 = F_2 \Rightarrow S = 2.0$, i.e., full duplex is twice as fast).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Channel Utilization Comparison

**Problem Setup:** Compute the channel utilization $\eta$ for each transmission mode when an A-to-B file transfer of size $F$ must complete within time $T$, and B must reply with an acknowledgment (ACK) of size $A \ll F$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Simplex: only A → B possible. B cannot reply on the same link.} \quad & \eta_{S} = \frac{F}{F} = 100\% \text{ (single direction, no ACK possible)} \\
\\
\text{Half Duplex: A sends file, B sends ACK sequentially.} \quad & t_{HD} = \frac{F + A}{R} \approx \frac{F}{R} \\
\\
\text{Full Duplex: A sends file while B sends ACK in parallel.} \quad & t_{FD} = \frac{\max(F, A)}{R} = \frac{F}{R} \quad (\text{since } F \gg A) \\
\\
\text{Bidirectional Utilization (Full Duplex).} \quad & \eta_{FD} = \frac{C_{fwd} \cdot t + C_{rev} \cdot t}{2 \cdot C_{max} \cdot t} = \frac{2R}{2R} = 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Key Takeaway:** Full duplex achieves **100% utilization in both directions concurrently**, while half duplex leaves one direction idle for half the transmission window.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Simplex | Half Duplex | Full Duplex |
|-----------|---------|-------------|-------------|
| Direction of Data Flow | One way only | Both, but **alternating** | Both, **simultaneous** |
| Example | TV broadcast, Keyboard | Walkie-talkie, old Hub | Mobile phone, Switch port |
| Throughput Multiplier | $1 \times R$ | $1 \times R$ (time-shared) | $2 \times R$ aggregate |
| CSMA/CD Needed? | No | Yes (on shared medium) | **No** (collision-free) |
| Latency for 2-way comm. | Infinite (impossible) | High (sequential) | Low (concurrent) |
| Hardware Complexity | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Comparison of the Three Transmission Modes

The Mermaid diagram below visually distinguishes Simplex, Half-Duplex, and Full-Duplex communication using a clean block-level functional architecture (Node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid safety rules).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SIMPLEX["SIMPLEX MODE - One Way Only"]
        S1["Device A - Transmitter Only"] -->|Data flows one direction| S2["Device B - Receiver Only"]
    end

    subgraph HALFDUPLEX["HALF DUPLEX MODE - Alternating Two Way"]
        H1["Device A - Tx or Rx"] <-->|Takes turns| H2["Device B - Tx or Rx"]
    end

    subgraph FULLDUPLEX["FULL DUPLEX MODE - Simultaneous Two Way"]
        F1["Device A - Tx and Rx"] <-->|Both directions at once| F2["Device B - Tx and Rx"]
        F1 -. Forward Channel A to B .-> F2
        F2 -. Reverse Channel B to A .-> F1
    end
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Full-Duplex Link

This block diagram illustrates how a full-duplex Ethernet link isolates transmit and receive paths using separate physical pairs, while presenting a single logical link to the upper layers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HOSTA["Host A - Network Interface"]
        AAPP["Application Layer Data"]
        ATCP["TCP - Segment Builder"]
        AIP["IP - Packet Builder"]
        AMAC["MAC - Frame Builder"]
        ATX["Physical TX Driver"]
        ARX["Physical RX Receiver"]
        AMAC2["MAC - Frame Parser"]
        AIP2["IP - Packet Parser"]
        ATCP2["TCP - Segment Parser"]
        AAPP2["Application Layer Data"]
    end

    subgraph LINK["Physical Full Duplex Link - Two Copper Pairs"]
        PAIR2["Pair 2 - Pins 1 and 2 - TX"]
        PAIR3["Pair 3 - Pins 3 and 6 - RX"]
    end

    subgraph HOSTB["Host B - Network Interface"]
        BRX["Physical RX Receiver"]
        BMAC2["MAC - Frame Parser"]
        BIP2["IP - Packet Parser"]
        BTCP2["TCP - Segment Parser"]
        BAPP2["Application Layer Data"]
        BTX["Physical TX Driver"]
        BMAC["MAC - Frame Builder"]
        BIP["IP - Packet Builder"]
        BTCP["TCP - Segment Builder"]
        BAPP["Application Layer Data"]
    end

    AAPP --> ATCP --> AIP --> AMAC --> ATX
    ATX --> PAIR2
    PAIR2 --> BRX --> BMAC2 --> BIP2 --> BTCP2 --> BAPP2

    BAPP --> BTCP --> BIP --> BMAC --> BTX
    BTX --> PAIR3
    PAIR3 --> ARX --> AMAC2 --> AIP2 --> ATCP2 --> AAPP2
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow — When to Use Full Duplex

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{"Is simultaneous bidirectional traffic required?"}
    Q -- "No, one way only" --> R1["Use Simplex - e.g. TV broadcast"]
    Q -- "Yes, but can be sequential" --> R2{"Can devices tolerate turn-taking latency?"}
    R2 -- "Yes" --> R3["Use Half Duplex - e.g. walkie talkie"]
    R2 -- "No, real time needed" --> R4["Use Full Duplex - e.g. VoIP phone call"]
    R4 --> R5["Ensure physical channel separation - pairs, FDM, or TDD"]
    R5 --> R6["Disable CSMA CD - collision free operation"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern. Marks are distributed according to the **CO1 / K2–K3** cognitive levels of the Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** Define *Full Duplex Communication*. Give one real-world example and state one key advantage over half duplex.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Full Duplex Communication is a mode of data transmission in which two devices can **transmit and receive data simultaneously** over the same link, in both directions at the same instant.
- **Example (1 mark):** A telephone conversation (or full-duplex Ethernet switch port) — both parties can speak and listen at the same time.
- **Advantage over half duplex (1 mark):** It **doubles the effective aggregate throughput** and **eliminates the need for CSMA/CD** on point-to-point links, since collisions cannot occur when Tx and Rx use separate physical channels.

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** List the **three transmission modes** in data communication. State one distinguishing feature of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Mode | Feature (1 mark each) |
|------|------------------------|
| Simplex | Communication is **unidirectional** — data flows only from sender to receiver (e.g., TV broadcast). |
| Half Duplex | Communication is **bidirectional but not simultaneous** — devices take turns (e.g., walkie-talkie). |
| Full Duplex | Communication is **bidirectional and simultaneous** — both devices transmit and receive at the same time (e.g., mobile phone call). |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (ESE Pattern)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** *Answer either Question A **or** Question B in full.*

---

#### 🔹 Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **three modes of data transmission** (Simplex, Half Duplex, Full Duplex) with suitable **diagrams** and **one real-world example** for each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Simplex Mode (2 marks):**
  - Data travels in **one direction only**. The sender (Tx) and receiver (Rx) roles are **fixed by design**.
  - **Diagram:** Device A (Tx) → → → → Device B (Rx)
  - **Examples:** Television broadcast, radio transmission, keyboard-to-CPU data flow, public address system.
  - **Key property:** The reverse channel does not exist on the same medium.
