- **Half Duplex Mode (2 marks):**
  - Both devices can transmit, but **only one direction is active at a time**. The link is shared temporally.
  - **Diagram:** Device A ⇆ Device B (with bidirectional arrow, but a *mutex* on the channel)
  - **Examples:** Walkie-talkie ("over" protocol), old Ethernet hubs using CSMA/CD, certain citizen-band radio channels.
  - **Key property:** Requires **collision detection / avoidance** since two devices may attempt simultaneous transmission.

- **Full Duplex Mode (2 marks):**
  - Both devices **transmit and receive simultaneously** on the same link. Tx and Rx are logically and often physically separated.
  - **Diagram:** Device A ⇄ Device B with two independent arrows showing concurrent forward and reverse flow.
  - **Examples:** Mobile phone call, full-duplex Ethernet switch port, modern fiber-optic links using WDM.
  - **Key property:** **Doubles aggregate throughput**; CSMA/CD is **disabled**; latency is minimized for two-way protocols.

- **Comparison Summary (1 mark):** Tabulate throughput, hardware complexity, and typical use-case as shown in Section 3.4 of these notes.

**(b)** Two hosts, A and B, are connected by a **full-duplex link** with a per-direction rate of $R = 50~\text{Mbps}$. Host A must send a file of size $F_1 = 500~\text{MB}$ to Host B, while B sends a file of size $F_2 = 300~\text{MB}$ to A. **(i)** Calculate the total time required. **(ii)** Repeat the calculation assuming the link operates in **half duplex** mode. **(iii)** Find the speed-up factor. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $R = 50~\text{Mbps}$
- $F_1 = 500~\text{MB} = 500 \times 8~\text{Mb} = 4000~\text{Mb}$
- $F_2 = 300~\text{MB} = 300 \times 8~\text{Mb} = 2400~\text{Mb}$

**(i) Full Duplex Time (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{FD} = \frac{\max(F_1, F_2)}{R} = \frac{4000}{50} = 80~\text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Half Duplex Time (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{HD} = \frac{F_1 + F_2}{R} = \frac{4000 + 2400}{50} = \frac{6400}{50} = 128~\text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Speed-up Factor (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S = \frac{t_{HD}}{t_{FD}} = \frac{128}{80} = 1.6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (2 marks):** Full duplex delivers a **1.6× speed-up** for this workload. The improvement would be exactly **2.0×** if both file sizes were equal ($F_1 = F_2$), illustrating that full duplex is most beneficial when bidirectional traffic is balanced.

---

#### 🔹 Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Discuss how **full-duplex operation is achieved in modern Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)**. Explain the role of **separate physical channels** and the **elimination of CSMA/CD**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Channel Separation in Ethernet (2 marks):** In 10BASE-T and 100BASE-TX, the RJ-45 cable contains **4 twisted pairs**, of which **Pair 2 (pins 1, 2)** is dedicated to *transmit* and **Pair 3 (pins 3, 6)** is dedicated to *receive*. Each pair forms an independent physical sub-channel, enabling full duplex.

- **Gigabit and Beyond (2 marks):** 1000BASE-T uses **all four pairs bidirectionally** with **echo cancellation** circuitry and **4D-PAM5** encoding to separate simultaneous Tx and Rx on the same wire. 10GBASE-T and faster variants use similar techniques at higher symbol rates.

- **CSMA/CD Elimination (2 marks):** In half-duplex shared Ethernet (hubs), CSMA/CD detects collisions and triggers back-off. With full duplex on a **point-to-point switch port**, the link is *collision-free by construction* — there is exactly one possible transmitter on each sub-channel. Therefore, **CSMA/CD is disabled** and the link can run continuously at line rate, doubling usable throughput.

- **Practical Benefit (1 mark):** A full-duplex 1 Gbps switch port delivers **2 Gbps aggregate** (1 Gbps in + 1 Gbps out) with **deterministic latency** — critical for data centers, VoIP backbones, and storage networks (iSCSI, FCoE).

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **functional block diagram of a full-duplex communication system**, identifying the **forward channel, reverse channel, and the point of channel separation**. List **three advantages** and **two disadvantages** of full-duplex communication. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Functional Block Diagram (3 marks):** [Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes.] The diagram should show: Source → Encoder → TX Modulator → [Channel Separator] → Physical Medium (with two arrows, one per direction) → RX Demodulator → Decoder → Destination, with the reverse path mirroring the forward one. The **channel separator** is the key block (could be a hybrid transformer, a duplexer, or separate wire pairs).

- **Three Advantages (3 marks — 1 each):**
  1. **Doubled aggregate throughput** — $C_{FD} = 2R$ for symmetric links.
  2. **Collision-free operation** — CSMA/CD becomes unnecessary on point-to-point full-duplex links.
  3. **Lower latency** for two-way protocols such as TCP, VoIP, and interactive SSH sessions, since no turn-taking delay exists.

- **Two Disadvantages (1 mark — 0.5 each):**
  1. **Higher hardware cost and complexity** — requires separate Tx/Rx circuitry, echo cancellers, or extra cabling.
  2. **Channel separation overhead** — wasted bandwidth if traffic is heavily asymmetric (e.g., video streaming one way only).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing "1 Gbps full duplex" with "2 Gbps simplex."** A 1 Gbps full-duplex link delivers **1 Gbps in each direction**, not 2 Gbps one-way. Aggregate is 2 Gbps only if you sum both directions.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply MB by 8** when converting to Mb for time calculations. Always write: $\text{MB} \times 8 = \text{Mb}$.
> 3. **Drawing a single bidirectional arrow for full duplex** in diagrams — the examiner expects **two distinct arrows** showing concurrent forward and reverse flow. A single ⇆ arrow is acceptable for half duplex only.
> 4. **Writing CSMA/CD for full-duplex Ethernet.** It is *disabled* (not "not needed" — it is explicitly turned off per IEEE 802.3).
> 5. **Mixing up TDD and FDD.** TDD shares time on one frequency; FDD uses two frequencies. Both can support full duplex, but they are *different* mechanisms — do not interchange them.
> 6. **Skipping units** in the final answer. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing units like "seconds" or "Mbps."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Full Duplex Communication**

- **Definition:** Bidirectional, **simultaneous** data transfer on a single link.
- **Three Modes Family:** Simplex (one-way) · Half Duplex (alternating) · **Full Duplex (simultaneous)**.
- **Key Equation:** $C_{FD} = C_{fwd} + C_{rev}$ (for symmetric: $C_{FD} = 2R$).
- **File Transfer Times:** $t_{FD} = \dfrac{\max(F_1, F_2)}{R}$ · $t_{HD} = \dfrac{F_1 + F_2}{R}$.
- **CSMA/CD:** **Disabled** in full-duplex Ethernet; **enabled** in half-duplex shared-medium Ethernet.
- **Physical Implementations:** Separate twisted pairs · Frequency Division (FDM) · Time Division Duplexing (TDD) · Wavelength Division (WDM) on fiber.
- **Real-World Examples:** Mobile phone call, full-duplex switch port, fiber backbone, VoIP.
- **Standard Reference:** IEEE **802.3x** — full-duplex flow control for Ethernet.
- **Advantage Highlights:** Doubled throughput · Collision-free · Lower latency for 2-way protocols.
- **Disadvantage Highlights:** Higher cost · Echo-cancellation complexity · Wasted capacity on asymmetric traffic.
- **Exam Mantra:** "**Two arrows, both alive at the same time.**" If your diagram shows only one arrow, it is **not** full duplex.
- **Common Pitfall:** "1 Gbps full duplex" = 1 Gbps each way (aggregate 2 Gbps), **not** 2 Gbps one-way.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Switching Network Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT414 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Switching Network Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Switching Network Criteria

A **Switching Network** is a structured arrangement of switches (cross-points, space switches, or time switches) used to establish temporary communication paths between an input port and an output port in a digital communication or telephone exchange system. The term **"Criteria"** refers to the standardized quantitative and qualitative benchmarks used by network engineers to evaluate, design, and optimize these switching fabrics.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 1 – Switching Networks)**, the *Switching Network Criteria* are the engineering performance measures that determine whether a given switch architecture is acceptable for real-world deployment. These include:

- **Blocking Probability ($P_b$):** The probability that a call/connection request is denied due to lack of an available path.
- **Grade of Service (GoS):** A probabilistic measure of the ability of a switch to carry user traffic.
- **Delay (Latency, $D$):** The time a packet or call must wait before being served.
- **Throughput ($T$):** The number of packets or calls successfully handled per unit time, measured in **bits/second** or **Erlangs**.
- **Traffic Intensity / Load ($\rho$):** The ratio of arrival rate to service rate, dimensionless.
- **Network Capacity ($C$):** The maximum load the network can carry under defined GoS.
- **Non-Blocking Condition:** A property guaranteeing that a free input can always be connected to a free output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 explicitly mandates the study of *switching network criteria* before introducing actual switch designs. The two most-tested criteria in KTU exams are the **Clos Criterion (Non-Blocking Switches)** and the **Erlang Loss Formula (GoS calculation)**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

### 🏢 Analogy: A Hotel Reception Desk

Imagine a **3-star hotel** with 10 receptionists and 100 guests arriving randomly. The hotel manager must decide:
- How many receptionists are enough? *(Switch Size / Capacity)*
- What if all 10 receptionists are busy and a guest walks in? *(Blocking)*
- How long does a guest wait on average? *(Delay)*
- How many guests are served per hour? *(Throughput)*

The **"criteria"** are the manager's tools to answer these questions. If the manager accepts that 1 in 20 guests can be turned away (GoS = 0.05), then she needs only **8 receptionists** instead of 10. This saves cost but introduces *blocking*.

In the same way, **switching network criteria** are the tools used to size, dimension, and operate telephone exchanges, ATM switches, and IP routers.

### 🛣️ Analogy: A Multi-Lane Highway Toll Plaza

A 4-lane toll plaza can be modeled as a **4×4 crossbar switch**. Each car arriving is a packet; the toll booth is the cross-point. The criteria (cars/hour, wait time, queue length) directly mirror network metrics.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Erlang Load (Full) | $\rho = 1$ | Dimensionless | Switch continuously busy |
| Grade of Service | $GoS$ | Typically $0.01$ to $0.05$ | Acceptable loss probability |
| Blocking Probability | $P_b$ | $0 \le P_b \le 1$ | Probability of call rejection |
| Offered Traffic | $A$ | Erlangs | $A = \lambda / \mu$ |
| Mean Call Holding Time | $H$ | Seconds | Average call duration ($1/\mu$) |
| Call Arrival Rate | $\lambda$ | Calls/second | Poisson process assumption |
| Switch Size | $N \times M$ | Pure number | $N$ inputs, $M$ outputs |
| Bit Duration | $T_b$ | Seconds | Inverse of bit rate |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Erlang** (symbol **E**) is the dimensionless international unit of telephone traffic, named after Danish engineer **Agner Krarup Erlang** (1878–1929). It represents one hour of continuous call occupation of a single channel.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Switching Networks (Recap for Context)

Before deriving criteria, we classify the networks the criteria apply to:

1. **Space-Division Switches (SDS):** Physical path established in space (e.g., crossbar).
2. **Time-Division Switches (TDS):** Time-slot interchange in a shared bus.
3. **Combination / Multi-Stage Switches:** e.g., **Clos**, **Banyan**, **Benes** networks.
4. **Blocking vs. Non-Blocking:**
   - **Strictly Non-Blocking (SNB):** Any free input can always reach any free output without rearranging existing calls.
   - **Rearrangeably Non-Blocking (RNB):** Rearranging existing connections allows the new call to be set up.
   - **Blocking:** A new call may be denied even if inputs/outputs are free.

The *criteria* we study are the **mathematical tools to decide which class a design belongs to**.

---

## 1.5 Syllabus Mapping & Course Outcomes (KTU 2024)

| KTU Course Outcome (CO) | Mapped Concept |
|---|---|
| **CO1:** Understand fundamental concepts of data communication and switching networks. | Blocking, GoS, Erlang formulas |
| **CO2:** Analyze performance of switching networks. | Delay, Throughput, Traffic intensity |
| **CO3:** Design non-blocking and blocking switch architectures. | Clos Criterion, Strict-sense non-blocking |
| **CO4:** Evaluate Grade of Service for circuit-switched networks. | Erlang B (Loss Formula), Erlang C (Delay Formula) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Switching Network Criteria

The KTU 2024 module organizes "Switching Network Criteria" around **five core pillars**. Each pillar is a measurable benchmark.

### 🏛️ Pillar 1: Blocking Probability ($P_b$)

**Conceptual Foundation:**
When multiple input lines contend for fewer output paths (or internal links), some requests are denied. The probability of denial is $P_b$.

**Why does blocking happen?**
- **Internal Blocking:** Even if input and output are free, no internal path exists (e.g., a 2-stage Clos network can be internally blocked).
- **Output Blocking:** The output itself is busy.

**Mathematical Definition:**

$$P_b = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\text{Number of blocked calls}}{\text{Number of offered calls}}$$

For an $M/M/m/m$ queueing model (no waiting room, $m$ servers, $m$ positions):

$$P_b = \frac{A^m / m!}{\sum_{k=0}^{m} A^k / k!}$$

This is the famous **Erlang B Formula**.

---

### 🏛️ Pillar 2: Grade of Service (GoS)

**GoS** is the *engineer's contractual* measure of blocking. It is defined as the **probability that a user receives a "no" signal** (busy tone) during the **busy hour** of operation.

$$GoS = P[\text{blocked}] = P_b \quad \text{(at busy hour)}$$

A typical design specification in KTU problems: **"Design a switch for GoS = 0.01"** means we tolerate 1 blocked call per 100 attempted calls.

---

### 🏛️ Pillar 3: Traffic Intensity (Offered Load, $A$)

**Traffic intensity** is the average number of simultaneous calls in progress, measured in **Erlangs**.

**Derivation Logic:**
- Calls arrive according to a **Poisson process** with mean rate $\lambda$ (calls/sec).
- Each call holds the switch for an average duration $H$ seconds.
- During a long observation window $T$:

$$\text{Total call-seconds} = (\text{Number of calls in T}) \times H$$

$$A = \frac{\lambda \cdot H}{1} = \lambda H \quad \text{[Erlangs]}$$

If $\lambda = 100$ calls/hour and average holding time $H = 3$ minutes $= 0.05$ hours:

$$A = 100 \times 0.05 = 5 \text{ Erlangs}$$

---

### 🏛️ Pillar 4: Switch Delay ($D$)

Delay in a switch consists of four components:

1. **Propagation Delay ($D_p$):** Through the switching element (typically nanoseconds).
2. **Processing / Setup Delay ($D_s$):** Time to establish a path (microseconds).
3. **Queuing Delay ($D_q$):** Waiting for a free time slot or output (microseconds to milliseconds).
4. **Transmission Delay ($D_t$):** Time to push bits onto the link.

$$D_{total} = D_p + D_s + D_q + D_t$$

For an $M/M/1$ queue:

$$D_q = \frac{\rho}{\mu(1-\rho)} \quad \text{where } \rho = \lambda / \mu$$

---

### 🏛️ Pillar 5: Throughput ($T$)

Throughput is the *effective* number of calls (or bits) successfully delivered per unit time, **excluding blocked calls**.

$$T = \lambda (1 - P_b) \quad \text{[calls/sec]}$$

For a digital switch:

$$T = \frac{N_{carried} \times L}{T_{observation}} \quad \text{[bits/sec]}$$

---

## 2.2 Clos Criterion (The Most Tested KTU Topic)

The **Clos criterion** determines when a **3-stage space-division network** is **strictly non-blocking**.

### 2.2.1 Clos Network Topology

A Clos network is denoted **$C(m, n, r)$**:
- $r$ first-stage switches, each of size $n \times m$.
- $m$ middle-stage switches, each of size $r \times r$.
- $r$ third-stage switches, each of size $m \times n$.

**Total inputs** = $N = r \cdot n$.

### 2.2.2 Clos Non-Blocking Theorem (1953)

> **Charles Clos (1953):** A 3-stage Clos network is **strictly non-blocking** if and only if:

$$m \ge 2n - 1$$

**Intuitive Explanation:**
In the worst case, $n - 1$ middle links from the first-stage switch and $n - 1$ middle links to the third-stage switch are already busy. To guarantee one *additional* free middle switch to route the new call, we need at least $(n - 1) + (n - 1) + 1 = 2n - 1$ middle switches.

### 2.2.3 Rearrangeably Non-Blocking Clos

A more relaxed condition exists for **rearrangeable non-blocking** networks (Beneš):

$$m \ge n$$

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Condition | Cost (Cross-points) | Blocking Allowed? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Strictly Non-Blocking | $m \ge 2n - 1$ | $2r(N) + 2r(2n-1)r$ ≈ High | Never |
| Rearrangeable | $m \ge n$ | $2r(N) + r^2 n$ ≈ Lower | Rearrangement allowed |
| Blocking | $m < n$ | Lowest | Yes |

---

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table** for KTU exams. Every formula here has appeared in past ESE papers.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Standard Units | Reference / When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \lambda H$ | Offered Traffic | Erlangs | Always: traffic load |
| 2 | $B(A,m) = \dfrac{A^m/m!}{\sum_{k=0}^{m} A^k/k!}$ | Erlang B Loss | Dimensionless | GoS for loss systems |
| 3 | $C(A,m) = \dfrac{A^m/m!}{(1 - \rho)\sum_{k=0}^{m-1} A^k/k!}$ | Erlang C Delay | Dimensionless | GoS for delay systems |
| 4 | $\rho = A/m$ | Server utilization | Dimensionless | System load factor |
| 5 | $P_{\text{wait}} = C(A,m)$ | Probability of waiting | Dimensionless | $M/M/m$ with queue |
| 6 | $D_q = \dfrac{P_{\text{wait}} \cdot H}{m - A}$ | Average queuing delay | Seconds | After Erlang C |
| 7 | $D_{total} = D_p + D_s + D_q + D_t$ | Total switch delay | Seconds | Performance analysis |
| 8 | $T = \lambda (1 - P_b)$ | Throughput | calls/sec | Effective carried traffic |
| 9 | $m \ge 2n - 1$ | Clos SNB condition | Integer | Strict non-blocking design |
| 10 | $m \ge n$ | Clos RNB condition | Integer | Rearrangeable design |
| 11 | $C_{\text{xpt}} = 2Nr + (2n-1)r^2$ | Clos cross-points | Pure number | Cost calculation |
| 12 | $C_{\text{crossbar}} = N^2$ | Crossbar cross-points | Pure number | Baseline cost |
| 13 | $L_q = \dfrac{\rho \cdot C(A,m)}{1 - \rho}$ | Average queue length | Packets/Calls | $M/M/m$ system |
| 14 | $\eta = (1 - P_b) \cdot \rho$ | Channel utilization | Dimensionless | Efficiency metric |
| 15 | $f = 1/T_b$ | Bit rate | bits/sec (bps) | Time-slot calculation |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas above are **dimensionally verified** and algebraically self-consistent. The **vertical bar symbol** for absolute value or conditional probability is intentionally written as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

### 🌐 Application 1: Telephone Exchange Design
The Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN) uses **Erlang B** to dimension trunk groups. When you dial an emergency number, the local exchange has been pre-sized to handle the **busy hour offered load** at a GoS of 0.01.

### 🌐 Application 2: ATM / MPLS Switches
ATM switches used the Clos topology (e.g., **FORE Systems ASX-200**, **Lucent GX 550**). The Clos criterion was essential to design non-blocking 160 Gbps fabrics.

### 🌐 Application 3: Modern Data Center Networks
Modern **leaf-spine** data center fabrics (e.g., Facebook's 6-pack, Google's Jupiter) are abstracted Clos networks. The non-blocking criterion is the *same*, just with optical fiber links instead of copper.

### 🌐 Application 4: 5G Core Network
In 5G AMF/SMF (Access and Mobility Management Function / Session Management Function) load balancers, traffic intensity modeling with Erlang C governs how many worker threads to provision for HTTP/2 signaling.

---

## 2.5 Key Theoretical Assumptions (Held by KTU Problems)

When KTU sets a switching network problem, the following assumptions are *implicit* unless stated otherwise:

1. **Poisson Arrivals:** Calls arrive with rate $\lambda$ following an exponential inter-arrival distribution.
2. **Exponential Holding Time:** Call duration is exponentially distributed with mean $H$.
3. **Stationary System:** Statistical equilibrium is reached (i.e., $\rho < 1$).
4. **Memoryless Property:** Markovian (no history dependence).
5. **Infinite Source Population:** A blocked call is *lost* (no retry assumed in Erlang B).
6. **Statistical Multiplexing:** Many low-rate users share a high-rate link.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Offered Traffic (Erlang) — $A = \lambda H$

This is the most fundamental derivation in the chapter. We derive it line-by-line.

**Given:**
- Calls arrive as a Poisson process with rate $\lambda$ (calls per second).
- Mean holding time of a call: $H$ seconds.

**Goal:** Express the *average number of calls in progress* at any instant.

---

### Step 1: Define Call Arrivals Over a Time Window

Consider a long observation window of duration $T$ seconds.

Number of calls arriving in window $T$:

$$N_{arrivals} = \lambda \cdot T$$

---

### Step 2: Compute Total Call-Seconds (Total Holding Time)

Each call occupies a channel for an average of $H$ seconds. So, the **total channel-seconds consumed** in window $T$ is:

$$S_{total} = N_{arrivals} \cdot H = \lambda T \cdot H$$

---

### Step 3: Compute Average Number of Simultaneous Calls

The average number of calls in progress at any instant is the total channel-seconds divided by the observation window:

$$A = \frac{S_{total}}{T} = \frac{\lambda T H}{T}$$

$$\boxed{A = \lambda H \quad [\text{Erlangs}]}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** If 1 call arrives every second ($\lambda = 1$ call/s) and each lasts 100 seconds ($H = 100$ s), then $A = 100$ Erlangs — meaning we need **at least 100 channels** to carry the load with no blocking.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Erlang B Formula (Loss Formula)

The Erlang B formula gives the **probability that all $m$ channels are busy** (and hence the call is lost/blocked). Derivation requires the **Birth-Death process** for an $M/M/m/m$ queue.

### Step 1: Set Up the Birth-Death Equations

A **Birth-Death process** models a system where the state $k$ = number of busy channels. Birth rate (arrival) and death rate (departure) are state-dependent.

For a Poisson arrival process:

$$\lambda_k = \lambda \quad \text{(constant for all } k < m)$$

$$\mu_k = k \cdot \mu \quad \text{(departure rate is proportional to busy channels)}$$

where $\mu = 1/H$ is the service rate per channel.

---

### Step 2: Apply the Detailed Balance Equations

In statistical equilibrium, the flow into state $k$ equals the flow out:

$$\lambda_{k-1} \cdot P_{k-1} = \mu_k \cdot P_k$$

Iterating from $k = 0$:

$$P_1 = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} P_0 = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} P_0 = A \cdot P_0$$

$$P_2 = \frac{\lambda}{2\mu} P_1 = \frac{A}{2} \cdot A \cdot P_0 = \frac{A^2}{2!} P_0$$

By induction:

$$P_k = \frac{A^k}{k!} P_0$$

---

### Step 3: Use the Normalization Constraint

The probabilities must sum to 1:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{m} P_k = 1$$

$$P_0 \sum_{k=0}^{m} \frac{A^k}{k!} = 1$$

$$P_0 = \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{m} \frac{A^k}{k!} \right]^{-1}$$

---

### Step 4: Define the Blocking Probability

A new call is blocked when **all $m$ channels are already busy**, i.e., when the system is in state $m$:

$$P_b = P_m = \frac{A^m / m!}{\sum_{k=0}^{m} A^k / k!}$$

$$\boxed{B(A, m) = \frac{A^m / m!}{\sum_{k=0}^{m} A^k / k!}}$$

This is the **Erlang B Loss Formula**.

---

### Step 5: Numerical Sanity Check (Important for KTU)

For $A = 2$ Erlangs, $m = 3$ channels:

$$B(2, 3) = \frac{2^3 / 3!}{2^0/0! + 2^1/1! + 2^2/2! + 2^3/3!}$$

$$B(2, 3) = \frac{8/6}{1 + 2 + 2 + 1.333} = \frac{1.333}{6.333} = 0.2105$$

So $GoS \approx 21\%$. High blocking — system is **under-provisioned**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Clos Non-Blocking Condition

**Theorem:** A 3-stage Clos network $C(m, n, r)$ is **strictly non-blocking** iff $m \ge 2n - 1$.

---

### Step 1: Identify the Worst-Case Path Blocking

Consider establishing a new connection from input $i$ (in input module $I_1$) to output $j$ (in output module $O_1$).

There are $m$ middle-stage switches, each providing **one link** between $I_1$ and $O_1$.

- In the **worst case**, $I_1$ has $n - 1$ of its $n$ inputs already busy, each occupying a different middle switch.
- Similarly, $O_1$ has $n - 1$ of its $n$ outputs already busy, each occupied by a different middle switch.

---

### Step 2: Count the Busy Middle Switches

The number of middle switches that are **already engaged** (in the worst case) is:

$$N_{busy} = (n - 1) + (n - 1) = 2n - 2$$

This uses the fact that the worst case is when the busy calls in $I_1$ and $O_1$ use **disjoint** middle switches (so that no middle switch is double-counted, maximizing the count).

---

### Step 3: Guarantee a Free Middle Switch

To guarantee that *at least one* middle switch is still free for the new call, we need:

$$m > 2n - 2 \implies m \ge 2n - 1$$

$$\boxed{m \ge 2n - 1}$$

This completes the proof of the Clos non-blocking condition.

---

### Step 4: Cost (Cross-Point) Comparison

| Architecture | Number of Cross-Points |
|---|---|
| Single-stage $N \times N$ crossbar | $N^2$ |
| 3-stage Clos with $m = 2n-1$ | $2r(2n-1)r + 2Nr$ |
| 3-stage Clos with $m = n$ (RNB) | $r^2 n + 2Nr$ |

For $N = 100$, $n = 10$, $r = 10$:

- **Crossbar:** $100^2 = 10,000$ cross-points
- **Clos SNB:** $\approx 4000 + 2000 = 6000$ cross-points (40% saving)
- **Clos RNB:** $\approx 1000 + 2000 = 3000$ cross-points (70% saving)

---

## 3.4 The "Analytical Matrix" — Mapping Criteria to Switch Architectures

| Criterion | Crossbar | Clos SNB | Clos RNB | Banyan | Benes | Time-Slot Interchange |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Blocking** | Never | Never (by design) | Never (rearrange) | Yes | Never (rearrange) | Never |
| **GoS** | 0 | 0 | 0 | $P_b > 0$ | 0 | 0 |
| **Delay** | Zero (cut-through) | Low | Medium (rearr.) | Medium | Medium | One frame |
| **Cost** | $N^2$ (High) | Medium | Low | $N \log N$ | $N \log N$ | $N$ (TDM bus) |
| **Scalability** | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Good | Limited by clock |
| **Used in** | Small PABX | Telephone CO, ATM | PSTN, MPLS | Parallel routers | Optical switches | Digital PSTN |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Clos Design)

> **Problem:** Design a strictly non-blocking Clos network for $N = 64$ inputs and 64 outputs. Choose the optimal values of $n$, $r$, and $m$.

### Solution:

We want to **minimize cross-points** while satisfying $m \ge 2n - 1$.

**Total inputs:** $N = r \cdot n = 64$.

The total cross-point count for SNB Clos is:

$$X = 2Nr + (2n - 1)r^2$$

We can try different factorizations of 64:

| $n$ | $r = 64/n$ | $m = 2n - 1$ | $X = 2(64)r + (2n-1)r^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 16 | 7 | 2048 + 7 × 256 = 2048 + 1792 = **3840** |
| 8 | 8 | 15 | 1024 + 15 × 64 = 1024 + 960 = **1984** |
| 16 | 4 | 31 | 512 + 31 × 16 = 512 + 496 = **1008** |
| 32 | 2 | 63 | 256 + 63 × 4 = 256 + 252 = **508** |

**Optimal choice:** $n = 32, r = 2, m = 63 \implies X = 508$ cross-points.

Compare with crossbar: $X = 64^2 = 4096$ cross-points.

**Savings:** $\dfrac{4096 - 508}{4096} = 87.6\%$.

This example is **classic KTU ESE material**.

---

## 3.6 Derivation of Mean Queuing Delay (M/M/1)

For a single-server system with Poisson arrivals (rate $\lambda$) and exponential service (rate $\mu$):

**Utilization:** $\rho = \lambda / \mu$.

**Probability of $k$ jobs in system:**

$$P_k = (1 - \rho) \rho^k$$

**Mean number in system (Little's Law application):**

$$L = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} k P_k = \frac{\rho}{1 - \rho}$$

**Mean time in system (Little's Law, $L = \lambda W$):**

$$W = \frac{L}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$$

**Mean waiting time (excluding service):**

$$W_q = W - \frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{\rho}{\mu(1-\rho)}$$

This is the famous $M/M/1$ mean delay.

---

## 3.7 Little's Law (The "Master Theorem" of Queueing)

For any queueing system in steady state:

$$L = \lambda W$$

where $L$ is the mean number in system, $\lambda$ is the arrival rate, and $W$ is the mean time in system. This law is **model-independent** and applies to switches, routers, highways, and hospitals alike.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Switching Network Evaluation Pipeline

This block-level functional architecture illustrates how the **five switching network criteria** are applied sequentially during switch design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Traffic Specification] --> B[Traffic Model: Poisson Arrivals]
    B --> C[Compute Offered Load A lambda H]
    C --> D[Apply Erlang B or Erlang C]
    D --> E{GoS within Limit?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Final Switch Design: 1-Stage 2-Stage 3-Stage Clos]
    E -- No --> G[Increase Channels m or Switch Size N]
    G --> C
    F --> H[Output: Validated Switch with No Blocking or Acceptable GoS]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (`A`, `B`, etc.) to comply with Mermaid compilation safeguards. Labels are clean uppercase text — no markdown bold/italic tags inside quoted strings.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Three-Stage Clos Network

The following block diagram shows the canonical $C(m, n, r)$ Clos network with labeled first-stage, middle-stage, and third-stage switch modules.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputStage[First Stage: n by m switches]
        I1[Input Module 1: n inputs]
        I2[Input Module 2: n inputs]
        I3[Input Module 3: n inputs]
        Ir[Input Module r: n inputs]
    end

    subgraph MiddleStage[Middle Stage: m switches of size r by r]
        M1[Middle Switch 1]
        M2[Middle Switch 2]
        M3[Middle Switch 3]
        Mm[Middle Switch m]
    end

    subgraph OutputStage[Third Stage: m by n switches]
        O1[Output Module 1: n outputs]
        O2[Output Module 2: n outputs]
        O3[Output Module 3: n outputs]
        Or[Output Module r: n outputs]
    end

    I1 --> M1
    I1 --> M2
    I1 --> Mm
    I2 --> M1
    I2 --> M2
    I2 --> Mm
    I3 --> M1
    I3 --> M2
    I3 --> Mm
    Ir --> M1
    Ir --> M2
    Ir --> Mm

    M1 --> O1
    M1 --> O2
    M1 --> Or
    M2 --> O1
    M2 --> O2
    M2 --> Or
    Mm --> O1
    Mm --> O2
    Mm --> Or
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each `I_k` (input module) has $n$ incoming lines and $m$ outgoing middle links.
- Each `M_k` (middle switch) is an $r \times r$ crossbar.
- Each `O_k` (output module) has $m$ incoming middle links and $n$ outgoing lines.
- Total $N = rn$ input ports.

---

## 4.3 State Transition Diagram for M/M/m/m Erlang B System

The birth-death process for an $M/M/m/m$ loss system.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> S0
    S0 --> S1 : Arrival rate lambda
    S1 --> S0 : Departure rate mu
    S1 --> S2 : Arrival rate lambda
    S2 --> S1 : Departure rate 2 mu
    S2 --> S3 : Arrival rate lambda
    S3 --> S2 : Departure rate 3 mu
    S3 --> S4 : Arrival rate lambda
    S4 --> S3 : Departure rate 4 mu
    S4 --> Sm : Arrival rate lambda
    Sm --> S4 : Departure rate m mu
    Sm --> [*] : Blocking occurs state m full
```

**Interpretation:** The system moves between states $0, 1, 2, \ldots, m$. The arrival rate is constant $\lambda$ (Poisson), while the departure rate scales with the number of busy servers. State $m$ represents the **blocked state**.

---

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart: Choosing the Right Switch Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: New Switch Design] --> Q1{Need zero blocking?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is cost primary concern?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Real-time packets?}

    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4[Use 3-Stage Clos RNB: m greater than or equal to n]
    Q2 -- No --> Q5[Use 3-Stage Clos SNB: m greater than or equal to 2n minus 1]

    Q3 -- Yes --> Q6[Use Banyan or Benes: N log N cost]
    Q3 -- No --> Q7[Use Time Division: TDM bus with TSI]

    Q5 --> End[Validated Design]
    Q4 --> End
    Q6 --> End
    Q7 --> End
```

---

## 4.5 Comparative Schematic: Crossbar vs. Clos Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Crossbar[Single-Stage N by N Crossbar]
        C_Input1[Input 1]:::inNode
        C_Input2[Input 2]:::inNode
        C_InputN[Input N]:::inNode
        C_Out1[Output 1]:::outNode
        C_Out2[Output 2]:::outNode
        C_OutN[Output N]:::outNode
        C_Input1 --- C_Out1
        C_Input1 --- C_Out2
        C_Input1 --- C_OutN
        C_Input2 --- C_Out1
        C_Input2 --- C_Out2
        C_Input2 --- C_OutN
        C_InputN --- C_Out1
        C_InputN --- C_Out2
        C_InputN --- C_OutN
    end

    classDef inNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#003366
    classDef outNode fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#663300
```

The crossbar requires **$N^2$ cross-points** — every input connects to every output. The Clos architecture reduces this dramatically by introducing middle-stage switches (as shown in section 4.2).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
> **Define the term "Grade of Service" (GoS) in a switching network. How is it different from "Blocking Probability"?**

### Model Answer (3 marks):
**Grade of Service (GoS):** GoS is a *probabilistic* measure of the ability of a switching system to carry the offered traffic during the **busy hour**. It is defined as the ratio of blocked calls to offered calls, typically expressed as a probability or a fraction. *(1.5 marks)*

**Difference from Blocking Probability:** Blocking probability ($P_b$) is the *general* probability that a call is denied. GoS is the **engineered acceptable value of $P_b$** at the busy hour — a contractual performance target. For example, *"Design for GoS = 0.01"* means $P_b$ should not exceed 0.01 during the busiest hour. *(1.5 marks)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, S1]**
> **What is meant by the term "Erlang" in switching networks? A group of 200 subscribers generates 80 calls per hour with an average call duration of 3 minutes. Find the offered traffic in Erlangs.**

### Model Answer (3 marks):
**Definition of Erlang (1 mark):** An Erlang is the dimensionless unit of telephone traffic, representing **one channel occupied continuously for one hour**, or equivalently, the ratio of call arrival rate to mean service rate ($A = \lambda / \mu$).

**Numerical Computation (2 marks):**
- $\lambda = 80$ calls/hour
- Mean holding time: $H = 3$ min $= 0.05$ hour
- $A = \lambda \times H = 80 \times 0.05 = \mathbf{4 \text{ Erlangs}}$

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) — Clos Network Design Focus

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a **3-stage Clos network** $C(m, n, r)$. State and prove the **Clos criterion** for strictly non-blocking operation. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Design a strictly non-blocking 3-stage Clos network for $N = 100$ inputs/outputs. Determine the optimal values of $n$, $r$, and $m$ that minimize the total number of cross-points. Compute the percentage saving compared to a single-stage $100 \times 100$ crossbar. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Architecture (3 marks):**
- A 3-stage Clos network $C(m, n, r)$ consists of:
  - $r$ first-stage switches, each of size $n \times m$.
  - $m$ middle-stage switches, each of size $r \times r$.
  - $r$ third-stage switches, each of size $m \times n$.
- Total input/output ports: $N = r \cdot n$.
- The first and third stages are symmetric.

**Clos Criterion Statement (1 mark):**
A 3-stage Clos network is **strictly non-blocking** if and only if $m \ge 2n - 1$.

**Proof (3 marks):**
Consider a new call from input $i \in I_1$ to output $j \in O_1$.
- **Worst case:** $I_1$ has $n - 1$ other active calls, each routed via a *distinct* middle switch. So $n - 1$ middle switches are blocked from $I_1$'s side.
- Similarly, $O_1$ has $n - 1$ other active outputs, occupying $n - 1$ *different* middle switches from the output side.
- In the absolute worst case (disjoint middle switches), the total occupied middle switches = $2(n-1) = 2n - 2$.
- For a guaranteed free middle switch: $m > 2n - 2 \Rightarrow m \ge 2n - 1$. ∎

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

We need $N = rn = 100$. Try all divisor pairs and use the formula:

$$X = 2Nr + (2n - 1)r^2$$

| $n$ | $r = 100/n$ | $m = 2n - 1$ | $X$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | 20 | 9 | 4000 + 9 × 400 = **7600** |
| 10 | 10 | 19 | 2000 + 19 × 100 = **3900** |
| 20 | 5 | 39 | 1000 + 39 × 25 = **1975** |
| 25 | 4 | 49 | 800 + 49 × 16 = **1584** |
| 50 | 2 | 99 | 400 + 99 × 4 = **796** |

**Optimal:** $n = 50, r = 2, m = 99, X_{Clos} = 796$ cross-points.

**Crossbar cost:** $X_{crossbar} = N^2 = 100^2 = 10,000$ cross-points.

**Percentage saving:**

$$\text{Saving} = \frac{10000 - 796}{10000} \times 100\% = \boxed{92.04\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **$m$ (middle stage count)** with **$r$ (number of first/third stage switches)**. Remember: $m$ is the *vertical* dimension (middle column), $r$ is the *horizontal* dimension (input/output modules). Mixing them up leads to **wrong cross-point counts and zero marks on the design part**.

---

### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — Erlang Formulas Focus

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** Derive the **Erlang B Loss Formula** from first principles using the birth-death process. State the assumptions clearly. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A telephone exchange has 8 trunk lines. During the busy hour, the offered traffic is $A = 5$ Erlangs. Calculate: (i) the Grade of Service, (ii) the carried traffic, and (iii) the probability that exactly 3 lines are busy. *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Poisson arrivals with rate $\lambda$.
2. Exponential holding time with mean $H = 1/\mu$.
3. $m$ servers, $m$ positions — no waiting room ($M/M/m/m$).
4. Lost calls cleared (blocked calls do not retry).
5. Statistical equilibrium.

**Birth-Death Equations (2 marks):**
State $k$ = number of busy servers.

$$\lambda_k = \lambda \quad (0 \le k < m), \quad \mu_k = k\mu$$

**Detailed Balance:** $P_k = \dfrac{A^k}{k!} P_0$, where $A = \lambda / \mu$.

**Normalization (1.5 marks):**
$$P_0 = \left[\sum_{k=0}^{m} \frac{A^k}{k!}\right]^{-1}$$

**Final Result (1.5 marks):**
$$B(A, m) = P_m = \frac{A^m / m!}{\sum_{k=0}^{m} A^k / k!} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Given: $m = 8$, $A = 5$ Erlangs.

**(i) Grade of Service = Blocking Probability (3 marks):**

$$B(5, 8) = \frac{5^8 / 8!}{\sum_{k=0}^{8} 5^k / k!}$$

Compute denominator:
- $k=0$: $1$
- $k=1$: $5$
- $k=2$: $12.5$
- $k=3$: $20.833$
- $k=4$: $26.042$
- $k=5$: $26.042$
- $k=6$: $21.701$
- $k=7$: $15.501$
- $k=8$: $9.688$

Sum $= 138.307$.

Numerator: $5^8 / 8! = 390625 / 40320 = 9.688$.

$$GoS = B(5, 8) = \frac{9.688}{138.307} = \mathbf{0.0700} \quad (7\%)$$

**(ii) Carried Traffic (2 marks):**

$$A_{carried} = A \times (1 - GoS) = 5 \times (1 - 0.0700) = \mathbf{4.65 \text{ Erlangs}}$$

**(iii) Probability that exactly 3 lines are busy (2 marks):**

$$P_3 = \frac{A^3 / 3!}{\sum_{k=0}^{8} A^k / k!} = \frac{5^3 / 6}{138.307} = \frac{20.833}{138.307} = \mathbf{0.1506}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** In Erlang B problems, students often compute only the **numerator** ($A^m / m!$) and forget the **denominator sum**. This gives an *un-normalized* probability and loses 2–3 marks. Always show the full denominator expansion. Also, **carried traffic ≠ offered traffic** — it is the *successful* portion, i.e., $A \cdot (1 - P_b)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Read this section 10 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

### ✅ Must-Know Definitions
- [ ] **Erlang (E):** Traffic unit; 1 E = 1 channel continuously busy.
- [ ] **Offered Traffic ($A$):** $A = \lambda H$, dimensionless, in Erlangs.
- [ ] **Carried Traffic:** $A_c = A(1 - P_b)$.
- [ ] **Grade of Service (GoS):** Blocking probability at the busy hour.
- [ ] **Blocking Probability ($P_b$):** Probability of a call being denied.

### ✅ Must-Know Formulas (Memorize Symbolically)
- [ ] $A = \lambda H$
- [ ] $B(A, m) = \dfrac{A^m/m!}{\sum_{k=0}^{m} A^k/k!}$
- [ ] $C(A, m) = \dfrac{A^m/m!}{(1 - \rho)\sum_{k=0}^{m-1} A^k/k!}$, where $\rho = A/m$
- [ ] $D_q = \dfrac{C(A,m) \cdot H}{m - A}$
- [ ] Clos SNB: $m \ge 2n - 1$
- [ ] Clos RNB: $m \ge n$
- [ ] Little's Law: $L = \lambda W$

### ✅ Must-Know Theorems
- [ ] **Clos (1953):** 3-stage network SNB iff $m \ge 2n - 1$.
- [ ] **Beneš:** For RNB, $m \ge n$ suffices.
- [ ] **Erlang B:** Loss system; no waiting.
- [ ] **Erlang C:** Delay system; infinite waiting room.

### ✅ Must-Know Numerical Tricks
- [ ] For $A = \lambda$ in calls/min and $H$ in minutes, the Erlangs are unitless.
- [ ] For a Crossbar: cost is $N^2$ (always baseline).
- [ ] For Clos: cost is $2Nr + (2n-1)r^2$ — minimize by choosing $n$ close to $N$ (i.e., $r = 2$ is often best).

### ✅ Common KTU Exam Pitfalls to Avoid
1. ❌ Confusing $\lambda$ (arrival rate) with $\mu$ (service rate).
2. ❌ Computing Erlang B numerator only — must include denominator sum.
3. ❌ Mixing $m$ and $r$ in Clos notation.
4. ❌ Forgetting that Erlang B is for *loss* systems; Erlang C is for *delay* systems.
5. ❌ Using $P_b$ and $GoS$ interchangeably without stating the busy-hour assumption.

### ✅ One-Sentence Summary
> **Switching network criteria are the five pillars — Blocking, GoS, Traffic Intensity, Delay, and Throughput — combined with the Clos non-blocking condition and the Erlang B/C formulas — that allow engineers to design cost-effective, performant, and reliable switching fabrics for telephone, data, and optical networks.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT414 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Physical Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

In the context of computer networks, **Physical Structures** refer to the geometric arrangement of nodes (devices) and links (transmission media) that constitute a computer network. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, a network's physical structure is defined by two principal attributes:

1. **The Type of Connection** (Point-to-Point vs. Multipoint)
2. **The Physical Topology** (Mesh, Star, Bus, Ring, Hybrid, Tree)

A **Physical Topology** describes the configuration of the network's hardware layout — i.e., *how* devices are physically cabled, while **Logical Topology** describes *how* data flows regardless of physical wiring.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, Module 1 of PEEVT414 mandates the study of physical structures, types of connections, physical topologies, and categories of networks (LAN, MAN, WAN, PAN, CAN). Expect at least one 7-mark question on this in the ESE.

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Road Network of a City**
> - **Devices = Houses** (endpoints)
> - **Links = Roads** (communication paths)
> - **Topology = City Map Layout** (planned vs. unplanned)
>
> Just as some cities use a **ring road** (ring topology), others use a **central hub with radiating streets** (star topology), or a **grid of cross-streets** (mesh topology) — networks adopt similar strategies to move "traffic" (data) efficiently. A *Mesh* city has roads between every pair of houses (robust but expensive), while a *Bus* city has one main road with houses on either side (cheap but a single roadblock halts everything).

### Key Terminology

| Term | Definition | Standard Metric |
|------|------------|-----------------|
| **Node** | A device (PC, router, switch) connected to a network | Count |
| **Link** | Communication pathway between two devices | Mbps / Gbps |
| **Topology** | Geometric layout of nodes & links | Category |
| **Throughput** | Actual data transferred per unit time | bits/second (bps) |
| **Bandwidth** | Maximum theoretical capacity of a link | Hz or bps |
| **Propagation Delay** | Time for a bit to travel from source to destination | seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constant:** Speed of light in fiber optics $\approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s. This is the upper bound for any signal propagating through optical fiber.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Connections

**A. Point-to-Point Connection**
- A dedicated link connects exactly **two devices**.
- The **entire capacity** of the link is reserved for transmission between those two nodes.
- Example: A wired connection between your laptop and a printer via USB; a leased line between two offices.
- *Implication:* When $N$ devices need full interconnection via point-to-point, you need a *mesh* structure.

**B. Multipoint (Multidrop) Connection**
- Three or more devices **share a single link**.
- Capacity is *shared* (temporal or spatial multiplexing).
- Also called **broadcast** networks — every device hears every transmission.
- Example: Traditional Ethernet bus, Wi-Fi, satellite communication.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for KTU exams?** Often a 3-mark question asks to "Differentiate between point-to-point and multipoint connections." Memorize the **capacity, channel allocation, and example** columns.

### 2.2 Detailed Topology Analysis

#### i. Mesh Topology
- Every device has a **dedicated point-to-point link** to every other device.
- For $N$ devices, the number of physical links required is:
$$L_{\text{mesh}} = \binom{N}{2} = \frac{N(N-1)}{2}$$
- Each device needs $N-1$ input/output ports.
- **Advantages:** Robust, no traffic problems, privacy, fault isolation.
- **Disadvantages:** Huge cabling, expensive hardware ports, impractical for $N > 8$ except in backbone networks.

#### ii. Star Topology
- All devices connect to a **central hub** (or switch).
- Each device has **one dedicated link** to the hub.
- For $N$ devices, the number of links $= N$.
- **Advantages:** Easy to install/modify, single device failure doesn't kill the network, robust.
- **Disadvantages:** Hub is the single point of failure; requires more cabling than bus but less than mesh.

#### iii. Bus Topology
- All devices share a single backbone cable (the **bus**).
- **Taps** (drop lines) connect devices to the main cable.
- Signal travels in both directions from source until absorbed by **terminators** at the ends.
- **Advantages:** Minimal cable, simple, cheap.
- **Disadvantages:** Single backbone failure halts entire network; performance degrades with heavy traffic; difficult troubleshooting.

#### iv. Ring Topology
- Each device connects to exactly **two neighbors**, forming a circular path.
- Data travels in **one direction** (unidirectional) — called a *single ring*.
- A *dual ring* allows data flow in both directions (used in SONET/SDH backbones).
- **Advantages:** Easy to identify faults, predictable performance, no need for a server to control access (Token Ring).
- **Disadvantages:** Single node/link failure breaks the loop (mitigated by dual rings); reconfiguration is hard.

#### v. Hybrid Topology
- A **combination of two or more different topologies** integrated to leverage each one's strengths.
- Example: A *star-of-stars* (hierarchical star) used in large corporate networks; a *star-ring* hybrid common in campus networks.

#### vi. Tree (Hierarchical) Topology
- A variation of star topology where hubs are arranged in a hierarchy.
- Also called a **star-bus** hybrid.
- The "root" sits at the top, leaves at the bottom.

### 2.3 Categories of Networks (by Scale)

| Category | Full Form | Geographic Span | Typical Speed | Example |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|---------------|---------|
| **PAN** | Personal Area Network | < 10 m | < 1 Mbps | Bluetooth, IR, USB |
| **LAN** | Local Area Network | Building / Campus (1 m – 1 km) | 100 Mbps – 10 Gbps | Office Ethernet, Wi-Fi |
| **CAN** | Campus Area Network | Multiple buildings (1–5 km) | 1 Gbps – 10 Gbps | University network |
| **MAN** | Metropolitan Area Network | City (5–50 km) | 100 Mbps – 10 Gbps | Cable TV network, Metro-Ethernet |
| **WAN** | Wide Area Network | Country / World (50 km+) | 1.5 Mbps – 100 Gbps | Internet, leased lines |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` or `\mid` in tables — never the pipe `|`.**

| Formula Name | Equation | Description / Use |
|--------------|----------|--------------------|
| Number of links in Mesh | $L_{\text{mesh}} = \frac{N(N-1)}{2}$ | Full mesh with $N$ nodes |
| Number of links in Star | $L_{\text{star}} = N$ | One link per node to hub |
| Number of links in Ring | $L_{\text{ring}} = N$ | Each node connects to 2 others |
| Number of links in Bus | $L_{\text{bus}} = 1$ | Single shared backbone |
| Propagation Delay | $D_{\text{prop}} = \frac{D}{S}$ | $D$ = distance, $S$ = propagation speed |
| Transmission Delay | $D_{\text{trans}} = \frac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = packet length (bits), $R$ = bandwidth (bps) |
| Total Delay (approx.) | $D_{\text{total}} = D_{\text{prop}} + D_{\text{trans}} + D_{\text{proc}} + D_{\text{queue}}$ | End-to-end latency |
| Bandwidth-Delay Product | $BDP = R \times D_{\text{prop}}$ | Bits in-flight on the link |
| Throughput (multi-link) | $T = \min(R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n)$ | Bottleneck determines rate |

> **NOTE on units:** Propagation speed $S$ for copper $\approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s; for fiber $\approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s; for wireless $\approx 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Physical structure selection directly impacts:
- **Cost (CAPEX)** — Mesh uses $\frac{N(N-1)}{2}$ ports and cables; star uses only $N$.
- **Latency** — Bus suffers from collisions; ring introduces token-passing delay.
- **Fault tolerance** — Mesh is most resilient; star/bus are vulnerable at the hub/backbone.
- **Scalability** — Star scales best in enterprise settings (hence dominance of Ethernet star).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Number of Links in a Full Mesh Topology

**Given:** A network of $N$ devices arranged in a full mesh topology.
**To find:** Total number of dedicated point-to-point links $L$.

**Step 1 — Count links per device.**
Each device must have a direct link to every other device. The number of other devices is:
$$
\text{links per device} = N - 1
$$

**Step 2 — Avoid double-counting.**
If we naïvely multiply, we get $N \times (N-1)$ link-ends. But each link has **two ends** (one at each device), so each physical link is counted twice.

**Step 3 — Divide by 2.**
$$
L_{\text{mesh}} = \frac{N(N-1)}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent combinatorial form.**
This is precisely the binomial coefficient $\binom{N}{2}$, the number of ways to choose 2 devices out of $N$ for a link.

**Step 5 — Example.**
For $N = 5$ devices:
$$
L_{\text{mesh}} = \frac{5 \times 4}{2} = 10 \text{ links}
$$
Each device needs $N - 1 = 4$ ports.

**Step 6 — Growth behavior.**
For large $N$, links grow **quadratically** $O(N^2)$, which is why full mesh is impractical for $N > 8$ to $10$ in LANs. Backbone mesh topologies (e.g., internet core) use a *partial mesh* to balance cost and redundancy.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Total Delay on a Link

**Given:** A packet of $L$ bits transmitted over a link of bandwidth $R$ bps and physical length $D$ meters with propagation speed $S$.

**Step 1 — Transmission Delay.**
The time the router/sender takes to push all $L$ bits onto the wire:
$$
D_{\text{trans}} = \frac{L}{R} \quad [\text{seconds}]
$$

**Step 2 — Propagation Delay.**
The time the first bit takes to travel the length of the medium:
$$
D_{\text{prop}} = \frac{D}{S} \quad [\text{seconds}]
$$

**Step 3 — Total nodal delay (ignoring queueing for simplicity).**
$$
D_{\text{total}} = D_{\text{trans}} + D_{\text{prop}} + D_{\text{proc}} + D_{\text{queue}}
$$
where $D_{\text{proc}}$ is processing delay (typically $\mu$s) and $D_{\text{queue}}$ is queueing delay (variable, depends on congestion).

**Step 4 — Worked example.**
A 1000-bit packet over a 100 Mbps link, 2000 m of fiber ($S = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s):

$$
D_{\text{trans}} = \frac{1000}{100 \times 10^6} = 10 \, \mu s
$$

$$
D_{\text{prop}} = \frac{2000}{2 \times 10^8} = 10 \, \mu s
$$

$$
D_{\text{total (approx.)}} = 10 + 10 = 20 \, \mu s
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For short LANs, $D_{\text{trans}} \gg D_{\text{prop}}$. For long satellite links, $D_{\text{prop}} \gg D_{\text{trans}}$. This dictates where you optimize in design.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Topology Comparison

| Property | Mesh | Star | Bus | Ring | Tree |
|----------|------|------|-----|------|------|
| Number of links | $\frac{N(N-1)}{2}$ | $N$ | $1$ | $N$ | $N-1$ (approx.) |
| Cost (links) | Very High | Moderate | Low | Moderate | Moderate |
| Fault tolerance | Excellent | Poor (hub failure) | Poor | Moderate (Dual ring better) | Moderate |
| Scalability | Poor | Excellent | Poor | Poor | Good |
| Cabling complexity | Very High | Moderate | Low | Moderate | Moderate |
| Typical Use | Internet backbone, ISP core | LANs (Ethernet) | Legacy Ethernet, cable | Token Ring, SONET/SDH | Hierarchical LAN |
| KTU mark weight | High | High | High | High | Medium |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Bottleneck Throughput in a Multi-Hop Path

**Given:** A path with 3 links of bandwidth $R_1 = 10$ Mbps, $R_2 = 5$ Mbps, $R_3 = 8$ Mbps.

**Step 1 — Apply the bottleneck principle.**
$$
T_{\text{path}} = \min(R_1, R_2, R_3) = \min(10, 5, 8) = 5 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation.**
The 5 Mbps link is the bottleneck — the entire path cannot transfer faster than the slowest segment, regardless of how fast the others are.

**Step 3 — End-to-end delay for a 4 Mbit file.**
$$
\text{Time} = \frac{4 \times 10^6 \text{ bits}}{5 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 0.8 \text{ seconds}
$$
(Ignoring propagation and processing for this example.)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mesh Topology (Full Mesh, N = 5)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[NODE A] --- B1[NODE B]
    A1 --- C1[NODE C]
    A1 --- D1[NODE D]
    A1 --- E1[NODE E]
    B1 --- C1
    B1 --- D1
    B1 --- E1
    C1 --- D1
    C1 --- E1
    D1 --- E1
```

> **Block Architecture Note:** Every node in the above graph is a device; every edge is a dedicated point-to-point link. Total links = 10 (as derived in §3.1).

### 4.2 Star Topology (N = 5)

```mermaid
graph TD
    H[HUB SWITCH] --- N1[NODE 1]
    H --- N2[NODE 2]
    H --- N3[NODE 3]
    H --- N4[NODE 4]
    H --- N5[NODE 5]
```

> **Block Architecture Note:** All communication in a star passes through the central hub. If the hub fails, the entire network goes down — a classic single-point-of-failure design.

### 4.3 Bus Topology

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1[TERMINATOR] --- BUS((BACKBONE BUS))
    BUS --- N1[NODE 1]
    BUS --- N2[NODE 2]
    BUS --- N3[NODE 3]
    BUS --- N4[NODE 4]
    BUS --- N5[NODE 5]
    BUS --- T2[TERMINATOR]
```

> **Block Architecture Note:** All nodes tap into a single shared medium. Terminators (resistors) at both ends absorb the signal to prevent reflection.

### 4.4 Ring Topology (Unidirectional)

```mermaid
graph LR
    N1[NODE 1] --> N2[NODE 2]
    N2 --> N3[NODE 3]
    N3 --> N4[NODE 4]
    N4 --> N5[NODE 5]
    N5 --> N1
```

> **Block Architecture Note:** A token circulates in one direction. A node can transmit only when it holds the token. Failure of any single node or link breaks the loop — hence dual rings in modern SONET/SDH.

### 4.5 Hybrid Topology (Star-of-Rings, Typical Campus Network)

```mermaid
graph TB
    CORE[CORE HUB A] --- R1[RING 1 HUB]
    CORE --- R2[RING 2 HUB]
    CORE --- R3[RING 3 HUB]
    R1 --- S1A[STAR A]
    R1 --- S1B[STAR B]
    R2 --- S2A[STAR C]
    R2 --- S2B[STAR D]
    R3 --- S3A[STAR E]
    R3 --- S3B[STAR F]
    S1A --- DEV1[DEVICES]
    S1B --- DEV2[DEVICES]
    S2A --- DEV3[DEVICES]
    S2B --- DEV4[DEVICES]
    S3A --- DEV5[DEVICES]
    S3B --- DEV6[DEVICES]
```

> **Block Architecture Note:** Rings connect at the core (high reliability), and each ring fans out into multiple stars (cost-effective edge connectivity). This is the canonical **enterprise campus design**.

### 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Internetwork

```mermaid
graph TB
    LAN1[LAN SEGMENT 1] --- R1[ROUTER A]
    LAN2[LAN SEGMENT 2] --- R1
    LAN3[LAN SEGMENT 3] --- R2[ROUTER B]
    LAN4[LAN SEGMENT 4] --- R2
    R1 --- WAN[WAN BACKBONE]
    R2 --- WAN
    WAN --- R3[ROUTER C]
    R3 --- LAN5[LAN SEGMENT 5]
    R3 --- LAN6[LAN SEGMENT 6]
```

> **Block Architecture Note:** Routers interconnect LANs over a WAN. Each LAN may internally use any physical topology (usually star), but the inter-router links form a partial mesh at the WAN layer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define physical topology. Differentiate between physical and logical topology.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

*Physical topology* refers to the actual geometric arrangement of nodes and links in a network — i.e., the **physical layout** of cables and devices. Example: Bus, Star, Mesh, Ring, Tree, Hybrid.

*Logical topology* refers to the way data flows within the network, **regardless of physical wiring**. Example: A network physically wired as a star may logically operate as a bus if all signals are broadcast through the hub (as in early Ethernet hubs).

| Aspect | Physical Topology | Logical Topology |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Definition | Hardware layout | Data flow pattern |
| Change requires | New cabling | Software reconfiguration |
| Example | Star-wired office | Token-passing protocol |

*(3 marks — Definition 1, Difference table 2)*

---

#### **Question 2: List any three categories of networks based on geographic span.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **LAN (Local Area Network)** — spans a building or campus, typically up to 1 km, speeds of 100 Mbps to 10 Gbps.
2. **MAN (Metropolitan Area Network)** — spans a city, typically 5–50 km, used by telecom operators and ISPs.
3. **WAN (Wide Area Network)** — spans a country or globe, with public/private infrastructure; the Internet is the largest example.

*(3 marks — 1 mark per category with span/speed detail)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Pattern (per KTU 2024 ESE):** Answer **ONE** of the two alternatives.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — OR — Question B (14 Marks)**

---

### **Question A:**
**(a)** Explain the **Mesh topology** in detail. Derive the expression for the number of links required for $N$ devices. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Star, Bus, and Ring topologies** with neat diagrams. In which scenario would you prefer a star topology over a bus? Justify. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A(a)**

**Mesh Topology Explanation:**

In a mesh topology, every device is connected to every other device via a **dedicated point-to-point link**. There are two variants:
- **Full Mesh** — every node connects to every other node.
- **Partial Mesh** — only some nodes have direct links; the rest connect via intermediate nodes.

**Derivation of number of links:**

Each of the $N$ devices must have a link to the remaining $N-1$ devices. Counting link-ends gives $N(N-1)$. Since each link is shared between two devices (double-counted), the actual number of physical links is:

$$
L_{\text{mesh}} = \frac{N(N-1)}{2}
$$

**Example:** For $N = 6$:
$$
L_{\text{mesh}} = \frac{6 \times 5}{2} = 15 \text{ links}
$$
Each device needs $N - 1 = 5$ I/O ports.

**Advantages:**
1. **Robustness** — alternative paths exist between any pair of devices.
2. **No traffic congestion** — dedicated links eliminate sharing.
3. **Privacy and security** — point-to-point links are inherently more secure.
4. **Fault isolation** — one link failure affects only two devices.
5. **Predictable performance.**

**Disadvantages:**
1. **High cost** — both cable and port count grow quadratically.
2. **Installation complexity** — $\frac{N(N-1)}{2}$ cables must be routed.
3. **Impractical for large $N$** — fine for $N \le 8$; too expensive beyond.
4. **Bulk of hardware** — each device needs $N-1$ ports.

**Use case:** Internet backbone, ISP core, critical military/financial networks where downtime is unacceptable.

*(Mark split: Definition 1, Derivation 3, Advantages 2, Disadvantages 1)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A(b)**

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Star | Bus | Ring |
|----------|------|-----|------|
| Layout | Devices → central hub | Devices → single backbone | Closed loop |
| Links required | $N$ | $1$ | $N$ |
| Cost | Moderate | Low | Moderate |
| Fault tolerance | Hub is SPOF | Backbone is SPOF | Single link/node breaks ring |
| Performance | High (no collisions) | Degrades with traffic | Predictable (token) |
| Scalability | Excellent | Poor | Poor |
| Best for | Modern LANs | Legacy/small networks | Token Ring, SONET |

**Diagrams (as in §4.2, 4.3, 4.4):**

- *Star:* A central hub with each device connected by a dedicated cable.
- *Bus:* A long backbone cable with T-taps to each device, terminators at ends.
- *Ring:* Each device has exactly two neighbors, forming a circular path.

**When to prefer Star over Bus — Justification:**

A star topology should be preferred over a bus topology when:
1. **Scalability is required** — adding a new device in a star means running one cable to the hub, while a bus may require breaking the backbone (causing downtime).
2. **Fault isolation matters** — a single cable or port failure in star only affects one device, whereas a backbone break in a bus halts the whole segment.
3. **Performance under load is critical** — bus suffers from collisions as traffic grows; star with a switch provides dedicated, collision-free segments.
4. **Ease of troubleshooting** — star's centralized design simplifies network management and diagnostics.

Hence, **modern Ethernet LANs universally use star topology** (technically star-of-hubs or star-of-switches), even though the original 10BASE-5/10BASE-2 Ethernet was bus-based.

*(Mark split: Comparison table 3, Diagrams 2, Justification 2)*

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice):**
**(a)** What is a **Hybrid topology**? Describe the **Star-of-Rings** and **Star-of-Bus** configurations with suitable diagrams. List any four advantages of hybrid topology. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(b)** With neat diagrams, explain **Tree** and **Ring** topologies. A company has 6 branches in a city. Each branch has 50 computers. Design a suitable topology and justify. **(7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B(a)**

**Hybrid Topology Definition:**
A hybrid topology is a network layout that combines two or more different basic topologies (mesh, star, bus, ring) to take advantage of each one's strengths while mitigating their weaknesses.

**Star-of-Rings Configuration:**

- Multiple **rings** are interconnected through a **central star hub** (or via a core router).
- Each ring serves a department or floor; the central hub interconnects all rings.
- *Use case:* Token Ring LANs in campus environments.

**Diagram (refer §4.5):** Multiple rings feed into a central core node, with stars fanning from each ring.

**Star-of-Bus (Tree) Configuration:**

- Several **stars** are connected to a common **bus backbone**.
- Each star cluster serves a workgroup; the bus interconnects the clusters.
- *Use case:* Hierarchical corporate networks, 10BASE-T Ethernet with hub-based segments.

**Diagram:**
```
[Bus Backbone]
   |      |      |
[Star] [Star] [Star]
  /\     /\     /\
 PCs   PCs   PCs
```

**Advantages of Hybrid Topology:**
1. **Scalable** — easy to expand by adding new segments.
2. **Flexible** — can choose topology per segment to match workload.
3. **Reliable** — failure of one segment does not crash the whole network.
4. **Cost-effective** — only critical segments use expensive mesh; the rest use cheap star/bus.
5. **Easier troubleshooting** — modular design aids diagnostics.

*(Mark split: Definition 1, Star-of-Rings 2, Star-of-Bus 2, Advantages 2)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B(b)**

**Tree Topology:**
A tree (or hierarchical) topology arranges nodes in a parent-child hierarchy, resembling an inverted tree. The root node connects to intermediate nodes, which in turn connect to leaf nodes. It is essentially a **collection of stars connected to a bus**.

**Diagram (refer §4.5 root structure):**
- Root hub → secondary hubs → end devices.

**Advantages:** Scalable, easy to segment and isolate.
**Disadvantages:** Root failure is catastrophic; deep trees add latency.

**Ring Topology:**
In a ring, each node connects to exactly two neighbors, forming a closed loop. Data flows unidirectionally (or bidirectionally in dual rings). Token-passing ensures orderly access.

**Diagram (refer §4.4):** Five nodes connected in a closed circular chain.

**Design for 6 branches × 50 computers each:**

A **Star-of-Bus (Hierarchical Tree / Star-of-Stars)** is recommended:

- **Level 1 (Core):** A central **router or L3 switch** at the company HQ.
- **Level 2 (Aggregation):** A **star** in each branch — a central Ethernet switch connecting the 50 PCs.
- **Level 3 (Backbone):** A **bus or ring** (e.g., leased line / MPLS / fiber ring) connects the 6 branch stars to the core.

**Justification:**
1. **Per-branch reliability:** A star with a switch isolates faults to a single port.
2. **Backbone reliability:** A ring (or dual-ring) connecting branches provides redundancy — if one segment fails, traffic flows the other way.
3. **Scalability:** Adding a new branch only requires one new connection to the backbone ring.
4. **Manageability:** Each branch's star can be administered locally; the core manages inter-branch routing.
5. **Cost-effectiveness:** Stars use inexpensive Cat6 cabling within branches; the inter-branch ring uses higher-grade fiber.

*(Mark split: Tree explanation 1.5, Ring explanation 1.5, Design 2, Justification 2)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by 2 in mesh link derivation** — losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing physical and logical topology** — they are *not* the same. A 1-mark difference is awarded.
> 3. **Drawing diagrams with no labels** — even in star/ring, label the hub and at least two nodes.
> 4. **Missing "advantages" in hybrid questions** — KTU specifically awards 2 marks for advantages.
> 5. **Units and propagation speed confusion** — if a numerical delay problem appears, always state $S = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s explicitly for fiber/copper.
> 6. **No mention of single point of failure** in star/bus — a key examiner-trap.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ **Physical topology** = hardware layout; **Logical topology** = data flow path.
- ☐ **Point-to-point** = dedicated link between two devices; **Multipoint** = shared medium.
- ☐ **Mesh links formula:** $L = \frac{N(N-1)}{2}$ — *must* divide by 2 (double-count correction).
- ☐ **Star links:** $L = N$ — single point of failure at hub.
- ☐ **Bus links:** $L = 1$ — terminators required at both ends.
- ☐ **Ring links:** $L = N$ — token-based access; unidirectional (or dual).
- ☐ **Categories by span:** PAN < LAN < CAN < MAN < WAN (in terms of geographic coverage).
- ☐ **Hybrid topology** = combination of ≥ 2 basic topologies (Star-of-Rings, Star-of-Bus).
- ☐ **Tree topology** = hierarchical star clusters; root failure is catastrophic.
- ☐ **Propagation delay** $D_p = D/S$; **Transmission delay** $D_t = L/R$.
- ☐ Speed of light in fiber: $2 \times 10^8$ m/s; in air/vacuum: $3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- ☐ Bottleneck throughput = $\min(R_1, R_2, \dots, R_n)$ for a multi-hop path.
- ☐ Modern Ethernet LAN = **star** topology (10BASE-T, 100BASE-TX, 1000BASE-T).
- ☐ Internet = **partial mesh** of routers at the WAN level; **star** of switches at the LAN level.
- ☐ For KTU 2024, expect 1 long question (7–14 marks) and possibly 1 short (3 marks) on this module.
- ☐ Always draw **labelled** diagrams in ESE — no marks for unlabeled sketches.
- ☐ Use **Mermaid or block diagrams** in your preparation; examiners reward clear structure.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT414 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Network Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Network Model** is a structured, layered architectural blueprint that decomposes the complex process of data communication into a series of well-defined, self-contained functional layers. Each layer performs a specific set of communication functions, communicates only with the layers directly above and below it, and provides well-defined services to the layer above while requesting services from the layer below. The two most prominent network models taught under the KTU 2024 Scheme are the **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Reference Model** (a 7-layer conceptual framework defined by **ISO** and **ITU-T**) and the **TCP/IP Protocol Suite** (a 4/5-layer practical model pioneered by **DARPA** and the **IETF**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU Board consistently tests the OSI 7-layer model, the TCP/IP 4/5-layer model, the function of each layer, the concept of **PDUs (Protocol Data Units)**, **encapsulation/decapsulation**, and the **peer-to-peer communication** paradigm. Memorize the layer names in order — both top-down and bottom-up.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Letter" System

Imagine you want to send a 50-page legal document from your office in Trivandrum to a colleague in Berlin:

| Real-World Analogy | Network Model Equivalent |
|---|---|
| You **write the letter** (content creation) | **Application Layer** — generates user data |
| You **convert** it to PDF / encrypt it / compress it | **Presentation Layer** — formatting, encryption, compression |
| You **decide** the language/handshake for conversation | **Session Layer** — establishes, manages, terminates sessions |
| You **hand it to a registered courier** (reliable delivery guarantee) | **Transport Layer** — end-to-end reliability, segmentation |
| You **write the destination address** (country → city → street) | **Network Layer** — logical addressing & routing |
| The courier **loads it onto a specific truck** and tracks it locally | **Data Link Layer** — framing, MAC addressing, error detection |
| The truck **physically drives** on the road using diesel & signals | **Physical Layer** — bits as electrical/optical/radio signals |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Layering?** Layering promotes **modularity**, **interoperability**, **simplified design**, and **ease of troubleshooting**. A change in one layer (e.g., replacing Wi-Fi with Li-Fi) does not require changes in higher layers. This is the **"Service Abstraction Principle"** of the OSI model.

### 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

- **PDU (Protocol Data Unit):** The formatted unit of data exchanged between two peer entities at a given layer. It consists of **Header (Control Information)** + **Payload (Data from the upper layer)**, and optionally a **Trailer (Error detection bits like CRC)**.
- **SDU (Service Data Unit):** The actual user data passed unchanged from a higher layer to a lower layer.
- **ICI (Interface Control Information):** Control parameters passed across a service access point.
- **SAP (Service Access Point):** The logical "port" through which a layer requests services from the layer below. Example: Port 80 in TCP, MAC address 0x1234 in Data Link.
- **Peer-to-Peer Process:** Two entities at the *same layer* on *different machines* communicate logically as if directly connected. The actual physical hop-by-hop journey is transparent to them.
- **Encapsulation:** The process of a lower layer *wrapping* the upper layer's data inside its own header/trailer.
- **Decapsulation:** The reverse — each layer strips its own header as the data moves upward toward the destination application.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Reference Bodies

- **OSI Model Layers (Bottom-Up):** Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application. Mnemonic: **"Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away"**.
- **TCP/IP Layers (Bottom-Up):** Network Access (or Link), Internet, Transport, Application. Mnemonic: **"All I Need Is Coffee AT Night"** (with Network Access split as "Network Interface" / "Link").
- **Standardizing Bodies:** **ISO** (OSI Model), **ITU-T** (X.200 recommendation), **IEEE** (802.x LAN standards), **IETF** (TCP/IP RFCs), **EIA/TIA** (Physical layer cabling).
- **Maximum Theoretical PDU Sizes (for analytical questions):** Ethernet MTU = **1500 bytes**, IP Header = **20–60 bytes**, TCP Header = **20–60 bytes**, UDP Header = **8 bytes**.
