# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Interconnection

This diagram captures the *logical* flow of an IP packet as it traverses the layered internetwork. It is the most frequently asked 14-mark KTU diagram for Module 1.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Application Layer: HTTP FTP DNS SMTP] --> B[Transport Layer: TCP or UDP]
    B --> C[Network Layer: IP Packet Encapsulation]
    C --> D[Data Link Layer: Frame Encapsulation MAC Addressing]
    D --> E[Physical Layer: Bit Transmission over Cable or Wireless]

    E --> F[Hop 1 Router: Decapsulates Frame, Reads IP]
    F --> G[Hop 1 Routing Table Lookup]
    G --> H[Re-encapsulation on Outgoing Interface]
    H --> I[Hop 2 Router: Decapsulates Frame, Reads IP]
    I --> J[Hop 2 Routing Table Lookup]
    J --> K[Re-encapsulation on Outgoing Interface]
    K --> L[Hop 3 Final Hop to Destination LAN]
    L --> M[Destination Host: Full Decapsulation]
    M --> N[Application Layer Receives Data]

    subgraph L3_PATH[Layer 3 Logical Internetwork]
      C
      F
      I
      M
    end

    subgraph L2_HOP[Hop-Level Layer 2 Switching]
      D
      H
      K
    end
```

## 4.2 The Internet as a Hierarchical Network of Networks

This diagram illustrates the **autonomous-system hierarchy** of the global Internet. It is the *second* most important KTU diagram for Module 1, often paired with a 7-mark explanation.

```mermaid

    P1[End User Host A] --> P2[Access ISP: Local Cable or DSL]

    P5 --> P6[IXP: London LINX]
    P6 --> P7[Tier 1 Transit Provider AS 7018]
    P7 --> P8[Regional ISP AS 65020]
    P8 --> P9[Access ISP: Another Country]

    subgraph GLOBAL_CORE[Global Internet Core]
      P5

      P7

    subgraph EDGE_NETWORKS[Edge Networks]

      P2
      P3

```

## 4.3 TCP/IP Protocol Stack vs. OSI Reference Model (Interconnection View)

This Mermaid block is a **functional architecture comparison** highlighting how the TCP/IP stack — the protocol foundation of network interconnection — maps to the OSI model. Use this when a 7-mark question asks *"Compare the OSI and TCP/IP models in the context of internetworking."*

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph OSI_MODEL[OSI Seven Layer Model]
      O1[7 Application]
      O2[6 Presentation]
      O3[5 Session]
      O4[4 Transport]
      O5[3 Network]
      O6[2 Data Link]
      O7[1 Physical]
    end

    subgraph TCPIP_MODEL[TCP/IP Four Layer Model]
      T1[4 Application: HTTP DNS FTP SMTP]
      T2[3 Transport: TCP UDP]
      T3[2 Internet: IP ICMP ARP]
      T4[1 Network Access: Ethernet WiFi PPP]
    end

    O1 --- T1
    O2 --- T1
    O3 --- T1
    O4 --- T2
    O5 --- T3
    O6 --- T4
    O7 --- T4
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Mnemonic — "All People Seem To Need Data Processing":** OSI top-to-bottom (Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical). For TCP/IP, just collapse the top three into **Application** and the bottom two into **Network Access**.

## 4.4 Interconnection Device Decision Tree (Block Flow)

When a packet arrives at a device, the device must decide *how* to forward it. The decision tree below summarizes the routing logic for any router in an internetwork.

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1[Packet Arrives at Router Interface] --> Q2{Destination IP in My Directly Connected Network?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Use ARP to Find Destination MAC]
    Q3 --> Q4[Forward Packet Directly to Host]
    Q2 -- No --> Q5{Longest Prefix Match Found in Routing Table?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> Q6[Forward to Specified Next Hop]
    Q5 -- No --> Q7{Default Route Exists?}
    Q7 -- Yes --> Q8[Forward via Default Gateway]
    Q7 -- No --> Q9[Send ICMP Destination Unreachable to Source]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEEVT414]**
*Define the term "Interconnection of Networks" and list any two advantages of using the TCP/IP protocol suite for internetworking.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Interconnection of networks (Internetworking) is the process of connecting two or more distinct computer networks — which may use different hardware, link technologies, and protocols — through intermediate devices (routers/gateways) using a common logical protocol suite, so that hosts on any one network can communicate seamlessly with hosts on any other.

**Two Advantages of TCP/IP:**
1. **Protocol Independence from Physical Medium:** TCP/IP is *not* tied to Ethernet, Wi-Fi, or any specific link-layer technology. The same IP datagram can be carried over copper, fiber, radio, or satellite — a property called **network layer independence**. This was the first of Cerf & Kahn's 1974 design goals.
2. **Scalable Addressing and Routing:** TCP/IP's hierarchical 32-bit (IPv4) / 128-bit (IPv6) addressing and classless routing (CIDR) allow the Internet to scale to billions of hosts. The hierarchical aggregation into Autonomous Systems (AS) means routing tables in core routers contain only summary prefixes, not individual host routes.

> [!TIP]
> For full 3 marks, you must give the **definition (1 mark) + two distinct advantages (1 mark each)**. Avoid writing generic phrases like "it is widely used" — write specific TCP/IP properties.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, PEEVT414]**
*With a neat diagram, explain the role of a router in interconnecting two different networks.*

**Model Answer:**

A **router** is a Layer-3 (Network Layer) device that interconnects two or more IP networks. Its role is to receive IP packets from one network, examine the destination IP address, consult its routing table, and forward the packet toward the correct next-hop network — even if the incoming and outgoing links use completely different physical media (e.g., Ethernet ↔ Wi-Fi ↔ Fiber).

**Roles:**
- **Logical Addressing:** Operates on IP addresses (not MAC).
- **Path Selection:** Uses routing algorithms (OSPF, BGP) to find the best path.
- **Packet Re-encapsulation:** Strips the incoming frame, looks up the next hop, builds a new frame for the outgoing link.
- **Network Isolation:** Each interface is a separate broadcast domain, reducing congestion.

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Host A in Network 1 Ethernet] --> P1[Router Interface 1]
    P1 --> R[Router Central Processor and Routing Table]
    R --> P2[Router Interface 2]
    P2 --> B[Host B in Network 2 WiFi]
```

> [!NOTE]
> A 3-mark answer expects: **Definition (1 mark) + Role list (1 mark) + Diagram (1 mark)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEEVT414, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **TCP/IP protocol suite** with a neat layered diagram. Compare it with the **OSI reference model** in the context of internetworking. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A message of **2.4 Kbits** is to be sent across an internetwork consisting of **5 identical links**, each of bandwidth **500 Kbps**, propagation speed **$2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s**, and length **1500 m**. Compute the **total end-to-end delay**, assuming **negligible processing and queuing delay**. Also compute the **link utilization** if Stop-and-Wait ARQ is used. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Solution to Question A (a)

**1. The TCP/IP Protocol Suite (4 marks):**

The TCP/IP (Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol) suite is the foundational protocol family that enables heterogeneous network interconnection. It is a **4-layer model** defined by the IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force) and is used by the global Internet.

**Layer 1 — Application Layer (Processes Layer):**
- Provides network services to end-user applications.
- Protocols: **HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SSH, Telnet, SNMP**.

**Layer 2 — Transport Layer (Host-to-Host Layer):**
- Provides end-to-end communication services for applications.
- Two main protocols: **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)** — reliable, connection-oriented, byte-stream; and **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)** — unreliable, connectionless, message-oriented.
- Adds **port numbers** for multiplexing.

**Layer 3 — Internet Layer (Network Layer):**
- Provides logical (IP) addressing and best-effort global packet delivery.
- Core protocols: **IPv4, IPv6, ICMP (errors), IGMP (multicast), ARP (address resolution)**.

**Layer 4 — Network Access Layer (Link/Physical Layer):**
- Handles framing, MAC addressing, and physical signaling.
- Examples: **Ethernet, Wi-Fi (802.11), PPP, ATM, SONET**.

**2. Comparison with OSI (3 marks):**

| Aspect | OSI (7 layers) | TCP/IP (4 layers) |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | ISO Standard | ARPA / IETF |
| **Layers** | 7 (App, Pres, Sess, Trans, Net, Data Link, Phys) | 4 (App, Trans, Internet, Net Access) |
| **Session & Presentation** | Explicitly defined | Merged into Application |
| **Data Link & Physical** | Separated | Merged into Network Access |
| **Adoption** | Reference model, rarely implemented | Universally deployed |
| **Internetworking View** | Treats each layer as independent | Emphasizes end-to-end IP as the "narrow waist" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why TCP/IP wins in internetworking:** It achieves **network independence** by keeping IP as a *thin* layer that any underlying network can carry. OSI tried to standardize *all* layers, which slowed its adoption.

---

#### Solution to Question A (b)

**Given:** Message size $L = 2.4 \text{ Kbits} = 2400 \text{ bits}$. Number of links $n = 5$. Per-link bandwidth $B = 500 \text{ Kbps} = 5 \times 10^{5} \text{ bits/s}$. Per-link length $d = 1500 \text{ m}$. Propagation speed $s = 2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$.

**Step 1 — Compute transmission delay per link.**

$$
t_{\text{trans}} = \frac{L}{B} = \frac{2400}{5 \times 10^{5}} = 4.8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 4.8 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute propagation delay per link.**

$$
t_{\text{prop}} = \frac{d}{s} = \frac{1500}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 7.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 0.0075 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute total end-to-end delay.**

$$
t_{\text{total}} = n \times (t_{\text{trans}} + t_{\text{prop}}) = 5 \times (4.8 + 0.0075) = 5 \times 4.8075 = 24.0375 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the transmission-to-propagation ratio $a$.**

$$
a = \frac{t_{\text{prop}}}{t_{\text{trans}}} = \frac{0.0075}{4.8} \approx 0.00156
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Stop-and-Wait utilization.**

$$
U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 2(0.00156)} = \frac{1}{1.003125} \approx 0.9969 = 99.69\%
$$

**Final Answer:** Total delay $\approx 24.04 \text{ ms}$, Utilization $\approx 99.69\%$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Insight:** Utilization is nearly 100% because $a \ll 1$ — the link is *fast* and *short*. For high-altitude satellite links (where $a \gg 1$), Stop-and-Wait would be catastrophically inefficient, and a sliding-window protocol would be mandatory.

---

### Question B
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, PEEVT414, Module 1]**

**(a)** Describe the **hierarchical architecture of the Internet** as a network of networks, with reference to **Access Networks, ISPs, Backbones, and IXPs**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A company is allocated the IP block **200.50.0.0/16**. The network administrator wishes to create **8 equal subnets**. For **Subnet 5** (counting from 0), determine: the **subnet mask**, the **network address**, the **first and last usable host addresses**, and the **broadcast address**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Solution to Question B (a)

**1. End Hosts (1 mark):**
- The leaves of the internetwork tree.
- Examples: laptops, smartphones, IoT devices, servers.
- They use the network only as consumers or producers of traffic.

**2. Access Networks (2 marks):**
- The "first mile" that connects end hosts to the rest of the Internet.
- Examples: **DSL, Cable, FTTH (Fiber-to-the-Home), 4G/5G, Wi-Fi hotspots, Ethernet LANs**.
- They are owned by **Access ISPs** (e.g., BSNL, Airtel, Jio, Comcast).

**3. ISPs and Regional Backbones (2 marks):**
- **Tier-3 ISPs** are local access providers; they purchase transit from higher tiers.
- **Tier-2 ISPs** are regional providers that interconnect with multiple Tier-1 networks and peer at **IXPs**.
- Each ISP runs an **Autonomous System (AS)** identified by a unique ASN (Autonomous System Number).

**4. Tier-1 Backbone and IXPs (2 marks):**
- **Tier-1 ISPs** (e.g., AT&T, Lumen, NTT, Telia) operate the **global backbone** — high-capacity fiber rings with 100 Gbps+ links that span continents.
- They **peer freely** with each other (settlement-free peering).
- **IXPs (Internet Exchange Points)** like **LINX (London), AMS-IX (Amsterdam), DE-CIX (Frankfurt), NIXI (India)** are physical locations where ASes meet to exchange traffic via shared Layer-2 switches.

**Diagrammatic Representation:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[End Host] --> B[Access Network DSL Cable Fiber]
    B --> C[Tier 3 Regional ISP]
    C --> D[Tier 2 National ISP]
    D --> E[Tier 1 Global Backbone]
    E --> F[IXP Peering Point]
    F --> G[Another Tier 1 Backbone]
    G --> H[Tier 2 of Destination Country]
    H --> I[Destination End Host]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Internet's resilience comes from its **mesh-like peering at the Tier-1 layer** — multiple redundant paths exist between any two end hosts, so a single link failure does not isolate a region.

---

#### Solution to Question B (b)

**Given:** IP block **200.50.0.0/16**, need **8 subnets**, find details of **Subnet 5**.

**Step 1 — Compute the number of borrowed bits.**

$$
2^m = 8 \;\Rightarrow\; m = 3 \text{ bits borrowed from the host portion}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the new prefix length.**

$$
\text{New Prefix} = 16 + 3 = 19
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the subnet mask in binary and dotted-decimal.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mask (binary)} &= 11111111.11111111.11100000.00000000 \\
\text{Mask (decimal)} &= 255.255.224.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the size (block size) of each subnet.**

With 3 bits borrowed, the remaining host bits are $32 - 19 = 13$, so each subnet has $2^{13} = 8192$ addresses. The block size (increment in the 3rd octet) is $2^{(8-3)} = 2^{5} = 32$.

**Step 5 — List the starting points of all 8 subnets.**

Subnets begin at the 3rd octet values: 0, 32, 64, 96, 128, 160, 192, 224.

**Step 6 — Compute the four critical addresses of Subnet 5 (3rd octet = 160).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Network Address} &= 200.50.160.0 \quad \text{(host bits all 0)} \\
\text{First Usable Host} &= 200.50.160.1 \quad \text{(network + 1)} \\
\text{Last Usable Host} &= 200.50.191.254 \quad \text{(broadcast - 1)} \\
\text{Broadcast Address} &= 200.50.191.255 \quad \text{(host bits all 1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Subnet 5 range:** 200.50.160.0 → 200.50.191.255, with subnet mask **255.255.224.0** (or /19). Total usable hosts per subnet = $2^{13} - 2 = 8190$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Subnetting Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "$-$2" in host count** — students often write 8192 instead of 8190. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Wrong "first subnet" assumption** — Subnet 0 is *always* 200.50.0.0; the question's "Subnet 5" means the 6th block (0-indexed). Some textbooks use 1-indexing; read the question carefully.
> 3. **Computing 160 + 32 = 192 for the *next* subnet's start** is correct, but the *current* subnet's broadcast is **192 - 1 = 191**. Off-by-one errors are the most common mark-loss trap.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Interconnection of Networks" (Module 1):**

- **Core Idea:** Internetworking is the *logical* binding of heterogeneous physical networks into one addressable internetwork using **IP** as the universal layer-3 protocol.

- **Four Design Goals of Cerf and Kahn (1974):** Network independence, best-effort delivery, gateways for routing, end-to-end acknowledgement.

- **TCP/IP is 4 layers; OSI is 7 layers.** TCP/IP merges OSI's Application/Presentation/Session into one *Application* layer, and merges Data Link/Physical into one *Network Access* layer.

- **Routers operate at Layer 3 (IP).** They use **routing tables** populated by protocols like **OSPF (intra-AS)** and **BGP (inter-AS)**.

- **The Internet is hierarchical:** End Hosts → Access Networks → Regional ISPs → Tier-1 Backbone → IXPs. It is **not** a flat mesh.

- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
   * $t_{\text{trans}} = L / B$
   * $t_{\text{prop}} = d / s$
   * $t_{\text{total}} = \sum (t_{\text{trans}} + t_{\text{prop}})$ across all hops.
   * Subnet host count = $2^{n} - 2$.
   * Subnet count = $2^{m}$ where $m$ = borrowed bits.

- **Devices to Distinguish:** Hub (Layer 1, broadcast blindly), Bridge/Switch (Layer 2, MAC-based), Router (Layer 3, IP-based), Gateway (Layer 4–7, protocol translation).

- **Encapsulation is end-to-end for IP datagrams, hop-by-hop for frames** — this is the *defining feature* of the TCP/IP internetworking model.

- **Subnetting Rule of Thumb:** The block size in any octet is $2^{(8-k)}$ where $k$ is the number of network bits in that octet. Borrowed bits $\Rightarrow$ larger prefix, smaller subnets.

- **Common 3-Mark Questions:** Define internetworking, list Cerf-Kahn goals, draw TCP/IP stack, distinguish router vs gateway, list advantages of TCP/IP.

- **Common 14-Mark Questions:** TCP/IP vs OSI comparison, subnetwork design, end-to-end delay calculation with utilization, hierarchical Internet architecture with IXP discussion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OSI Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT414 - COMPUTER NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** OSI Model ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Model** is a **7-layer conceptual reference framework** standardized by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** under the standard **ISO/IEC 7498-1**. It was developed to standardize the communication functions of a telecommunication or computing system, **without regard to its underlying internal structure and technology**. Its goal is to enable **interoperability** between diverse systems produced by different manufacturers by partitioning the complex task of network communication into manageable, independent, and abstract layers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The OSI model is purely a *reference model*. The actual protocol suite used in the modern Internet is the **TCP/IP model**. Students must understand both the theoretical 7-layer OSI and the practical 4/5-layer TCP/IP. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Postal System" Imagine you are sending a registered parcel from Kerala to Germany: - **You** write the letter (your data — the *message*). - You place it in an **envelope** with the destination address (this is like adding a *header*). - The envelope is given to a **post office** that sorts it (similar to *routing*). - It travels via **trucks, planes, ships** (the *physical medium*). - In Germany, the **recipient's post office** delivers it to the recipient. The postal system breaks this complex task into discrete steps handled by different entities. **OSI does exactly the same for network communication** — each layer handles a specific concern, hands off its work to the next layer, and relies on the corresponding layer at the receiver to do the reverse. ### 1.3 Design Principles of the OSI Model > [!NOTE] > The OSI model was created following five core design principles: > 1. **Layering** — Divide a complex system into smaller, manageable sub-problems. > 2. **Abstraction** — Each layer provides services to the layer above and uses services of the layer below, hiding internal details. > 3. **Well-defined interfaces** — Standardized boundaries between layers. > 4. **Encapsulation** — Each layer adds its own control information (header/trailer) to the data. > 5. **Modularity** — A change in one layer should not require changes in others. ### 1.4 Physical & Logical Standards Used | Standardization Body | Role in OSI | |---|---| | **ISO** | Overall framework owner (ISO/IEC 7498) | | **ITU-T** | Co-developed the standard; telecom alignment | | **IEEE** | Defines sub-layer standards (e.g., IEEE 802.3 for Ethernet) | | **ANSI** | American adoption and related standards | The layers use **protocols, services, and interfaces** — three key OSI terms students must memorize. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Seven Layers — Functions, PDUs, and Services The OSI model partitions the communication process into **seven hierarchical layers**. We can group them into three logical categories for easier memorization (the KTU-recommended grouping): > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic — "Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away"** (Top → Bottom: 7 → 1) > - **P**hysical, **D**ata Link, **N**etwork, **T**ransport, **S**ession, **P**resentation, **A**pplication #### Layer 1 — Physical Layer - **Function:** Transmits raw **bits** (0s and 1s) over a physical medium. - **Concerns:** Voltage levels, cable specs, connectors, bit synchronization, topology, modulation. - **PDU:** **Bit**. - **Examples:** Cables (Twisted Pair, Coaxial, Fiber), Hubs, Repeaters, Network Interface Cards (physical). #### Layer 2 — Data Link Layer - **Function:** Provides **node-to-node data transfer** — a reliable link between two directly connected nodes. - **Sub-layers:** **LLC (Logical Link Control)** and **MAC (Media Access Control)**. - **Concerns:** Framing, physical addressing (**MAC address — 48 bits**), error detection (CRC), flow control. - **PDU:** **Frame**. - **Examples:** Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), Switches, Bridges. #### Layer 3 — Network Layer - **Function:** Routes packets across **multiple networks** (end-to-end logical addressing & routing). - **Concerns:** Logical addressing (**IP address — 32 bits (IPv4) / 128 bits (IPv6)**), routing, fragmentation, congestion control. - **PDU:** **Packet** (or Datagram). - **Examples:** IPv4, IPv6, ICMP, OSPF, BGP, Routers. #### Layer 4 — Transport Layer - **Function:** Ensures **end-to-end communication** between processes (ports). Provides reliable (TCP) or best-effort (UDP) delivery. - **Concerns:** Segmentation, flow control, error recovery, port addressing. - **PDU:** **Segment** (TCP) or **Datagram** (UDP). - **Examples:** **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)**, **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)**. #### Layer 5 — Session Layer - **Function:** Manages and controls **connections (sessions)** between two communicating hosts. - **Concerns:** Session establishment, maintenance, termination, synchronization, dialog control (half/full duplex). - **PDU:** **Data**. - **Examples:** NetBIOS, RPC, PPTP, SIP. #### Layer 6 — Presentation Layer - **Function:** Translates data between application and network formats — acts as the **data translator / formatter**. - **Concerns:** Data encoding (e.g., **ASCII, EBCDIC**), compression (e.g., MPEG, ZIP), encryption/decryption (e.g., SSL, TLS). - **PDU:** **Data**. - **Examples:** SSL/TLS, JPEG, MPEG, ASCII, EBCDIC. #### Layer 7 — Application Layer - **Function:** Closest to the **end user**; provides network services directly to user applications. - **Concerns:** High-level APIs, file transfers, email, web browsing, network management. - **PDU:** **Data**. - **Examples:** **HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS, SNMP, Telnet, POP3, IMAP**. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Layer Summary Table | Layer \# | Layer Name | PDU | Address Used | Key Device | Example Protocol | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 7 | Application | Data | — | Gateway | HTTP, FTP, DNS | | 6 | Presentation | Data | — | Gateway | TLS, JPEG, MPEG | | 5 | Session | Data | — | Gateway | NetBIOS, RPC | | 4 | Transport | Segment $\mid$ Datagram | Port \# (16-bit) | — | TCP, UDP | | 3 | Network | Packet | IP (32/128 bit) | Router | IPv4, IPv6, ICMP | | 2 | Data Link | Frame | MAC (48 bit) | Switch, Bridge | Ethernet, Wi-Fi | | 1 | Physical | Bit | — | Hub, Repeater | IEEE 802.3, RS-232 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Distinction:** Only the **lower 3 layers (Network, Data Link, Physical)** are implemented in **network hardware** (routers/switches). The **upper 4 layers** are typically implemented in **software at the end hosts**. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The OSI model is used in: - **Network troubleshooting** (the "bottom-up" or "top-down" approach — checking layers sequentially). - **Protocol design** — engineers design protocols mapped to specific layers. - **Vendor interoperability** — different manufacturers can build products that communicate seamlessly. - **Security analysis** — firewalls operate at Layers 3, 4, and 7; intrusion detection at various layers. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Encapsulation — Step-by-Step Data Flow When a user sends data (e.g., an HTTP GET request), the data travels **down the layers** on the sender side, getting **encapsulated** at each stage. The reverse happens at the receiver (decapsulation). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Tip:** "Encapsulation" is the **process of adding a header (and sometimes trailer)** to a data unit as it passes from a higher layer to a lower layer. The data unit changes its name at each layer. #### Mathematical Representation of Total Data Size Let: - $D$ = Original data size from application layer (in bytes) - $H_i$ = Header added at layer $i$ (in bytes) - $T$ = Trailer added (if any) **Total size of the transmitted bit stream on the physical medium:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Size} &= D + H_7 + H_6 + H_5 + H_4 + H_3 + H_2 + T_2 \\ \text{where } T_2 &= \text{Trailer (FCS) added at Data Link layer} \\ \text{Note:} \quad \text{Layers 5, 6, 7 typically add NO header (PDU is "Data")} \\ \Rightarrow \text{Total Size} &\approx D + H_4 + H_3 + H_2 + T_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Overhead percentage** (a frequently asked KTU numerical): $$ \text{Overhead \%} = \left( \frac{\text{Total Size} - D}{\text{Total Size}} \right) \times 100 $$ #### Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical) **Q:** A message of **800 bytes** is passed from the Application layer to the Transport layer. The Transport layer adds an **20-byte TCP header**, the Network layer adds a **20-byte IP header**, and the Data Link layer adds a **14-byte Ethernet header and 4-byte Ethernet trailer**. Calculate: - (a) The size of the **frame** transmitted on the physical medium. - (b) The **overhead percentage** introduced by the headers. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(a) Frame Size} &= D_{\text{App}} + H_{\text{TCP}} + H_{\text{IP}} + H_{\text{Ethernet}} + T_{\text{Ethernet}} \\ &= 800 + 20 + 20 + 14 + 4 \\ &= 858 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{(b) Overhead \%} &= \left( \frac{858 - 800}{858} \right) \times 100 \\ &= \left( \frac{58}{858} \right) \times 100 \\ &\approx 6.76\% \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2 Decapsulation (Receiver Side) The receiver performs the **reverse** of encapsulation: 1. **Physical Layer** receives **bits** → converts to **frame** → passes to Data Link. 2. **Data Link** removes Ethernet header/trailer → **packet** → passes to Network. 3. **Network** removes IP header → **segment** → passes to Transport. 4. **Transport** removes TCP header → **data** → passes to Application. 5. **Application** delivers the **original 800-byte data** to the user. ### 3.3 Peer-to-Peer Logical Communication Although data physically travels from Sender Layer 1 → Receiver Layer 1, **logically, each layer communicates with its peer** at the destination. This is called **peer-to-peer communication**, enforced by **protocols** at each layer. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Sender Layer } N &\xleftrightarrow{\text{Protocol}} \text{Receiver Layer } N \\ \uparrow & \qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad \quad \uparrow \\ \text{Via lower layers (physical transport)} & \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Insight:** When asked "How does Layer N communicate with Layer N on the other side?" — answer: **via the protocol defined for that layer**, while the actual data moves down through the lower layers of the sender, across the physical medium, and up through the lower layers of the receiver. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Service vs. Protocol vs. Interface | Concept | Definition | Example | |---|---|---| | **Service** | What a layer offers to the layer above (a *capability*) | Reliable byte-stream service (Transport) | | **Protocol** | A set of *rules* governing peer-to-peer communication at a layer | TCP, IP, HTTP | | **Interface** | Defines *how* the layer above accesses the services of the layer below | SAP (Service Access Point) | The **Service Access Point (SAP)** at the boundary between layer $N$ and layer $N+1$ is a conceptual "address" used by the upper layer to invoke the lower layer's services (e.g., **port numbers** at the Transport/Network boundary). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The OSI Seven-Layer Model — Vertical Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB L7["LAYER 7 - APPLICATION
PDU Data - HTTP FTP DNS SMTP"] L6["LAYER 6 - PRESENTATION
PDU Data - TLS JPEG MPEG"] L5["LAYER 5 - SESSION
PDU Data - NetBIOS RPC SIP"] L4["LAYER 4 - TRANSPORT
PDU Segment or Datagram - TCP UDP"] L3["LAYER 3 - NETWORK
PDU Packet - IPv4 IPv6 ICMP"] L2["LAYER 2 - DATA LINK
PDU Frame - Ethernet WiFi PPP"] L1["LAYER 1 - PHYSICAL
PDU Bits - Cables Hubs Repeaters"] L7 --> L6 L6 --> L5 L5 --> L4 L4 --> L3 L3 --> L2 L2 --> L1 ``` ### 4.2 Data Encapsulation Flow — Sender to Receiver ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SENDER["SENDING HOST - ENCAPSULATION"] direction TB S7["App Layer
DATA = Hello"] S6["Presentation
DATA encrypted"] S5["Session
DATA sessionID"] S4["Transport
SEGMENT = DATA plus TH"] S3["Network
PACKET = SEGMENT plus NH"] S2["Data Link
FRAME = PACKET plus DH plus DT"] S1["Physical
BITS transmitted"] S7 --> S6 --> S5 --> S4 --> S3 --> S2 --> S1 end S1 -. "Medium" .-> R1 subgraph RECEIVER["RECEIVING HOST - DECAPSULATION"] direction TB R1["Physical
BITS received"] R2["Data Link
FRAME = remove DH DT"] R3["Network
PACKET = remove NH"] R4["Transport
SEGMENT = remove TH"] R5["Session
DATA sessionID"] R6["Presentation
DATA decrypted"] R7["App Layer
DATA = Hello"] R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4 --> R5 --> R6 --> R7 end ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Peer Communication Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HOSTA["HOST A"] A7["App 7"] A6["Pres 6"] A5["Sess 5"] A4["Trans 4"] A3["Net 3"] A2["DL 2"] A1["Phys 1"] end subgraph HOSTB["HOST B"] B7["App 7"] B6["Pres 6"] B5["Sess 5"] B4["Trans 4"] B3["Net 3"] B2["DL 2"] B1["Phys 1"] end A7 -. "HTTP Protocol" .-> B7 A6 -. "TLS Protocol" .-> B6 A5 -. "RPC Protocol" .-> B5 A4 -. "TCP Protocol" .-> B4 A3 -. "IP Protocol" .-> B3 A2 -. "Ethernet Protocol" .-> B2 A1 <== "Physical Medium" ==> B1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The *dotted arrows* represent **logical peer-to-peer communication via protocols**. The *thick double arrow* between Host A Layer 1 and Host B Layer 1 represents the **actual physical transmission** of bits through the medium. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** *List the seven layers of the OSI model in the correct order. Write the function of any two layers.* **Model Answer:** The seven layers of the OSI model from top (Layer 7) to bottom (Layer 1) are: 1. **Application Layer** 2. **Presentation Layer** 3. **Session Layer** 4. **Transport Layer** 5. **Network Layer** 6. **Data Link Layer** 7. **Physical Layer** **Functions of two layers:** - **Transport Layer (Layer 4):** Responsible for **end-to-end delivery of the entire message**. It provides process-to-process delivery using **port addresses**, ensures error recovery, flow control, and segmentation. The PDU is a **segment** (for TCP) or **datagram** (for UDP). - **Network Layer (Layer 3):** Responsible for **routing of packets** from source to destination across multiple networks. It performs **logical addressing (IP addressing)**, routing, and fragmentation. The PDU is a **packet**, and the key device is a **Router**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** *What is the difference between TCP and UDP? At which layer of the OSI model do they operate?* **Model Answer:** | Feature | TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) | UDP (User Datagram Protocol) | |---|---|---| | Connection type | Connection-oriented | Connectionless | | Reliability | Reliable (ACK, retransmission) | Unreliable (best-effort) | | Order of delivery | Guaranteed in-order delivery | No ordering guarantee | | Flow control | Yes (sliding window) | No | | Overhead | High (20-byte min header) | Low (8-byte header) | | Speed | Slower | Faster | | Use cases | Web (HTTP), Email, File transfer | Video streaming, DNS, VoIP | **Both TCP and UDP operate at the **Transport Layer (Layer 4)** of the OSI model.** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q3. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **(a)** Explain the **OSI Reference Model** in detail. List all **seven layers** with their **functions and PDUs**. (7 Marks) **(b)** Describe the process of **encapsulation and decapsulation** with a neat diagram. (7 Marks) --- #### Model Solution for (a): The **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Reference Model** is a 7-layer abstract framework designed by the **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)** in **1984** to standardize network communication. It partitions the communication process into seven distinct layers, each performing a specific function and providing services to the layer above it. **The seven layers (top to bottom):** 1. **Application Layer (Layer 7):** Provides network services to end-user applications. **PDU: Data**. Example: HTTP, FTP, DNS. 2. **Presentation Layer (Layer 6):** Handles data translation, encryption, and compression. **PDU: Data**. Example: TLS, JPEG, ASCII. 3. **Session Layer (Layer 5):** Establishes, maintains, and terminates sessions between applications. **PDU: Data**. Example: NetBIOS, RPC. 4. **Transport Layer (Layer 4):** Provides process-to-process delivery using **port numbers** (16-bit). Ensures error recovery and flow control. **PDU: Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP)**. Example: TCP, UDP. 5. **Network Layer (Layer 3):** Performs logical addressing and routing of packets across networks. Uses **IP addresses**. **PDU: Packet**. Example: IP, ICMP. Device: Router. 6. **Data Link Layer (Layer 2):** Provides node-to-node transfer and handles framing, MAC addressing, and error detection. **PDU: Frame**. Example: Ethernet, Wi-Fi. Device: Switch. 7. **Physical Layer (Layer 1):** Transmits raw bits over a physical medium. **PDU: Bit**. Example: Cables, Hubs, Repeaters. #### Model Solution for (b): **Encapsulation** is the process by which a layer adds its **header (and sometimes trailer)** to the **PDU received from the upper layer** before passing it down to the next lower layer. **Decapsulation** is the **reverse process** at the receiver — each layer removes its corresponding header/trailer and passes the data to the layer above. **Sender Side (Encapsulation Steps):** 1. Application layer passes user **Data** to Transport. 2. Transport adds **TCP/UDP header** → forms **Segment**. 3. Network adds **IP header** → forms **Packet**. 4. Data Link adds **Ethernet header and trailer** → forms **Frame**. 5. Physical layer transmits the **bits** onto the medium. **Receiver Side (Decapsulation Steps):** 1. Physical receives **bits** → reassembles into **Frame** → passes to Data Link. 2. Data Link strips Ethernet header/trailer → **Packet** → passes to Network. 3. Network strips IP header → **Segment** → passes to Transport. 4. Transport strips TCP/UDP header → **Data** → passes to Application. 5. Application delivers original data to user. *(Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram for the visual representation.)* --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **Q4. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **functions of each layer** of the OSI model. Mention at least **one protocol/device** for each layer. (7 Marks) **(b)** Compare the **OSI model and the TCP/IP model** in terms of **number of layers, layer names, design philosophy, and practicality**. (7 Marks) --- #### Model Solution for (a): *(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram.)* The seven layers and their primary functions with protocols/devices are: | Layer | Function | Protocol/Device | |---|---|---| | 7 - Application | Network services to end user | HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS | | 6 - Presentation | Data formatting, encryption, compression | TLS, SSL, JPEG, MPEG | | 5 - Session | Session establishment and management | NetBIOS, RPC, PPTP | | 4 - Transport | End-to-end delivery, flow control, error recovery | TCP, UDP | | 3 - Network | Logical addressing and routing | IP, ICMP, OSPF, Router | | 2 - Data Link | Framing, MAC addressing, error detection | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, Switch | | 1 - Physical | Bit transmission over medium | Cables, Hub, Repeater | #### Model Solution for (b): | Parameter | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model | |---|---|---| | Number of layers | 7 | 4 (or 5 with Session/Presentation merged) | | Layer names | Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical | Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access (Link) | | Design origin | ISO (standard) | DARPA / ARPANET (practical implementation) | | Design philosophy | Reference model (theoretical) | Protocol suite (practical) | | Adopted in | Mainly academic/educational use | Real-world Internet | | Session & Presentation layers | Separate | Merged into Application | | Physical & Data Link layers | Separate | Merged into Network Access | **Conclusion:** The **OSI model** is a **theoretical reference** useful for teaching, design, and troubleshooting, while the **TCP/IP model** is the **practical protocol suite** that powers the modern Internet. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing PDUs and Addresses** — Students often mix up **MAC address (Layer 2, 48-bit)** with **IP address (Layer 3, 32-bit)** and **Port number (Layer 4, 16-bit)**. Memorize this clearly. > 2. **Stating the wrong number of layers** — TCP/IP has **4 (or 5) layers**, NOT 7. OSI has 7. > 3. **Forgetting the trailer** — The Data Link layer adds BOTH a **header AND a trailer (FCS for CRC)**. Mark loss is common in numerical problems. > 4. **Mixing up "device" and "protocol"** — A router is a *device*; IP is a *protocol*. Both are at Layer 3, but the question wording must be matched. > 5. **Saying "OSI is the Internet protocol"** — This is a **factual error**. The Internet uses **TCP/IP**, not OSI. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for the OSI Model:** - [x] OSI = **Open Systems Interconnection**, ISO standard, 7 layers, 1984. - [x] Mnemonic top-to-bottom: **"Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away"**. - [x] **PDU names** (top → bottom): Data, Data, Data, Segment/Datagram, Packet, Frame, Bit. - [x] **Addressing scheme**: Port (L4, 16-bit) → IP (L3, 32/128-bit) → MAC (L2, 48-bit). - [x] **Devices per layer**: Hub/Repeater (L1), Switch/Bridge (L2), Router (L3), Gateway (L5–L7). - [x] **Encapsulation** = adding headers going DOWN; **Decapsulation** = removing headers going UP. - [x] **Peer-to-peer** communication happens at the SAME layer using **protocols**. - [x] **Overhead formula**: $\text{Overhead \%} = \frac{\text{Total} - \text{Data}}{\text{Total}} \times 100$. - [x] TCP/IP has 4/5 layers — it is **NOT** the same as OSI. - [x] The **Application layer (Layer 7)** is closest to the **end user**; the **Physical layer (Layer 1)** is closest to the **hardware/medium**. - [x] **SAP (Service Access Point)** = conceptual interface between adjacent layers (e.g., port numbers). - [x] **Physical Layer** transmits **bits**; **Data Link** transmits **frames**; **Network** transmits **packets**. --- **End of Notes — OSI Model (Module 1, PEEVT414)** *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme — Outcome-Based Education (OBE) aligned.*

topics.title: TCP/IP Protocol Suite
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT414 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** TCP/IP Protocol Suite

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **TCP/IP Protocol Suite** (Transmission Control Protocol / Internet Protocol Suite) is the foundational, practical networking framework that governs the global Internet and virtually all modern private/local networks. It is a **layered, modular communication architecture** that defines a standardized set of protocols enabling heterogeneous devices to exchange data across diverse physical media reliably, efficiently, and transparently.

Originally developed under the **DARPA (Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency)** research program in the **1970s** by Vint Cerf and Bob Kahn, the suite is formally documented in the **RFC (Request for Comments)** series maintained by the **IETF (Internet Engineering Task Force)**. The model is intrinsically designed to be **protocol-independent at the physical layer**, meaning it can operate over any transmission medium — copper wire, optical fiber, radio waves, or satellite links.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The TCP/IP Suite is studied under Module 1 as the *de-facto* reference architecture. Students must master the layer responsibilities, protocol mappings, and the relationship between the TCP/IP model and the **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) 7-layer reference model** by ISO.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Postal Service

Imagine you are sending a **gift box from Kerala to a friend in Germany**:

| Real-World Postal Analogy | TCP/IP Equivalent |
|---------------------------|-------------------|
| Your handwritten letter inside a sealed envelope | **Application Data (Payload)** |
| Writing the destination address & sender address on the envelope | **IP Header (Logical Addressing)** |
| The local post office sorting the mail | **Network Layer (IP Routing)** |
| The DHL courier truck / cargo plane | **Data Link & Physical Layers** |
| Postcard acknowledgment ("I received your gift!") | **TCP Acknowledgment (ACK)** |
| Pasting stamps = postage rules | **Transport Layer Flow Control** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight:** Just as the postal service guarantees the *transport of your package* but doesn't care *what is inside*, the TCP/IP suite guarantees *end-to-end delivery of data* without enforcing what the application does with that data. This separation of **transport mechanism** from **application semantics** is the suite's greatest design strength.

## 1.3 The Layering Philosophy

The suite is built on the **"Hourglass" or "Doormat" model**:

```
        Application Layer           (Wide top)
              |
        Transport Layer
              |
        Network Layer                (Narrow waist — IP)
              |
        Link / Physical Layer       (Wide bottom)
```

The **narrow waist** corresponds to the **Internet Protocol (IP)**, sitting on top of virtually any link-layer technology and below virtually any application. This design ensures **universal interoperability**.

> [!TIP]
> The TCP/IP Suite is officially a **4-layer model** in RFC standards, but academically (and in KTU) it is commonly taught as a **5-layer model** by splitting the Link layer into **Data Link** and **Physical** sub-layers for pedagogical clarity.

## 1.4 TCP/IP vs. OSI — KTU-Ready Definition

The **OSI model** is a **theoretical, ISO-standardized 7-layer reference model** used for design and teaching, while **TCP/IP** is a **practical 4-layer implementation** that actually powers the Internet. The OSI model was conceived *after* TCP/IP and primarily serves as a teaching abstraction. The KTU syllabus emphasizes the **mapping table** between these two models, which is a guaranteed high-weightage question.

| Feature | OSI Model | TCP/IP Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Layers** | 7 | 4 (or 5) |
| **Origin** | ISO Standard (1984) | DARPA / IETF (1970s) |
| **Purpose** | Reference / theoretical | Practical implementation |
| **Adoption** | Mostly academic | Universal (Internet) |
| **Protocol Dependency** | Protocol-independent model | Built around IP |
| **Session & Presentation Layers** | Explicit | Absent (folded into Application) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often state TCP/IP "has no Session or Presentation layers." This is **partially correct**. In practice, these functions are *absorbed* into the Application layer on a per-protocol basis (e.g., TLS handles Presentation functions for HTTPS, SIP handles Session signaling). Don't write "they don't exist" — write "they are merged into the Application layer."

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 5-Layer TCP/IP Architecture — Exhaustive Breakdown

### **Layer 5: Application Layer**

- **Position in Suite:** Topmost layer; closest to the **end-user**.
- **Core Responsibility:** Provides **network services directly to user applications** (browsers, email clients, file transfer tools).
- **Data Unit:** **Message** (or **Data stream**).
- **Key Protocols:**

| Protocol | Full Form | Default Port | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **HTTP** | HyperText Transfer Protocol | 80 | Web page retrieval |
| **HTTPS** | HTTP Secure (over TLS) | 443 | Secure web communication |
| **FTP** | File Transfer Protocol | 20/21 | File upload/download |
| **SMTP** | Simple Mail Transfer Protocol | 25 | Email sending |
| **POP3** | Post Office Protocol v3 | 110 | Email retrieval |
| **IMAP** | Internet Message Access Protocol | 143 | Remote email management |
| **DNS** | Domain Name System | 53 | Name-to-IP resolution |
| **DHCP** | Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol | 67/68 | Automatic IP assignment |
| **TELNET** | TELetype NETwork | 23 | Remote text-based login |
| **SSH** | Secure Shell | 22 | Encrypted remote login |
| **SNMP** | Simple Network Management Protocol | 161/162 | Network device management |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sockets:** An application communicates over TCP/IP using a **socket**, which is the combination of an **IP address + Port number** (e.g., `192.168.1.10:80`). This is the logical endpoint of communication.

---

### **Layer 4: Transport Layer**

- **Core Responsibility:** Provides **process-to-process (host-to-host application) data delivery**. Ensures **end-to-end communication, reliability, flow control, and multiplexing**.
- **Data Unit:** **Segment** (for TCP) or **Datagram** (for UDP).
- **Two Principal Protocols:**

#### **A. TCP — Transmission Control Protocol (Connection-Oriented)**
- **Reliable, ordered, error-checked, congestion-controlled** delivery.
- Uses a **3-way handshake** (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK) to establish a connection.
- Uses **sequence numbers** and **ACKs** to guarantee in-order delivery.
- Implements **sliding window flow control** and **congestion control** (Slow Start, Congestion Avoidance, Fast Retransmit, Fast Recovery).
- **Header Size:** **20 bytes** (without options), up to 60 bytes with options.

#### **B. UDP — User Datagram Protocol (Connectionless)**
- **Unreliable, best-effort, low-overhead** delivery.
- **No handshake**, no sequence numbers, no ACK, no retransmission.
- Suitable for **real-time applications** (VoIP, video streaming, DNS queries, online gaming) where speed matters more than perfect reliability.
- **Header Size:** Fixed **8 bytes** — the smallest in the suite.

**TCP vs. UDP Comparison Table (High-Yield for KTU):**

| Feature | TCP | UDP |
|---|---|---|
| Connection Type | Connection-oriented | Connectionless |
| Reliability | Guaranteed (ACK + retransmit) | Best-effort (no ACK) |
| Ordering | Yes (sequence numbers) | No |
| Flow Control | Yes (sliding window) | No |
| Congestion Control | Yes | No |
| Header Size | 20–60 bytes | 8 bytes |
| Speed | Slower | Faster |
| Use Cases | Web (HTTP), Email (SMTP), File Transfer (FTP) | DNS, VoIP, Video, Gaming, TFTP |
| Multiplexing | Port numbers | Port numbers |

---

### **Layer 3: Internet (Network) Layer**

- **Core Responsibility:** **Logical addressing**, **routing**, and **packet forwarding** across interconnected networks. Best-effort delivery — no reliability guarantee.
- **Data Unit:** **Packet** (or **Datagram**).
- **Central Protocol:** **IPv4 / IPv6 (Internet Protocol)**.
- **Other Protocols:**

| Protocol | Function |
|---|---|
| **IP (IPv4 / IPv6)** | Logical addressing & routing |
| **ICMP** | Internet Control Message Protocol — error reporting & diagnostics (used by `ping`, `traceroute`) |
| **IGMP** | Internet Group Management Protocol — multicast group management |
| **ARP** | Address Resolution Protocol — maps IP ↔ MAC address (operates at Link layer boundary) |
| **RARP** | Reverse ARP — maps MAC ↔ IP |
| **OSPF, BGP, RIP** | Routing protocols |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IP Addressing — Key Concept:**
> - **IPv4 Address:** 32 bits → ~**4.29 × 10⁹** unique addresses (now exhausted).
> - **IPv6 Address:** 128 bits → **3.4 × 10³⁸** addresses (virtually unlimited).
> - **IP Address Classes (IPv4):** Class A, B, C, D (Multicast), E (Experimental).

---

### **Layer 2: Data Link Layer**

- **Core Responsibility:** **Node-to-node (hop-to-hop) framing, MAC addressing, error detection, medium access control.**
- **Data Unit:** **Frame**.
- **Key Protocols/Standards:** **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)**, **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)**, **PPP (Point-to-Point Protocol)**, **HDLC**.
- **Addressing:** **MAC (Media Access Control) Address** — 48-bit hardware address burned into the NIC.
- **Functions:** Framing, physical addressing, error detection (CRC), flow control (link-level).

---

### **Layer 1: Physical Layer**

- **Core Responsibility:** **Raw bit transmission** over the physical medium.
- **Data Unit:** **Bits** (electrical signals, light pulses, radio waves).
- **Concerns:** Voltage levels, cable specs, connector types, modulation, bit synchronization, transmission rate.
- **Examples:** Cat5e/Cat6 cables, fiber-optic strands, radio frequencies, repeaters, hubs.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula & Metric Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| IPv4 total addresses | $2^{32}$ | ≈ 4.29 billion |
| IPv6 total addresses | $2^{128}$ | ≈ 3.4 × 10³⁸ |
| MAC address size | 48 bits | 6-byte hexadecimal |
| TCP header (min) | 20 bytes | Without options |
| TCP header (max) | 60 bytes | With options (40 bytes) |
| UDP header | 8 bytes | Fixed |
| IPv4 header | 20 bytes | Min, up to 60 with options |
| IPv6 header | 40 bytes | Fixed |
| Port number range | 0 – 65535 | 16-bit unsigned |
| Well-known ports | 0 – 1023 | Reserved for system services |
| Registered ports | 1024 – 49151 | Assigned by IANA |
| Dynamic/Private ports | 49152 – 65535 | Ephemeral client ports |
| MTU (Ethernet default) | **1500 bytes** | Max payload per frame |
| MSS (TCP default) | **1460 bytes** | MTU − 40 (IP + TCP headers) |
| Encapsulation overhead | $\text{Header}_\text{IP} + \text{Header}_\text{TCP} + \text{Header}_\text{Link}$ | Typically 20+20+18 = 58 bytes |
| Throughput efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{L_\text{payload}}{L_\text{payload} + H_\text{total}}$ | Ratio of useful data to total frame size |

> [!TIP]
> **Efficiency Calculation Example:** If payload $L = 1460$ bytes and total headers/overhead $H = 40$ bytes, then $\eta = \frac{1460}{1500} \approx 97.3\%$. KTU may ask you to compute efficiency for given frame/packet sizes.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The TCP/IP suite is not just an academic construct — it powers:
- **The World Wide Web** (HTTP over TCP)
- **Email infrastructure** (SMTP, IMAP, POP3 over TCP)
- **Voice/Video over IP (VoIP)** (RTP over UDP)
- **Industrial IoT** (MQTT, CoAP over UDP/TCP)
- **Cloud computing & data centers** (HTTP/REST, gRPC over TCP)
- **5G/4G mobile core networks** (all-IP architecture since 4G)

The ability to **decouple applications from transport** and **transport from network** is why a video call on Wi-Fi can seamlessly hand over to 4G mid-stream without breaking the application session.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Encapsulation & Decapsulation — The Core Mechanism

Every time data traverses the TCP/IP stack, it undergoes **encapsulation** (sender side) and **decapsulation** (receiver side). This is the **single most important process** in the suite.

### **Sender-Side Encapsulation (Top-Down)**

| Step | Layer | Action Taken | New Data Unit Name |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Application | User data is created (e.g., HTTP request) | **Message / Data** |
| 2 | Transport | TCP/UDP header is prepended with **source & destination port numbers** | **Segment (TCP)** or **Datagram (UDP)** |
| 3 | Internet (Network) | IP header is prepended with **source & destination IP addresses** | **Packet (IP Datagram)** |
| 4 | Data Link | Frame header (MAC) + trailer (CRC) are added | **Frame** |
| 5 | Physical | Bits are encoded as electrical/optical/radio signals | **Bits** |

### **Receiver-Side Decapsulation (Bottom-Up)**

| Step | Layer | Action Taken |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Physical | Bits received, signal decoded |
| 2 | Data Link | MAC address checked; CRC verified; frame stripped of header/trailer |
| 3 | Internet | Destination IP matched; packet stripped of IP header |
| 4 | Transport | Port number identifies target application; segment/datagram stripped of transport header |
| 5 | Application | Data delivered to the process (e.g., browser) |

## 3.2 Worked Derivation: End-to-End Frame Composition

**Problem:** A user requests a web page via HTTP over TCP over IPv4 over Ethernet. Calculate the total size of the on-the-wire frame for a 1000-byte HTTP payload.

**Given:**
- HTTP payload: $L_{\text{app}} = 1000$ bytes
- TCP header: $H_{\text{TCP}} = 20$ bytes
- IPv4 header: $H_{\text{IP}} = 20$ bytes
- Ethernet header: $H_{\text{eth}} = 14$ bytes
- Ethernet trailer (CRC): $T_{\text{eth}} = 4$ bytes
- Preamble/SFD (Physical layer): $P = 8$ bytes (not strictly part of frame, but transmitted)

**Step 1: Calculate the TCP Segment**

$$
L_{\text{seg}} = L_{\text{app}} + H_{\text{TCP}} = 1000 + 20 = 1020 \text{ bytes}
$$

The TCP segment carries 1000 bytes of application data plus a 20-byte TCP header containing source port, destination port (80), sequence number, ACK number, flags, window size, checksum, and urgent pointer.

**Step 2: Calculate the IP Packet**

$$
L_{\text{pkt}} = L_{\text{seg}} + H_{\text{IP}} = 1020 + 20 = 1040 \text{ bytes}
$$

The IP header contains version, IHL, total length, identification, flags, fragment offset, TTL, protocol (6 for TCP), header checksum, source IP, and destination IP.

**Step 3: Calculate the Ethernet Frame**

$$
L_{\text{frame}} = L_{\text{pkt}} + H_{\text{eth}} + T_{\text{eth}} = 1040 + 14 + 4 = 1058 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4: Total on-the-wire bytes (including preamble):**

$$
L_{\text{wire}} = L_{\text{frame}} + P = 1058 + 8 = 1066 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 5: Efficiency of the transmission:**

$$
\eta = \frac{L_{\text{app}}}{L_{\text{wire}}} = \frac{1000}{1066} \approx 0.9381 = 93.81\%
$$

This means **6.19% of every byte transmitted is overhead** for headers, trailers, and physical-layer preamble.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Protocol Function Mapping

This matrix maps **what function** is provided at **which layer** and **which protocol** handles it:

| Function | OSI Layer | TCP/IP Layer | Protocol(s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Web browsing (HTML transfer) | Application | Application | HTTP, HTTPS |
| Email sending | Application | Application | SMTP |
| Email retrieval | Application | Application | POP3, IMAP |
| Domain name resolution | Application | Application | DNS |
| Automatic IP assignment | Application | Application | DHCP |
| Reliable byte-stream delivery | Transport | Transport | TCP |
| Fast, connectionless delivery | Transport | Transport | UDP |
| End-to-end flow & error control | Transport | Transport | TCP |
| Process multiplexing (ports) | Transport | Transport | TCP, UDP |
| Logical addressing (IP) | Network | Internet | IPv4, IPv6 |
| Routing across networks | Network | Internet | IP, OSPF, BGP |
| Error & diagnostic messages | Network | Internet | ICMP |
| Logical-to-physical address mapping | Data Link | Data Link | ARP |
| Framing & MAC addressing | Data Link | Data Link | Ethernet (802.3), Wi-Fi (802.11) |
| Error detection (CRC) | Data Link | Data Link | Ethernet |
| Bit-level transmission | Physical | Physical | Cables, Fiber, Radio |

## 3.4 IP Address & Subnet — Quick Analytical Insight

For a **Class B IPv4 network** with default mask `/16`:

- **Network bits:** 16 → usable for network ID
- **Host bits:** 16 → usable for host IDs
- **Total hosts per network:** $2^{16} - 2 = 65{,}534$
- **Subnet mask:** `255.255.0.0`
- **CIDR notation:** `/16`

> **Engineering Rule:** Subtract **2** from $2^n$ (where $n$ = host bits) because:
> - The **all-zeros** host portion is the **network address** (used to identify the network itself).
> - The **all-ones** host portion is the **directed broadcast address** (used to message all hosts in the network).

For **Class C** with default `/24` mask:
- Host bits = 8
- Total hosts = $2^8 - 2 = 254$ hosts
- Subnet mask = `255.255.255.0`

For **Class A** with default `/8` mask:
- Host bits = 24
- Total hosts = $2^{24} - 2 = 16{,}777{,}214$ hosts
- Subnet mask = `255.0.0.0`

## 3.5 Three-Way Handshake — TCP Connection Establishment (Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **guaranteed KTU question** — either as a Part A 3-mark question or a sub-part of a Part B 14-mark question.

**Step 1 — SYN (Client → Server)**
- Client sends a TCP segment with **SYN flag = 1**.
- Client picks an **Initial Sequence Number (ISN)** $S_c$ (randomly).
- Segment contains: `SEQ = $S_c$, ACK = 0, SYN = 1`.
- Client enters **SYN-SENT** state.

**Step 2 — SYN-ACK (Server → Client)**
- Server responds with **SYN = 1** and **ACK = 1**.
- Server's sequence number: $S_s$ (its own random ISN).
- Server's ACK number: $S_c + 1$ (acknowledging client's SYN).
- Server enters **SYN-RECEIVED** state.

**Step 3 — ACK (Client → Server)**
- Client sends **ACK = 1**.
- Sequence number: $S_c + 1$.
- ACK number: $S_s + 1$.
- Connection **ESTABLISHED** on both sides.

**Mathematical Representation of Sequence Number Flow:**

$$
\text{Client ISN} = S_c, \quad \text{Server ISN} = S_s
$$

$$
\text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN, SEQ}=S_c} \text{Server}
$$

$$
\text{Server} \xrightarrow{\text{SYN-ACK, SEQ}=S_s, \text{ACK}=S_c+1} \text{Client}
$$

$$
\text{Client} \xrightarrow{\text{ACK, SEQ}=S_c+1, \text{ACK}=S_s+1} \text{Server}
$$

> **Total messages exchanged:** 3 → hence the name **"3-way handshake."**

For **connection termination**, TCP uses a **4-way handshake** using **FIN** flags — both sides must independently close their half of the connection.

## 4.4 TCP 3-Way Handshake — Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant S as Server

    Note over C,S: INITIAL STATE: CLOSED
    C->>S: SYN (SEQ = x, ACK = 0)
    Note over C: State: SYN-SENT
    Note over S: State: LISTEN → SYN-RECEIVED
    S->>C: SYN-ACK (SEQ = y, ACK = x+1)
    Note over S: State: SYN-RECEIVED
    C->>S: ACK (SEQ = x+1, ACK = y+1)
    Note over C: State: ESTABLISHED
    Note over S: State: ESTABLISHED
    Note over C,S: CONNECTION OPEN — DATA TRANSFER BEGINS
```

    A4 --> T2

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Insight:** This hourglass is the **key reason** the Internet scaled so successfully. Any new application can use TCP or UDP, and any new link-layer technology only needs to carry IP packets. **You can swap the top and bottom freely** — the narrow waist (IP) remains stable.
