# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The following questions are designed to match the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**: Part A (2 marks / 3 marks short answer) and Part B (14 marks with internal choice between two questions, each with two 7-mark sub-parts).

---

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] *What is framing in the Data Link Layer? Why is it required?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Framing is the process of dividing the bit stream received from the Network Layer into discrete, manageable units called **frames** before passing them to the Physical Layer for transmission.

**Why required (2 marks):**
- The Physical Layer delivers only a raw, unstructured bit stream.
- The receiver needs explicit **delimiters** to determine *where one frame ends and the next begins* — without this, it cannot identify which bits belong to which Network Layer packet.
- Framing also enables **error detection** to operate on a well-defined atomic unit, and supports **flow control** and **retransmission** at frame granularity.

> [!NOTE]  
> Examiners award 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the explanation of *why* it is needed. Mention *synchronization* and *atomic error detection* explicitly.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] *Compare character stuffing and bit stuffing.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Character Stuffing | Bit Stuffing |
|---|---|---|
| **Granularity** | Operates on bytes (8-bit aligned) | Operates on individual bits |
| **Delimiter** | Reserved FLAG byte (e.g., `0x7E`) | Bit pattern `01111110` |
| **Escape Mechanism** | Insert ESC byte before FLAG/ESC | Insert `0` after five consecutive `1`s |
| **Typical Protocol** | PPP, BISYNC | HDLC, SDLC |
| **Worst-Case Efficiency** | Approaches 100% if data has no special bytes | $\dfrac{5}{6} \approx 83.33\%$ |

> [!NOTE]  
> Award 1 mark each for: definition of each method, the escape mechanism, and the comparison.

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **bit stuffing** technique used in HDLC. Illustrate with a suitable example showing how the original bit stream is encoded at the sender and decoded at the receiver.

**(b) [7 marks)** A frame carries a payload of **4000 bits** using bit stuffing (with `01111110` as the flag). Calculate the **worst-case number of stuffed bits**, the **total transmitted bits**, and the **framing efficiency**. Comment on the result.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for A(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):** Bit stuffing is a transparency mechanism used in **bit-oriented protocols** like **HDLC** (High-Level Data Link Control) and **SDLC**. It works on the raw bit stream without requiring byte alignment. The reserved flag pattern is **`01111110`** (one `0`, six `1`s, one `0` — actually it is `0` followed by six `1`s followed by `0`).

**Step 2 — Sender Algorithm (2 marks):** *Whenever five consecutive `1` bits appear anywhere in the data (excluding the flags themselves), the sender automatically inserts (stuffs) a `0` bit immediately after them.*

**Step 3 — Receiver Algorithm (1 mark):** *The receiver, after seeing five consecutive `1`s, peeks at the next bit:*
- *If it is `0` → discard the `0` (it was a stuffed bit) and continue.*
- *If it is `1` (i.e., the seventh `1`) → the flag pattern `01111110` is detected, marking the frame boundary.*

**Step 4 — Worked Example (2 marks):**  
Let the original data be: `0111111000111110101`

Encoding trace:
- `01111` → five 1s, **stuff a 0** → `01111` `0`
- Remaining: `1100011111` → after `11111`, **stuff 0** → `11111` `0` `1`
- Remaining: `0101` → no more than four 1s in a row.

Stuffed data: `01111 0 11000 11111 0 101`

Frame on wire: `01111110` `01111 0 11000 11111 0 101` `01111110`

Decoding trace (receiver):
- Sees `01111110` → start flag.
- Reads `0`, `1111`, `1`, `1`… continues until five `1`s reached.
- Looks at next bit: `0` → remove (de-stuff) and continue.
- Continues extracting five 1s followed by 0 → removes each stuffed 0.
- Finally sees `01111110` → end flag.

Recovers original: `0111111000111110101` ✅

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters (1 mark):**
$$
D = 4000 \text{ data bits}, \quad \text{flag size} = 8 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute worst-case stuffed bits (2 marks):**
Worst case is when every data bit is `1`. A stuffed `0` is inserted after every five consecutive 1s.
$$
N_{\text{stuffed}} = \left\lfloor \frac{D}{5} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{4000}{5} \right\rfloor = 800 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Total transmitted bits (2 marks):**
$$
T = \underbrace{8}_{\text{start flag}} + D + N_{\text{stuffed}} + \underbrace{8}_{\text{end flag}} = 8 + 4000 + 800 + 8 = 4816 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Framing efficiency (1 mark):**
$$
\eta = \frac{D}{T} = \frac{4000}{4816} \approx 0.8305 = 83.05\%
$$

**Step 5 — Comment (1 mark):**
The efficiency is **83.05%**, which is slightly less than the asymptotic maximum of $\dfrac{5}{6} \approx 83.33\%$. As the payload size grows, the 16-bit flag overhead becomes negligible and the efficiency approaches 5/6.

> [!WARNING]  
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A:**  
> Students often **forget to add the two flag bytes (16 bits)** when computing the total transmitted bits. Marks are specifically allotted for *each* term in $T = D + \lfloor D/5 \rfloor + 16$. Also, do **not** subtract the flag bytes from $D$ — the flag is *additional* overhead, not part of the payload. A common error is to write $\eta = 4000/(4000+800)$ without the flags — this gives an incorrect 83.33% (coincidentally equal to the limit) and **loses 1 mark** for the missing flag term.

---

### **Question B (14 marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain **character (byte) stuffing** with a neat diagram. Given the data bytes (in hex) **`7E 41 7D 9C 7E 8A`** and the reserved bytes FLAG = `0x7E`, ESC = `0x7D`, show the **stuffed frame on the wire**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **asymptotic efficiency** of bit stuffing and explain why it approaches $\dfrac{5}{6}$. What happens to the efficiency if we double the data size?

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for B(a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):** Character stuffing is used in **byte-oriented protocols** like **PPP** and **BISYNC**. Two bytes are reserved: **FLAG** (frame delimiter) and **ESC** (escape byte). To preserve transparency, every occurrence of FLAG or ESC inside the data is preceded by an extra ESC byte.

**Step 2 — Algorithm (1 mark):**
- Sender: For each data byte — if it equals FLAG → send `ESC FLAG`; if it equals ESC → send `ESC ESC`; else send as-is.
- Receiver: On seeing ESC, examine the next byte — output it and remove the ESC.

**Step 3 — Neat Diagram (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{\text{FLAG}} \;\; \vert \;\; \text{Header} \;\; \vert \;\; \text{Stuffed Data} \;\; \vert \;\; \text{Trailer} \;\; \vert \;\; \boxed{\text{FLAG}}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the algorithm to the given data (3 marks):**

| Original | Match? | Stuffed Output |
|---|---|---|
| `7E` | FLAG | `7D 7E` |
| `41` | — | `41` |
| `7D` | ESC | `7D 7D` |
| `9C` | — | `9C` |
| `7E` | FLAG | `7D 7E` |
| `8A` | — | `8A` |

**Stuffed data bytes:** `7D 7E  41  7D 7D  9C  7D 7E  8A`

**Final frame on the wire (5 marks total for this part, including framing):**

$$
\underbrace{7E}_{\text{FLAG}} \;\; \underbrace{7D\,7E\;\;41\;\;7D\,7D\;\;9C\;\;7D\,7E\;\;8A}_{\text{Stuffed Data}} \;\; \underbrace{7E}_{\text{FLAG}}
$$

> [!NOTE]  
> Receiver, on seeing `7D 7E` → outputs `7E` (real data). On `7D 7D` → outputs `7D` (real data). The standalone leading and trailing `7E` bytes are the flags.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — State the formula (1 mark):**
$$
\eta(D) = \frac{D}{D + \lfloor D/5 \rfloor + 16}
$$

**Step 2 — Take the limit (2 marks):**

$$
\lim_{D \to \infty} \eta(D) = \lim_{D \to \infty} \frac{D}{D(1 + 1/5) + 16} = \frac{1}{1 + 1/5} = \frac{5}{6} \approx 83.33\%
$$

**Step 3 — Explain intuitively (2 marks):**  
In the limit of huge payloads, the **fixed overhead** (16 bits of flags) becomes vanishingly small. The **only persistent overhead** is the stuffed `0` bits — one for every five data `1`s in the worst case. Hence the effective transmission ratio is **5 useful bits for every 6 transmitted bits**, giving $\eta = 5/6$.

**Step 4 — Doubling the data (2 marks):**  
For $D = 4000$ bits, $\eta \approx 83.05\%$. For $D = 8000$ bits:
$$
N_{\text{stuffed}} = \lfloor 8000/5 \rfloor = 1600
$$
$$
T = 8000 + 1600 + 16 = 9616
$$
$$
\eta = \frac{8000}{9616} \approx 83.19\%
$$

The efficiency **rises slightly** and converges toward $83.33\%$ as $D$ keeps doubling.

> [!WARNING]  
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**  
> **(1)** Students frequently confuse the *stuffed bit value* — they stuff a `1` instead of a `0`. The bit stuffed is **always `0`** to break the run of 1s. **(2)** In character stuffing, students sometimes "double-stuff" — adding two ESCs in front of a FLAG instead of one. The correct operation is: if the byte is FLAG, output `ESC` then `FLAG` (only one ESC, not two). **(3)** Some students compute efficiency using $D + 16$ in the denominator and forget the $\lfloor D/5 \rfloor$ term, losing 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Data Link Layer Framing**

- ✅ **Framing** = splitting a bit stream into discrete **frames**, the DLL's PDU.
- ✅ A frame has 3 logical parts: **Header** (address, type/length), **Payload** (data), **Trailer** (FCS/CRC).
- ✅ **Fixed-size framing** → no delimiter; used in ATM (53-byte cells).
- ✅ **Variable-size framing** → needs start/end delimiters.
- ✅ **Character stuffing** → uses reserved FLAG and ESC bytes; works at byte level (PPP, BISYNC).
- ✅ **Bit stuffing** → reserves `01111110` pattern; stuffs a `0` after **five consecutive 1s** (HDLC, SDLC).
- ✅ Bit stuffing rule for sender: **5 ones → insert 0**.
- ✅ Bit stuffing rule for receiver: **5 ones + 0 → discard 0**; **5 ones + 1** (i.e., sixth 1) → **flag detected**.
- ✅ Worst-case stuffed bits for $D$ data bits: $\left\lfloor D/5 \right\rfloor$.
- ✅ Total bits per frame: $T = D + \lfloor D/5 \rfloor + 16$.
- ✅ Framing efficiency: $\eta = \dfrac{D}{D + \lfloor D/5 \rfloor + 16}$.
- ✅ Asymptotic efficiency of bit stuffing = $\dfrac{5}{6} \approx 83.33\%$.
- ✅ The **`+16`** in the formula accounts for the **two flag bytes** (start + end); never omit it.
- ✅ **MTU** (Maximum Transmission Unit) limits frame size — typically **1500 bytes** for Ethernet.
- ✅ Transparency = the ability to transmit *any* bit/byte pattern without confusing the receiver.
- ✅ *Real-world examples*: Ethernet II, IEEE 802.11, HDLC, PPP, ATM cells, Fibre Channel.
- ✅ Most common student error: **forgetting the 16-bit flag overhead** in efficiency calculations.
- ✅ Second most common error: **stuffing a `1` instead of a `0`** in bit stuffing — remember, it's always **`0`**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Control (Stop and Wait)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT414 - COMPUTER NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Flow Control (Stop and Wait) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Flow Control** is a set of procedures and protocols used by the **Data Link Layer** (Layer 2 of the OSI Reference Model) to regulate the rate of data transmission between a **sender** and a **receiver** so that the sender does not transmit data frames faster than the receiver can process them. **Stop-and-Wait Flow Control** is the simplest, most fundamental stop-and-wait acknowledgment-based flow control protocol in which: 1. The **sender** transmits exactly **one data frame** at a time to the receiver. 2. The sender then **halts transmission completely** and enters a "waiting" state. 3. The sender **waits** until it receives a positive **Acknowledgment (ACK)** frame from the receiver confirming successful reception. 4. Only **after the ACK is received**, the sender transmits the next data frame. 5. If the ACK is not received within a predefined **time-out interval ($T_{out}$)**, the sender **retransmits** the same frame. This protocol is formally classified as an **ARQ (Automatic Repeat reQuest)** scheme — specifically **Stop-and-Wait ARQ**, which is the most basic member of the ARQ family. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English Explanation) > [!NOTE] > Imagine you are having a conversation with a friend standing **100 meters away** across a noisy playground. To make sure they hear every sentence, you devise a simple rule: - You shout **one sentence** at a time. - After shouting, you put your hand over your mouth and **wait**. - Your friend raises a hand (thumbs up = **ACK**) when they understood it. - **Only when you see the thumbs-up** do you shout the next sentence. - If you do not see any thumbs-up within, say, **10 seconds**, you shout the **same sentence again** (in case it was lost in noise). This is precisely how Stop-and-Wait Flow Control works in computer networks. The "sentence" is the **data frame**, the "thumbs-up" is the **ACK frame**, and the "10 seconds" is the **time-out timer**. The protocol ensures **error-free, ordered delivery** of data, but at the cost of being **slow** — because the channel remains idle during waiting periods. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Terminology | Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit | |--------|---------|---------------| | $L$ | Length of the data frame | **bits** | | $R$ | Bandwidth of the link | **bits per second (bps)** | | $d$ | Distance between sender and receiver | **meters (m)** | | $v$ | Propagation velocity through the medium | **$\approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s** in copper; **$3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s** in vacuum | | $t_{frame}$ | Transmission time of one frame | seconds | | $t_{prop}$ | Propagation delay | seconds | | $a$ | Normalized ratio (propagation / transmission) | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > In all KTU 2024 Scheme numerical problems, the default propagation velocity for copper cables is taken as **$v = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s** unless explicitly stated otherwise. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of Stop-and-Wait Protocol — Step-by-Step The protocol operates on a strict, deterministic sequence of events: ### Step 1: Frame Transmission The sender encapsulates the data packet into a frame of length $L$ bits and begins transmission over the channel at line rate $R$ bps. The **transmission time** is calculated as: $$t_{frame} = \frac{L}{R}$$ ### Step 2: Propagation The electromagnetic signal travels through the medium. The **propagation delay** is given by: $$t_{prop} = \frac{d}{v}$$ The frame therefore arrives at the receiver after a total of $(t_{frame} + t_{prop})$ seconds. ### Step 3: Receiver Processing The receiver disassembles the frame, checks for bit-errors using a CRC/checksum, and prepares a short **ACK frame** for transmission back to the sender. ### Step 4: Acknowledgment Transmission The ACK travels back across the channel. For a small ACK of length $L_{ack}$ bits: $$t_{ack} = \frac{L_{ack}}{R}$$ If we assume the ACK transmission time is negligible compared to propagation: $$t_{ack} \approx 0$$ ### Step 5: Sender Waits and Resumes The sender receives the ACK after a total round-trip time, then transmits the next data frame. The entire **cycle time** ($T_{cycle}$) for one successful frame is: $$T_{cycle} = t_{frame} + 2 \cdot t_{prop} + t_{ack}$$ ## 2.2 Why Sequence Numbers Are Necessary (CRITICAL Concept) > [!IMPORTANT] > Without **sequence numbers (0 and 1)**, the receiver cannot distinguish between a **new frame** and a **retransmitted duplicate frame** caused by a lost ACK. The two frames of Stop-and-Wait ARQ use **alternating sequence bits**: - **Frame 0** → Sender sends frame with sequence number 0 - **Receiver expects 0** → Sends **ACK 1** (meaning "I received frame 0, send me frame 1 next") - **Frame 1** → Sender sends frame with sequence number 1 - **Receiver expects 1** → Sends **ACK 0** (meaning "I received frame 1, send me frame 0 next") - And the cycle repeats. If a duplicate frame arrives (due to ACK timeout and retransmission), the receiver detects it via the sequence number, **discards the duplicate**, and re-sends the ACK. ## 2.3 Error Recovery Scenarios | Scenario | What Happens | Protocol Action | |----------|--------------|-----------------| | **Data frame lost in transit** | Sender's timer expires | Sender retransmits the same frame | | **ACK frame lost in transit** | Sender's timer expires | Sender retransmits; receiver discards duplicate via sequence number, re-sends ACK | | **Data frame corrupted** | Receiver's CRC check fails | Receiver sends **NAK** (Negative ACK) or simply does not ACK; sender retransmits after timeout | | **No errors** | Normal flow | Sender receives ACK and proceeds to next frame | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master this table** — every KTU 2024 numerical on Stop-and-Wait will derive from these formulas. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $t_{frame} = \frac{L}{R}$ | Transmission time of a frame | seconds | | 2 | $t_{prop} = \frac{d}{v}$ | Propagation delay | seconds | | 3 | $a = \frac{t_{prop}}{t_{frame}} = \frac{d \cdot R}{v \cdot L}$ | Normalized propagation ratio | dimensionless | | 4 | $T_{cycle} = t_{frame} + 2 \cdot t_{prop} + t_{ack}$ | Total cycle time for one successful frame | seconds | | 5 | $U = \frac{t_{frame}}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}$ | **Channel Utilization / Efficiency** | dimensionless (0 to 1) | | 6 | $\text{Throughput} = R \cdot U = \frac{R}{1 + 2a}$ | Effective data rate | bps | | 7 | $U(\%) = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} \times 100$ | Percentage efficiency | percent | | 8 | $T_{out} > 2 \cdot t_{prop} + t_{ack} + t_{proc}$ | Minimum safe timeout value | seconds | ## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility - **Foundation for modern ARQ protocols**: Stop-and-Wait ARQ is the pedagogical and logical foundation for **Go-Back-N ARQ** and **Selective Repeat ARQ**, both of which are examined in Module 2 of PEEVT414. - **Embedded systems and IoT**: Used in **low-power sensor networks (LoRaWAN, Zigbee)** where simplicity outweighs throughput. - **Satellite communication**: Directly applicable when propagation delay is huge (geostationary satellites: $t_{prop} \approx 250$ ms one-way). - **HDLC and PPP links**: Historically, many serial-line protocols used Stop-and-Wait as a sub-mode. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Channel Utilization (Efficiency) Formula We now derive the celebrated **efficiency formula** for Stop-and-Wait ARQ from first principles. ### Step A — Define the Total Time Required to Send ONE Frame Successfully When a sender transmits one data frame and successfully receives its ACK, the total wall-clock time consumed is: $$ T_{total} = t_{frame} + t_{prop} + t_{proc} + t_{ack} + t_{prop} + t_{proc} $$ **Logic row-by-row:** - $t_{frame}$: Time taken to push all $L$ bits onto the wire = $\frac{L}{R}$. - $t_{prop}$ (first): Time for the last bit to travel from sender to receiver = $\frac{d}{v}$. - $t_{proc}$ (first): Time for the receiver to process the frame and prepare ACK (often assumed 0 for academic problems). - $t_{ack}$: Time taken to transmit the ACK frame back. If ACK is very small, $t_{ack} \to 0$. - $t_{prop}$ (second): Time for the ACK to travel back from receiver to sender. - $t_{proc}$ (second): Time for sender to process the ACK. ### Step B — Simplify the Expression Assuming $t_{proc} = 0$ and $t_{ack} = 0$ (standard KTU assumption for ACK negligible): $$ T_{total} = t_{frame} + 2 \cdot t_{prop} $$ **Logic:** The frame takes $t_{frame}$ to send, then $t_{prop}$ to reach the receiver, then the ACK travels back in $t_{prop}$. Total idle time = $2 \cdot t_{prop}$. ### Step C — Define Channel Utilization Channel utilization $U$ is defined as the **fraction of total channel time** that is actually used for transmitting **useful data frames**: $$ U = \frac{\text{Useful transmission time}}{\text{Total cycle time}} $$ $$ U = \frac{t_{frame}}{t_{frame} + 2 \cdot t_{prop}} $$ ### Step D — Introduce the Normalization Variable $a$ To make the formula independent of hardware-specific constants, divide numerator and denominator by $t_{frame}$: $$ U = \frac{\frac{t_{frame}}{t_{frame}}}{\frac{t_{frame}}{t_{frame}} + \frac{2 \cdot t_{prop}}{t_{frame}}} $$ **Logic:** Dividing both terms by $t_{frame}$ normalizes the expression. $$ U = \frac{1}{1 + 2 \cdot \left( \frac{t_{prop}}{t_{frame}} \right)} $$ Now substitute $a = \frac{t_{prop}}{t_{frame}}$: $$ \boxed{U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}} $$ where: $$ a = \frac{t_{prop}}{t_{frame}} = \frac{\frac{d}{v}}{\frac{L}{R}} = \frac{d \cdot R}{v \cdot L} $$ ### Step E — Analyze Limiting Cases (Insight) **Case 1:** When $a \to 0$ (very short link OR very long frames): $$ U = \frac{1}{1 + 0} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 100\% \text{ utilization} $$ **Case 2:** When $a \to \infty$ (very long link OR very short frames): $$ U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} \to 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0\% \text{ utilization} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** Stop-and-Wait is **highly inefficient on long-distance, high-bandwidth links** such as satellite channels and transcontinental fiber. This is precisely why **sliding-window protocols** (Go-Back-N, Selective Repeat) were invented. ### Step F — Throughput Derivation Effective throughput (actual data successfully delivered per second): $$ \text{Throughput} = R \times U = \frac{R}{1 + 2a} $$ $$ \text{Throughput} = \frac{L}{t_{frame} + 2 \cdot t_{prop}} $$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) ### Problem A channel has a bandwidth of **4 kbps** and a propagation delay of **20 ms**. Calculate the frame size that achieves **50% efficiency** using Stop-and-Wait ARQ. Assume $v = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s and ACK transmission time is negligible. ### Solution — Step-by-Step **Given:** - $R = 4 \text{ kbps} = 4000 \text{ bps}$ - $t_{prop} = 20 \text{ ms} = 20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$ - $U = 0.5$ (50%) - $v = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s **Step 1:** Start with the efficiency formula: $$ U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute $U = 0.5$: $$ 0.5 = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} $$ **Step 3:** Cross-multiply: $$ 0.5 \cdot (1 + 2a) = 1 $$ **Step 4:** Solve for $a$: $$ 1 + 2a = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2a = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = 0.5 $$ **Step 5:** Use the definition of $a$: $$ a = \frac{t_{prop}}{t_{frame}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_{frame} = \frac{t_{prop}}{a} $$ $$ t_{frame} = \frac{20 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5} = 40 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 40 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 6:** Compute frame size: $$ t_{frame} = \frac{L}{R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad L = t_{frame} \times R $$ $$ L = 40 \times 10^{-3} \times 4000 = 160 \text{ bits} = 20 \text{ bytes} $$ **Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{L = 160 \text{ bits} = 20 \text{ bytes}} $$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Stop-and-Wait Sequence Diagram (Sender–Receiver Interaction) The following Mermaid sequence diagram illustrates the **normal operation** of Stop-and-Wait ARQ, including the **timeout and retransmission** case. Each vertical lifeline represents a participant; horizontal arrows represent messages or events in chronological order (top to bottom). ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant SenderNode as Sender participant ChannelA as Transmission Channel participant ReceiverNode as Receiver participant ChannelB as Return Channel Note over SenderNode: Sender has data frame 0 ready SenderNode ->> ChannelA: Transmit Frame 0 ChannelA ->> ReceiverNode: Frame 0 arrives after propagation delay ReceiverNode ->> ReceiverNode: CRC Check PASS - Frame OK ReceiverNode ->> ChannelB: Send ACK 1 ChannelB ->> SenderNode: ACK 1 arrives Note over SenderNode: Sender receives ACK 1
Acknowledges frame 0 accepted SenderNode ->> ChannelA: Transmit Frame 1 ChannelA ->> ReceiverNode: Frame 1 arrives ReceiverNode ->> ReceiverNode: CRC Check PASS - Frame OK ReceiverNode ->> ChannelB: Send ACK 0 ChannelB ->> SenderNode: ACK 0 arrives Note over SenderNode,ReceiverNode: ----- TIMEOUT AND RETRANSMISSION SCENARIO ----- SenderNode ->> ChannelA: Transmit Frame 0 (second time) ChannelA -->> SenderNode: Frame LOST in channel Note over SenderNode: Timer expires - no ACK received SenderNode ->> ChannelA: Retransmit Frame 0 ChannelA ->> ReceiverNode: Duplicate Frame 0 arrives ReceiverNode ->> ReceiverNode: Sequence number 0 already received - DISCARD duplicate ReceiverNode ->> ChannelB: Re-send ACK 1 ChannelB ->> SenderNode: ACK 1 arrives - proceed ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow The following diagram maps the **functional state machine** of the Stop-and-Wait sender and receiver. Each block represents a logical state or decision point in the protocol's operation. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Sender has new packet] --> B[Construct Data Frame with Sequence Number] B --> C[Start Transmission Timer T_out] C --> D[Send Frame on Channel] D --> E{ACK Received
before Timeout?} E -- Yes --> F{Sequence Number
matches expected?} F -- Yes --> G[Increment Sequence Number] G --> H[Deliver Data to Upper Layer] H --> A E -- No --> I[Timeout Event Triggered] I --> J[Retransmit Same Frame] J --> C F -- No --> K[Discard Duplicate Frame] K --> L[Re-send ACK for last correctly received frame] L --> M[Continue waiting for next frame] style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000000 style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000000 style C fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000000 style D fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000000 style E fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,color:#000000 style F fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#C2185B,color:#000000 style G fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000000 style H fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,color:#000000 style I fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#D32F2F,color:#000000 style J fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#D32F2F,color:#000000 style K fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2,color:#000000 style L fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2,color:#000000 style M fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.3 Timing Diagram Schematic (Cycle Time Visualization) The following chart-style diagram shows the **timeline of events** during one complete Stop-and-Wait cycle. This is the exact figure KTU examiners expect students to draw for full marks in 14-mark questions. ```mermaid gantt title Stop-and-Wait ARQ Timing Diagram - One Complete Cycle dateFormat ss.SSS axisFormat %S.%L section Sender Activity Sender transmits Frame 0 :a1, 00.000, 00.040 Sender waits idle :a2, 00.040, 00.080 section Receiver Activity Receiver receives Frame 0 :b1, 00.040, 00.040 Receiver processes :b2, 00.040, 00.040 Receiver transmits ACK 1 :b3, 00.040, 00.001 ACK travels back :b4, 00.041, 00.040 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Stop-and-Wait Flow Control Protocol `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** Stop-and-Wait Flow Control is a Data Link Layer protocol in which the sender transmits **one data frame** at a time and then **stops** (halts transmission) until it receives an **acknowledgment (ACK)** from the receiver confirming successful receipt. The key features are: 1. Only one outstanding (unacknowledged) frame exists in the channel at any time. 2. Sequence numbers (0 and 1) are used to distinguish new frames from duplicate retransmissions. 3. A **timer** is maintained at the sender; upon expiry, the sender retransmits the frame. This protocol belongs to the **ARQ (Automatic Repeat reQuest)** family and provides both **flow control** and **error control**. --- ### Question 2: What is the role of sequence numbers in Stop-and-Wait ARQ? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** Sequence numbers (alternating between **0** and **1**) serve a critical role in Stop-and-Wait ARQ: 1. **Duplicate detection:** If the ACK for a frame is lost, the sender retransmits the frame after timeout. Without sequence numbers, the receiver cannot tell whether the incoming frame is a brand-new frame or a duplicate of a previously received frame. 2. **Synchronization:** The sequence number in the ACK (e.g., ACK 1 means "I received frame 0, send frame 1 next") keeps the sender and receiver in lock-step. 3. **Minimum requirement:** Since only one frame is in transit at a time, **only 1-bit sequence numbers (0 or 1)** are sufficient — no large sliding window needed. Without sequence numbers, the protocol would fail in the presence of any ACK loss. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the Stop-and-Wait ARQ protocol with a neat diagram. Discuss how it handles the cases of (i) lost frame and (ii) lost acknowledgment.** **Model Solution:** **Definition and Working (3 marks):** Stop-and-Wait ARQ is a flow and error control protocol in which the sender transmits one frame and waits for a positive acknowledgment (ACK) before transmitting the next frame. If the ACK does not arrive within a predefined timeout $T_{out}$, the sender retransmits the frame. Sequence numbers 0 and 1 are used alternately. **Diagram (2 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.1 sequence diagram above for the exact figure to draw.)* The diagram shows sender transmitting frame 0, receiver acknowledging with ACK 1, sender transmitting frame 1, and so on. **Case (i): Lost Frame (1 mark):** - Sender transmits frame 0. - Frame is corrupted or lost in transit. - Receiver never receives it, so no ACK is sent. - Sender's timer expires after $T_{out}$ seconds. - Sender **retransmits frame 0**. - Receiver correctly receives the retransmitted frame, sends ACK 1. **Case (ii): Lost ACK (1 mark):** - Sender transmits frame 0 successfully. - Receiver receives frame 0 correctly and sends ACK 1. - ACK 1 is lost in transit. - Sender's timer expires; sender retransmits frame 0. - Receiver checks sequence number — recognizes it is a **duplicate** of frame 0. - Receiver **discards** the duplicate frame and **re-sends ACK 1**. - Sender eventually receives ACK 1 and proceeds normally. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A channel has a bandwidth of 1 Mbps and a propagation delay of 10 ms. The frame size is 1500 bytes. Compute (i) the transmission time, (ii) the propagation time, (iii) the value of 'a', and (iv) the channel utilization for Stop-and-Wait ARQ. Assume ACK transmission time is negligible.** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $R = 1 \text{ Mbps} = 10^{6}$ bps - $t_{prop} = 10 \text{ ms} = 10 \times 10^{-3}$ s - $L = 1500 \text{ bytes} = 1500 \times 8 = 12000$ bits **(i) Transmission time of frame:** $$ t_{frame} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{12000}{10^{6}} = 0.012 \text{ s} = 12 \text{ ms} $$ **(ii) Propagation time:** $$ t_{prop} = 10 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(given)} $$ **(iii) Normalized ratio $a$:** $$ a = \frac{t_{prop}}{t_{frame}} = \frac{10 \text{ ms}}{12 \text{ ms}} = 0.833 $$ **(iv) Channel utilization:** $$ U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 2 \times 0.833} = \frac{1}{1 + 1.667} = \frac{1}{2.667} $$ $$ \boxed{U = 0.375 = 37.5\%} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to **convert bytes to bits** (multiply by 8). If you write $L = 1500$ bits directly, your $t_{frame}$ will be off by a factor of 8, and ALL subsequent answers will be wrong. Always check the units before plugging into the formula. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **With a suitable timing diagram, derive the expression for channel utilization of Stop-and-Wait ARQ. State clearly all assumptions made.** **Model Solution:** **Timing Diagram (3 marks):** Draw a horizontal timeline showing: | Time Interval | Event | |---------------|-------| | $0$ to $t_{frame}$ | Sender transmits the data frame | | $t_{frame}$ to $t_{frame} + t_{prop}$ | Frame propagates to receiver; sender is **idle** | | $t_{frame} + t_{prop}$ | Receiver receives complete frame | | $t_{frame} + t_{prop}$ to $t_{frame} + t_{prop} + t_{ack}$ | Receiver transmits ACK | | $t_{frame} + t_{prop} + t_{ack}$ to $t_{frame} + 2t_{prop} + t_{ack}$ | ACK propagates back to sender | | $t_{frame} + 2t_{prop} + t_{ack}$ | Sender receives ACK; cycle complete | **Assumptions (1 mark):** 1. ACK transmission time is negligible ($t_{ack} \approx 0$). 2. Processing delays at sender and receiver are negligible. 3. No errors occur (lossless channel). 4. Channel is full-duplex (can carry data and ACK simultaneously). **Derivation (3 marks):** Total cycle time: $$ T_{cycle} = t_{frame} + 2 \cdot t_{prop} + t_{ack} \approx t_{frame} + 2 \cdot t_{prop} $$ Channel utilization (ratio of useful transmission time to total cycle time): $$ U = \frac{t_{frame}}{t_{frame} + 2 \cdot t_{prop}} $$ Dividing numerator and denominator by $t_{frame}$ and substituting $a = \frac{t_{prop}}{t_{frame}}$: $$ \boxed{U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}, \quad \text{where } a = \frac{t_{prop}}{t_{frame}} = \frac{d \cdot R}{v \cdot L}} $$ #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A geostationary satellite is at an altitude of 36,000 km. It is used to transmit data at 1 Mbps using Stop-and-Wait ARQ. The frame size is 4 KB. Compute the channel utilization. Take $v = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Distance $d = 36{,}000 \text{ km} = 36 \times 10^{6}$ m - $R = 1 \text{ Mbps} = 10^{6}$ bps - $L = 4 \text{ KB} = 4 \times 1024 \times 8 = 32{,}768$ bits - $v = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s **Step 1: Compute transmission time:** $$ t_{frame} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{32{,}768}{10^{6}} = 32.768 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \approx 32.77 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 2: Compute propagation delay (one-way):** $$ t_{prop} = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{36 \times 10^{6}}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 0.12 \text{ s} = 120 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 3: Compute the ratio $a$:** $$ a = \frac{t_{prop}}{t_{frame}} = \frac{120 \text{ ms}}{32.77 \text{ ms}} \approx 3.662 $$ **Step 4: Compute channel utilization:** $$ U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 2 \times 3.662} = \frac{1}{1 + 7.324} = \frac{1}{8.324} $$ $$ \boxed{U \approx 0.1201 = 12.01\%} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > 1. **Kilobyte (KB) vs. Kilobit (Kb) confusion:** $1 \text{ KB} = 1024 \text{ bytes} = 8192 \text{ bits}$, not $4000$ bits. KTU accepts both $1024$ and $1000$ as base, but **be consistent**. > 2. **One-way vs. round-trip propagation:** Stop-and-Wait formula already accounts for **round-trip** propagation ($2 \cdot t_{prop}$). Do NOT multiply $t_{prop}$ by 2 again. > 3. **Satellite links** typically yield very low utilization (5–15%) with Stop-and-Wait, motivating sliding-window protocols. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Definition:** Stop-and-Wait ARQ sends **one frame at a time**, waits for ACK, then sends the next. - **Sequence numbers:** Only **2 values (0 and 1)** are needed because at most one unacknowledged frame is in transit. - **Three critical time components:** $t_{frame}$, $t_{prop}$, $t_{ack}$ (often assumed negligible). - **Cycle time formula:** $T_{cycle} = t_{frame} + 2 \cdot t_{prop} + t_{ack}$. - **Golden efficiency formula:** $U = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}$, where $a = \frac{t_{prop}}{t_{frame}} = \frac{d \cdot R}{v \cdot L}$. - **Limiting behavior:** $U \to 1$ when frames are very large or links very short; $U \to 0$ when frames are very small or links very long. - **Timer rule:** $T_{out}$ must be **slightly greater than** $2 \cdot t_{prop} + t_{ack} + t_{proc}$. - **Error recovery:** Both lost frames AND lost ACKs trigger retransmission; the receiver uses sequence numbers to discard duplicates. - **Real-world weakness:** Stop-and-Wait is **inefficient for high-bandwidth, long-distance links** — this is the exact reason Go-Back-N and Selective Repeat exist. - **Throughput:** Effective throughput = $R \times U = \frac{R}{1 + 2a}$ bps. - **Standard KTU values:** Copper cable $v \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s; Fiber/Vacuum $v \approx 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s; Geostationary satellite altitude $\approx 36{,}000$ km. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare Stop-and-Wait with Sliding Window protocols," always emphasize that Stop-and-Wait has a **window size of 1**, which is the root cause of its inefficiency. The solution to low utilization is to **increase the window size** — which gives you Go-Back-N (Module 2 next topic) and Selective Repeat ARQ.

topics.title: Sliding Window Flow Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEVT414 - COMPUTER NETWORKS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Sliding Window Flow Control # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sliding Window Flow Control** is a Data Link Layer protocol that allows a sender to transmit multiple data frames (or packets) sequentially before requiring an acknowledgment from the receiver, governed by a **window size (W)**. The "window" represents the maximum number of unacknowledged frames that can be outstanding at any given time. As acknowledgments are received, the window "slides" forward, permitting the transmission of new frames. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the sliding window mechanism encompasses three principal variants: | Variant | Send Window ($W_s$) | Receive Window ($W_r$) | Key Property | |---|---|---|---| | **One-Bit Sliding Window** | **1** | **1** | Stop-and-Wait behavior with seq/ack bits | | **Go-Back-N ARQ** | **$2^k - 1$** | **1** | Receiver discards erroneous and subsequent frames | | **Selective Repeat ARQ** | **$2^{k-1}$** | **$2^{k-1}$** | Receiver buffers out-of-order frames | Where **$k$** represents the number of bits in the sequence number field. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — The Conveyor Belt Picker** > Imagine a warehouse with a long conveyor belt carrying boxes. A picker (sender) can place a **maximum of N boxes** (window size) on the belt ahead of any box that has been confirmed picked (acknowledged). As the confirmer (receiver) picks each box and flashes a thumbs-up, the picker immediately places a new box at the tail of the belt. The "window" thus slides forward continuously, ensuring the belt always carries the maximum allowable number of in-transit boxes — this is the essence of pipelining through sliding window flow control. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants - **Sequence Number Range:** $0$ to $2^k - 1$ (where $k$ = number of bits) - **Default Maximum Window Size (Go-Back-N):** $W \le 2^k - 1$ - **Default Maximum Window Size (Selective Repeat):** $W \le 2^{k-1}$ - **Acknowledgment Type:** Cumulative (Go-Back-N) or Individual (Selective Repeat) > [!TIP] > The cardinal rule: **$W_s + W_r \le 2^k$** for unambiguous sequence number reuse, guaranteeing that the receiver never confuses a new frame with a retransmission of an old frame. --- # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principles (Step-by-Step) ### Step 1: Window Initialization - Both sender and receiver agree on a **window size $W$** and **sequence number bit length $k$**. - The sender initializes its send pointer at frame **$0$**; the receiver initializes its expected frame at **$0$**. ### Step 2: Frame Transmission Phase - The sender transmits all frames within its current window **without waiting for individual ACKs**. - A **timer** is started for the earliest unacknowledged frame. ### Step 3: Acknowledgment Reception - On receiving a valid frame, the receiver returns an **ACK** carrying the next expected sequence number. - On receiving the ACK, the sender **slides its window** forward by one or more positions (depending on cumulative ACKs). ### Step 4: Error Handling - **Go-Back-N:** On timeout, the sender retransmits *all* frames from the lost frame onward. - **Selective Repeat:** On timeout, the sender retransmits *only* the lost frame; the receiver buffers correctly received subsequent frames. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why pipelining?** The naive Stop-and-Wait protocol leaves the channel **idle** during ACK transmission. With bandwidth-delay product ($B \times D$) often large in modern networks, the sliding window fully utilizes the link by keeping it saturated with frames. ## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Variant - **One-Bit Sliding Window:** Pedagogical baseline — it is essentially Stop-and-Wait with sequence numbers to disambiguate retransmissions. - **Go-Back-N:** Simpler receiver design (no buffer for out-of-order frames), at the cost of potentially wasting bandwidth on retransmissions. - **Selective Repeat:** More complex receiver (requires sorting buffer), but maximizes throughput on lossy links. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates **all derivation formulas** required for KTU University Exam numerical problems on this topic. | # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Condition | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **One-Bit Stop-and-Wait Efficiency** | $E = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$ | $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t}$ | | 2 | **Sliding Window Efficiency (no errors)** | $E = \dfrac{W}{1 + 2a}$ | Valid only if $W < 1 + 2a$ | | 3 | **Sliding Window Efficiency (saturated)** | $E = 100\%$ | Valid only if $W \ge 1 + 2a$ | | 4 | **Frame Transmission Time** | $T_t = \dfrac{L}{B}$ | $L$ = frame length (bits), $B$ = bandwidth (bps) | | 5 | **Propagation Time** | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | $d$ = link length, $v$ = signal velocity ($\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s) | | 6 | **Parameter $a$** | $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t} = \dfrac{B \cdot d}{L \cdot v}$ | Dimensionless ratio | | 7 | **Bandwidth-Delay Product** | $BDP = B \cdot T_p$ | Optimal window size in bits | | 8 | **Max Window in Frames** | $W_{opt} = \dfrac{B \cdot T_p}{L} = 2a + 1$ | Minimum window to fully utilize link | | 9 | **Go-Back-N Max Window** | $W_{GBN} \le 2^k - 1$ | Sequence number constraint | | 10 | **Selective Repeat Max Window** | $W_{SR} \le 2^{k-1}$ | Sequence number constraint | | 11 | **Throughput (with errors, GBN)** | $S = \dfrac{1 - P}{1 + 2a}$ | $P$ = frame error probability | | 12 | **Throughput (with errors, SR)** | $S = \dfrac{1 - P}{1 + 2a(1 - P)}$ | $P$ = frame error probability | > [!WARNING] > When using absolute values in derivations (e.g., $\vert a - b \vert$), use **$\mid$** or **$\vert$** — never the pipe character `|` — to prevent markdown table corruption. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **TCP Sliding Window:** The Transmission Control Protocol implements a variable-size sliding window (rwnd, cwnd) directly derived from this concept. - **Satellite Communications:** High $T_p$ makes Stop-and-Wait infeasible — Sliding Window is mandatory. - **Data Center Networks:** Selective Repeat variants underpin RDMA (Remote Direct Memory Access) retransmission strategies. --- # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Sliding Window Protocol Efficiency > [!IMPORTANT] > This derivation is **highly recurring** in KTU University Exams. Master every step. ### Setup Consider a link of bandwidth $B$ bps, frame length $L$ bits, propagation delay $T_p$ seconds. **Step 1 — Express Frame Transmission Time:** $$T_t = \dfrac{L}{B}$$ **Step 2 — Express Total Cycle Time (Stop-and-Wait Baseline):** A complete cycle in Stop-and-Wait comprises: send frame → wait for ACK → receive ACK. $$\text{Cycle Time} = T_t + T_p + T_{ack} + T_p$$ **Step 3 — Approximate by ignoring ACK transmission time** (since $T_{ack} \ll T_t$): $$\text{Cycle Time} \approx T_t + 2 T_p$$ **Step 4 — Define the dimensionless parameter $a$:** $$a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t}$$ **Step 5 — Compute Stop-and-Wait Efficiency:** $$E_{SAW} = \dfrac{\text{Useful Time}}{\text{Total Cycle Time}} = \dfrac{T_t}{T_t + 2 T_p} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$$ **Step 6 — Generalize to Sliding Window of size $W$:** Now $W$ frames are sent in $W \cdot T_t$ seconds, and ACKs arrive after $T_t + 2T_p$ for the *first* frame. The window can pump out $W$ frames per cycle: $$\text{Total Time for } W \text{ frames} = T_t + 2T_p$$ $$\text{Useful Transmission Time} = W \cdot T_t$$ $$E_{SW} = \dfrac{W \cdot T_t}{T_t + 2T_p} = \dfrac{W}{1 + 2a}$$ **Step 7 — Saturate the link:** If $W \ge 1 + 2a$, the sender never has to wait — the channel is fully utilized: $$E_{SW} = 1 = 100\%$$ > [!TIP] > **Critical KTU Insight:** The minimum window size needed to fully utilize the link is $W = 2a + 1$, where $a = T_p / T_t$. Memorize this — it appears in nearly every numerical problem. ### Numerical Worked Example > **Problem:** A channel has bandwidth $B = 1$ Mbps, one-way propagation delay $T_p = 20$ ms, and frame length $L = 1000$ bits. Determine: > (a) the parameter $a$, > (b) the Stop-and-Wait efficiency, > (c) the minimum sliding window size for 100% utilization. **Solution:** $$T_t = \dfrac{L}{B} = \dfrac{1000}{10^6} = 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}$$ $$a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t} = \dfrac{20 \text{ ms}}{1 \text{ ms}} = 20$$ $$E_{SAW} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 40} = \dfrac{1}{41} \approx 2.44\%$$ $$W_{opt} = 2a + 1 = 2(20) + 1 = 41 \text{ frames}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Achievable Window Size **Step 1 — Recall the sequence number reuse condition:** For Selective Repeat, the receiver must distinguish between a *new* frame and a *retransmission*. The safe upper bound is: $$W_s + W_r \le 2^k$$ **Step 2 — Apply to Go-Back-N (where $W_r = 1$):** $$W_s + 1 \le 2^k \implies W_s \le 2^k - 1$$ **Step 3 — Apply to Selective Repeat (where $W_s = W_r = W$):** $$2W \le 2^k \implies W \le 2^{k-1}$$ **Step 4 — Justify the difference:** > [!NOTE] > Why is GBN allowed a larger window? Because the receiver discards all out-of-order frames, it can never accept a frame with sequence number equal to one it has already acknowledged. SR's buffering makes stricter accounting necessary. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | One-Bit Sliding Window | Go-Back-N | Selective Repeat | |---|---|---|---| | Sender Complexity | Low | Medium | High | | Receiver Complexity | Low | Low | High (needs buffer) | | Wasted Bandwidth (loss) | High | High | Low | | Suitable for | Pedagogy | Low error rate | High error rate | | Sequence number bits ($k=3$) | $W \le 1$ | $W \le 7$ | $W \le 4$ | --- # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Sliding Window Protocol — Block-Level Functional Architecture > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block renders the functional flow of a generic sliding window protocol, mapping sender-side and receiver-side state transitions. ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Protocol Init]) --> InitWS["Initialize W and k
Set send_base = 0
Set next_seq = 0"] InitWS --> CheckWin{"Window Full?
next_seq less than
send_base + W"} CheckWin -- No --> SendFrame["Transmit Frame
next_seq"] SendFrame --> StartTimer["Start Timer for
send_base"] StartTimer --> IncSeq["next_seq = next_seq + 1
mod 2^k"] IncSeq --> CheckWin CheckWin -- Yes --> WaitAck["Wait for ACK"] WaitAck --> RecvAck{ACK Received?} RecvAck -- No --> Timeout{"Timer
Expired?"} Timeout -- No --> WaitAck Timeout -- Yes --> Retransmit["Retransmit from
send_base"] Retransmit --> StartTimer RecvAck -- Yes --> SlideWin["Slide Window Forward
send_base = ack_num"] SlideWin --> StopTimer["Stop Timer
if all ACKed"] StopTimer --> CheckWin InitWS --> InitWR["Receiver: Set
expected = 0"] InitWR --> RecvFrame{"Frame Received?
seq == expected?"} RecvFrame -- Yes --> Deliver["Deliver to Network Layer
Send ACK expected + 1"] Deliver --> IncExp["expected = expected + 1
mod 2^k"] IncExp --> RecvFrame RecvFrame -- No GBN["Go-Back-N:
Discard Frame
Resend Last ACK"] RecvFrame -- No SR["Selective Repeat:
Buffer Frame
Send ACK seq"] No GBN --> RecvFrame No SR --> RecvFrame ``` ## 4.2 Window Sliding — Sequence Number Lifecycle ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant S as Sender participant R as Receiver Note over S: W = 4, send_base = 0 S->>R: Frame 0 S->>R: Frame 1 S->>R: Frame 2 S->>R: Frame 3 R-->>S: ACK 1 Note over S: Window slides
send_base = 1 S->>R: Frame 4 R-->>S: ACK 2 Note over S: Window slides
send_base = 2 S->>R: Frame 5 Note over S: Timer expires for
Frame 2 S->>R: Retransmit Frame 2 R-->>S: ACK 3 Note over S: send_base = 3 S->>R: Frame 6 S->>R: Frame 7 ``` ## 4.3 State Transition Diagram for Sender ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Ready Ready --> Transmitting: New Frame in Window Transmitting --> Waiting: Frame Sent, Awaiting ACK Waiting --> Ready: Cumulative ACK Received Waiting --> Retransmitting: Timeout Retransmitting --> Waiting: N Frames Resent Retransmitting --> Ready: ACK After Resend Ready --> [*]: All Frames Acknowledged ``` --- # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Differentiate between Go-Back-N ARQ and Selective Repeat ARQ with respect to receiver window size and bandwidth utilization.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Criterion | Go-Back-N ARQ | Selective Repeat ARQ | |---|---|---| | Receiver Window Size ($W_r$) | **1** | **$2^{k-1}$** | | Out-of-order Frame Handling | Discarded | Buffered | | Bandwidth Utilization on Loss | Low (resends $N$ frames) | High (resends 1 frame) | | Sequence Number Requirement | $W \le 2^k - 1$ | $W \le 2^{k-1}$ | | Receiver Complexity | Low | High | *(1 mark for receiver window, 1 mark for buffering behavior, 1 mark for bandwidth utilization comparison.)* ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Define the term "sliding window" in Data Link Layer protocols. Why is the window size bounded by $2^{k-1}$ in Selective Repeat ARQ?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **sliding window** is a flow control mechanism that allows the sender to transmit multiple frames within a defined window before requiring acknowledgments, thereby pipelining the link. *(1 mark)* The bound $W \le 2^{k-1}$ arises from the **sequence number reuse condition** $W_s + W_r \le 2^k$. Since $W_s = W_r = W$ in Selective Repeat, we get $2W \le 2^k \Rightarrow W \le 2^{k-1}$. This ensures the receiver can unambiguously distinguish between a newly transmitted frame and a retransmission of an old one. *(2 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **Q: (a)** A satellite link has a one-way propagation delay of $250$ ms, transmits at $1$ Mbps, and uses frames of $1250$ bytes. Calculate the parameter $a$ and the minimum window size for full utilization. Discuss why Stop-and-Wait is unsuitable for satellite channels. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the operation of Go-Back-N ARQ with a sequence number bit length $k = 3$. Illustrate the protocol behavior with a scenario where the third frame is lost, and compute the maximum number of frames that can be sent before an ACK is received. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(a) > [!NOTE] > **Step 1 — Frame Transmission Time** $$T_t = \dfrac{L}{B} = \dfrac{1250 \times 8}{10^6} = \dfrac{10000}{10^6} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Step 2 — Parameter $a$** $$a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t} = \dfrac{250 \text{ ms}}{10 \text{ ms}} = 25$$ > [!NOTE] > **Step 3 — Stop-and-Wait Efficiency** $$E_{SAW} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 50} = \dfrac{1}{51} \approx 1.96\%$$ > [!NOTE] > **Step 4 — Minimum Window for 100% Utilization** $$W_{opt} = 2a + 1 = 2(25) + 1 = 51 \text{ frames}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Step 5 — Justification of Why Stop-and-Wait is Unsuitable** The total RTT is $2 T_p = 500$ ms, and the frame transmission time is only $10$ ms. With Stop-and-Wait, the channel lies **idle for $500 - 10 = 490$ ms** out of every $510$ ms cycle — a utilization of just $\approx 2\%$. For a satellite link, where bandwidth is premium, this is highly inefficient. A sliding window of size $51$ would fully saturate the link. **Mark Allocation:** $T_t$ calculation (2 marks), $a$ (1 mark), $E_{SAW}$ (1 mark), $W_{opt}$ (2 marks), discussion (1 mark) = **7 marks total**. --- #### Model Solution for Q.A(b) > [!NOTE] > **Step 1 — Sequence Number Range for $k=3$** $$0 \le \text{seq} \le 2^3 - 1 = 7$$ > [!NOTE] > **Step 2 — Maximum Go-Back-N Window** $$W_{GBN} = 2^k - 1 = 7 \text{ frames}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Step 3 — Scenario: Third Frame Lost** | Step | Sender Action | Receiver Action | Window State | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Send Frame 0 | Receive 0, send ACK 1 | [0..6] | | 2 | Send Frame 1 | Receive 1, send ACK 2 | [0..6] | | 3 | Send Frame 2 | **LOST** — no ACK | [0..6] | | 4 | Send Frame 3 | Discard (out-of-order), re-ACK 2 | [0..6] | | 5 | Send Frame 4 | Discard, re-ACK 2 | [0..6] | | 6 | **Timer expires for Frame 2** | — | [0..6] | | 7 | Retransmit Frames 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 | Accept 2, ACK 3 ... | [0..6] | > [!NOTE] > **Step 4 — Maximum Number of Frames Sent Before First ACK** In a clean channel with no loss, the sender can push out $W = 7$ frames before needing the first cumulative ACK to slide the window. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse $W = 2^k$ (incorrect) with $W = 2^k - 1$ for Go-Back-N. The minus-one is **mandatory** — exceeding it causes sequence number ambiguity. **Mark Allocation:** Sequence range (1 mark), max window (1 mark), scenario tabulation (3 marks), max frames before ACK (2 marks) = **7 marks total**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Q: (a)** With neat sequence diagrams, explain the working of Selective Repeat ARQ. Compare its efficiency with Go-Back-N ARQ under a frame error probability $P = 0.1$ and $a = 1$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A $1$ Mbps link uses Selective Repeat ARQ with $k = 4$ and a window size of $8$. Calculate the maximum possible throughput if the round-trip time is $40$ ms and the frame size is $500$ bits. State why $W = 8$ satisfies the design constraint. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(a) > [!NOTE] > **Step 1 — Selective Repeat Operation** The sender maintains a window of size $W$ and the receiver maintains a window of equal size. Only the *specific* lost frame is retransmitted (hence "selective"). The receiver buffers out-of-order frames and delivers them in sequence. ``` Sender Window: [3 4 5 6 7 0 1 2] W = 8, k = 4 Receiver Window: [3 4 5 6 7 0 1 2] ← accepts and buffers ``` > [!NOTE] > **Step 2 — Throughput Formulae** $$S_{GBN} = \dfrac{1 - P}{1 + 2a} = \dfrac{1 - 0.1}{1 + 2(1)} = \dfrac{0.9}{3} = 0.3$$ $$S_{SR} = \dfrac{1 - P}{1 + 2a(1 - P)} = \dfrac{1 - 0.1}{1 + 2(1)(0.9)} = \dfrac{0.9}{2.8} \approx 0.321$$ > [!NOTE] > **Step 3 — Comparison** Selective Repeat offers a slight edge ($\approx 0.321$ vs. $0.3$). The advantage grows dramatically as $P$ increases — at $P = 0.5$, $S_{GBN} \approx 0.167$ while $S_{SR} \approx 0.333$, demonstrating SR's superiority on lossy links. **Mark Allocation:** SR explanation (2 marks), GBN throughput (2 marks), SR throughput (2 marks), comparison (1 mark) = **7 marks total**. --- #### Model Solution for Q.B(b) > [!NOTE] > **Step 1 — Frame Transmission Time** $$T_t = \dfrac{L}{B} = \dfrac{500}{10^6} = 0.5 \text{ ms}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Step 2 — Propagation Time** $$T_p = \dfrac{RTT}{2} = \dfrac{40}{2} = 20 \text{ ms}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Step 3 — Parameter $a$** $$a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t} = \dfrac{20}{0.5} = 40$$ > [!NOTE] > **Step 4 — Throughput in Error-Free Case** Since the window is large enough to keep the channel saturated (assuming $W \ge 2a + 1 = 81$ is the optimum, but our $W = 8$ is not — so we use the unsaturated formula): $$S = \dfrac{W}{1 + 2a} \times B = \dfrac{8}{1 + 80} \times 10^6 = \dfrac{8}{81} \times 10^6 \approx 98,765 \text{ bps}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Step 5 — Verify Design Constraint** For Selective Repeat, $W \le 2^{k-1} = 2^3 = 8$. The given $W = 8$ exactly satisfies the upper bound — this is the **maximum permissible window** for $k = 4$. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to divide RTT by 2 to get one-way $T_p$, leading to $a = 80$ instead of $a = 40$ and halving the computed throughput. **Mark Allocation:** $T_t$ (1 mark), $T_p$ (1 mark), $a$ (1 mark), throughput (3 marks), design constraint (1 mark) = **7 marks total**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Deduction Zones for Sliding Window Problems:** > > 1. **Mixing up window bounds:** Students often write $W \le 2^k$ for Go-Back-N. The correct bound is $W \le 2^k - 1$. Loss: **1–2 marks**. > 2. **Forgetting RTT vs. One-Way Delay:** The propagation delay $T_p$ is **one-way**. RTT = $2 T_p$. Mixing these inflates $a$ by 2x. Loss: **1–2 marks**. > 3. **Skipping the "Why" behind formulas:** Examiners award marks for explaining *why* $W = 2a+1$ is the saturation threshold, not just stating it. Loss: **1 mark**. > 4. **Confusing cumulative vs. individual ACKs:** Go-Back-N uses *cumulative* ACKs (one ACK acks all in-order frames up to N-1); Selective Repeat ideally uses *individual* ACKs. Loss: **1 mark**. > 5. **Not stating units:** Always annotate $T_t$, $T_p$ with ms or seconds and throughput with bps or kbps. Loss: **0.5 mark per instance**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** > > - **Sliding Window** = pipelined flow control governed by a window of size $W$. > - **Three Variants:** One-Bit ($W=1$), Go-Back-N ($W \le 2^k - 1$), Selective Repeat ($W \le 2^{k-1}$). > - **Key Formula:** $E = \dfrac{W}{1 + 2a}$, where $a = T_p / T_t = B \cdot d / (L \cdot v)$. > - **Saturation Threshold:** Window $W \ge 2a + 1$ is required for $100\%$ link utilization. > - **Bandwidth-Delay Product:** $BDP = B \cdot T_p$ bits — defines the optimal window in *bits*; divide by $L$ to get frames. > - **GBN Receiver Window = 1:** Discards out-of-order frames; resends last ACK. > - **SR Receiver Window = $2^{k-1}$:** Buffers out-of-order frames; sends individual ACKs. > - **Error-Free Throughput (GBN):** $S = \dfrac{1 - P}{1 + 2a}$. > - **Error-Free Throughput (SR):** $S = \dfrac{1 - P}{1 + 2a(1 - P)}$. > - **Sequence Number Rule:** $W_s + W_r \le 2^k$ (unambiguity condition). > - **Satellite / WAN Reality:** Very high $T_p$ makes Stop-and-Wait catastrophic — sliding window is **mandatory**. > - **TCP Connection:** Uses a *variable* sliding window derived from min(rwnd, cwnd) — same mathematical lineage. > - **Default Velocity:** $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s for signal propagation in copper/fiber (use $3 \times 10^8$ if unspecified, but state your assumption). ---

topics.title: Error Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT414 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Error Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Error Control** in the context of the Data Link Layer (DLL) of the OSI reference model refers to the set of mechanisms, protocols, and algorithms employed to detect and/or correct errors that occur during the transmission of data frames across an unreliable physical medium. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for *PEEVT414 – Computer Networks*, Error Control is positioned as a sub-layer function of Layer 2, sitting alongside **Framing**, **Flow Control**, and **Medium Access Control**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Error Control encompasses two principal categories:
> 1. **Error Detection** — Parity Check, Checksum, and Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC).
> 2. **Error Correction** — Forward Error Correction (FEC) and Automatic Repeat Request (ARQ) protocols (Stop-and-Wait, Go-Back-N, Selective Repeat).

Mathematically, an **error** is defined as a flip in one or more bits of a transmitted frame during transit. If a frame of $n$ bits is transmitted, the probability of an error occurring in any bit is the **Bit Error Rate (BER)**, denoted as $p$. The probability of a frame arriving error-free is:

$$
P_{good} = (1 - p)^n
$$

The probability of at least one error in the frame is therefore:

$$
P_{err} = 1 - (1 - p)^n
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Postal Letter with a Watermark"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The Sealed Courier Envelope**
> Imagine you are sending a sensitive legal document across a city using a chain of courier handovers. You cannot personally stand at every handover point. To ensure the document arrives intact, you take three precautions:
> 1. **Watermark (Error Detection via Parity/CRC):** You add a unique mathematical watermark on the last page. The receiver recomputes the watermark and compares — if the watermark doesn't match, the letter is tampered.
> 2. **Backup Photocopy (Error Correction — FEC):** You include a redundant photocopy of every page so that even if a corner of one page is smudged, the receiver can reconstruct the missing words.
> 3. **Acknowledgment Slip (ARQ Protocols):** You ask the receiver to send back a signed receipt. If you don't receive the receipt, you re-send the letter. If you get a "resend" request, you re-send.
>
> These three mechanisms — **detection, correction, and retransmission** — are exactly what Error Control does in computer networks.

## 1.3 Types of Errors — KTU Taxonomy

| **Error Class** | **Definition** | **Typical Cause** |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Bit Error** | Only **1 bit** in a frame of $n$ bits is flipped | Low-BER channels, thermal noise |
| **Burst Error** | A contiguous sequence of $b$ bits (where $b \geq 2$) is corrupted | Impulse noise, lightning, fading |
| **Multiple Random Errors** | Non-contiguous bits corrupted | Severe channel degradation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metric:** The **Burst Length (B)** is typically measured in bits. For most practical channels, burst errors are statistically far more common than single-bit errors, which is why **CRC** is the de-facto industry choice for error detection (e.g., Ethernet uses CRC-32).

## 1.4 Why Error Control is Non-Negotiable

- **BER is never zero** — even fibre-optic cables exhibit a non-zero bit error rate ($\approx 10^{-12}$ for high-quality fibre, higher for copper/wireless).
- **Application Sensitivity:** A bank transaction cannot tolerate a 1-bit error in the amount field. A corrupted TCP segment will trigger a timeout, costing throughput.
- **Data Link Layer Contract:** Layer 2 promises the Network Layer a "reliable link" abstraction; Error Control is what fulfills that promise.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Error Detection Mechanisms — Operational Decomposition

### 2.1.1 Parity Check (Simplest KTU-Level Detection)

- **Logic:** A single **parity bit** is appended to each data unit (typically 7 or 8 bits) to make the total number of 1s either **even** (even parity) or **odd** (odd parity).
- **Why it works:** It changes the **Hamming Distance** of the code from 1 to 2, meaning any single-bit error will violate the parity rule.
- **Why it fails:** Parity check **cannot detect 2-bit errors** (since 2 flips restore parity) and offers **no correction capability**.

**Operation:**
1. Sender counts 1-bits in the data word $D$.
2. If **even parity** is used: append bit $p$ such that total 1s is even: $p = D_0 \oplus D_1 \oplus \cdots \oplus D_{n-1}$.
3. Receiver recomputes parity; mismatch $\Rightarrow$ error detected.

> [!NOTE]
> **2-Dimensional Parity:** KTU often tests the *2D Parity* concept where parity bits are computed both row-wise AND column-wise. This upgrades the scheme to detect (and even correct) **single-bit errors** in a 2D block.

---

### 2.1.2 Checksum (Used in TCP/UDP/IPv4 Headers)

- **Logic:** The data is segmented into $k$-bit blocks (typically 16-bit words). These blocks are **summed using 1's complement arithmetic**, and the 1's complement of the final sum is transmitted as the checksum.
- **Why it works:** Any bit-flip in transit will likely alter the recomputed checksum at the receiver, triggering detection.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Break message into $n$ blocks of 16 bits each: $W_1, W_2, \ldots, W_n$.
2. Compute $S = W_1 + W_2 + \cdots + W_n$ (using **1's complement addition** — i.e., end-around carry).
3. Transmit: $\bar{S}$ (1's complement of $S$).
4. Receiver recomputes sum of all received words **including** the checksum $\bar{S}$. The result must equal all 1s (0xFFFF in 16-bit).
5. If not all 1s $\Rightarrow$ error detected.

---

### 2.1.3 Cyclic Redundency Check (CRC) — The Industry Standard

**CRC is the most critical sub-topic in KTU Module 2 for ESE questions.**

**Conceptual Foundation:**
CRC is based on **polynomial arithmetic over GF(2)** (Galois Field of order 2 — i.e., modulo-2 arithmetic, where addition and subtraction are both XOR).

- A binary string of $n$ bits is interpreted as a polynomial of degree $n-1$.
- Example: $1101 \rightarrow M(x) = x^3 + x^2 + 1$.

**Generation Logic — Step-by-Step:**

1. Sender and receiver agree on a **Generator Polynomial** $G(x)$ of degree $r$.
2. Sender appends $r$ zero bits to the message $M(x)$, yielding $x^r \cdot M(x)$.
3. Divide $x^r \cdot M(x)$ by $G(x)$ using **modulo-2 division** to get remainder $R(x)$ of degree $< r$.
4. Transmit $T(x) = x^r \cdot M(x) \oplus R(x)$.
5. Receiver divides received $T(x)$ by $G(x)$. If remainder is 0, no error detected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why CRC is Powerful:** A generator polynomial of degree $r$ can detect:
> - **All single-bit errors** ✓
> - **All double-bit errors** ✓ (provided $G(x)$ has at least 3 terms)
> - **All burst errors of length $\leq r$** ✓
> - **Burst errors of length $> r$** with probability $1 - 2^{-r}$

---

### 2.1.4 Hamming Code — Error Correction (Forward Error Correction)

**Core Concept:** Hamming codes add redundant bits at **specific positions** (powers of 2) so that the receiver can identify *which* bit is in error using **parity checks over overlapping groups**.

- For $m$ data bits, the number of redundant bits $r$ must satisfy:
$$
2^r \geq m + r + 1
$$

- The code can correct **all single-bit errors**.

**Position Rule:** Redundant bits $r_1, r_2, r_3, \ldots$ are placed at bit positions $1, 2, 4, 8, 16, \ldots$ (i.e., $2^0, 2^1, 2^2, 2^3, 2^4$).

- Bit at position $i$ is covered by all parity bits whose position in the binary representation of $i$ has a `1`.

---

## 2.2 ARQ (Automatic Repeat Request) Protocols — Layer-2 Retransmission

ARQ is the **backbone of reliable data link protocols** in the KTU syllabus. There are three variants:

### 2.2.1 Stop-and-Wait ARQ

- Sender transmits **one frame** at a time.
- Waits for **ACK** (positive) or **NAK** (negative) before sending the next.
- Uses a **1-bit sequence number** to distinguish a new frame from a retransmission.

**Efficiency Formula (KTU-High-Yield):**

$$
\eta_{SAW} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}
$$

where $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_f}$ is the ratio of propagation time $T_p$ to frame transmission time $T_f$.

### 2.2.2 Go-Back-N ARQ

- Sender can transmit up to **N frames** (window size) without ACK.
- On error, sender **retransmits that frame AND all subsequent frames** (goes back).
- Receiver discards any out-of-order frame and accepts only in-order frames.

**Efficiency Formula (KTU-High-Yield):**

$$
\eta_{GBN} = \frac{N}{1 + 2a}
$$

(valid when $N \geq 2a + 1$)

### 2.2.3 Selective Repeat ARQ

- Sender can transmit up to **N frames** without ACK.
- On error, sender retransmits **ONLY the erroneous frame**.
- Receiver buffers out-of-order frames and delivers in order to Network Layer.

**Efficiency Formula (KTU-High-Yield):**

$$
\eta_{SR} = \frac{N}{1 + 2a}
$$

(Same as GBN, but practical behavior is far superior for noisy channels.)

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Equations — They Appear in ~70% of KTU ESE Module 2 Numerical Questions.**

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Variables / Units** | **Boundary Conditions** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Frame Error Probability | $P_{err} = 1 - (1 - p)^n$ | $p$ = BER, $n$ = frame bits | Valid for i.i.d. bit errors |
| 2 | Parity Bit (Even) | $p = D_0 \oplus D_1 \oplus \cdots \oplus D_{n-1}$ | $D_i$ = data bits | Detects odd-bit errors only |
| 3 | CRC Remainder | $R(x) = [x^r \cdot M(x)] \bmod G(x)$ | $G(x)$ = generator poly degree $r$ | $R(x)$ has degree $\leq r-1$ |
| 4 | Hamming Redundant Bits | $2^r \geq m + r + 1$ | $m$ = data bits, $r$ = redundant bits | Smallest $r$ satisfying this |
| 5 | Hamming Code Rate | $R = \dfrac{m}{m + r}$ | Dimensionless efficiency | Lower $r \Rightarrow$ higher rate |
| 6 | SAW ARQ Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$ | $a = T_p / T_f$ | $a > 1$ is "long-fat pipe" |
| 7 | Go-Back-N Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{N}{1 + 2a}$ | $N$ = window size | $N \geq 2a + 1$ for max |
| 8 | Selective Repeat Eff. | $\eta = \dfrac{N}{1 + 2a}$ | $N$ = window size | $N \leq 2^a$ (to avoid ambiguity) |
| 9 | Throughput | $Throughput = \eta \cdot B$ | $B$ = link bandwidth (bps) | Net useful data rate |
| 10 | Burst Error Detection | $P_{detect} = 1 - 2^{-r}$ | $r$ = CRC degree | For burst length $> r$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** CRC-32 is used in **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)**, CRC-16-CCITT in **HDLC**, and CRC-8 in **Bluetooth**. Hamming(7,4) is used in **ECC DRAM memory**. Stop-and-Wait ARQ is the basis of **HDLC's** Normal Response Mode. Go-Back-N is used in **classical TCP Tahoe**. Selective Repeat is the basis of **TCP Reno** selective acknowledgment (SACK).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked-Out Derivations — Model Questions for KTU Numerical Practice**

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: CRC Encoding of a Bit Stream

**Problem Setup (KTU-Style):**
A message $M = 1101011011$ is to be transmitted using CRC with generator polynomial $G = 10011$ (degree $r = 4$). Compute the transmitted codeword $T$.

**Step 1 — Polynomial Mapping**

Convert $M$ and $G$ to polynomials:

$$
M(x) = 1\cdot x^9 + 1\cdot x^8 + 0\cdot x^7 + 1\cdot x^6 + 0\cdot x^5 + 1\cdot x^4 + 1\cdot x^3 + 0\cdot x^2 + 1\cdot x^1 + 1\cdot x^0
$$

$$
M(x) = x^9 + x^8 + x^6 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1
$$

$$
G(x) = x^4 + x + 1
$$

**Step 2 — Append $r = 4$ Zero Bits**

$$
x^4 \cdot M(x) = 11010110110000
$$

(The degree is now 13, so we have 14 bits, of which 10 are message and 4 are zeros.)

**Step 3 — Modulo-2 Division of $x^4 M(x)$ by $G(x)$**

Perform polynomial long division in GF(2). Every subtraction is XOR (no borrows).

Dividing $11010110110000$ by $10011$:

| **Step** | **Dividend** | **Divisor** | **Quotient bit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial | 11010110110000 | 10011 | — |
| 1 | 10011 ⊕ 11010 = 01001 (shifted) | 10011 | 1 |
| 2 | 10011 ⊕ 10010 = 00001 | 10011 | 1 |
| ... (continue full division) | ... | ... | ... |

The division yields remainder $R(x)$ of degree $\leq 3$. After full division, we obtain:

$$
R(x) = 1110
$$

(Equivalent to the bit pattern `1110`, i.e., $x^3 + x^2 + x$.)

**Step 4 — Form the Codeword $T$**

$$
T = x^4 \cdot M(x) \oplus R(x) = 11010110111110
$$

(Original 10-bit message `1101011011` followed by 4-bit CRC `1110`.)

**Step 5 — Receiver Verification (Proof of Correctness)**

At the receiver, divide $T$ by $G(x)$:

$$
T \div G = \text{some quotient, remainder } 0000
$$

If the remainder is zero, the frame is **assumed error-free**. If non-zero, an error pattern is detected (though CRC does not specify *which* bit is in error — it only signals *that* an error occurred).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hamming(7,4) Code Construction

**Problem Setup:**
Encode the 4-bit data word $D = d_3 d_2 d_1 d_0 = 1011$ using a Hamming(7,4) code.

**Step 1 — Determine Number of Redundant Bits $r$**

We need the smallest $r$ such that $2^r \geq m + r + 1$:

$$
2^2 = 4 \quad (\text{not enough: } 4 \geq 4+2+1 = 7? \text{ No})
$$

$$
2^3 = 8 \quad (\text{OK: } 8 \geq 4+3+1 = 8? \text{ Yes})
$$

So $r = 3$. Total codeword length $n = m + r = 7$.

**Step 2 — Place Bits in Positions 1 to 7**

Position 1, 2, 4 are reserved for parity bits $p_1, p_2, p_3$. Positions 3, 5, 6, 7 hold data bits.

| **Position** | **7** | **6** | **5** | **4** | **3** | **2** | **1** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Content** | $d_3$ | $d_2$ | $d_1$ | $p_3$ | $d_0$ | $p_2$ | $p_1$ |
| **Value** | 1 | 0 | 1 | ? | 1 | ? | ? |

**Step 3 — Compute Parity Bits Using Even Parity**

- **$p_1$ (Position 1)** checks positions whose binary has bit 0 set: $\{1, 3, 5, 7\} \Rightarrow \{p_1, d_0, d_1, d_3\}$
$$
p_1 \oplus d_0 \oplus d_1 \oplus d_3 = 0
$$

$$
p_1 \oplus 1 \oplus 1 \oplus 1 = 0 \Rightarrow p_1 = 1
$$

- **$p_2$ (Position 2)** checks positions with bit 1 set: $\{2, 3, 6, 7\} \Rightarrow \{p_2, d_0, d_2, d_3\}$
$$
p_2 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 = 0 \Rightarrow p_2 = 0
$$

- **$p_3$ (Position 4)** checks positions with bit 2 set: $\{4, 5, 6, 7\} \Rightarrow \{p_3, d_1, d_2, d_3\}$
$$
p_3 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 = 0 \Rightarrow p_3 = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Final Codeword**

$$
\boxed{T = 1010101}
$$

(Positions 1→7: $p_1 p_2 d_0 p_3 d_1 d_2 d_3 = 1\,0\,1\,0\,1\,0\,1$)

**Step 5 — Decoding Proof: Suppose Bit 5 Flips in Transit**

Receiver gets $R = 1010001$. Recompute syndrome bits:

- $s_1 = p_1 \oplus d_0 \oplus d_1 \oplus d_3 = 1 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 = 1$
- $s_2 = p_2 \oplus d_0 \oplus d_2 \oplus d_3 = 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 = 0$
- $s_3 = p_3 \oplus d_1 \oplus d_2 \oplus d_3 = 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 = 1$

Syndrome $(s_3 s_2 s_1) = (101)_2 = 5$. The error is at **position 5** ✓ — correct!

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Stop-and-Wait ARQ Efficiency — Full Derivation

**Setup:**
- Frame size: $L$ bits
- Bandwidth: $B$ bps
- Propagation delay: $T_p$ seconds
- Round-trip-time: $RTT = 2T_p$
- Frame transmission time: $T_f = L / B$

**Total Time to Send One Frame and Receive ACK:**

$$
T_{total} = T_f + T_p + T_{ack} + T_p
$$

Assuming ACK transmission time is negligible ($T_{ack} \approx 0$):

$$
T_{total} = T_f + 2T_p
$$

**Useful Time** (only $T_f$ carries data):

$$
T_{useful} = T_f
$$

**Efficiency (Utilization) of the Link:**

$$
\eta = \frac{T_{useful}}{T_{total}} = \frac{T_f}{T_f + 2T_p}
$$

Let $a = T_p / T_f$:

$$
\eta = \frac{1}{1 + 2 \cdot (T_p / T_f)} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-Style):**
- $L = 1000$ bits, $B = 1$ Mbps, distance $d = 2000$ km, propagation speed $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s.

Compute $T_f$ and $T_p$:

$$
T_f = \frac{L}{B} = \frac{1000}{10^6} = 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
T_p = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 10^{-2} \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
a = \frac{T_p}{T_f} = \frac{10}{1} = 10
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{1}{1 + 2(10)} = \frac{1}{21} \approx 4.76\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** For long-fat pipes (high $a$), Stop-and-Wait is catastrophically inefficient — this is **why Go-Back-N and Selective Repeat were invented.**

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Go-Back-N ARQ Throughput with Errors

**Setup:** Suppose bit error rate is $p$, frame size $n$ bits, window size $N$.

**Frame Error Probability** (independent assumption):

$$
P_{frame\_err} = 1 - (1 - p)^n
$$

**Probability of $k$ consecutive successful frames, then one error:**

$$
P_k = (1 - P_{frame\_err})^k \cdot P_{frame\_err}
$$

**Average number of frames transmitted per successful delivery (in steady state):**

$$
E[\text{transmissions}] = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} (k+1) \cdot P_k = \frac{1}{1 - P_{frame\_err}}
$$

**Throughput in the presence of errors** (assuming $N \geq 2a+1$ so the pipeline is full):

$$
S = \frac{N \cdot (1 - P_{frame\_err})}{1 + 2a}
$$

This is the **generalized efficiency formula** for Go-Back-N with errors.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Error Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Sender["SENDER SIDE"]
        A[Application Data] --> B[Data Link Layer]
        B --> C[Error Encoder]
        C1[Parity Generator] --> C
        C2[CRC Divider] --> C
        C3[Checksum Summer] --> C
        C --> D[Frame Builder]
        D --> E[Physical Layer Transmit]
    end

    E --> F[Noisy Channel with BER]

    subgraph Receiver["RECEIVER SIDE"]
        F --> G[Physical Layer Receive]
        G --> H[Frame Parser]
        H --> I[Error Decoder]
        I --> J{Residue Zero?}
        J -->|Yes| K[Accept Frame]
        J -->|No| L[Discard or Correct]
        K --> M[ARQ Decision Unit]
        L --> M
        M -->|ACK| N[Send ACK]
        M -->|NAK| O[Send NAK]
        O --> P[Retransmit Trigger]
        N --> P
    end
```

## 4.2 Stop-and-Wait ARQ Timeline

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Sender
    participant R as Receiver
    Note over S,R: Frame 0 transmitted
    S->>R: DATA Frame Seq=0
    Note over S,R: Propagation delay Tp
    R-->>S: ACK Seq=0
    Note over S,R: Frame 1 transmitted
    S->>R: DATA Frame Seq=1
    Note over S,R: Frame lost or NAK
    R-->>S: NAK Seq=1
    Note over S,R: Sender retransmits Frame 1
    S->>R: DATA Frame Seq=1 (retransmit)
    R-->>S: ACK Seq=1
```

## 4.3 Go-Back-N ARQ — Sliding Window & Loss Recovery

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SenderWindow["SENDER WINDOW (Size N=4)"]
        W1[Frame 0 Sent]
        W2[Frame 1 Sent]
        W3[Frame 2 Sent]
        W4[Frame 3 Sent]
        W5[Frame 4 In Window]
    end

    W1 --> X1[Lost in Transit]
    W2 --> X2[Discarded by Rx]
    W3 --> X3[Discarded by Rx]
    W4 --> X4[Discarded by Rx]

    X1 --> R1[Receiver NAK for Frame 0]
    X2 --> R1
    X3 --> R1
    X4 --> R1

    R1 --> Retx[Retransmit Frame 0,1,2,3]
    Retx --> Ack[Receiver accepts all in order]
```

## 4.4 Selective Repeat ARQ — Selective Retransmission

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SWin["SENDER WINDOW"]
        F0[Frame 0]
        F1[Frame 1]
        F2[Frame 2]
        F3[Frame 3]
    end

    F0 --> Rx0[Received OK]
    F1 --> Rx1[Received OK]
    F2 --> Rx2[Lost]
    F3 --> Rx3[Received OK, Buffered]

    Rx2 --> NAK[NAK for Frame 2 only]
    NAK --> Retx[Sender Retransmits ONLY Frame 2]
    Retx --> Final[Receiver Delivers in-order to NL]
```

## 4.5 CRC Encoder Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In[Message M bit stream] --> Shift[Shift Register n bits]
    Shift --> Xor1[XOR Gate with Feedback]
    Xor1 --> Div[Modulo-2 Divider by G x]
    Div --> Rem[Remainder R of degree r-1]
    Rem --> Mux[Append to M]
    Mux --> Out[Transmitted Codeword T]
```
