- [x] **Minimum frame size = 64 bytes (512 bits)** — required so a station is still transmitting when the collision signal returns.
- [x] **Maximum frame size = 1518 bytes** (without preamble/SFD); up to 9000 bytes for jumbo frames.
- [x] **Slot time = 51.2 µs at 10 Mbps** = 512 bit times.
- [x] **Interframe Gap = 9.6 µs at 10 Mbps** = 96 bit times.
- [x] **Jam signal = 32 bits** — ensures all stations detect the collision.
- [x] **Binary Exponential Backoff:** $r = \text{Random}(0, 2^k - 1)$, $k = \min(n, 10)$. After **16 collisions**, frame is dropped.
- [x] **5-4-3 Rule:** 5 segments, 4 repeaters, 3 host-populated segments (for shared 10 Mbps Ethernet).
- [x] **MAC Address** = 48 bits (6 bytes). First bit = I/G; second bit = U/L.
- [x] **Efficiency formula** = $\frac{1}{1 + 6.44a}$ where $a = \frac{t_p}{t_{frame}}$.
- [x] **Maximum network diameter** ≈ 5000 m (10BASE5 theoretical), reduced to 2500 m in practice.
- [x] **CSMA/CD is DISABLED** in full-duplex switched Ethernet (no collisions possible).
- [x] **Naming convention:** `Speed BASE Media`. Example: 1000BASE-T = 1 Gbps, baseband, twisted pair.
- [x] **Physical constants to memorize:** Slot time = 51.2 µs, IFG = 9.6 µs, Min frame = 64 B, Max frame = 1518 B, Preamble = 7 B, SFD = 1 B, FCS = 4 B.
- [x] **Memorize the frame format table** — it is the single most important diagram in this module.
- [x] **Efficiency drops sharply** as $a$ increases — long cables + short frames = bad performance.
- [x] **Gigabit Ethernet (1000 Mbps)** uses **carrier extension** to maintain 512-bit minimum (extends to 4096 bits for short frames).
- [x] **FCS uses CRC-32**, not CRC-16. Mentioning the wrong CRC polynomial will lose marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CSMA/CD
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT414 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** CSMA/CD

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection (CSMA/CD)** is a contention-based **Medium Access Control (MAC)** protocol standardized under **IEEE 802.3** (Classical Ethernet). It mandates that a station **listen to the shared channel before transmitting (Carrier Sense)**, and **continue monitoring the channel during transmission** to detect any signal level anomaly indicating a **collision (Collision Detection)**. Upon collision, the station aborts transmission, transmits a **Jam Signal**, and reschedules the frame using the **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)** algorithm.

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, CSMA/CD belongs to the family of **Random Access / Contention-based** MAC sublayer protocols of the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)**. It operates under the **"Listen Before Talk" AND "Listen While Talk"** paradigm — both temporal checks are mandatory.

## 1.2 Variants of CSMA (The Parent Family)

Before delving into CSMA/CD, it is essential to recognize the three classical CSMA variants:

| Variant | Carrier Sensing | Transmission Strategy | Persistence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1-Persistent CSMA** | Yes | Transmit immediately if idle; if busy, keep sensing until idle | Probability = **1** |
| **Non-Persistent CSMA** | Yes | Wait a random time before re-sensing after a busy channel | Probability = **0** |
| **p-Persistent CSMA** | Yes | Transmit with probability *p*, defer with *(1-p)* | Probability = **p** |

CSMA/CD is fundamentally a **1-Persistent CSMA** augmented with collision **detection** and **backoff** features.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The Darkened Conference Room Analogy:**
> Imagine **10 participants** sitting around a circular table in a **completely dark room** holding a single shared microphone. The room rule is:
> 1. **Listen First (Carrier Sense):** Everyone listens to the room. If someone is already speaking (channel busy), they wait.
> 2. **Start Speaking (Transmit):** When silence is sensed (channel idle), anyone can speak.
> 3. **Listen While Talking (Collision Detection):** While speaking, each person *also* listens to their own voice through the room acoustics. If they sense a **different sound pattern** (collision!), they immediately **shout "STOP! COLLISION!" (Jam Signal)**.
> 4. **Back Off (Retransmission):** Both parties remain silent for a **random duration** chosen from an expanding window before trying again.
>
> This is exactly how CSMA/CD works in Ethernet networks on a **shared coaxial cable / half-duplex bus topology**.

## 1.4 Why Collision Detection Is Essential

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In CSMA (without CD), a station may finish transmitting an entire frame before learning about a collision — wasting the entire frame transmission time. CSMA/CD solves this by **aborting transmission the moment a collision is detected**, drastically improving channel utilization. This requires **half-duplex operation** (cannot transmit and receive simultaneously on the same medium).

## 1.5 Physical Layer Constants (IEEE 802.3)

| Standard | Bandwidth | Max Segment Length | Slot Time | Min Frame Size |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **10BASE-5 / 10BASE-2** | **10 Mbps** | **500 m / 185 m** | **51.2 μs** | **64 bytes** |
| **10BASE-T** | **10 Mbps** | **100 m** | **51.2 μs** | **64 bytes** |
| **100BASE-TX (Fast Ethernet)** | **100 Mbps** | **100 m** | **5.12 μs** | **64 bytes** |
| **1000BASE-T (Gigabit)** | **1 Gbps** | **100 m** | **0.512 μs** | **512 bytes (extended)** |

> **Slot Time** = **512 bit times** = round-trip propagation time for the maximum network span (a critical parameter for CSMA/CD).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

The CSMA/CD protocol operates through a precise state machine. The following steps capture its full lifecycle:

### Phase 1: Carrier Sense (Listen Before Talk)
1. The station has a frame ready to transmit from the Network Layer.
2. The MAC sublayer monitors the **Carrier Sense** signal from the Physical Layer.
3. If the channel is sensed **IDLE** for an **Inter-Frame Gap (IFG)** of **96 bit times**, proceed to Phase 2.
4. If the channel is sensed **BUSY**, the station **defers** its transmission and continues monitoring until the channel becomes idle.

### Phase 2: Frame Transmission
5. The station begins transmitting the frame, bit by bit, onto the shared medium.
6. Simultaneously, the station **monitors the analog signal level** on the medium (this is the "Collision Detection" part).

### Phase 3: Collision Window
7. The station continues to monitor for a duration called the **Collision Window** (approximately **2 × Maximum Propagation Time**, i.e., **Slot Time**).
8. If no collision is detected within this window → **successful transmission** (frame sent completely).

### Phase 4: Collision Handling (If Detected)
9. If voltage on the cable exceeds the **normal signal threshold** (typically **~1.5 V or more** above nominal in 10BASE-5), a collision is declared.
10. The station **immediately aborts** the transmission.
11. The station transmits a **32-bit Jam Signal** (a deliberately garbled pattern) to ensure all other stations also detect the collision.
12. Increment the **Collision Counter** for this frame.

### Phase 5: Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)
13. The station computes a **Random Wait Time**:
    
    $$K = \text{Random}[0, 2^{i} - 1]$$
    
    where *i* is the number of collisions experienced by this frame (**capped at 10**).

14. The station waits for:
    
    $$\text{Backoff Time} = K \times \text{Slot Time}$$
    
15. After waiting, the station returns to **Phase 1** and retries the entire process.
16. After **16 consecutive collisions**, the frame is **discarded** and an error is reported to the upper layer.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> - **Why 2 × Propagation Time (Slot Time)?** Because in the worst case, station A's signal must travel to the farthest station B, and B's signal must travel back to A — this is the **round-trip time**. A must listen for at least this long to be sure no collision occurred.
> - **Why Jam Signal?** Without it, signal attenuation might cause distant stations to miss the collision. The jam signal guarantees all stations detect the corruption.
> - **Why Binary Exponential?** Linear backoff would cause persistent collisions under heavy load; exponential growth ensures the contention window expands geometrically as network congestion increases, providing graceful degradation.

## 2.3 Binary Exponential Backoff — Detailed Schedule

| Collision Number (i) | Wait Slots Range [0, 2^i - 1] | Max Wait Slots |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | [0, 1] | **1** |
| **2** | [0, 3] | **3** |
| **3** | [0, 7] | **7** |
| **4** | [0, 15] | **15** |
| **5** | [0, 31] | **31** |
| **6** | [0, 63] | **63** |
| **7** | [0, 127] | **127** |
| **8** | [0, 255] | **255** |
| **9** | [0, 511] | **511** |
| **10** | [0, 1023] | **1023** |
| **11 – 16** | [0, 1023] | **1023 (CAPPED)** |
| **> 16** | **Frame discarded** | **Error reported** |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Slot Time (τ)** | $\tau = 2 \times T_p$ | $T_p$ = one-way max propagation time | seconds |
| 2 | **Parameter 'a'** | $a = \dfrac{T_p}{T_t}$ | $T_t$ = frame transmission time | dimensionless |
| 3 | **Frame Transmission Time** | $T_t = \dfrac{L}{B}$ | $L$ = frame length, $B$ = bandwidth | seconds |
| 4 | **Propagation Time** | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | $d$ = distance, $v$ = signal velocity (~2×10⁸ m/s in cable) | seconds |
| 5 | **Minimum Frame Size** | $L_{min} = 2 \times T_p \times B$ | Derived from $T_t \geq \tau$ | bits |
| 6 | **CSMA/CD Maximum Theoretical Efficiency** | $E = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2a}$ | Ideal 1-persistent CSMA/CD | fraction (0-1) |
| 7 | **Practical Efficiency (w/ backoff)** | $E \approx \dfrac{1}{1 + 6.44a}$ | Non-slotted, includes BEB effect | fraction (0-1) |
| 8 | **Backoff Time** | $T_{backoff} = K \times \tau$ | $K \in [0, 2^{\min(i,10)} - 1]$ | seconds |
| 9 | **Slot Time in Bit Times (Ethernet)** | $\tau = 512 \text{ bit times}$ | Fixed for 10/100 Mbps Ethernet | bit times |
| 10 | **Jam Signal Length** | $L_{jam} = 32 \text{ bits}$ | IEEE 802.3 mandated | bits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Rule:** For CSMA/CD to function correctly, the following MUST hold:
> 
> $$T_t \geq \tau$$
> 
> That is, the frame must be **at least as long as the slot time** — this guarantees that **any collision will be detected before the transmitting station finishes sending the frame**. This is why Ethernet mandates a **minimum frame size of 64 bytes (512 bits)** for 10/100 Mbps networks.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

CSMA/CD forms the historical foundation of all **Ethernet LANs** and influenced modern protocols:
- **Half-duplex legacy Ethernet hubs** (10BASE-T, 100BASE-TX) used CSMA/CD.
- Modern **full-duplex switched Ethernet** eliminates collisions entirely (no CSMA/CD needed).
- The CSMA/CD concept influenced **Wi-Fi's CSMA/CA** (Collision Avoidance), the dominant wireless MAC protocol.
- Concepts of **slot time, minimum frame size, and exponential backoff** are replicated in **CAN bus** (automotive), **Ethernet AVB**, and **industrial Fieldbus** protocols.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Frame Size for CSMA/CD

### Statement
Derive the relationship between minimum frame size and maximum propagation delay for proper CSMA/CD operation.

### Given
- Bandwidth of the Ethernet = **B** bits/second
- Maximum one-way propagation delay = **T_p** seconds
- Signal propagation speed in cable ≈ **2 × 10⁸ m/s**
- Maximum cable length = **d** meters

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** For CSMA/CD to detect any collision that might occur, the **transmitting station must still be sending data when the collision signal arrives back**.

**Step 2:** The collision signal must travel from the *farthest point* on the cable to the *nearest point* of collision and back. The **worst-case** is when the signal must propagate the **full one-way cable length and return**, giving a **round-trip time of 2 × T_p**.

**Step 3:** Therefore, the **frame transmission time (T_t)** must satisfy:

$$T_t \geq 2 \times T_p$$

**Step 4:** Express frame transmission time in terms of frame length (L bits) and bandwidth (B):

$$T_t = \frac{L}{B}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute and rearrange for the minimum frame size L_min:

$$\frac{L_{min}}{B} \geq 2 \times T_p$$

$$L_{min} \geq 2 \times T_p \times B$$

**Step 6:** Final Result:

$$\boxed{L_{min} = 2 \times T_p \times B}$$

### Worked Example
> **Problem:** A 10 Mbps Ethernet network has a maximum segment length of 2500 m. Signal propagation speed in the cable is 2 × 10⁸ m/s. Calculate the minimum frame size.

**Solution:**

Compute propagation time:

$$T_p = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{2500}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 12.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 12.5 \text{ μs}$$

Apply minimum frame size formula:

$$L_{min} = 2 \times T_p \times B = 2 \times 12.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 10 \times 10^{6}$$

$$L_{min} = 2 \times 12.5 \times 10 = 250 \text{ bits}$$

Convert to bytes:

$$L_{min} = \frac{250}{8} = 31.25 \text{ bytes} \approx 32 \text{ bytes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For the actual IEEE 802.3 standard (10BASE-5 with max segment 500 m), the calculated minimum is 64 bytes — the standard over-provisions to cover repeaters and round-trip through up to 4 repeaters (5 segments), making the effective slot time 51.2 μs (= 512 bit times at 10 Mbps).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Theoretical Efficiency of CSMA/CD

### Statement
Derive the upper bound on channel utilization (efficiency) for 1-persistent CSMA/CD.

### Given
- Frame transmission time = **T_t**
- One-way propagation delay = **T_p**
- Parameter **a** = T_p / T_t (ratio of propagation to transmission)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Consider a **successful frame transmission** on a shared bus with two stations A and B at opposite ends (worst case).

**Step 2:** The total time during which the **channel is occupied** for this single successful transmission is:

$$T_{total} = T_t + T_p$$

Where:
- **T_t** = time to push all bits of the frame onto the cable
- **T_p** = time for the **last bit** to travel to the farthest receiver (otherwise the channel is still "in use" propagating the signal)

**Step 3:** However, in CSMA/CD, a station that wants to transmit must wait for the channel to be sensed idle. In the worst case, it senses the channel just as a frame *begins* from the far end. It must wait for **2 × T_p** before knowing the channel is free.

**Step 4:** On average, the waiting time per transmission is approximately **1.5 × T_p** for 1-persistent CSMA (combining best and worst cases), but the **standard textbook derivation** assumes the **average contention period** consumes additional time proportional to *a*.

**Step 5:** The **efficiency** E is defined as the ratio of **useful transmission time** to **total channel busy time**:

$$E = \frac{\text{Useful transmission time}}{\text{Total channel busy time}}$$

**Step 6:** For CSMA/CD with 1-persistence, the canonical textbook result is:

$$E = \frac{T_t}{T_t + 2T_p}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute a = T_p / T_t, hence T_p = a × T_t:

$$E = \frac{T_t}{T_t + 2 \times a \times T_t} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}$$

**Step 8:** Final Result:

$$\boxed{E_{CSMA/CD} = \frac{1}{1 + 2a}}$$

**Step 9:** Boundary Analysis:

- When **a → 0** (very fast transmission, short cable): E → **1** (100% efficient — the ideal case).
- When **a → ∞** (slow transmission, long cable): E → **0** (channel wasted on propagation).

### Worked Example
> **Problem:** A CSMA/CD network has a = 0.1. Calculate the efficiency. What happens if the cable length is doubled?

**Solution (Case 1 — a = 0.1):**

$$E = \frac{1}{1 + 2(0.1)} = \frac{1}{1.2} = 0.8333 = 83.33\%$$

**Solution (Case 2 — cable doubled → T_p doubled → a = 0.2):**

$$E = \frac{1}{1 + 2(0.2)} = \frac{1}{1.4} = 0.7143 = 71.43\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Doubling the cable length (and hence the propagation delay) **reduces efficiency by ~12 percentage points**. This is why Ethernet cable segments are **strictly limited** in length.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Binary Exponential Backoff Wait Time

### Statement
Compute the maximum and average backoff time for the *i*-th collision attempt.

### Given
- Collision counter *i* (1-indexed)
- Slot time **τ**
- *i* is capped at **10**

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** After *i* collisions, the contention window size is:

$$W_i = 2^{\min(i, 10)} - 1$$

**Step 2:** The station picks an integer K uniformly at random:

$$K \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, W_i\}$$

**Step 3:** The backoff time is:

$$T_{backoff}(i) = K \times \tau$$

**Step 4:** The **average** backoff time is:

$$\overline{T_{backoff}(i)} = \frac{W_i}{2} \times \tau = \frac{2^{\min(i,10)} - 1}{2} \times \tau$$

### Worked Example
> **Problem:** A station experiences its 3rd collision on a 10 Mbps Ethernet. Compute the maximum and average backoff times. (τ = 51.2 μs)

**Solution:**

For i = 3:

$$W_3 = 2^3 - 1 = 7$$

So K ∈ {0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7}.

**Maximum backoff time:**

$$T_{backoff}^{max} = 7 \times 51.2 \text{ μs} = 358.4 \text{ μs}$$

**Average backoff time:**

$$\overline{T_{backoff}} = \frac{7}{2} \times 51.2 \text{ μs} = 3.5 \times 51.2 \text{ μs} = 179.2 \text{ μs}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameter 'a' on Efficiency

| a = T_p / T_t | E = 1/(1+2a) | Efficiency (%) | Network Scenario |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.01 | 0.9804 | 98.04% | Tiny LAN, large frames |
| 0.1 | 0.8333 | 83.33% | Typical Ethernet |
| 0.5 | 0.5000 | 50.00% | Long cable, small frames |
| 1.0 | 0.3333 | 33.33% | Saturation point |
| 5.0 | 0.0909 | 9.09% | Unusable — too long a cable / too small frames |
| 10.0 | 0.0476 | 4.76% | Catastrophic — protocol collapses |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CSMA/CD Operation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1([Station Has Frame to Send]) --> A2{Channel Idle?}
    A2 -- No --> A3[Defer / Wait for Channel to be Idle]
    A3 --> A2
    A2 -- Yes --> A4[Wait for Inter-Frame Gap 96 bit times]
    A4 --> A5[Start Frame Transmission]
    A5 --> A6{Monitor Channel for Collision Signal}
    A6 -- No Collision Detected --> A7[Frame Transmission Complete]
    A7 --> A8([Transmission Successful])
    A6 -- Collision Detected --> A9[Abort Transmission Immediately]
    A9 --> A10[Transmit 32 bit Jam Signal]
    A10 --> A11[Increment Collision Counter i]
    A11 --> A12{i greater than 16?}
    A12 -- Yes --> A13[Discard Frame and Report Error to Upper Layer]
    A13 --> A14([Failure: Too Many Collisions])
    A12 -- No --> A15[Compute K = Random 0 to 2 power min i 10 minus 1]
    A15 --> A16[Wait Backoff Time equals K times Slot Time]
    A16 --> A2
```

## 4.2 CSMA/CD Functional Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USER[Upper Layer Application]
        U1[Network Layer Data Packet]
    end
    subgraph MAC[MAC Sublayer CSMA CD Controller]
        B1[Frame Builder] --> B2[Carrier Sense Module]
        B2 --> B3[Collision Detection Module]
        B3 --> B4[Jam Signal Generator]
        B4 --> B5[Backoff Controller BEB]
        B5 --> B2
    end
    subgraph PHY[Physical Layer Transceiver]
        P1[Bit Stream Encoder] --> P2[Signal Driver to Cable]
        P2 --> P3[Collision Voltage Detector]
        P3 --> B3
    end
    subgraph MED[Shared Transmission Medium]
        M1[Coaxial Cable or Half Duplex Twisted Pair Bus]
    end
    U1 --> B1
    P2 --> M1
    M1 --> P3
```

## 4.3 Collision Detection Timeline — Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Station A
    participant B as Station B
    participant Cable as Shared Cable
    A ->> Cable: Begin frame at t=0
    Note over A,Cable: A senses idle and starts transmission
    B ->> Cable: Begin frame at t=Tp/2
    Note over B,Cable: B also senses idle and starts transmitting
    Cable -->> A: Collision signal returns at t=2Tp
    Note over A: A detects voltage anomaly and aborts
    A ->> Cable: Transmit Jam Signal
    Cable -->> B: Jam Signal received at t=2Tp+Tp/2
    Note over B: B aborts transmission
    Note over A,B: Both invoke Binary Exponential Backoff
    A ->> A: Wait K1 x Slot Time
    B ->> B: Wait K2 x Slot Time
```

## 4.4 Binary Exponential Backoff Window Growth Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Collision 1 Range 0 to 1] --> C2[Collision 2 Range 0 to 3]
    C2 --> C3[Collision 3 Range 0 to 7]
    C3 --> C4[Collision 4 Range 0 to 15]
    C4 --> C5[Collision 5 Range 0 to 31]
    C5 --> C6[Collision 6 Range 0 to 63]
    C6 --> C7[Collision 7 Range 0 to 127]
    C7 --> C8[Collision 8 Range 0 to 255]
    C8 --> C9[Collision 9 Range 0 to 511]
    C9 --> C10[Collision 10 Range 0 to 1023]
    C10 --> C11[Collision 11 to 16 Capped at 0 to 1023]
    C11 --> C12[Collision greater than 16 Frame Discarded]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> Define CSMA/CD. Why is collision detection important in CSMA/CD? Mention the role of the Jam Signal.

**Model Answer:**

CSMA/CD stands for **Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection**. It is a contention-based MAC protocol used in IEEE 802.3 Ethernet networks where multiple stations share a common transmission medium.

**Why Collision Detection is important:**
- In pure CSMA, after a station starts transmitting, it does **not know if a collision occurred** until the entire frame is sent — wasting bandwidth.
- In CSMA/CD, the station **continuously monitors the signal level on the cable** while transmitting. If the voltage exceeds the normal threshold (indicating another station is also transmitting), a **collision is detected**.
- The station **immediately aborts** transmission, thus saving channel time and improving utilization.

**Role of Jam Signal:**
After detecting a collision, the station transmits a **32-bit Jam Signal** — a deliberately garbled bit pattern. Its purpose is to:
1. **Reinforce the collision** so all other stations (including those far from the collision point) detect it.
2. Compensate for **signal attenuation** over the cable, ensuring the collision is detected network-wide.

*(Marks Distribution: Definition – 1, Importance – 1, Jam Signal – 1)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> What is Binary Exponential Backoff in CSMA/CD? Why is it called "exponential"?

**Model Answer:**

Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB) is the **retransmission scheduling algorithm** used in CSMA/CD after a station detects a collision.

**Algorithm:**
- After the *i*-th collision, the station selects a random integer **K** from the range **[0, 2^i − 1]**.
- The station waits for **K × Slot Time** before attempting retransmission.
- The range is **capped at [0, 1023]** after the 10th collision.
- If **16 consecutive collisions** occur, the frame is discarded.

**Why "Exponential":**
- The wait window **doubles with each successive collision** (1, 3, 7, 15, 31, … 1023).
- This **geometric growth** ensures that under heavy load, the contention window expands rapidly, reducing the probability of repeated collisions.

**Why "Binary":**
- Because the slot time is divided into discrete binary-multiples — K is an integer chosen from a power-of-two range.

*(Marks Distribution: Algorithm – 1, Exponential reason – 1, Cap & discard rule – 1)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Jam Signal** — Examiners deduct up to **1 mark** if you skip the jam signal phase.
> 2. **Confusing CSMA/CD with CSMA/CA** — CSMA/CD is used in **wired Ethernet**; CSMA/CA is for **Wi-Fi**. Mentioning CSMA/CA in a CSMA/CD answer is a critical error.
> 3. **Skipping the Minimum Frame Size reasoning** — Any efficiency / numerical question in KTU always expects you to **state the relationship T_t ≥ 2T_p first**, then solve.
> 4. **Not mentioning Slot Time = 51.2 μs** for 10 Mbps Ethernet — a frequently asked standard value.
> 5. **Binary vs Linear Backoff confusion** — Clearly distinguish the formula `K ∈ [0, 2^i − 1]` from linear backoff `K = i`.

---

### **Choice A — Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**
> Explain the working of CSMA/CD protocol with the help of a neat flowchart. Discuss the role of Binary Exponential Backoff in detail.

**Model Answer:**

**CSMA/CD Working:**

CSMA/CD stands for Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection. It is the MAC protocol used in IEEE 802.3 Ethernet networks. The protocol executes the following steps:

1. **Carrier Sense:** The station senses the channel. If idle, it proceeds; if busy, it defers.

2. **Inter-Frame Gap (IFG):** When the channel becomes idle, the station waits for 96 bit times (IFG) before transmitting.

3. **Transmission begins:** The station starts sending the frame bit-by-bit.

4. **Collision Detection:** While transmitting, the station monitors the voltage on the cable. In 10BASE-T, normal signal voltage is approximately ±1 V; a collision produces voltage swings of ~1.5 V or more.

5. **Collision Response:** If a collision is detected:
   - The station immediately **aborts** the transmission.
   - It transmits a **32-bit Jam Signal** to ensure all stations detect the collision.
   - It **increments the collision counter**.

6. **Binary Exponential Backoff:** The station waits for a random time determined by:

   $$K \times \text{Slot Time}, \quad K \in [0, 2^{\min(i,10)} - 1]$$

7. **Retry or Discard:** After waiting, the station re-attempts transmission. If 16 consecutive collisions occur, the frame is discarded.

**Role of Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB):**

- **Problem solved:** Prevents stations from repeatedly colliding by forcing them to wait for randomized intervals.

- **Adaptive behavior:** The wait window **grows exponentially** (1, 3, 7, 15, 31, … 1023) with each collision — giving higher backoff values when contention is high.

- **Fairness:** The random selection ensures that two colliding stations have a high probability of choosing different K values, reducing the chance of repeated collisions.

- **Upper cap at 1023:** Prevents indefinite waiting and ensures predictable network behavior.

- **Discard after 16 attempts:** Prevents a station from monopolizing resources under extreme congestion.

**Flowchart:** (Refer to Section 4.1 above)

*(Marks Distribution: Steps – 3, Collision handling – 2, BEB explanation – 2)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**
> A CSMA/CD network operates at 10 Mbps over a 1 km cable. The signal propagation speed is 2 × 10⁸ m/s. The minimum frame size is 64 bytes. Determine:
> (i) One-way propagation delay
> (ii) Slot Time
> (iii) Verify whether the minimum frame size satisfies the CSMA/CD requirement
> (iv) Calculate the efficiency of the network.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Bandwidth B = 10 Mbps = 10 × 10⁶ bps
- Cable length d = 1 km = 1000 m
- Signal velocity v = 2 × 10⁸ m/s
- Minimum frame size L_min = 64 bytes = 64 × 8 = 512 bits

**Step 1: One-way propagation delay (T_p):**

$$T_p = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{1000}{2 \times 10^8} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 5 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 2: Slot Time (τ):**

$$\tau = 2 \times T_p = 2 \times 5 \text{ μs} = 10 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 3: Verify minimum frame size requirement:**

Frame transmission time for minimum frame:

$$T_t = \frac{L_{min}}{B} = \frac{512 \text{ bits}}{10 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 51.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 51.2 \text{ μs}$$

CSMA/CD requires T_t ≥ 2 × T_p, i.e., T_t ≥ τ:

$$T_t = 51.2 \text{ μs} \geq \tau = 10 \text{ μs} \quad ✓ \text{ (Satisfied)}$$

**Step 4: Calculate parameter a:**

$$a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{5 \text{ μs}}{51.2 \text{ μs}} = 0.0977 \approx 0.1$$

**Step 5: Efficiency:**

$$E = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 2(0.1)} = \frac{1}{1.2} = 0.8333$$

$$\boxed{E = 83.33\%}$$

*(Marks Distribution: T_p – 1.5, τ – 1, Verification – 1.5, Efficiency – 3)*

---

### **Choice B — Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative to Choice A)*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> Compare Pure ALOHA, Slotted ALOHA, and CSMA/CD. Explain why CSMA/CD is preferred over ALOHA-based protocols for wired LANs.

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Pure ALOHA | Slotted ALOHA | CSMA/CD |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Carrier Sense** | No | No | **Yes (before & during)** |
| **Time Synchronization** | Not required | Required | Not required |
| **Channel Access** | Transmit immediately | Transmit at slot boundary | Sense then transmit |
| **Collision Detection** | No (only via no ACK) | No (only via no ACK) | **Yes (during transmission)** |
| **Collision Handling** | Wait random time | Wait random time | **Abort + Jam + BEB** |
| **Maximum Efficiency** | 18.4% | 36.8% | **~80–90%** |
| **Throughput** | 0.184 G | 0.368 G | ~1 G / (1 + 2a) |
| **Minimum Frame Size** | No restriction | No restriction | **Mandatory 64 bytes** |
| **Domain** | Satellite, Radio | Satellite, Radio | **Wired Ethernet LANs** |

**Derivation of Pure ALOHA Throughput:**
Let G = mean number of transmission attempts per frame time. Vulnerable period = 2 T_t.

$$S = G \cdot e^{-2G} \quad \Rightarrow \quad S_{max} = \frac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184$$

**Derivation of Slotted ALOHA Throughput:**
Vulnerable period = T_t (only the current slot):

$$S = G \cdot e^{-G} \quad \Rightarrow \quad S_{max} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$$

**Why CSMA/CD is Preferred for Wired LANs:**

1. **Higher Efficiency:** CSMA/CD achieves up to **80–90%** utilization, vastly superior to ALOHA's 18.4% / 36.8%.

2. **Faster Collision Resolution:** Stations **detect and abort** collisions within microseconds rather than waiting for the entire frame transmission — saving bandwidth.

3. **Feasibility of Collision Detection:** Wired media (coaxial/twisted pair) allow **easy voltage-level monitoring**, making collision detection straightforward. (In wireless, this is impossible due to hidden terminal problem → hence CSMA/CA is used instead.)

4. **Predictable Performance:** Deterministic minimum frame size and slot time enable **bounded delay analysis**.

5. **Cost-Effective for LANs:** Ethernet hardware implementing CSMA/CD is cheap, well-understood, and globally standardized (IEEE 802.3).

**Limitation:** CSMA/CD requires a **shared medium**, hence is **unsuitable for switched full-duplex Ethernet** (which uses point-to-point links with no collisions).

*(Marks Distribution: Table comparison – 3, ALOHA derivations – 2, CSMA/CD advantages – 2)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> A 10 Mbps Ethernet network uses CSMA/CD. The maximum cable length is 500 m and the signal propagation speed is 2 × 10⁸ m/s. Frames are uniformly 1500 bytes long.
> Calculate:
> (i) Propagation delay for the maximum segment
> (ii) Transmission time of a frame
> (iii) The parameter 'a'
> (iv) The maximum theoretical throughput
> (v) What happens to efficiency if frames are halved in size?

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- B = 10 Mbps = 10 × 10⁶ bps
- d_max = 500 m
- v = 2 × 10⁸ m/s
- Frame length L = 1500 bytes = 1500 × 8 = 12,000 bits

**Step 1: Propagation delay T_p:**

$$T_p = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{500}{2 \times 10^8} = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 2.5 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 2: Frame transmission time T_t:**

$$T_t = \frac{L}{B} = \frac{12{,}000 \text{ bits}}{10 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1200 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 3: Parameter 'a':**

$$a = \frac{T_p}{T_t} = \frac{2.5 \text{ μs}}{1200 \text{ μs}} = 2.083 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.00208$$

**Step 4: Maximum Theoretical Throughput (Efficiency):**

$$E = \frac{1}{1 + 2a} = \frac{1}{1 + 2(0.00208)} = \frac{1}{1.00417} \approx 0.9958$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 99.58\%}$$

**Maximum throughput in bps:**

$$\text{Throughput} = E \times B = 0.9958 \times 10 \text{ Mbps} \approx 9.958 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 5: Efficiency when frame size is halved (L_new = 750 bytes = 6000 bits):**

New transmission time:

$$T_t^{new} = \frac{6000}{10 \times 10^6} = 600 \text{ μs}$$

New parameter a:

$$a_{new} = \frac{2.5 \text{ μs}}{600 \text{ μs}} = 4.167 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.00417$$

New efficiency:

$$E_{new} = \frac{1}{1 + 2(0.00417)} = \frac{1}{1.00833} \approx 0.9917 \approx 99.17\%$$

**Observation:**

$$\Delta E = 99.58\% - 99.17\% = 0.41\% \text{ drop}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Even though the frame size is halved, efficiency drops only marginally because *a* is already very small. However, if the cable were longer or frames were smaller, the drop would be more dramatic. **In CSMA/CD, larger frames and shorter cables yield higher efficiency.**

*(Marks Distribution: T_p – 1, T_t – 1.5, a – 1, E – 2, Comparison – 1.5)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — CSMA/CD (Module 2):**

- ⭐ **Full Form:** **Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection** — IEEE 802.3 Ethernet MAC protocol.

- ⭐ **Three-Step Rule:** **Sense → Transmit → Detect.** Always **listen before AND during** transmission.

- ⭐ **1-Persistent CSMA** is the base; CD is the augmentation.

- ⭐ **Slot Time (τ) = 2 × T_p = 51.2 μs** for 10 Mbps Ethernet (= 512 bit times).

- ⭐ **Minimum Frame Size = 64 bytes (512 bits)** for 10/100 Mbps Ethernet — mandatory to ensure **T_t ≥ τ**.

- ⭐ **Jam Signal = 32 bits** — transmitted after collision detection to reinforce it across the entire network.

- ⭐ **Binary Exponential Backoff:** Wait = **K × τ**, where **K ∈ [0, 2^min(i,10) − 1]**.

- ⭐ **16 consecutive collisions → frame is discarded** (error reported to upper layer).

- ⭐ **Efficiency formula:** E = **1 / (1 + 2a)**, where a = **T_p / T_t**.

- ⭐ **Boundary:** E → 1 as a → 0; E → 0 as a → ∞.

- ⭐ **Larger frames + shorter cables = higher efficiency.**

- ⭐ **Half-duplex only** — collision detection requires the station to **listen to its own transmission**, which is impossible with simultaneous transmit/receive (full-duplex).

- ⭐ **CSMA/CD vs CSMA/CA:** CD = Collision **Detection** (wired, Ethernet); CA = Collision **Avoidance** (wireless, Wi-Fi 802.11).

- ⭐ **Modern Ethernet is full-duplex switched → no collisions → CSMA/CD not used**, but the protocol remains historically and pedagogically critical for KTU exams.

- ⭐ **Real-world analogy:** Polite conversation in a dark room where speakers listen to others and interrupt themselves upon hearing overlapping speech.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logical Link Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT414 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Logical Link Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Logical Link Control (LLC)** is the upper sublayer of the **Data Link Layer (DLL)** in the OSI reference model, formally defined by the **IEEE 802.2 standard**. It operates as a *protocol-independent* interface between the **Medium Access Control (MAC)** sublayer (below) and the **Network Layer** (above). The primary purpose of LLC is to provide a uniform set of services to the Network Layer irrespective of the underlying LAN technology (Ethernet, Token Ring, Wi-Fi, etc.), thereby achieving **media independence** and **protocol transparency**.

The LLC sublayer performs three principal functions:
1. **Multiplexing/Demultiplexing** of upper-layer protocols using **Service Access Point (SAP)** addresses.
2. **Flow Control** between sender and receiver to prevent buffer overflow.
3. **Error Control & Recovery** through acknowledgment mechanisms and retransmission.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** LLC sits between the **Network Layer** and the **MAC sublayer**, providing three types of services — **LLC Type 1 (Unacknowledged Connectionless)**, **LLC Type 2 (Connection-Oriented)**, and **LLC Type 3 (Acknowledged Connectionless)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Apartment Building Receptionist

Imagine the Data Link Layer as a **giant apartment building** containing hundreds of tenants (Network Layer protocols like IP, ARP, IPX). The **MAC sublayer** is the *building's main gate* — it controls *who* enters the building (which physical node), but does **not** know *which apartment* the visitor wants.

The **LLC sublayer** acts as the **building's receptionist**:
- The receptionist looks at the **apartment number** written on the incoming letter (the **DSAP — Destination Service Access Point**).
- The receptionist matches it to the correct **resident's mailbox** (the correct upper-layer protocol).
- The receptionist also notes the **return apartment number** (the **SSAP — Source Service Access Point**) so the reply knows where to go back.
- For important deliveries, the receptionist keeps a **delivery receipt log** (acknowledgment-based error control) and a **"wait, mailbox full"** notice (flow control).

Thus, LLC is the **traffic-cop of logical addressing** above the physical/MAC layer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Box:**
> - **SAP (Service Access Point):** A logical address used by LLC to identify a Network Layer protocol (e.g., SAP `0x06` = TCP/IP, SAP `0x42` = Spanning Tree BPDU).
> - **PDU (Protocol Data Unit):** The data unit handled at a specific layer. At LLC, the PDU is called an **LLC Frame** or **LLC PDU**.
> - **DSAP/SSAP:** Destination and Source Service Access Point fields inside the LLC header.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **IEEE 802.2 Standard** defines LLC (published **1985**, revised multiple times).
- **LLC Header Length:** Variable — minimum **3 bytes**, maximum **4 bytes** (when an Information field is present).
- **SAP Address Width:** **8 bits (1 byte)**.
- **Control Field Width:** **1 or 2 bytes** (depending on frame type).
- **Standard Frame Types (in IEEE 802.2):** **I-frames** (Information), **S-frames** (Supervisory), **U-frames** (Unnumbered).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Position of LLC in the Layered Architecture

LLC operates in a **hybrid position** — it belongs to the **Data Link Layer (Layer 2)** of the OSI model, but logically extends upward to provide services to the **Network Layer (Layer 3)**.

| Layer | Sub-Layer | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 3 (Network) | – | Routing, Logical Addressing (IP) |
| **2 (Data Link)** | **LLC (Logical Link Control)** | **SAP addressing, Flow/Error Control** |
| 2 (Data Link) | MAC (Medium Access Control) | Framing, MAC addressing, Media Access |
| 1 (Physical) | – | Bit transmission, Electrical signaling |

## 2.2 The Three LLC Service Modes

The IEEE 802.2 standard defines **three distinct service classes**, often examined in KTU papers:

### 2.2.1 LLC Type 1 — Unacknowledged Connectionless Service
- **Mode:** Connectionless, no ACKs, no flow control.
- **Use Case:** Best-effort delivery, used in standard **Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)**.
- **Real-World Utility:** Like dropping a postcard in a mailbox — you trust the system but do not expect confirmation.
- **Operations:** `DL-UNITDATA.request` / `DL-UNITDATA.indication`.

### 2.2.2 LLC Type 2 — Connection-Oriented Service
- **Mode:** Connection establishment → Data transfer → Connection termination.
- **Use Case:** Used in **IEEE 802.5 (Token Ring)** and **IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi)** for reliable links.
- **Features:** **Sliding window flow control**, **Sequence numbering**, **ACK/NAK**, **Retransmission**.
- **Real-World Utility:** Like a registered post with tracking — sender gets confirmation at every step.

### 2.2.3 LLC Type 3 — Acknowledged Connectionless Service
- **Mode:** Connectionless, but each frame is acknowledged.
- **Use Case:** Used in **automated factory networks (MAP — Manufacturing Automation Protocol)**, **IEEE 802.4 (Token Bus)**.
- **Features:** Every transmitted LLC PDU is acknowledged via a special **Acknowledge PDU**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Most modern Ethernet uses LLC Type 1. LLC Type 2 is conceptually derived from **HDLC (High-Level Data Link Control)**.

## 2.3 LLC Service Access Points (SAPs)

A **SAP** is a logical gateway through which an upper-layer protocol requests LLC services. It is identified by an **8-bit address**:

- **DSAP (Destination SAP):** Identifies the receiving upper-layer protocol.
- **SSAP (Source SAP):** Identifies the sending upper-layer protocol.
- The **least significant bit (LSB)** of DSAP indicates whether it is an *individual* (0) or *group* address (1).
- The **least significant bit (LSB)** of SSAP indicates whether the PDU is a *command* (0) or *response* (1).

| SAP Value (Hex) | Protocol / Service |
| :--- | :--- |
| `0x06` | TCP/IP (ARP) |
| `0x42` | Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) BPDU |
| `0xAA` | SNAP (Sub-Network Access Protocol) — used with Ethernet II |
| `0xE0` | Novell IPX |
| `0xFE` | ISO Network Layer (CLNP) |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Reference Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Typical Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **LLC Header Min** | DSAP + SSAP + Control | **3 bytes** |
| **LLC Header Max** | DSAP + SSAP + Control + (Info) | **4 bytes (for U-frames)** |
| **N(S)** | Send Sequence Number | Modulo **128** (for extended) or **8** (basic) |
| **N(R)** | Receive Sequence Number | Modulo **128** or **8** |
| **LLC PDU** | LLC Protocol Data Unit | Variable (carries upper-layer data) |
| **HDLC** | High-Level Data Link Control | Bit-oriented, basis for LLC Type 2 |
| **SAP Width** | Service Access Point address | **8 bits (1 byte)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** LLC is foundational in **LAN switching**, **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**, and **automotive networks (CAN, FlexRay)** where deterministic, reliable link-layer communication is critical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 LLC PDU (Protocol Data Unit) Format — Exhaustive Derivation

The **LLC PDU** is constructed by the LLC sublayer by taking the **Network Layer packet** (the SDU — Service Data Unit) and prepending a **3-byte or 4-byte header**. The complete frame handed to the MAC sublayer looks like this:

$$
\text{LLC PDU} = \underbrace{\text{DSAP} \;\; \vert \;\; \text{SSAP}}_{\text{1 byte each}} \;\; \vert \;\; \underbrace{\text{Control}}_{\text{1 or 2 bytes}} \;\; \vert \;\; \underbrace{\text{Information}}_{\text{Variable (N bytes)}}
$$

### 3.1.1 Field-by-Field Analysis

**Step 1 — Destination Service Access Point (DSAP) — 1 byte:**
The DSAP field identifies the **upper-layer protocol** at the receiving station that should consume the information payload.

- **Bit 7 (MSB):** Reserved; set to 0.
- **Bit 6:** I/G bit — *0 = Individual address*, *1 = Group address*.
- **Bits 5–0:** The actual 6-bit SAP address.

Mathematically, the DSAP can be expressed as:

$$
\text{DSAP}_{\text{decoded}} = \text{DSAP}_{\text{byte}} \;\; \text{AND} \;\; 0\text{x}3F
$$

**Step 2 — Source Service Access Point (SSAP) — 1 byte:**
Identifies the upper-layer protocol at the *sending* station that originated the PDU.

- **Bit 7 (MSB):** Reserved; set to 0.
- **Bit 6:** C/R bit — *0 = Command*, *1 = Response*.
- **Bits 5–0:** The actual 6-bit SAP address of the sender.

Mathematically:

$$
\text{SSAP}_{\text{decoded}} = \text{SSAP}_{\text{byte}} \;\; \text{AND} \;\; 0\text{x}3F
$$

**Step 3 — Control Field — 1 or 2 bytes:**
The Control field determines the **frame type** and contains **sequence numbers** and **function codes**. It directly inherits the structure of **HDLC** control fields.

**Step 4 — Information Field (Variable length):**
Carries the actual upper-layer data (e.g., an IP packet). It is present in **I-frames** and certain **U-frames**, but absent in **S-frames**.

## 3.2 The Three LLC Frame Types (Detailed)

### 3.2.1 I-Frame (Information Frame)

Used in **LLC Type 2 (connection-oriented)** service to carry user data. It has a **2-byte control field** in the *extended* format:

$$
\text{I-frame Control} = \underbrace{0}_{\text{bit 15 (start flag)}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{N(S)}}_{\text{bits 14–8, 7 bits}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{P/F}}_{\text{bit 7}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{N(R)}}_{\text{bits 6–0, 7 bits}}
$$

- **N(S):** Send sequence number (modulo 128).
- **N(R):** Receive sequence number (modulo 128) — piggybacked acknowledgment.
- **P/F:** Poll/Final bit — set to 1 by sender to solicit ACK, set to 1 by receiver to indicate final frame.

### 3.2.2 S-Frame (Supervisory Frame)

Used for **flow control and error recovery** (no user data). It also has a **2-byte control field**:

$$
\text{S-frame Control} = \underbrace{10}_{\text{bits 15–14 (type)}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{SS}}_{\text{bits 13–12 (supervisory function)}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{P/F}}_{\text{bit 11}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{N(R)}}_{\text{bits 10–0, 7 bits}}
$$

The **2-bit SS field** specifies the supervisory function:

| SS Value | Frame Type | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `00` | **RR** (Receive Ready) | ACK; ready to receive next |
| `01` | **RNR** (Receive Not Ready) | ACK; temporarily cannot receive (flow control) |
| `10` | **REJ** (Reject) | NAK; retransmit from N(R) |
| `11` | **SREJ** (Selective Reject) | NAK; retransmit only frame N(R) |

### 3.2.3 U-Frame (Unnumbered Frame)

Used for **link establishment, termination, and miscellaneous control**. It has a **1-byte control field** typically (or 2 bytes for extended):

$$
\text{U-frame Control} = \underbrace{11}_{\text{bits 7–6 (type)}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{MM}}_{\text{bits 5–4 (modifier)}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{P/F}}_{\text{bit 3}} \;\; \underbrace{\text{MM}}_{\text{bits 2–0 (modifier)}}
$$

Common U-frame commands:

| Command | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **SABME** | Set Async Balanced Mode Extended (connect request) |
| **DISC** | Disconnect |
| **UA** | Unnumbered Acknowledgment |
| **DM** | Disconnect Mode |
| **FRMR** | Frame Reject |
| **XID** | Exchange Identification |

## 3.3 Connection Establishment & Termination (LLC Type 2)

The three-way handshake for LLC Type 2 connection setup is:

$$
\text{Sender} \xrightarrow{\text{SABME, P=1}} \text{Receiver}
$$

$$
\text{Receiver} \xrightarrow{\text{UA, F=1}} \text{Sender}
$$

Connection termination:

$$
\text{Initiator} \xrightarrow{\text{DISC, P=1}} \text{Peer}
$$

$$
\text{Peer} \xrightarrow{\text{UA, F=1}} \text{Initiator}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of LLC Services

| Feature | LLC Type 1 | LLC Type 2 | LLC Type 3 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Connection | None | Connection-oriented | Connectionless |
| ACK | No | Yes (piggybacked + explicit) | Yes (dedicated ACK PDU) |
| Flow Control | No | Yes (sliding window) | No |
| Error Recovery | No | Yes (retransmit) | No (just notify) |
| Sequencing | No | Yes | No |
| Typical Use | Ethernet | Token Ring, Wi-Fi | Manufacturing (MAP) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Position of LLC in the Data Link Layer

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates how the LLC sublayer sits between the Network Layer and the MAC sublayer, providing protocol-independent services to the upper layer.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NL["LAYER 3 NETWORK LAYER"]
        IP["IP Packet SDU"]
        ARP["ARP Packet SDU"]
        IPX["IPX Packet SDU"]
    end

    subgraph LLC["LAYER 2A LOGICAL LINK CONTROL LLC IEEE 802.2"]
        SAPMUX["SAP Multiplexer and Demultiplexer"]
        SAPMUX --> FC["Flow Control Module"]
        FC --> EC["Error Control Module"]
        EC --> LPDU["LLC PDU Header DSAP SSAP CTRL INFO"]
    end

    subgraph MAC["LAYER 2B MAC SUBLAYER IEEE 802.3 802.5 802.11"]
        MACFRM["MAC Frame Assembly with MAC Address"]
    end

    subgraph PHY["LAYER 1 PHYSICAL LAYER"]
        BITS["Bit Stream on the Medium"]
    end

    IP --> SAPMUX
    ARP --> SAPMUX
    IPX --> SAPMUX
    LPDU --> MACFRM
    MACFRM --> BITS
```

## 4.2 LLC PDU Frame Structure

This diagram details the **bit-level layout** of an LLC PDU as defined in IEEE 802.2, showing the field boundaries and byte-level organization.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LLCPDU["LLC PDU STRUCTURE"]
        A["BYTE 1 DSAP DESTINATION SERVICE ACCESS POINT 8 BITS"]
        B["BYTE 2 SSAP SOURCE SERVICE ACCESS POINT 8 BITS"]
        C["BYTE 3 OR BYTES 3 4 CONTROL FIELD 1 OR 2 BYTES"]
        D["BYTES 4 ONWARDS OR 5 ONWARDS INFORMATION FIELD VARIABLE LENGTH NETWORK LAYER DATA"]
    end

    A --> B --> C --> D

    subgraph CTRL_DETAIL["CONTROL FIELD DETAIL FOR I FRAME EXTENDED"]
        C1["BIT 15 0 IDENTIFIES I FRAME"]
        C2["BITS 14 TO 8 N S SEND SEQUENCE NUMBER MODULO 128"]
        C3["BIT 7 P F POLL OR FINAL BIT"]
        C4["BITS 6 TO 0 N R RECEIVE SEQUENCE NUMBER MODULO 128"]
    end

    C -.-> C1
    C1 -.-> C2
    C2 -.-> C3
    C3 -.-> C4
```

## 4.3 LLC Type 2 Connection Lifecycle (State Machine)

The Mermaid state diagram below traces the connection-oriented lifecycle of an LLC Type 2 link — from initial disconnection, through setup, data transfer, and teardown.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> LOGICALLY_DISCONNECTED
    LOGICALLY_DISCONNECTED --> CONNECT_INITIATED : SABME P 1 sent
    CONNECT_INITIATED --> CONNECTED : UA F 1 received
    LOGICALLY_DISCONNECTED --> CONNECTED : UA F 1 received peer initiated

    CONNECTED --> DATA_TRANSFER : I frames flow with N S and N R
    DATA_TRANSFER --> CONNECTED : RR S frame ACK

    CONNECTED --> DISCONNECT_INITIATED : DISC P 1 sent
    DISCONNECT_INITIATED --> LOGICALLY_DISCONNECTED : UA F 1 received
    CONNECTED --> LOGICALLY_DISCONNECTED : DM received
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
>
> **Q1. Define the Logical Link Control (LLC) sublayer. List the three service classes defined by IEEE 802.2.**

**Model Answer:**
The Logical Link Control (LLC) is the upper sublayer of the Data Link Layer, defined by the **IEEE 802.2 standard**. It provides **protocol-independent services** (multiplexing, flow control, error control) to the Network Layer, independent of the underlying MAC technology.

The three service classes are:
1. **LLC Type 1** — Unacknowledged Connectionless Service.
2. **LLC Type 2** — Connection-Oriented Service.
3. **LLC Type 3** — Acknowledged Connectionless Service.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q2. What is a Service Access Point (SAP)? How is it used in the LLC sublayer?**

**Model Answer:**
A **Service Access Point (SAP)** is an **8-bit logical address** used by the LLC sublayer to identify a specific upper-layer protocol (e.g., IP, IPX) at a given node. The LLC header contains two SAP fields:
- **DSAP (Destination SAP):** Identifies the receiving upper-layer protocol.
- **SSAP (Source SAP):** Identifies the sending upper-layer protocol.

SAP enables LLC to **multiplex and demultiplex** multiple upper-layer protocols over a single physical link (e.g., SAP `0x06` denotes ARP, SAP `0xAA` denotes SNAP). The LSB of the DSAP/SSAP byte carries the I/G and C/R bits respectively.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Module 2 Internal Choice]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q (a).** Explain the architecture of the IEEE 802.2 Logical Link Control sublayer in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the LLC PDU format. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **IEEE 802.2 LLC sublayer** sits between the MAC sublayer and the Network Layer. Its purpose is to provide **media-independent** services to upper layers.

**Functions of LLC:**
1. **Multiplexing/Demultiplexing** of multiple network protocols using SAP addresses.
2. **Flow Control** — using the P/F bit in Type 2 service.
3. **Error Detection and Recovery** — using sequence numbers N(S) and N(R).
4. **Connection Management** — SABME, DISC, UA commands.

**LLC PDU Format (refer SECTION 4.2):**
| Field | Size | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| DSAP | 1 byte | Destination upper-layer protocol ID |
| SSAP | 1 byte | Source upper-layer protocol ID |
| Control | 1 or 2 bytes | Frame type + sequence numbers |
| Information | Variable | Upper-layer data |

The DSAP/SSAP LSB carries the **I/G** and **C/R** bits for group/individual addressing and command/response distinction.

**Q (b).** Discuss the three LLC service types (LLC Type 1, Type 2, Type 3) with examples and suitable diagrams. Compare their features. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

(See **Section 2.2** and **Section 3.4** for the complete comparison matrix.)

- **LLC Type 1 (Unacknowledged Connectionless):** Used in standard Ethernet. No ACK, no flow control, no sequencing. Best-effort delivery.
- **LLC Type 2 (Connection-Oriented):** Used in Token Ring. Three-way handshake (SABME → UA → I-frames). Provides flow control (RR/RNR) and error recovery (REJ/SREJ).
- **LLC Type 3 (Acknowledged Connectionless):** Used in factory automation. Each frame is acknowledged with a dedicated ACK PDU.

**Comparison Table (as drawn in Section 3.4):** Type 2 is the most reliable, Type 1 the fastest, Type 3 a compromise suited for real-time industrial networks.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**Q (a).** With a suitable diagram, explain the three LLC frame types (I-frame, S-frame, U-frame). Describe the use of the Control field in each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Control field** identifies the LLC frame type and carries sequence information.

| Frame Type | Control Field Size | Purpose | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **I-frame** | 2 bytes | Carries user data with N(S) and N(R) | Data transfer |
| **S-frame** | 2 bytes | Flow & error control only | RR, RNR, REJ, SREJ |
| **U-frame** | 1 byte | Connection management | SABME, DISC, UA, FRMR |

**I-frame Control (16 bits):** `0  N(S)[7]  P/F  N(R)[7]`
**S-frame Control (16 bits):** `10  SS[2]  P/F  N(R)[7]`
**U-frame Control (8 bits):** `11  MM[2]  P/F  MM[3]`

**I-frames** are used for user data with piggybacked ACKs. **S-frames** (Supervisory) carry explicit ACKs (RR) or rejection (REJ). **U-frames** (Unnumbered) carry link-management commands like SABME (connect request) and DISC (disconnect).

**Q (b).** Explain the connection establishment, data transfer, and connection termination phases of LLC Type 2 service. Use state-transition descriptions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Phase 1 — Connection Establishment (Three-Way Handshake):**
1. Sender transmits **SABME** (Set Async Balanced Mode Extended) with P=1.
2. Receiver responds with **UA** (Unnumbered Acknowledgment) with F=1.
3. Logical connection is now **established**; both sides exchange I-frames.

**Phase 2 — Data Transfer:**
- Sender transmits **I-frames** with N(S) (send seq #) and N(R) (next expected seq #).
- Receiver sends **RR** (Receive Ready) S-frames as ACKs.
- If a frame is lost, receiver sends **REJ** with N(R) pointing to the missing frame.
- The **P/F bit** is used for polling (P=1) and final bit (F=1).

**Phase 3 — Connection Termination:**
1. Initiator sends **DISC** with P=1.
2. Peer responds with **UA** with F=1.
3. Logical connection is **released**; both sides return to *LOGICALLY DISCONNECTED* state.

(Refer to the state diagram in **Section 4.3** for visual confirmation of the lifecycle.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in LLC Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing SAP with MAC address** — SAP is an *8-bit logical upper-layer ID*; MAC is a *48-bit physical address*. Examiners deduct heavily if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Omitting I/G and C/R bits** — Always state that the **LSB of DSAP** is the I/G bit and the **LSB of SSAP** is the C/R bit. Many students forget this.
> 3. **Wrong control field size** — I/S frames use **2 bytes (extended)**; U-frames use **1 byte** (basic). Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting to draw the diagram** — For 7-mark sub-questions, a neat LLC PDU diagram is **mandatory**. Without it, expect a 1–2 mark deduction.
> 5. **Saying LLC provides security** — LLC does **not** provide encryption; it provides flow + error control + multiplexing only.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LLC** is the **upper sublayer of the Data Link Layer** defined by **IEEE 802.2**.
- It provides **media independence** — works over Ethernet, Token Ring, Wi-Fi, etc.
- **Three service types:** Type 1 (connectionless, no ACK), Type 2 (connection-oriented, full reliability), Type 3 (connectionless with ACK).
- **LLC PDU = DSAP (1B) + SSAP (1B) + Control (1 or 2B) + Info (variable).**
- **SAP is 8 bits**; LSB of DSAP = I/G; LSB of SSAP = C/R.
- **Three frame types:** **I-frame** (data), **S-frame** (control: RR, RNR, REJ, SREJ), **U-frame** (link mgmt: SABME, DISC, UA, DM, FRMR).
- **LLC Type 2 control field uses 2 bytes (extended)**: N(S) and N(R) are 7 bits each, modulo 128.
- **P/F bit** is used in all three frame types — P in commands, F in responses.
- **LLC is conceptually derived from HDLC** — this is a favorite KTU theory question.
- LLC does **not** perform **bit-level framing, MAC addressing, or media access** — those are MAC sublayer jobs.
- **LLC Type 2 lifecycle:** SABME → UA → I-frames → DISC → UA.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireless LAN (802.11)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT414 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wireless LAN (802.11)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**IEEE 802.11** is a family of Wireless Local Area Network (WLAN) standards defined by the Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE) for implementing *Wi-Fi* communication in the **2.4 GHz**, **5 GHz**, and **6 GHz** unlicensed Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) radio bands. The standard specifies both the **Physical Layer (PHY)** and the **Medium Access Control (MAC) Sublayer** of the OSI Data Link Layer, governing how wireless stations (STAs) discover, associate with, and contend for a shared, broadcast RF medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus:** The primary emphasis in Module 2 of *PEEVT414* is the **802.11 MAC sublayer protocol**, the **CSMA/CA** contention mechanism, the **RTS/CTS handshake**, the **architecture (BSS, ESS, DS)**, and the **802.11 MAC frame format**. Physical-layer encoding schemes (DSSS/OFDM) are touched upon only at a conceptual level.

### Family of 802.11 Amendments

| Standard | Year | Band | Max Data Rate | Modulation (PHY) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **802.11** (legacy) | 1997 | 2.4 GHz | **2 Mbps** | DSSS / FHSS |
| **802.11b** | 1999 | 2.4 GHz | **11 Mbps** | DSSS (HR-DSSS) |
| **802.11a** | 1999 | 5 GHz | **54 Mbps** | OFDM |
| **802.11g** | 2003 | 2.4 GHz | **54 Mbps** | OFDM |
| **802.11n (Wi-Fi 4)** | 2009 | 2.4 / 5 GHz | **600 Mbps** | OFDM + MIMO |
| **802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5)** | 2014 | 5 GHz | **6.93 Gbps** | MU-MIMO OFDM |
| **802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6)** | 2019 | 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz | **9.6 Gbps** | OFDMA + MU-MIMO |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Walkie-Talkie" Model

Imagine a busy restaurant where multiple tables are conversing without any waiter coordinating the floor.

- **The Restaurant Air = Shared RF Medium:** Just like the air carries every conversation simultaneously, the 2.4 GHz band carries every Wi-Fi frame from every device within range.
- **"Listen Before You Speak" = CSMA/CA:** Before talking, you would naturally *listen* (carrier sense). If the air is silent, you may speak. If two people begin simultaneously, both realize it and stop, then wait a *random* short time before retrying (the **backoff** procedure).
- **The Loud "SHHH!" = RTS/CTS:** To avoid two loud customers on opposite sides of the room from colliding mid-sentence (the **hidden-node problem**), one customer first yells a short *Request To Speak* ("RTS"). If acknowledged by the receiver (*Clear To Send*), the room effectively agrees to remain quiet for the duration.
- **The Receptionist = Access Point (AP):** In *infrastructure mode*, a central "receptionist" directs traffic and authenticates customers — without one, devices may still talk *directly* to each other (ad-hoc / IBSS mode).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use CSMA/CD (collision detection) like Ethernet?**
> Wireless transceivers cannot reliably *detect* a collision on the airwaves because:
> 1. The **received signal strength** from the remote transmitter is often weaker than the **local transmitted signal** ("near-far problem").
> 2. Detecting collisions in the RF domain would require **full-duplex radios** with sophisticated self-interference cancellation.
> Therefore, 802.11 uses **CSMA/CA — Collision Avoidance** (preventive) rather than CSMA/CD (reactive).

---

## 1.3 Key Architectural Building Blocks (At a Glance)

| Acronym | Full Form | One-line Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **STA** | Station | Any Wi-Fi-capable device (phone, laptop, IoT sensor). |
| **AP** | Access Point | The "base station" of a Wi-Fi cell (translates wireless ↔ wired). |
| **BSS** | Basic Service Set | The radio cell formed by one AP and its associated STAs. |
| **IBSS** | Independent BSS | An ad-hoc peer-to-peer cell with **no** AP. |
| **DS** | Distribution System | The wired Ethernet back-haul interconnecting APs. |
| **ESS** | Extended Service Set | Multiple BSSs linked by a DS, roaming as one logical LAN. |
| **SSID** | Service Set Identifier | The human-readable "network name" (e.g., `KTU_WiFi`). |
| **NAV** | Network Allocation Vector | A virtual "do-not-transmit" timer carried in each frame. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 802.11 Network Architecture — The Reference Model

### 2.1.1 Infrastructure Mode (BSS / ESS)

- **Basic Service Set (BSS)** is the *fundamental* topological unit. It consists of **one AP** plus zero or more **STAs** that have successfully completed the **association** handshake.
- The AP operates as a **Layer-2 bridge**: it relays MAC frames between the wireless BSS and the wired Distribution System.
- The **BSSID** (Basic Service Set Identifier) is, in practice, the **48-bit MAC address** of the AP's wireless interface. This uniquely identifies the BSS in the universe of overlapping Wi-Fi cells.
- An **Extended Service Set (ESS)** is a collection of one or more BSSs interconnected by a common **Distribution System (DS)** — typically a switched Ethernet backbone. From a STA's perspective, the ESS appears as a single, transparent, contiguous Layer-2 network, enabling seamless **roaming** as the user physically moves between BSSs.

### 2.1.2 Independent (Ad-Hoc) Mode — IBSS

- In an **IBSS**, STAs form a peer-to-peer mesh **without** any AP. Discovery is performed by broadcasting **probe request / probe response** frames.
- Use cases: file transfer between two laptops in a coffee shop, military tactical networks, IoT bootstrapping.

### 2.1.3 Distribution System (DS) & Portal

- The **DS** is the logical backbone that inter-AP transport mechanism (wired Ethernet, optical fiber, or even a wireless WDS bridge).
- A **Portal** is the logical gateway that integrates the 802.11 LAN with **non-802.11** networks (e.g., a wired Ethernet LAN). The modern equivalent is a Wi-Fi router, which contains an AP, a DS switch, and a portal/router in one chassis.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Marker Tip:** When asked to "draw the 802.11 architecture", always show: STAs ⇢ AP ⇢ DS ⇢ AP ⇢ STAs, with the ESS enclosing the whole topology and the Portal connecting to the external network.

---

## 2.2 The 802.11 MAC Sublayer — Why and How It Works

The 802.11 MAC sublayer performs three critical duties that wired Ethernet does not need to perform so aggressively:

1. **Channel access arbitration** (DCF + optional PCF/HCF).
2. **Reliability over an unreliable medium** — every unicast data frame is acknowledged with a short **ACK**; absence of ACK implies a collision or channel error, and triggers retransmission.
3. **Power management** for battery-operated STAs.

### 2.2.1 Distributed Coordination Function (DCF) — The Workhorse

DCF is the *mandatory*, CSMA/CA-based contention mechanism used by every 802.11-compliant device.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Sense the medium.** A STA that wishes to transmit performs a **Clear Channel Assessment (CCA)**.
2. **Wait for DIFS.** The medium must remain idle for a full **DCF Inter-Frame Space (DIFS)** period (longer than SIFS, to give priority to ACK/CTS/RTS responses).
3. **Enter the backoff procedure.** The STA selects a random **backoff counter** uniformly from $\{0, 1, \dots, CW\}$.
4. **Decrement slot-by-slot** the backoff counter only while the medium is sensed idle. The counter is *frozen* if the medium becomes busy and resumed when it is idle again for DIFS.
5. **Transmit** when the counter reaches zero.
6. **Wait for ACK.** The receiving STA, after a **SIFS** interval, transmits a short **ACK frame**. The sender considers the transmission successful *only* if this ACK is received correctly.
7. **If no ACK arrives** within the ACK timeout (which equals the AckTimeout bound), the sender **doubles the Contention Window** (up to **CWmax**), draws a new backoff, and retries — up to the **retry limit** (typically 7 for short frames).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why random backoff and not fixed?** A fixed wait would cause a *deterministic collision* every time two STAs wanted to talk simultaneously. Randomization statistically breaks the symmetry and ensures that, with high probability, exactly one STA draws the *smallest* backoff and wins the next contention cycle.

### 2.2.2 The Two IFS — SIFS vs DIFS

- **SIFS (Short Inter-Frame Space):** Used for **high-priority** in-frame exchanges: ACK, CTS, fragments of the same MSDU, and any frame in a contention-free burst. *Shortest* spacing — preserves atomicity.
- **DIFS (DCF Inter-Frame Space):** Used for **regular data frames** at the start of a new contention cycle. Longer than SIFS, ensuring that an ongoing in-frame exchange is never interrupted.

### 2.2.3 Point Coordination Function (PCF) — Legacy Optional Polling

PCF is an *optional* contention-free access method defined in the original 802.11 standard, where a **Point Coordinator (typically the AP)** polls STAs in a round-robin fashion during a **Contention-Free Period (CFP)** alternating with contention-based DCF periods. It is rarely implemented in practice and has been superseded by **HCF** (Hybrid Coordination Function) in 802.11e.

---

## 2.3 RTS / CTS Handshake — Solving the Hidden-Node Problem

### 2.3.1 The Hidden-Node Problem

Consider three STAs: **A**, **B**, **C**.
- A and C are both within range of B.
- A and C are **out of range** of each other.
- When A transmits to B, *C cannot sense* A's signal. Likewise, when C transmits to B, *A cannot sense* C.
- Therefore A and C may transmit simultaneously, **colliding** at B — yet each believes it has exclusive use of the medium. This is the **hidden-node problem**.

### 2.3.2 The RTS / CTS Solution

- Before sending a long data frame, the sender **A** transmits a short **RTS (Request To Send)** frame after contending for the channel.
- If B is ready, it replies (after **SIFS**) with a **CTS (Clear To Send)** frame.
- The CTS contains a **Duration field** that is heard by **every STA within B's range** (including the hidden C). All overhearing STAs set their **NAV** (a virtual carrier-sense timer) for the announced duration and refrain from transmitting.
- A then transmits its DATA frame; B replies with ACK. The whole exchange is **atomic** from the perspective of any hidden STA — its NAV prevents interference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** RTS/CTS itself can collide (two simultaneous RTS attempts), but since RTS frames are very short, the cost of such a collision is minimal — a *defensive* tradeoff. This is why RTS/CTS is *optional* and usually invoked only when the data frame exceeds a configurable **RTS_Threshold**.

---

## 2.4 Fragmentation & Reassembly

- Long MSDUs are broken into smaller **MPDUs (fragments)** to increase reliability in noisy RF environments — a corrupted fragment is retransmitted individually, not the whole MSDU.
- All fragments carry the same **Sequence Number** and incrementing **Fragment Number** (0..n). The receiver reassembles them in order.
- Fragments are transmitted with **SIFS** spacing, creating an atomic burst that is invulnerable to new contenders.

---

## 2.5 Power Management — The TIM/DTIM Mechanism

A STA may enter **doze state** to save power. Before dozing, it informs the AP via a frame header bit. The AP buffers frames destined to dozing STAs and announces pending traffic periodically via a **Traffic Indication Map (TIM)** inside every **Beacon** frame. **DTIM (Delivery TIM)** is a special TIM that signals broadcast/multicast buffered traffic.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **directly assessable** in KTU 2024 ESE numerical questions on 802.11 MAC timing.

| Quantity | Formula / Definition | Typical 802.11b/g Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Slot Time ($\sigma$)** | Fundamental time quantum | **9 $\mu$s** (b), **9 $\mu$s** (g) |
| **SIFS** | $SIFS = RX_{RFDelay} + RX_{PLCPDelay} + MAC_{ProcDelay} + RX_{TXTurnaround}$ | **10 $\mu$s** |
| **DIFS** | $DIFS = SIFS + 2 \cdot \sigma$ | **28 $\mu$s** |
| **EIFS** | Extended IFS — used when a corrupted frame is received | $SIFS + ACK_{tx} + DIFS$ |
| **Backoff Time** | $T_{BO} = \text{Uniform}(0, CW) \cdot \sigma$ | — |
| **Initial Contention Window** | $CW_{min}$ | **31** (b/g) |
| **Max Contention Window** | $CW_{max}$ | **1023** (b/g) |
| **CW Doubling Rule** | $CW_{new} = \min\!\left(\,(CW_{old}+1)\cdot 2 - 1,\; CW_{max}\,\right)$ | — |
| **Max Payload (MSDU)** | Frame Body limit | **2304 bytes** |
| **RTS Frame Size** | Fixed short control frame | **20 bytes** |
| **CTS / ACK Frame Size** | Fixed short control frame | **14 bytes** |
| **Total TX Time (success)** | $T_{success} = T_{DIFS} + T_{BO} + T_{DATA} + T_{SIFS} + T_{ACK}$ | — |
| **Total TX Time (collision)** | $T_{coll} = T_{DIFS} + T_{BO} + T_{DATA}^{*} \;/\; T_{RTS}$ | — |
| **Saturation Throughput** | $S = \dfrac{\mathbb{E}[P]}{\mathbb{E}[T_{slot}]}$ | — |

> **Engineering Insight:** $CW_{min}$ and $CW_{max}$ directly trade off **collision probability** (lower CW ⇒ higher collision rate) against **channel under-utilization** (higher CW ⇒ more idle time waiting for backoff to expire). The exponential backoff is a classic example of **binary exponential backoff (BEB)** adapted from 802.3 Ethernet.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise WLANs** (Cisco, Aruba, Ruckus): Use 802.11ac/ax with dynamic channel assignment, band-steering, and load-balancing — all built atop the 802.11 MAC defined here.
- **IoT / Smart Home**: 802.11ah (HaLow) at sub-1 GHz for low-power long-range; 802.11n in the home for multimedia.
- **Industrial Automation**: Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) extensions to 802.11 (802.11aa, 802.11ax) provide bounded latency for closed-loop control.
- **Vehicular Networks**: 802.11p (DSRC) for V2X communication uses a modified 802.11 MAC for rapidly moving environments.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are the **most-asked numerical sub-parts** in KTU ESE Module-2 questions. Master these three examples for full marks.

---

## 3.1 Derivation — DIFS, SIFS and Backoff Relationship (Slot Time Arithmetic)

**Given (802.11g parameters):**

$$
\sigma = 9\ \mu s, \quad SIFS = 10\ \mu s, \quad CW_{min} = 31
$$

**To Derive:** $DIFS$ and the **average initial backoff time**.

### Step 1 — Recall the Definitional Relationship

The IEEE 802.11 specification defines the DIFS as exactly **two slot times longer** than the SIFS:

$$
DIFS = SIFS + 2 \cdot \sigma
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Numerical Values

$$
DIFS = 10\ \mu s + 2 \times 9\ \mu s
$$

$$
\boxed{DIFS = 28\ \mu s}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Average Backoff Window

The backoff counter is drawn **uniformly** from $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, CW\}$. The expected (mean) value of a uniform discrete random variable on $\{0, \dots, N\}$ is $N / 2$. Thus, the average number of slot times waited in the first attempt is:

$$
\mathbb{E}[BO_{slots}] = \frac{CW_{min}}{2} = \frac{31}{2} = 15.5\ \text{slots}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Absolute Time

$$
\mathbb{E}[T_{BO}] = \mathbb{E}[BO_{slots}] \cdot \sigma = 15.5 \times 9\ \mu s = 139.5\ \mu s
$$

$$
\boxed{\mathbb{E}[T_{BO}] = 139.5\ \mu s}
$$

### Step 5 — Physical Interpretation

On average, every transmitting STA will wait approximately **139.5 $\mu$s** of idle medium time *after* DIFS before launching its first frame. This deliberate delay is the heart of **collision avoidance** — it statistically breaks the simultaneity of contending stations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Total Successful Transmission Time (RTS/CTS-Enabled)

**Given:** A station transmits a 1500-byte MSDU using 802.11b (1 Mbps DSSS, long preamble), with RTS/CTS enabled.

**Constants (802.11b long-preamble DSSS @ 1 Mbps):**

$$
\sigma = 20\ \mu s,\ SIFS = 10\ \mu s,\ DIFS = 50\ \mu s
$$

$$
T_{PHY} = 192\ \mu s \text{ (long preamble + header)},\quad R_{data} = 1\ Mbps,\quad R_{ctrl} = 1\ Mbps
$$

**Frame sizes:**

$$
L_{RTS} = 20\ \text{bytes},\quad L_{CTS} = 14\ \text{bytes},\quad L_{ACK} = 14\ \text{bytes}
$$

### Step 1 — Time to Transmit RTS

The *full RTS frame* includes the PHY preamble+header plus the MAC body, both transmitted at the control rate (1 Mbps here):

$$
T_{RTS} = T_{PHY} + \frac{L_{RTS} \cdot 8}{R_{ctrl}} = 192\ \mu s + \frac{20 \cdot 8}{1\ Mbps}
$$

$$
T_{RTS} = 192\ \mu s + 160\ \mu s = 352\ \mu s
$$

### Step 2 — Time to Transmit CTS

$$
T_{CTS} = T_{PHY} + \frac{L_{CTS} \cdot 8}{R_{ctrl}} = 192\ \mu s + \frac{14 \cdot 8}{1\ Mbps}
$$

$$
T_{CTS} = 192\ \mu s + 112\ \mu s = 304\ \mu s
$$

### Step 3 — Time to Transmit ACK

$$
T_{ACK} = 192\ \mu s + 112\ \mu s = 304\ \mu s
$$

### Step 4 — Time to Transmit DATA (including MAC header overhead)

802.11 MAC header (no WEP) = **34 bytes**; plus the **LLC/SNAP** header of 8 bytes. Total MAC overhead = **42 bytes**. Total frame size at the PHY:

$$
L_{DATA} = 1500 + 42 = 1542\ \text{bytes}
$$

$$
T_{DATA} = T_{PHY} + \frac{L_{DATA} \cdot 8}{1\ Mbps} = 192\ \mu s + 12{,}336\ \mu s = 12{,}528\ \mu s
$$

### Step 5 — Assemble the Full Successful-Exchange Timeline

$$
T_{success} = \underbrace{T_{DIFS}}_{DIFS} + \underbrace{T_{BO}}_{\text{avg backoff}} + \underbrace{T_{RTS}}_{RTS} + \underbrace{T_{SIFS}}_{SIFS} + \underbrace{T_{CTS}}_{CTS} + \underbrace{T_{SIFS}}_{SIFS} + \underbrace{T_{DATA}}_{DATA} + \underbrace{T_{SIFS}}_{SIFS} + \underbrace{T_{ACK}}_{ACK}
$$

$$
T_{success} = 50 + 310 + 352 + 10 + 304 + 10 + 12{,}528 + 10 + 304 \quad [\mu s]
$$

(Taking $\mathbb{E}[T_{BO}] = \frac{CW_{min}}{2} \cdot \sigma = 15.5 \cdot 20 = 310\ \mu s$.)

$$
\boxed{T_{success} \approx 13{,}878\ \mu s \approx 13.88\ ms}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Efficiency (Saturation Throughput Proxy)

$$
\eta = \frac{L_{payload}}{T_{success} \cdot R_{data}} = \frac{1500 \cdot 8}{13{,}878\ \mu s \cdot 1\ Mbps}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{12{,}000}{13{,}878} \approx 0.864 \implies \boxed{\eta \approx 86.4\%}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Even with RTS/CTS overhead, the channel efficiency is high because **one successful exchange amortizes all the control frames across a 1500-byte payload**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Exponential Backoff Contention Window Doubling

**Given:** A transmission fails three times in a row. Compute the Contention Window at the *start of the 4th attempt*.

**Initial:** $CW_{min} = 31,\ CW_{max} = 1023$.

### Step 1 — Apply BEB Recurrence

The 802.11 standard specifies:

$$
CW_{new} = \min\!\left((CW_{old} + 1) \cdot 2 - 1,\ CW_{max}\right)
$$

### Step 2 — Attempt 1 → Attempt 2 (after 1st collision)

$$
CW_1 = 31
$$

$$
CW_2 = \min\!\left((31 + 1) \cdot 2 - 1,\ 1023\right) = \min(63,\ 1023) = 63
$$

### Step 3 — Attempt 2 → Attempt 3 (after 2nd collision)

$$
CW_3 = \min\!\left((63 + 1) \cdot 2 - 1,\ 1023\right) = \min(127,\ 1023) = 127
$$

### Step 4 — Attempt 3 → Attempt 4 (after 3rd collision)

$$
CW_4 = \min\!\left((127 + 1) \cdot 2 - 1,\ 1023\right) = \min(255,\ 1023) = 255
$$

$$
\boxed{CW_4 = 255}
$$

### Step 5 — Verification (General Pattern)

After $k$ collisions:

$$
CW_k = \min\!\left(2^{k+4} - 1,\ 1023\right)
$$

(Valid for $k = 0, 1, 2, \dots$ until saturation at 1023.) For our case $k=3$: $CW_4 = \min(2^7 - 1, 1023) = \min(127, 1023)$. Wait — this gives 127, indicating the recurrence above starts from $CW_0 = 31 = 2^5 - 1$, so the *general* form is:

$$
CW_{k} = \min\!\left(2^{k+5} - 1,\ 1023\right)
$$

For $k = 3$: $CW_3 = \min(2^8 - 1, 1023) = \min(255, 1023) = 255$. ✓ **Consistent**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When Does RTS/CTS Make Sense?

| Scenario | Recommended Mode | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Short VoIP frames (< 200 B) | **Disable RTS/CTS** | RTS/CTS overhead exceeds data frame length — net efficiency loss. |
| Large file transfers (> 500 B) | **Enable RTS/CTS** | Cost of one RTS/CTS exchange (≈ 660 $\mu$s) is amortized over multi-ms data frame. |
| Dense Wi-Fi environment (many STAs) | **Enable RTS/CTS** | Hidden-node probability rises with spatial density; NAV-based reservation is highly effective. |
| Single-STA, line-of-sight link | **Disable RTS/CTS** | No hidden nodes, no benefit. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 802.11 Architecture — BSS / ESS / DS Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EXT["External Network (Internet / Ethernet)"]
        NET1["Server / Cloud"]
    end
    subgraph DS["Distribution System (Wired Ethernet Backbone)"]
        DSNODE1["DS Switch / Router"]
    end
    subgraph ESS["Extended Service Set (ESS)"]
        subgraph BSS1["BSS 1 - Floor 1"]
            AP1A["AP 1 - MAC AA:BB:CC:00:00:01"]
            STA1A["STA A - Laptop"]
            STA1B["STA B - Phone"]
            STA1C["STA C - IoT Sensor"]
        end
        subgraph BSS2["BSS 2 - Floor 2"]
            AP2A["AP 2 - MAC AA:BB:CC:00:00:02"]
            STA2A["STA D - Laptop"]
            STA2B["STA E - Tablet"]
        end
        subgraph BSS3["BSS 3 - IBSS Ad-Hoc"]
            STA3A["STA F"]
            STA3B["STA G"]
        end
    end
    PORTAL["Portal / Gateway Router"]

    NET1 --> PORTAL
    PORTAL --> DSNODE1
    DSNODE1 --> AP1A
    DSNODE1 --> AP2A
    AP1A --- STA1A
    AP1A --- STA1B
    AP1A --- STA1C
    AP2A --- STA2A
    AP2A --- STA2B
    STA3A <--> STA3B
```

> **Reading Guide:** Solid arrows denote *wired* DS links; plain lines denote *wireless* 802.11 associations. The IBSS (BSS 3) on the right intentionally has **no AP and no DS connection**.

---

## 4.2 CSMA/CA with Binary Exponential Backoff — Timing Diagram

```mermaid
gantt
    title CSMA/CA Channel Access Timeline - STA Wins on 2nd Attempt
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
```
