```mermaid
    section STA 1 Timeline
    DIFS Wait          :done, difs1, 0, 28
    Backoff 31 slots   :active, bo1, 28, 279
    DATA Frame TX      :crit, data1, 307, 1200
    SIFS Wait          :done, sifs1a, 1507, 10
    ACK Receive        :crit, ack1, 1517, 30

    section STA 2 Timeline
    DIFS Wait          :done, difs2, 0, 28
    Backoff Frozen     :crit, frozen2, 28, 270
    Backoff 15 slots   :active, bo2, 298, 135
    Defer - sets NAV   :crit, defer2, 433, 1114

    section Other STAs
    NAV Set from DATA Duration :crit, nav3, 307, 1230
```

> **Reading Guide:** Time advances left-to-right in microseconds. STA 1 transmits a frame; STA 2 hears the Duration field, sets its NAV, and **freezes** its own backoff counter until the medium has been idle for DIFS again.

---

## 4.3 RTS / CTS Handshake Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Sender (A)
    participant R as Receiver (B)
    participant H as Hidden Node (C)

    Note over S,H: All STAs Contend for Channel with CSMA/CA
    S->>S: Sense channel for DIFS
    S->>S: Wait random backoff
    S->>R: RTS [Duration = T_Data + 3*SIFS + T_ACK + T_CTS]
    Note over R: SIFS
    R->>S: CTS [Duration = T_Data + 2*SIFS + T_ACK]
    H->>H: Overhears CTS - sets NAV for the announced Duration
    Note over S: SIFS
    S->>R: DATA Frame
    Note over R: SIFS
    R->>S: ACK
    Note over H: NAV expires - allowed to contend again
```

> **Critical Insight:** The **hidden node C never sees the DATA frame** — yet it has been **silenced** because it heard the CTS (which contained a Duration value large enough to cover the entire DATA+ACK exchange).

---

## 4.4 MAC Frame Format — Block-Level Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FC["Frame Control - 2 bytes"]
        FC_PV["Protocol Version 2 bits"]
        FC_T["Type 2 bits"]
        FC_ST["Subtype 4 bits"]
        FC_TDS["To DS 1 bit"]
        FC_FDS["From DS 1 bit"]
        FC_MF["More Frag 1 bit"]
        FC_R["Retry 1 bit"]
        FC_PM["Power Mgmt 1 bit"]
        FC_MD["More Data 1 bit"]
        FC_P["Protected 1 bit"]
        FC_O["Order 1 bit"]
    end
    subgraph FR["Fixed & Optional Fields"]
        F_DUR["Duration / ID 2 bytes"]
        F_A1["Address 1 6 bytes"]
        F_A2["Address 2 6 bytes"]
        F_A3["Address 3 6 bytes"]
        F_SEQ["Sequence Control 2 bytes"]
        F_A4["Address 4 6 bytes - optional"]
    end
    subgraph BODY["Payload & Integrity"]
        F_BODY["Frame Body 0 to 2312 bytes"]
        F_FCS["FCS CRC-32 4 bytes"]
    end

    FC --> F_DUR --> F_A1 --> F_A2 --> F_A3 --> F_SEQ --> F_A4 --> F_BODY --> F_FCS
```

---

## 4.5 The Hidden-Node Problem (Cause → Effect → Solution)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["STA A - Sender"] -. "Cannot sense C" .-> X["??? Hidden Node C"]
    X -. "Cannot sense A" .-> A
    A -->|RTS| B["STA B - AP / Receiver"]
    B -->|CTS heard by both A and C| A
    B -->|CTS heard by both A and C| X
    X -->|"Sets NAV - Defers Transmission"| X
    A -->|"DATA - Protected by NAV"| B
    B -->|"ACK - Completes Atomic Burst"| A
```

> **Without RTS/CTS:** A's DATA and C's DATA collide *at B*. **With RTS/CTS:** C's NAV silences C before any collision can occur.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Mark-Loss Hotspots:**
> 1. **Confusing SIFS vs DIFS:** Students frequently invert the order of magnitude. Memorize: **DIFS > SIFS**; DIFS is used for new contention cycles, SIFS for in-frame atomic responses.
> 2. **Forgetting the PHY preamble ($T_{PHY}$) in timing calculations:** All 802.11 frames incur a 192 $\mu$s (long preamble) or 96 $\mu$s (short preamble) PHY-layer header overhead *on top of* the MAC body transmission time.
> 3. **Using CW instead of (CW+1):** The standard uses a uniform distribution over $\{0, \dots, CW\}$ — the **expected value is CW/2, not (CW+1)/2**.
> 4. **Missing the Frame Control sub-fields:** Frame format questions ask for *sub-fields within* the 2-byte FC. Listing only "FC = 2 bytes" earns zero marks; you must enumerate **Protocol Version, Type, Subtype, ToDS, FromDS, More Fragments, Retry, Power Mgmt, More Data, Protected, Order** — at least 7 of them for full credit.
> 5. **Forgetting BSSID = AP MAC address:** A very common short-answer trap.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define BSS, ESS, and DS in IEEE 802.11. State the relationship between them.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **BSS (Basic Service Set):** A BSS is the fundamental building block of an 802.11 WLAN. It consists of a single **Access Point (AP)** and the set of **Stations (STAs)** that have successfully associated with it. The AP acts as the central coordinator and Layer-2 bridge between the wireless STAs and the wired backbone. The **BSSID** uniquely identifies the BSS and is set to the **48-bit MAC address** of the AP's wireless interface.
- **DS (Distribution System):** The DS is the *logical* (typically wired) backbone that interconnects multiple APs. It transports MAC frames between BSSs so that frames can flow from an STA in one BSS to an STA in another BSS (or to the external network).
- **ESS (Extended Service Set):** An ESS is the *union* of one or more BSSs interconnected via a common DS, plus a **Portal** to the external network. From an STA's perspective, the ESS appears as a single, seamless Layer-2 network, enabling **roaming** between BSSs without changing IP layer identity.

**Relationship:** $ESS = \bigcup_{i} BSS_i \;+\; DS \;+\; \text{Portal}$. Each BSS is anchored to the DS by exactly one AP; the DS carries inter-BSS traffic; the Portal exposes the ESS to external (typically 802.3 Ethernet) networks.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]** Why does IEEE 802.11 use CSMA/CA instead of CSMA/CD? List any three reasons.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

IEEE 802.11 uses **CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance)** rather than CSMA/CD (Collision Detection) for the following reasons:

1. **Near-Far Problem & Collision Detection Impracticality:** A wireless transceiver transmits at a power level (e.g., 15–20 dBm) that is many orders of magnitude larger than any signal received from a distant station (often -90 dBm at the sensitivity floor). Detecting a remote collision while simultaneously transmitting one's own signal is therefore *physically infeasible* without expensive full-duplex radios and self-interference cancellation.
2. **Hidden Node Problem:** A STA may be unable to sense *some* other transmitting STAs (because of RF propagation obstacles or distance). Carrier sensing alone cannot guarantee a clean channel; CSMA/CA supplements physical sensing with **virtual sensing via NAV** and the **RTS/CTS handshake** to mitigate this.
3. **Unreliable Wireless Medium:** Wireless channels have a significantly higher bit error rate than wired media. CSMA/CA pairs every unicast frame with a positive **ACK** and exponential backoff on ACK loss, providing the link-layer reliability that the inherently lossy PHY cannot guarantee on its own.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Either **Question A** OR **Question B** is asked from this module — your preparation must cover both branches.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **IEEE 802.11 MAC frame format**. List and explain the four address fields and the role of the **To DS** and **From DS** bits. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (Part a):

**Introduction (1 mark):** The IEEE 802.11 MAC frame is the Layer-2 Protocol Data Unit exchanged between wireless STAs and APs. Its overall structure is shown below:

```
+----------+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------+
| Frame    | Dur /  | Addr 1 | Addr 2 | Addr 3 | Seq    | Addr 4 | Body   | FCS    |
| Control  | ID     |        |        |        | Ctrl   | (opt)  |        |        |
| 2 bytes  | 2 B    | 6 B    | 6 B    | 6 B    | 2 B    | 6 B    | 0-2312B| 4 B    |
+----------+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------+--------+
```

**Frame Control Sub-fields (1.5 marks):** The 2-byte FC contains:
- **Protocol Version (2 bits):** Currently set to `00` (version 0).
- **Type (2 bits):** 00 = Management, 01 = Control, 10 = Data.
- **Subtype (4 bits):** Further refines type (e.g., RTS, CTS, ACK, Beacon, Probe Req).
- **To DS (1 bit):** Set to 1 if the frame is destined to the DS (i.e., going to AP).
- **From DS (1 bit):** Set to 1 if the frame is exiting the DS (i.e., from AP).
- **More Fragments (1 bit):** Indicates more fragments of the same MSDU follow.
- **Retry (1 bit):** 1 if this is a retransmission.
- **Power Management (1 bit):** 1 if the STA will enter doze mode after this frame.
- **More Data (1 bit):** AP indicates buffered frames for a dozing STA.
- **Protected Frame (1 bit):** 1 if the Frame Body is encrypted (WEP/WPA).
- **Order (1 bit):** 1 if strict ordering is required.

**The Four Address Fields (2.5 marks):**
| Addr | Meaning | Content |
|---|---|---|
| **Addr 1** | Immediate receiver MAC address (RA) | Used for **filtering** at the PHY/MAC. |
| **Addr 2** | Immediate transmitter MAC address (TA) | Used for ACKs back to the source. |
| **Addr 3** | Original source or final destination | Depends on To/From DS bits. |
| **Addr 4** | Used only in **WDS (Wireless Distribution System)** / 4-address frame mode | Source when both To DS=1 and From DS=1 (inter-AP bridging). |

**To DS / From DS Interpretation Table (2 marks):**

| To DS | From DS | Meaning | Addr 3 | Addr 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | Direct STA→STA (ad-hoc) | Destination | Not used |
| 1 | 0 | STA → AP (uploading) | Source | Not used |
| 0 | 1 | AP → STA (downloading) | Destination | Not used |
| 1 | 1 | AP → AP (WDS bridge) | Destination | Source |

**Diagrams: (See SECTION 4.4 for the block-level layout.)**

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the **CSMA/CA** mechanism of IEEE 802.11 with a neat timing diagram. Clearly distinguish **DIFS**, **SIFS**, and **backoff**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (Part b):

**Definition (1 mark):** CSMA/CA is the **Distributed Coordination Function (DCF)** specified in 802.11, in which a station first *senses* the medium, *waits* for an idle DIFS, *backs off* for a random number of slots, then *transmits*. The receiver replies with an **ACK** after SIFS. The exchange is *atomic* from the perspective of any third STA observing the channel or the Duration/ID field.

**IFS Hierarchy (1.5 marks):** In increasing duration:
$$SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS$$
- **SIFS (Short Inter-Frame Space):** Used for ACK, CTS, RTS, and continuation fragments — atomic in-burst responses.
- **PIFS (PCF Inter-Frame Space):** Used by the PCF to gain priority access at the start of a CFP.
- **DIFS (DCF Inter-Frame Space):** Used by ordinary contending STAs at the start of a new contention cycle.
- **EIFS (Extended Inter-Frame Space):** Used when a STA receives a frame that it cannot decode — a *penalty* IFS to avoid corrupting an in-progress exchange.

Numerically: $DIFS = SIFS + 2 \cdot \sigma$.

**CSMA/CA Operational Steps (3 marks):**
1. STA senses the channel — **CCA** (Clear Channel Assessment).
2. If channel busy, STA **defers** and waits for it to become idle.
3. After channel idle for **DIFS**, the STA draws a **random backoff counter** $BO \sim U[0, CW]$.
4. The counter decrements one slot at a time *only while the channel remains idle*; it is **frozen** if the channel becomes busy and **resumes** after the next DIFS.
5. When $BO = 0$, the STA transmits. If the data frame is corrupted or collides (no ACK received), the STA enters **binary exponential backoff** — doubling $CW$ up to $CW_{max}$ and retrying.
6. The destination STA, after **SIFS**, replies with an **ACK**, completing the atomic exchange.

**Timing Diagram (1.5 marks):** Refer to **SECTION 4.2** for the detailed Gantt-chart timing diagram. The key timing relationships are:

$$
\boxed{T_{success} = T_{DIFS} + T_{BO} + T_{DATA} + T_{SIFS} + T_{ACK}}
$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q. (a)** What is the **hidden station problem** in wireless networks? Explain how the **RTS/CTS mechanism** in IEEE 802.11 resolves it. Include a sequence diagram in your explanation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (Part a):

**Definition of Hidden Station (1 mark):** In an 802.11 BSS, a station **C** is said to be *hidden* from **A** if the RF signal from **A** cannot be detected at **C** (e.g., due to distance, attenuation, or physical obstacles), even though both A and C are within reliable communication range of a common receiver **B**. Each can sense the channel as idle and proceed to transmit, but the two signals collide at B.

**Classic Scenario (2 marks):**

```
        A -----------> B <----------- C
       (Sender)      (AP/Recv)    (Hidden)
       A↔C: Out of Range
```

**Why Carrier Sensing Alone Fails (1 mark):** When A transmits, A's CCA reads idle because *C is invisible*. C simultaneously transmits to B. Both frames collide at B. Neither A nor C is aware of the collision — and 802.11 has *no* collision detection mechanism. The MSDU is silently lost; eventually the absence of an ACK reveals the failure.

**RTS/CTS Solution (2.5 marks):**
1. Before transmitting DATA, A sends a short **RTS (Request To Send)** after contending for the channel.
2. B replies after **SIFS** with a **CTS (Clear To Send)**.
3. Both RTS and CTS contain a **Duration field** that reserves the medium for the entire DATA + ACK transaction.
4. Every STA in B's reception range (including the hidden C) **overhears the CTS** and sets its **Network Allocation Vector (NAV)** to the announced duration.
5. C's NAV prevents it from transmitting during A's DATA exchange — collision is impossible.
6. RTS frames are short (20 bytes), so *if* two RTS frames collide, the cost is minimal.

**Sequence Diagram (0.5 mark):** Refer to **SECTION 4.3** for the complete sequence diagram showing SIFS spacing between RTS → CTS → DATA → ACK.

---

**Q. (b)** Consider a station using 802.11g DSSS-OFDM at the 24 Mbps PHY rate. The MSDU size is **1200 bytes**, MAC header is **34 bytes**, PHY preamble + header is **20 $\mu$s**, and $\sigma = 9\ \mu$s, SIFS = 10 $\mu$s, DIFS = 28 $\mu$s, $CW_{min} = 15$, $CW_{max} = 1023$. RTS and CTS use the **basic 1 Mbps** rate and each incur a 20 $\mu$s PHY overhead. RTS = 20 bytes, CTS = 14 bytes, ACK = 14 bytes. Compute **(i)** the average successful transmission time including RTS/CTS, and **(ii)** the channel efficiency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (Part b):

**Step 1 — Identify the Two Rates (0.5 mark):**
- Data rate $R_{data} = 24$ Mbps
- Control rate $R_{ctrl} = 1$ Mbps
- PHY overhead per frame $T_{PHY} = 20\ \mu s$ (assumed equal for both data and control)

**Step 2 — Compute Frame Transmission Times (2 marks):**

*DATA frame (MPDU + PHY):*
$$
L_{DATA} = 1200 + 34 = 1234\ \text{bytes}
$$
$$
T_{DATA} = T_{PHY} + \frac{1234 \cdot 8}{24\ Mbps} = 20\ \mu s + \frac{9872}{24}\ \mu s = 20 + 411.33 = 431.33\ \mu s
$$

*RTS frame (transmitted at 1 Mbps):*
$$
T_{RTS} = 20\ \mu s + \frac{20 \cdot 8}{1\ Mbps} = 20 + 160 = 180\ \mu s
$$

*CTS frame (1 Mbps):*
$$
T_{CTS} = 20\ \mu s + \frac{14 \cdot 8}{1\ Mbps} = 20 + 112 = 132\ \mu s
$$

*ACK frame (1 Mbps):*
$$
T_{ACK} = 20\ \mu s + 112\ \mu s = 132\ \mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Average Initial Backoff (1 mark):**
$$
\mathbb{E}[T_{BO}] = \frac{CW_{min}}{2} \cdot \sigma = \frac{15}{2} \cdot 9 = 67.5\ \mu s
$$

**Step 4 — Total Successful Exchange (2.5 marks):**

$$
T_{success} = T_{DIFS} + \mathbb{E}[T_{BO}] + T_{RTS} + T_{SIFS} + T_{CTS} + T_{SIFS} + T_{DATA} + T_{SIFS} + T_{ACK}
$$

$$
T_{success} = 28 + 67.5 + 180 + 10 + 132 + 10 + 431.33 + 10 + 132
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{success} = 1000.83\ \mu s \approx 1.001\ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Channel Efficiency (1 mark):**

$$
\eta = \frac{L_{MSDU} \cdot 8}{T_{success} \cdot R_{data}} = \frac{1200 \cdot 8}{1000.83\ \mu s \cdot 24\ Mbps}
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{9600}{24{,}019.92} \approx 0.3997
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta \approx 39.97\% \approx 40\%}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification (1 mark):** The efficiency is significantly lower than the 802.11b @ 1 Mbps case (86%) because the *control* frames (RTS/CTS/ACK) still use the slow 1 Mbps rate, while the data frame uses the fast 24 Mbps rate — a *mismatch* that drags down efficiency. This is exactly why modern 802.11n/ac use multi-rate control frames (e.g., 24 or 48 Mbps for RTS/CTS when channel conditions permit).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and stick it on your wall before the ESE.**

- [ ] **802.11 = WLAN standard family** covering PHY + MAC; operates in 2.4 / 5 / 6 GHz ISM bands.
- [ ] **Architecture pyramid:** STA → BSS (AP + STAs) → ESS (BSSs + DS) → Portal → External Network.
- [ ] **BSSID = MAC address of AP's wireless interface.** SSID = human-readable network name.
- [ ] **IBSS = ad-hoc** peer-to-peer cell with no AP.
- [ ] **MAC layer uses CSMA/CA, not CSMA/CD** — three reasons: near-far problem, hidden nodes, lossy medium.
- [ ] **IFS hierarchy:** $SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS$. **Numerically: $DIFS = SIFS + 2\sigma$** (slot time).
- [ ] **Backoff = Uniform(0, CW) slots; expected = CW/2 slots.**
- [ ] **Binary exponential backoff:** $CW_{new} = \min((CW_{old}+1)\cdot 2 - 1, CW_{max})$. For 802.11b/g: $CW_{min}=31$, $CW_{max}=1023$.
- [ ] **Hidden-node problem solved by RTS/CTS + NAV.** NAV = virtual carrier-sense timer.
- [ ] **Every unicast frame is ACK'd after SIFS.** Absence of ACK ⇒ collision or error ⇒ retry with larger CW.
- [ ] **Frame format:** FC(2) | Dur(2) | Addr1(6) | Addr2(6) | Addr3(6) | SeqCtrl(2) | Addr4(6) | Body(0-2312) | FCS(4).
- [ ] **FC sub-fields:** ProtocolVersion, Type, Subtype, ToDS, FromDS, MoreFrag, Retry, PwrMgmt, MoreData, Protected, Order.
- [ ] **ToDS/FromDS = 4 valid combinations**; each defines the meaning of Addr1-4.
- [ ] **Fragments carry same Sequence Number, incrementing Fragment Number;** transmitted with SIFS spacing (atomic).
- [ ] **Power save uses TIM/DTIM** in Beacons.
- [ ] **PHY rates:** 11b=11 Mbps DSSS; 11a/g=54 Mbps OFDM; 11n=600 Mbps MIMO-OFDM; 11ac=6.93 Gbps MU-MIMO; 11ax=9.6 Gbps OFDMA.
- [ ] **RTS threshold:** RTS/CTS used only if MSDU ≥ threshold (typically 500 B) — overhead vs amortisation tradeoff.
- [ ] **Common KTU error:** Forgetting the **PHY preamble overhead** ($20\ \mu$s short / $192\ \mu$s long) in any time calculation.
- [ ] **PCF** = legacy optional polling; **HCF** = modern QoS-aware (802.11e); **EDCA** = contention-based HCF channel access.
- [ ] **Hidden station ≠ Exposed station.** Exposed: A→B prevents C→D unnecessarily (over-deferral). Different problem, different solutions (RTS/CTS also helps exposed-node).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CSMA/CA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT414 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** CSMA/CA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance)** is a probabilistic media access control (MAC) protocol used in contention-based shared medium networks — most notably wireless LANs governed by the IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi) standard. It is a *proactive* variant of the CSMA family, designed to **avoid** rather than *detect* collisions, since wireless stations cannot simultaneously transmit and listen on the same frequency.

The protocol operates on three foundational principles:
1. **Carrier Sensing (CS)** — A station listens to the channel before transmitting to determine whether the medium is *Idle* or *Busy*.
2. **Multiple Access (MA)** — Multiple independent stations contend for the shared wireless medium without centralized coordination.
3. **Collision Avoidance (CA)** — Through randomized waiting (backoff), prioritized interframe spacing, and optional RTS/CTS handshaking, the probability of simultaneous transmission is statistically minimized.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** CSMA/CA is *the* foundational MAC protocol for IEEE 802.11 wireless networks. In KTU 2024 Module 2, it is studied under the Data Link Layer sublayer — specifically the **MAC sublayer** — and is universally compared with CSMA/CD (Ethernet) and ALOHA.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Polite Dinner Conversation

Imagine a **round-table discussion** in a quiet library:

- **Carrier Sense** → Before speaking, you *listen*. If someone is talking, you do not interrupt. (You are "sensing the carrier.")
- **Multiple Access** → Many people share the same room/air.
- **Collision Avoidance** → Even after the speaker finishes, you don't immediately jump in. You wait a *short polite pause* (DIFS), then pick a **random number of seconds** (backoff counter) and only start talking after that random countdown. If two people accidentally pick the same number, one of them defers (collision *avoidance*, not detection — because once you start talking, you can't "hear" the other person overlapping you in this wireless analogy).
- **Acknowledgment** → The listener nods ("ACK") within a *very short interval* (SIFS) confirming they heard you. No nod = assume your message got lost; try again with a longer random wait.

This is exactly how CSMA/CA behaves in Wi-Fi.

---

## 1.3 Why CSMA/CA and Not CSMA/CD in Wireless?

| Property | Wired (Ethernet) | Wireless (Wi-Fi) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Signal strength at Tx | Strong | Strong |
| Signal strength at Rx (from another station) | Very weak / isolated by cable | Comparably strong (near-far problem) |
| Can detect own collision? | **Yes** (CSMA/CD) | **No** — cannot hear while transmitting |
| Hidden terminal problem? | Negligible | Severe — must be handled by CA |
| Protocol used | CSMA/CD | **CSMA/CA** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hidden Terminal Problem:** In wireless, station A can reach B, and C can reach B, but A *cannot* hear C. Both may transmit to B simultaneously, causing a collision **at B** that neither A nor C can detect. CSMA/CA's RTS/CTS mechanism is specifically designed to mitigate this.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants (IEEE 802.11)

The following parameters are standardized values used in 802.11 DSSS (Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum) PHY layer:

$$\text{SIFS} = 10 \, \mu s$$
$$\text{Slot Time} = 20 \, \mu s$$
$$\text{CW}_{\min} = 31$$
$$\text{CW}_{\max} = 1023$$
$$\text{DIFS} = \text{SIFS} + 2 \times \text{Slot Time} = 50 \, \mu s$$

The minimum contention window is $CW_{\min}$, and the backoff counter is selected uniformly from $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, CW\}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CSMA/CA Operational Flow (DCF Mode)

The **Distributed Coordination Function (DCF)** is the mandatory mode of CSMA/CA in 802.11. It follows a strict sequence of states and timers.

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **A MAC frame arrives at a station** from the upper layer (LLC) ready to be transmitted.
2. **Carrier Sensing Phase** — The station senses the channel state. If energy is detected above the CCA (Clear Channel Assessment) threshold, the medium is marked **BUSY** and a NAV (Network Allocation Vector) timer is set.
3. **DIFS Deferral** — Once the medium becomes **IDLE**, the station waits for a **DIFS** period. If the medium stays idle throughout DIFS, the station proceeds.
4. **Random Backoff Selection** — A backoff counter $BO$ is drawn uniformly:
$$BO \sim \mathcal{U}\{0, 1, 2, \dots, CW\}$$
5. **Slotted Countdown** — For every subsequent *slot time* in which the medium remains idle, the counter decrements by 1. If the medium becomes busy, the countdown is **frozen** and resumed only after another DIFS idle period.
6. **Transmission** — When $BO = 0$, the station transmits the entire MAC frame.
7. **Wait for ACK** — The transmitting station waits **SIFS** (a shorter interval, giving ACK priority) for an Acknowledgment frame.
8. **ACK Received** → Success. Frame is removed from the queue.
9. **ACK Timeout / No ACK** → Assume failure (collision or error). **$CW$ is doubled** (Binary Exponential Backoff) and the algorithm re-enters from Step 1. Retry counter is incremented.
10. **Retry Limit Reached** → Frame is discarded; upper layer is notified.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Priority via IFS:** Different IFS values create implicit priority. **SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS.** Shorter IFS = higher priority. This is why ACKs (which use SIFS) preempt data frames (which use DIFS) — preventing the ACK itself from colliding with new data.

---

## 2.2 Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB)

After each failed transmission (no ACK), the contention window is doubled:

$$CW_i = \min\!\left(2^i \cdot (CW_{\min}+1) - 1, \; CW_{\max}\right)$$

where $i$ is the number of *consecutive* retransmission attempts for that frame. Note the $+1$ and $-1$ shift — many textbooks write it as $CW_i = \min(2^i \cdot CW_{\min}, CW_{\max})$ depending on indexing convention.

| Retry $i$ | $CW_i$ (using $CW_{\min}=31$) | Backoff Range |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 31 | $[0, 31]$ |
| 1 | 63 | $[0, 63]$ |
| 2 | 127 | $[0, 127]$ |
| 3 | 255 | $[0, 255]$ |
| 4 | 511 | $[0, 511]$ |
| 5 | 1023 | $[0, 1023]$ (capped) |
| $\geq 5$ | 1023 | $[0, 1023]$ |

**Purpose:** As congestion increases, the random wait grows exponentially, statistically reducing the chance two stations pick the same slot.

---

## 2.3 RTS/CTS Handshake (Optional but Vital for KTU)

The **Request-To-Send / Clear-To-Send** mechanism solves the *hidden terminal problem*. It is a 4-way exchange:

$$\text{STA}_A \xrightarrow{\text{RTS (DIFS)}} \text{AP} \xrightarrow{\text{CTS (SIFS)}} \text{STA}_A \xrightarrow{\text{DATA (SIFS)}} \text{AP} \xrightarrow{\text{ACK (SIFS)}} \text{STA}_A$$

- **RTS** contains a *duration field* announcing the time needed for the entire exchange.
- All stations overhearing **RTS or CTS** set their **NAV** (Network Allocation Vector) — a virtual carrier sense timer — and defer for that duration.
- A hidden terminal C hearing the CTS from AP will know not to transmit, even though it could not hear A's RTS.

> [!NOTE]
> **NAV (Network Allocation Vector):** A virtual carrier-sense mechanism. Each station maintains a counter that is decremented only when the physical channel is idle. Any non-zero NAV is treated equivalent to a busy channel.

---

## 2.4 Throughput Analysis (KTU High-Yield Formula)

The saturation throughput $S$ of CSMA/CA (in the Bianchi model) for a basic access mechanism (no RTS/CTS) is:

$$S = \frac{P_s \cdot P_{tr} \cdot E[P]}{(1-P_{tr}) \cdot \sigma + P_{tr} \cdot P_s \cdot T_s + P_{tr} \cdot (1-P_s) \cdot T_c}$$

Where:
- $P_{tr}$ = probability that at least one station transmits in a slot
- $P_s$ = conditional probability of successful transmission given a transmission occurs
- $E[P]$ = average payload size
- $\sigma$ = duration of an empty slot
- $T_s$ = time the channel is sensed busy by a successful transmission
- $T_c$ = time the channel is sensed busy during a collision

The standard slotted CSMA/CA throughput bound (no errors, no hidden terminals) reduces to a function of the contention window:

$$S \le \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot CW_{\min}}{n}}} \quad \text{(approximation)}$$

where $n$ is the number of contending stations. This shows **throughput decreases as $n$ or $CW_{\min}$ increases**.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Purpose / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Backoff slot selection | $BO \sim \mathcal{U}\{0, CW\}$ | Randomizes access — dimensionless |
| Binary Exponential Backoff | $CW_i = \min(2^i \cdot (CW_{\min}+1)-1, CW_{\max})$ | Adapts to congestion |
| DIFS | $DIFS = SIFS + 2 \times \sigma$ | Time in $\mu s$ |
| Total deferral before Tx | $T_d = \text{DIFS} + BO \cdot \sigma$ | Time in $\mu s$ |
| Throughput bound | $S \approx \dfrac{1}{1+\sqrt{\tfrac{2 CW_{\min}}{n}}}$ | Fraction (0–1) |
| IFS Priority Order | $SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS$ | Shorter IFS = higher priority |
| Channel utilization | $U = \dfrac{T_{\text{tx useful}}}{T_{\text{total slot}}}$ | Fraction (0–1) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** CSMA/CA underpins every Wi-Fi network on Earth — 802.11a/b/g/n/ac/ax. It is also adapted in 802.15.4 (ZigBee), WirelessHART, and certain satellite/underwater acoustic networks where collision detection is infeasible.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Computing Total Deferral Time Before Transmission

**Given:**
- A station chooses a backoff counter of $BO$ slots.
- DIFS = 50 µs, Slot Time $\sigma$ = 20 µs.
- Find the total waiting time before the frame is placed on the medium.

### Solution

The total time elapsed from "frame ready" to "first bit on wire" is the sum of the DIFS deferral and the backoff countdown:

$$T_d = \text{DIFS} + BO \cdot \sigma$$

Substituting the given constants:

$$T_d = 50 + BO \cdot 20 \quad [\mu s]$$

**Numerical examples** (for KTU-style problems):

| $BO$ (slots) | $T_d$ (µs) | $T_d$ (ms) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 50 | 0.05 |
| 5 | 150 | 0.15 |
| 15 | 350 | 0.35 |
| 31 (max first try) | 670 | 0.67 |

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Throughput Bound of Slotted CSMA/CA

We derive the simplified saturation throughput bound assuming $n$ stations always have a packet ready and perfect channel sensing (no hidden terminals, no errors, no RTS/CTS).

**Step 1** — Probability a station transmits in a randomly chosen slot:
A station transmits in a slot only if it has randomly selected that slot. The probability is $1/(CW+1)$ where $CW$ is the current contention window. In steady state (and assuming $CW = CW_{\min}$ for throughput upper bound approximation):

$$p_{tx} = \frac{1}{CW_{\min}+1}$$

**Step 2** — Probability that *exactly one* station transmits (success) in a slot:

$$P_s = n \cdot p_{tx} \cdot (1-p_{tx})^{n-1}$$

**Step 3** — Probability that *at least one* transmits (slot is used):

$$P_{tr} = 1 - (1-p_{tx})^n$$

**Step 4** — Conditional success probability:

$$P_{\text{success}} = \frac{P_s}{P_{tr}}$$

**Step 5** — Throughput (normalized to slot time $\sigma$, payload $E[P]$, successful transmission time $T_s$):

$$S = \frac{P_{\text{success}} \cdot P_{tr} \cdot E[P]/\sigma}{P_{tr} \cdot T_s/\sigma + (1-P_{tr}) \cdot 1}$$

**Step 6** — For large $n$ and $CW_{\min}$ small, the optimum occurs when $p_{tx} \cdot n \approx 1$, giving the well-known approximation:

$$\boxed{S_{\max} \approx \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot CW_{\min}}{n}}}}$$

**Interpretation:**
- As $n$ (contending stations) increases, $S$ decreases — a fundamental scalability limit of CSMA/CA.
- As $CW_{\min}$ decreases, the protocol becomes more aggressive but collision-prone, so $S$ first *increases* (better utilization) then *decreases* (more collisions). The optimum is at $CW_{\min} \approx n/2$.

**Example calculation (KTU-style):**
If $n = 10$ stations and $CW_{\min} = 31$:

$$S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 31}{10}}} = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{6.2}} = \frac{1}{1 + 2.49} = \frac{1}{3.49} \approx 0.286$$

So maximum theoretical throughput is **~28.6%** of the raw channel rate. This is a *classic* KTU-style numerical problem.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: CSMA/CA vs. CSMA/CD vs. ALOHA

| Feature | Pure ALOHA | Slotted ALOHA | CSMA/CD | **CSMA/CA** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Slotting | No | Yes | No (1-persistent) | Yes (slotted backoff) |
| Carrier Sense | No | No | Yes | Yes |
| Collision Handling | Detect (no recovery) | Detect (no recovery) | Detect & abort | **Avoid** via backoff |
| Max Throughput | 18.4% | 36.8% | ~80–90% | ~40–60% (practical) |
| Used in | Legacy satellite/radio | Legacy satellite | **Ethernet (wired)** | **Wi-Fi 802.11 (wireless)** |
| Hidden node mitigation | None | None | None (irrelevant wired) | **RTS/CTS + NAV** |
| Acknowledgment | No | No | No (Ethernet uses it optionally) | **Mandatory (SIFS ACK)** |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical: Average Backoff Calculation

**Problem:** A 802.11 station has $CW_{\min} = 31$ and $\sigma = 20 \, \mu s$. Calculate the **average backoff time** on the first transmission attempt.

**Solution:**

The backoff is drawn uniformly from $\{0, 1, \dots, 31\}$ — that's 32 possible values.

$$\text{Average slots} = \frac{0 + 1 + 2 + \dots + 31}{32} = \frac{31 \cdot 32 / 2}{32} = \frac{31}{2} = 15.5 \text{ slots}$$

$$\text{Average backoff time} = 15.5 \times 20 \, \mu s = 310 \, \mu s = 0.31 \, ms$$

> [!NOTE]
> **General formula:** The mean of a uniform distribution $\mathcal{U}\{0, N\}$ is $N/2$. So the average backoff is $\frac{CW}{2} \cdot \sigma$.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CSMA/CA State Transition Diagram (DCF — Basic Access)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Sensing : Frame Ready
    Sensing --> DIFS_Wait : Channel Idle
    Sensing --> NAV_Defer : Channel Busy / NAV non-zero
    NAV_Defer --> DIFS_Wait : NAV Expires and Channel Idle
    DIFS_Wait --> Backoff_Select : After DIFS, Channel Still Idle
    Backoff_Select --> Countdown : Pick BO in U[0,CW]
    Countdown --> Countdown : Slot Idle, BO = BO minus 1
    Countdown --> Frozen : Channel Busy
    Frozen --> Countdown : Channel Idle Again after DIFS
    Countdown --> Transmit : BO equals 0
    Transmit --> Wait_ACK : Wait SIFS
    Wait_ACK --> Success : ACK Received
    Wait_ACK --> Failure : ACK Timeout
    Failure --> Backoff_Select : Double CW, Retry i equals i plus 1
    Success --> Idle
    Failure --> Drop : Retry Limit Reached
    Drop --> Idle
```

---

## 4.2 RTS/CTS Handshake Timing Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant STA_A as Station A
    participant AP as Access Point
    participant STA_C as Station C (Hidden)
    STA_A->>STA_A: Sense Channel
    Note over STA_A: Wait DIFS (50 µs)
    STA_A->>AP: RTS (duration = T)
    Note over AP: Wait SIFS (10 µs)
    AP->>STA_A: CTS (duration = T minus RTS)
    Note over STA_C: Hear CTS, Set NAV = T
    Note over STA_A: Wait SIFS (10 µs)
    STA_A->>AP: DATA Frame
    Note over AP: Wait SIFS (10 µs)
    AP->>STA_A: ACK
    Note over STA_C: NAV active, defer transmission
    Note over STA_A: Success, next frame possible after DIFS
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of CSMA/CA

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Upper Layer Frame Arrives at MAC] --> B{Carrier Sense CCA}
    B -- Channel Busy --> C[Set NAV from Frame Header Duration Field]
    C --> D[Defer Transmission]
    D --> B
    B -- Channel Idle --> E[Wait DIFS Timer]
    E --> F{Channel Still Idle After DIFS}
    F -- No --> B
    F -- Yes --> G[Backoff Generator Selects BO in U 0 CW]
    G --> H[Slotted Countdown via Slot Time sigma]
    H --> I{BO Reaches Zero}
    I -- No, Slot Busy --> J[Freeze Counter]
    J --> K[Wait New DIFS After Idle]
    K --> H
    I -- Yes --> L{Use RTS CTS Flag}
    L -- Yes --> M[Send RTS Wait CTS]
    L -- No --> N[Transmit DATA Frame]
    M --> O[CTS Received]
    O --> N
    N --> P[Wait SIFS for ACK]
    P --> Q{ACK Received}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Success Update CW to CWmin]
    Q -- No --> S[Double CW via BEB]
    S --> T{Retry Limit Exceeded}
    T -- No --> G
    T -- Yes --> U[Drop Frame Notify Upper Layer]
    R --> A
    U --> A
```

---

## 4.4 Conceptual Hidden Terminal Scenario

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    STA_A[Station A] -- RTS --> AP[Access Point B]
    STA_C[Station C Hidden] -. cannot hear A .-> AP
    AP -- CTS heard by A and C --> STA_A
    AP -- CTS heard by A and C --> STA_C
    STA_C --> NAV[NAV set from CTS duration, defers transmission]
    STA_A -- DATA --> AP
    AP -- ACK --> STA_A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The dashed line between C and A indicates they cannot directly hear each other (hidden terminal). Without RTS/CTS, both A and C might transmit to AP simultaneously, colliding at AP. The CTS broadcast solves this because *all* stations within AP's range (including C) hear it.

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between CSMA/CD and CSMA/CA. In which type of network is each used and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | CSMA/CD | CSMA/CA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Full Form** | Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection | Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance |
| **Collision Action** | Detects collision, jams, retransmits | Avoids collision via backoff, never detects (cannot hear own signal) |
| **Used in** | Wired Ethernet (802.3) | Wireless LAN (802.11/Wi-Fi) |
| **Reason** | Wired stations can transmit and listen simultaneously with strong signal differentiation | Wireless stations cannot listen while transmitting; near-far problem prevents reliable collision detection |
| **Mechanism** | Slot time, jam signal, truncated binary backoff | DIFS/SIFS, random backoff, ACK, optional RTS/CTS |

**Mark Distribution (3 marks):**
- 1 mark: Definition of each
- 1 mark: Network type used in
- 1 mark: Reason for the choice

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the purpose of the Network Allocation Vector (NAV) in CSMA/CA? How is it set?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **NAV** is a *virtual carrier sense* timer maintained by every 802.11 station. It represents the duration for which the medium is *expected* to remain busy, allowing stations to defer transmission without physically sensing the channel.

**How it is set:**
- When a station receives **any frame** (DATA, RTS, CTS, ACK) whose MAC header contains a *Duration field*, it updates its NAV to the value indicated.
- The NAV counts down only when the physical channel is idle (logical AND of physical and virtual sensing).
- A non-zero NAV is treated as equivalent to a busy channel.

**Why it matters:** It solves the *hidden terminal problem* — a station that cannot physically sense a transmitter can still defer by hearing a CTS frame and setting its NAV accordingly.

**Mark Distribution:**
- 1 mark: NAV definition
- 1 mark: How it is set (Duration field)
- 1 mark: Role in hidden terminal mitigation

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — ESE Module Internal Choice)

> Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the working of CSMA/CA protocol with a neat timing diagram. Discuss the role of DIFS, SIFS, and the backoff counter. (7 marks)

**(b)** With an example, explain how the hidden terminal problem is solved in 802.11 using RTS/CTS mechanism. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution (a):

**CSMA/CA Working Steps (DCF Mode):**

1. A station with a frame to send **senses the channel** (Clear Channel Assessment).
2. If the channel is **idle**, it waits for a **DIFS** (DCF Interframe Space) period. If idle throughout DIFS, it proceeds.
3. A **backoff counter** is randomly chosen from $\{0, 1, \dots, CW\}$.
4. The counter decrements by 1 for every *slot time* in which the medium is idle. If the medium becomes busy, the counter is **frozen** and resumes only after another DIFS idle period.
5. When the counter reaches 0, the station **transmits the frame**.
6. The station waits **SIFS** for an **ACK** from the receiver. If ACK arrives, success. If not, the contention window is **doubled** (BEB) and the process restarts.

**Role of timing intervals:**

- **DIFS (50 µs):** Standard waiting time for data frames. Lower priority than SIFS/PIFS.
- **SIFS (10 µs):** Shortest interval, used for ACK, CTS, and fragment bursts. Provides **priority** to control frames.
- **Backoff counter:** Provides **statistical independence** among contenders, breaking the symmetry that would otherwise cause repeated collisions.

**Timing Diagram (Textual Representation for Answer Sheet):**

```
      |<-DIFS->|<--Backoff Slots (BO=5)-->|<-DATA->|<-SIFS->|<-ACK->|
TX    __________|_|_|_|_|_|FFFFFFFFFFFF___________________________
CHANNEL  BUSY  IDLE  IDLE IDLE IDLE IDLE IDLE     BUSY  IDLE BUSY IDLE
                                    Tx
```

*(Students should draw a clean diagram with time axis on X-axis and channel state on Y-axis.)*

**Mark Allocation (a):**
- 2 marks: Step-by-step explanation
- 2 marks: Role of DIFS, SIFS, Backoff
- 2 marks: Timing diagram
- 1 mark: Conclusion/clarity

#### Model Solution (b):

**Hidden Terminal Problem:**

Consider three stations: **A**, **B** (AP), **C**. A and C are within range of B but **out of range of each other**. If A and C both sense the channel as idle (since they cannot hear each other), they may transmit to B simultaneously, causing a collision at B that neither detects.

**RTS/CTS Solution:**

1. A senses the medium idle, waits DIFS, and sends an **RTS** frame to B. The RTS contains a *Duration* field specifying the total time required for the upcoming exchange.
2. B waits SIFS and replies with a **CTS** frame. The CTS also contains the *Duration* field.
3. **A hears CTS** → it knows the channel is reserved and starts transmitting DATA after SIFS.
4. **C hears CTS** (even though it never heard A's RTS) → C sets its **NAV** to the duration announced in the CTS and defers transmission for that entire period.
5. B waits SIFS and sends **ACK** upon successful receipt.
6. C's NAV has not yet expired, so it continues to defer.

**Result:** No collision occurs at B. The hidden terminal C is informed *via B* that the medium is busy.

**Mark Allocation (b):**
- 2 marks: Hidden terminal problem explanation
- 2 marks: RTS/CTS message exchange steps
- 2 marks: Role of Duration field and NAV
- 1 mark: Conclusion

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB) algorithm used in CSMA/CA. A station has $CW_{\min} = 31$ and $CW_{\max} = 1023$. Calculate the contention window size after 3 and 5 retries. (7 marks)

**(b)** A wireless LAN has 20 contending stations. Estimate the maximum throughput using the CSMA/CA bound formula with $CW_{\min} = 31$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution (a):

**BEB Algorithm:**

In CSMA/CA, after every failed transmission (no ACK within SIFS timeout), the contention window is doubled to spread contenders apart more widely and statistically reduce collision probability.

$$CW_i = \min\!\left(2^i \cdot (CW_{\min}+1) - 1, \; CW_{\max}\right)$$

```
Initial:        CW = 31
After 1 retry:  CW = 63
After 2 retries: CW = 127
After 3 retries: CW = 255
After 4 retries: CW = 511
After 5 retries: CW = 1023
After 6 retries: CW = 1023 (capped at CW_max)
```

**Neat Diagram (Backoff State Machine):**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> FirstAttempt
    FirstAttempt --> Success : ACK Received
    FirstAttempt --> Retry1 : ACK Timeout, CW = 63
    Retry1 --> Success2 : ACK Received
    Retry1 --> Retry2 : ACK Timeout, CW = 127
    Retry2 --> Success3 : ACK Received
    Retry2 --> Retry3 : ACK Timeout, CW = 255
    Retry3 --> Retry4 : CW = 511
    Retry4 --> Retry5 : CW = 1023
    Retry5 --> RetryCapped : CW stays 1023
    RetryCapped --> Drop : Retry Limit Exceeded
    Success --> [*]
    Success2 --> [*]
    Success3 --> [*]
    Drop --> [*]
```

**Calculations:**

**After 3 retries ($i = 3$):**
$$CW_3 = 2^3 \cdot (31+1) - 1 = 8 \cdot 32 - 1 = 256 - 1 = 255$$
Backoff range: $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, 255\}$ — i.e., **255**.

**After 5 retries ($i = 5$):**
$$CW_5 = 2^5 \cdot (31+1) - 1 = 32 \cdot 32 - 1 = 1024 - 1 = 1023$$
Backoff range: $\{0, 1, \dots, 1023\}$ — i.e., **1023** (this is also the maximum cap $CW_{\max}$).

**Mark Allocation (a):**
- 2 marks: BEB explanation and formula
- 1 mark: Diagram
- 2 marks: Calculation after 3 retries
- 2 marks: Calculation after 5 retries

#### Model Solution (b):

**Given:** $n = 20$ stations, $CW_{\min} = 31$.

Using the simplified CSMA/CA saturation throughput bound:

$$S_{\max} \approx \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot CW_{\min}}{n}}}$$

**Step 1 — Substituting values:**

$$S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot 31}{20}}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the inner fraction:**

$$\frac{2 \cdot 31}{20} = \frac{62}{20} = 3.1$$

**Step 3 — Take the square root:**

$$\sqrt{3.1} \approx 1.7607$$

**Step 4 — Add 1 in the denominator:**

$$1 + 1.7607 = 2.7607$$

**Step 5 — Compute the final throughput:**

$$S_{\max} = \frac{1}{2.7607} \approx 0.3622$$

**Result:** $S_{\max} \approx 0.362$ or **36.2%** of the raw channel capacity.

**Mark Allocation (b):**
- 2 marks: Formula statement
- 2 marks: Substitution
- 2 marks: Step-by-step arithmetic
- 1 mark: Final answer with units/interpretation

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in CSMA/CA Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing DIFS with SIFS** — DIFS is for *data* deferral; SIFS is for *control frames* (ACK, CTS, fragment bursts). Many students write "wait for SIFS before sending data" which is wrong.
> 2. **Forgetting the IFS priority order** — The correct order is `SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS`. A shorter IFS means higher priority. Examiners love to ask this.
> 3. **Wrong backoff formula** — The most common error is forgetting the $-1$ and using $2^i \cdot CW_{\min}$ directly. Use the canonical form $2^i \cdot (CW_{\min}+1) - 1$.
> 4. **Confusing the role of ACK** — In CSMA/CD (Ethernet), ACK is optional; in CSMA/CA (Wi-Fi), it is **mandatory** at the MAC layer. No ACK = failure assumption.
> 5. **Not mentioning NAV** — When explaining RTS/CTS, students often describe the message exchange but forget to mention the **Duration field** and **NAV setting**, which is what *actually* tells other stations to defer. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 6. **Wrong throughput formula** — The CSMA/CA throughput bound is $\dfrac{1}{1+\sqrt{2 CW_{\min}/n}}$. Don't confuse it with the ALOHA bound of $1/(2e)$ or slotted ALOHA's $1/e$.
> 7. **Hidden terminal = CSMA/CA, not CSMA/CD** — Always state clearly that CSMA/CD does *not* have this issue (it is irrelevant in wired media).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — CSMA/CA**

- ✅ **CSMA/CA = Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance** — used in **IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi)**.
- ✅ Three core ideas: **Sense, Avoid, Acknowledge**.
- ✅ Cannot detect collisions → must **avoid** them via **randomized backoff**.
- ✅ **Mandatory ACK** at the MAC layer; no ACK ⇒ assume failure.
- ✅ IFS priority order: **SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS** (shorter = higher priority).
- ✅ **DIFS = SIFS + 2 × Slot Time** for DSSS PHY: $10 + 2(20) = 50 \, \mu s$.
- ✅ Backoff range: $BO \in \{0, 1, \dots, CW\}$; average wait = $CW/2$ slots.
- ✅ **Binary Exponential Backoff (BEB):** $CW_i = \min(2^i \cdot (CW_{\min}+1) - 1, CW_{\max})$.
- ✅ Standard values: $CW_{\min} = 31$, $CW_{\max} = 1023$, slot = $20 \, \mu s$.
- ✅ **RTS/CTS** solves the **hidden terminal problem** by broadcasting a *Duration* field.
- ✅ **NAV (Network Allocation Vector)** = virtual carrier sense; set by Duration field in RTS/CTS/DATA headers.
- ✅ Throughput bound (Bianchi approximation): $S \approx \dfrac{1}{1 + \sqrt{2 CW_{\min}/n}}$.
- ✅ Throughput *decreases* with more stations and *initially increases* with smaller $CW_{\min}$ (then decreases due to collisions).
- ✅ Compare with: Pure ALOHA (18.4%), Slotted ALOHA (36.8%), CSMA/CD (Ethernet, high throughput).
- ✅ Used in: **Wi-Fi (802.11a/b/g/n/ac/ax), ZigBee (802.15.4), WirelessHART, certain IoT protocols**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routing and Forwarding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT414 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Routing and Forwarding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Routing — Formal Definition

In the architecture of the **Network Layer** (Layer 3 of the OSI/TCP-IP reference model), **Routing** is the **network-wide, control-plane process** of determining the end-to-end path that data packets should take from a source host to a destination host. It is executed by routing algorithms (protocols) operating on the routers distributed across the internetwork.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Routing is the process of building and maintaining a routing table at every router using distributed algorithms, deciding the *next-hop* for each known destination prefix. It is a **control-plane** function operating on time-scales of seconds to minutes.*

### 1.2 Forwarding — Formal Definition

**Forwarding** is the **local, data-plane (per-packet) action** performed by a router when an incoming packet arrives. The router inspects the destination address in the packet header, performs a lookup in its **Forwarding Information Base (FIB)** (built from the routing table), and switches the packet to the appropriate outgoing interface. It operates at line speed (microseconds to nanoseconds per packet).

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Indian Postal System**
> Think of the postal network of India.
> - **Routing** = The Postmaster General designing the routes, deciding which regional hub connects to which sorting office, and updating these maps as new cities are added. (Slow, strategic, network-wide.)
> - **Forwarding** = The local postman at the Kochi sorting office who simply reads the PIN code on an envelope, checks his delivery register, and drops it into the bucket marked "Trivandrum via Kollam." (Fast, local, per-letter.)
>
> The *postman* does not care *how* the city network was planned; he only uses the locally available register (the FIB).

### 1.3 Key Distinction Table

| Aspect | Routing (Control Plane) | Forwarding (Data Plane) |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Network-wide (all routers) | Local (single router) |
| Time scale | Seconds – Minutes (slow) | Nanoseconds – Microseconds (fast) |
| Function | Builds the routing table | Uses the routing table |
| Mechanism | Routing algorithms/protocols (OSPF, BGP, RIP) | Lookup in FIB, switch fabric |
| Trigger | Topology changes, link failures | Every arriving packet |
| Location | Software / routing process | Hardware (ASICs/TCAMs) |

### 1.4 Classification of Routing Algorithms (KTU 2024 Module-3 Map)

- **Static vs Dynamic Routing**
  - *Static*: Manual entries, no fault tolerance.
  - *Dynamic*: Adapts to topology/link-cost changes.
- **Centralized (Link-State) vs Decentralized (Distance-Vector)**
- **Intra-AS (Interior) vs Inter-AS (Exterior)**
  - Intra-AS Protocols: **RIP, OSPF**
  - Inter-AS Protocol: **BGP**
- **Single-Path vs Multi-Path Routing**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Routing Problem — Mathematical Formulation

We model a network as a **weighted graph** $G = (V, E)$, where:
- $V$ = set of nodes (routers and hosts)
- $E$ = set of edges (links)
- $c(x, v)$ = cost of the link from node $x$ to its neighbour $v$ (typically inverse of bandwidth, or simply hop count = 1).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** For every node $x$ and every destination $y$, find the **least-cost path** $P^{*}(x, y)$ and its associated cost $D_x(y)$.

### 2.2 The Bellman-Ford Equation (Foundation of Distance-Vector Routing)

The shortest-path cost from $x$ to $y$, denoted $D_x(y)$, satisfies the following **recurrence relation**, known as the **Bellman-Ford optimal-substructure equation**:

$$
D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \{\, c(x, v) + D_v(y) \,\}
$$

Where $N(x)$ is the set of *direct neighbours* of $x$.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Consider taking the first hop from $x$ to some neighbour $v \in N(x)$.
2. The cost of this first hop is $c(x, v)$.
3. From $v$, the least cost to reach $y$ is $D_v(y)$ (assumed known or learned).
4. The total cost via $v$ is $c(x, v) + D_v(y)$.
5. The minimum over **all** possible first-hop neighbours $v$ gives the true shortest path.
6. The neighbour $v^{*}$ achieving this minimum becomes the **next-hop** in the routing table of $x$.

### 2.3 Properties & Engineering Real-World Use

- **Self-Describing & Distributed**: Each node only needs to know its *direct* link costs and the *vectors* (distance estimates) advertised by its neighbours.
- **Iterative & Asynchronous**: Updates are exchanged periodically; no global synchronisation required.
- **Real-world deployment**: The Internet uses variants of this principle in **RIP** (hop-count metric) and in the path-vector extensions of **BGP** (with policy attributes).
- **Engineering fields**: Used in large-scale overlay networks, SDN control planes, and any graph-routing problem (VLSI layout, robotics path planning, airline scheduling).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Boundary Conditions

| Quantity / Symbol | Mathematical Expression | Meaning / Unit | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| Least-cost distance from $x$ to $y$ | $D_x(y)$ | Hop count, ms, or composite cost | $D_x(x) = 0$; $D_x(y) \geq 0$ |
| Link cost edge $(x,v)$ | $c(x,v)$ | Integer or float | $c(x,v) > 0$ (positive weights in DV) |
| Bellman-Ford recurrence | $D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \{c(x,v) + D_v(y)\}$ | Recursive definition | Foundation of DV routing |
| Update rule (iterative DV) | $D_x(y) \leftarrow \min_v \{c(x,v) + D_v(y)\}$ | Converges in at most $\vert V \vert - 1$ iterations | Count-to-infinity problem arises |
| Dijkstra step | $D(v) = \min\{ D(v),\, D(w) + c(w,v) \}$ | Greedy relaxation | Requires global topology at each node |
| Convergence (LS) | After $O(\vert E \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ with Fibonacci heap | Time | All nodes complete the SPF tree |
| Maximum hops (RIP) | $15$ | Hops | $\infty = 16$ (unreachable) |
| OSPF cost | $\text{Reference BW} \div \text{Link BW}$ | $c(x,v)$ | Default ref. BW = 100 Mbps |

### 2.5 Distance-Vector (DV) vs Link-State (LS) — Quick Reference

| Feature | Distance-Vector | Link-State |
|---|---|---|
| Information exchanged | Whole distance table to neighbours only | Link-state packets (LSP) to *all* nodes |
| Algorithm class | Iterative, asynchronous, distributed | Global, centralised computation at each node |
| Complexity (Bellman-Ford) | Per iteration: $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$ | Per run (Dijkstra): $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ naive, $O(\vert E \vert + \vert V \log \vert V \vert)$ with heap |
| Convergence speed | Slow (count-to-infinity possible) | Fast, no count-to-infinity |
| Robustness | A node's bad advertisement propagates widely | Each node computes its own table; errors localised |
| Example protocols | RIP, IGRP | OSPF, IS-IS |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Bellman-Ford Optimal-Substructure Equation

We formally prove that the shortest path $P^{*}(x, y)$ from $x$ to $y$ can be decomposed into a *first hop* and a *shortest sub-path*.

**Step 1 — Set up the path structure.**
Let $P^{*}(x, y)$ be a least-cost path from $x$ to $y$ with cost $D_x(y)$. Suppose the first edge of $P^{*}(x, y)$ goes from $x$ to a neighbour $v \in N(x)$.

$$
P^{*}(x, y) = \{x \rightarrow v \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow y\}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the sub-path.**
The remainder of the path, $P^{*}(v, y) = \{v \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow y\}$, must itself be a least-cost path from $v$ to $y$. If it were not, we could replace it with a cheaper sub-path and reduce the total cost, contradicting optimality of $P^{*}(x, y)$.

$$
\text{Cost of } P^{*}(v, y) = D_v(y)
$$

**Step 3 — Write the total cost as a sum.**
The cost of the full path is the cost of the first edge plus the cost of the optimal sub-path.

$$
D_x(y) = c(x, v) + D_v(y)
$$

**Step 4 — Optimise over the choice of the first-hop neighbour.**
Since we do not know *which* neighbour $v$ lies on the optimal path, we must minimise over the entire set of direct neighbours $N(x)$.

$$
D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \{\, c(x, v) + D_v(y) \,\}
$$

This is the **Bellman-Ford equation**. It forms the basis of the iterative Distance-Vector algorithm: a node repeatedly updates $D_x(\cdot)$ using only the latest distance vectors received from its neighbours, until no further changes occur (convergence).

> [!NOTE]
> The derivation is valid only when **all link costs are non-negative** ($c(x, v) \geq 0$). Negative cycles would invalidate the equation — fortunately, in real network routing, costs are always non-negative.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Distance-Vector Iteration (Count-to-Infinity)

Consider 3 routers $A$, $B$, $C$ in a line topology.

$$
A \;\overset{1}{\longleftrightarrow}\; B \;\overset{1}{\longleftrightarrow}\; C
$$

Suppose the link between $A$ and $B$ fails. Trace the DV table for $C$ (destination is $A$).

| Iteration | $C$'s DV entry for $A$ via $B$ | $B$'s DV entry for $A$ | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $t_0$ | $D_C(A) = 2$ | $D_B(A) = 1$ | Initial state, link $A$–$B$ alive |
| $t_1$ | $D_C(A) = 2$ | $D_B(A) = \infty$ | $A$–$B$ link dies, $B$ sets to 16 (RIP) |
| $t_2$ | $D_C(A) = 3$ | $D_B(A) = 4$ (via $C$) | $B$ hears $C$'s stale entry of 2, adds 1 |
| $t_3$ | $D_C(A) = 4$ | $D_B(A) = 3$ (via $C$) | $C$ hears $B$'s inflated 3, adds 1 |
| $t_4$ | $D_C(A) = 5$ | $D_B(A) = 4$ (via $C$) | Continue incrementing... |

This slow drift is the famous **count-to-infinity problem**, mitigated in practice by **split horizon**, **poison reverse**, and **triggered updates**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Dijkstra's Link-State Algorithm

Dijkstra's algorithm computes single-source shortest paths on a graph with **non-negative** weights.

**Inputs:**
- $c(x, v)$ for all edges, known to the source node $u$ (flooded via LSPs).
- Source node $u$.

**Outputs:**
- $D(v)$ = cost of shortest path $u \rightarrow v$.
- $p(v)$ = predecessor of $v$ on the shortest path.

**Data structures:**
- $N'$ = set of nodes whose final shortest path is *known* (the "permanently labelled" set).
- Initial: $N' = \{ u \}$, $D(u) = 0$, $D(v) = c(u, v)$ for $v \neq u$, $p(v) = u$, $D(v) = \infty$ if $v$ not a neighbour.

**Iterative Step:** Among all nodes $w \notin N'$, pick the one with the *minimum* tentative distance $D(w)$, add it to $N'$, and *relax* all outgoing edges of $w$:

$$
\forall v \notin N' : \quad D(v) \leftarrow \min\{\, D(v),\; D(w) + c(w, v) \,\}
$$

Repeat until $N' = V$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Dijkstra's Algorithm on a 6-Node Graph

**Topology** (weights are link costs):

| Edge | Cost | Edge | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| $(u, v)$ | 2 | $(w, y)$ | 1 |
| $(u, w)$ | 5 | $(w, z)$ | 5 |
| $(u, x)$ | 1 | $(x, y)$ | 1 |
| $(v, w)$ | 3 | $(y, z)$ | 2 |
| $(v, x)$ | 2 | | |

**Step 0 — Initialisation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N' &= \{ u \} \\
D(u) &= 0,\; D(v) = 2,\; D(w) = 5,\; D(x) = 1,\; D(y) = \infty,\; D(z) = \infty \\
p(v) &= u,\; p(w) = u,\; p(x) = u
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Pick minimum $D$ outside $N'$**

Minimum is $D(x) = 1$. Add $x$ to $N'$.

$$
N' = \{ u, x \}
$$

Relax edges from $x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D(v) &= \min\{ 2,\; 1 + 2 \} = 2 \quad (\text{no change, } p(v) = u) \\
D(w) &= \min\{ 5,\; 1 + 3 \} = 4 \quad (p(w) \leftarrow x) \\
D(y) &= \min\{ \infty,\; 1 + 1 \} = 2 \quad (p(y) \leftarrow x) \\
D(z) &= \min\{ \infty,\; 1 + \infty \} = \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Pick minimum**

Tie between $D(v) = 2$ and $D(y) = 2$. Pick $v$ first.

$$
N' = \{ u, x, v \}
$$

Relax edges from $v$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D(w) &= \min\{ 4,\; 2 + 3 \} = 4 \quad (\text{no change}) \\
D(y) &= \min\{ 2,\; 2 + \infty \} = 2 \quad (\text{no change}) \\
D(z) &= \min\{ \infty,\; 2 + \infty \} = \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Pick minimum $D(y) = 2$**

$$
N' = \{ u, x, v, y \}
$$

Relax edges from $y$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D(w) &= \min\{ 4,\; 2 + 1 \} = 3 \quad (p(w) \leftarrow y) \\
D(z) &= \min\{ \infty,\; 2 + 2 \} = 4 \quad (p(z) \leftarrow y)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Pick minimum $D(w) = 3$**

$$
N' = \{ u, x, v, y, w \}
$$

Relax edges from $w$:

$$
D(z) = \min\{ 4,\; 3 + 5 \} = 4 \quad (\text{no change})
$$

**Step 5 — Final node $z$**

$$
N' = \{ u, x, v, y, w, z \},\; D(z) = 4,\; p(z) = y
$$

---

### 3.5 Final Routing Table for Node $u$

| Destination | Shortest Path | Cost $D_u(\cdot)$ | Next Hop |
|---|---|---|---|
| $v$ | $u \rightarrow v$ | 2 | $v$ |
| $w$ | $u \rightarrow x \rightarrow y \rightarrow w$ | 3 | $x$ |
| $x$ | $u \rightarrow x$ | 1 | $x$ |
| $y$ | $u \rightarrow x \rightarrow y$ | 2 | $x$ |
| $z$ | $u \rightarrow x \rightarrow y \rightarrow z$ | 4 | $x$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the routing table only stores the **next hop** (one entry per destination), not the entire path. This is exactly the *forwarding* contract that the *routing* algorithm has computed.

---

### 3.6 Hierarchical Routing — Aggregation Analysis

The Internet is partitioned into **Autonomous Systems (AS)**, each owned by a single administrative domain (an ISP, a university, a corporate network).

- **Intra-AS routing**: Within an AS, routers run the *same* protocol (e.g., OSPF). Every router knows every destination inside its AS.
- **Inter-AS routing**: Between ASes, **BGP** is used; ASes advertise reachability of *prefixes* (aggregated address blocks).
- **Aggregation reduces table size**: A small AS in Kerala connected to a single upstream ISP only needs one default route plus any internal prefixes — not the full Internet table (~900,000+ prefixes).

**Mathematical Effect:** If an AS has $N$ routers and is grouped into $k$ clusters, the per-router table size shrinks from $O(N)$ to roughly $O(N/k + \text{external prefixes})$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Routing vs Forwarding Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CP["CONTROL PLANE  ROUTING"]
        A1[Routing Protocol Engine] --> A2[Routing Algorithm]
        A2 --> A3[Routing Table Builder]
        A3 --> A4[Routing Table RIB]
        N1[Neighbour DV or LS Updates] --> A1
    end
    subgraph DP["DATA PLANE  FORWARDING"]
        B1[Incoming Packet Arrives] --> B2[Parse Header Extract Dest IP]
        B2 --> B3[Lookup in FIB  Longest Prefix Match]
        B3 --> B4{Entry Found}
        B4 -- Yes --> B5[Switch Fabric]
        B5 --> B6[Output Queue on Egress Interface]
        B4 -- No --> B7[Send ICMP Unreachable to Source]
    end
    A4 -.Publishes best routes.-> B3
    N1 -.Topology changes trigger recompute.-> A1
```

### 4.2 Algorithm Flow — Dijkstra's SPF Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S0[Initialise N prime equals u] --> S1[Set D of u equals 0]
    S1 --> S2[Set D of v equals c of u comma v for all v]
    S2 --> S3{Any node outside N prime}
    S3 -- No --> S4[Algorithm complete, output tree]
    S3 -- Yes --> S5[Pick w with minimum D of w outside N prime]
    S5 --> S6[Add w to N prime]
    S6 --> S7[For each neighbour v of w not in N prime]
    S7 --> S8[D of v equals min of D of v and D of w plus c of w comma v]
    S8 --> S3
```

### 4.3 Autonomous System Hierarchy — The Internet Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Tier1["Tier 1 Transit AS  e g Tata Comm  Verizon"]
    subgraph KER["Kerala Region"]
        ISP1["AS 64512  Regional ISP A"]
        ISP2["AS 64513  Regional ISP B"]
        Campus["AS 64514  KTU Campus Network"]
        ISP1 --- Campus
        ISP2 --- Campus
    end
    Tier1 --- ISP1
    Tier1 --- ISP2
    ISP1 -- Intra AS OSPF --> R1A[Router A1]
    ISP1 -- Intra AS OSPF --> R1B[Router A2]
    ISP2 -- Intra AS OSPF --> R2A[Router B1]
    ISP2 -- Intra AS OSPF --> R2B[Router B2]
    Tier1 -- Inter AS BGP --> Tier2["Other Tier 1 AS"]
```

### 4.4 Convergence Sequence — Distance Vector Updates

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[t0  Initial  A to C equals 2] --> T1[t1  Link A B fails]
    T1 --> T2[t2  B advertises infinity for A]
    T2 --> T3[t3  C still believes 2  stale]
    T3 --> T4[t4  C sends 2 to B  poison reverse kicks in]
    T4 --> T5[t5  Slow count to infinity]
    T5 --> T6[t6  Triggered updates propagate]
    T6 --> T7[t7  Eventually both reach 16  unreachable]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid does not natively render physical network graphs with curved edges; the flowcharts above capture the **functional block-level architecture** of routing/forwarding, the SPF algorithm, the AS hierarchy, and the DV convergence timeline — all of which are common KTU 2024 diagram questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q.A1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Differentiate between routing and forwarding in computer networks.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Routing | Forwarding |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | A *control-plane* process that determines the end-to-end path for packets. | A *data-plane* action that moves a packet from an input port to the appropriate output port. |
| 2 | Operates on time-scales of seconds to minutes. | Operates on a per-packet basis at line speed. |
| 3 | Builds the routing table using algorithms such as Bellman-Ford or Dijkstra. | Uses the forwarding table (FIB) to make a *longest-prefix match* on the destination IP. |
| 4 | Triggered by topology changes. | Triggered by every packet arrival. |

*(Any 3 valid contrasting points → 3 marks.)*

---

**Q.A2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**What is the count-to-infinity problem in distance-vector routing? Mention two solutions.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The **count-to-infinity problem** occurs when a link failure causes distance vectors in a DV network to be updated slowly, with routers iteratively incrementing their distance to a now-unreachable destination until the metric reaches the maximum (e.g., 16 in RIP). This slow convergence wastes bandwidth and may cause loops.
**Two standard solutions:**
1. **Split Horizon** — Do not advertise a route back to the neighbour from whom the route was learned.
2. **Poison Reverse** — Advertise the route back with metric = $\infty$ (16) to immediately invalidate it.
3. **Triggered Updates** — Send updates immediately upon detecting a change instead of waiting for the periodic timer.
*(Any 2 solutions — 1 mark each; 1 mark for explaining the problem.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Q.B1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`**
**(a)** Explain the **Distance-Vector Routing** algorithm with a neat block diagram. Discuss the Bellman-Ford equation and the count-to-infinity problem. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For the given network, compute the distance-vector tables at all nodes iteratively until convergence. Identify the count-to-infinity behaviour. *(7 marks)*

*Network: Three routers A, B, C in a line. A—B cost = 1, B—C cost = 1. Initial distance from A to C is 2. The link A—B fails at t = 0.*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Distance-Vector is a distributed, iterative, asynchronous routing algorithm in which each router maintains a table (vector) giving the best known distance to every destination and shares it with directly connected neighbours.
- **Bellman-Ford Equation (2 marks):**
  $$D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \{ c(x, v) + D_v(y) \}$$
  Each node iteratively refines its distance estimates using only the vectors advertised by its neighbours.
- **Algorithm Steps (2 marks):**
  1. Initialise each node's distance to self = 0, to direct neighbours = link cost, to all others = $\infty$.
  2. Each node sends its DV to all neighbours.
  3. On receiving a neighbour's DV, recompute own DV using the Bellman-Ford update.
  4. If any entry changes, notify neighbours. Repeat until no change (convergence).
- **Count-to-Infinity (1 mark):** When a link fails, the old (stale) value of the lost destination slowly propagates back, causing the metric to increment one step per iteration — *e.g., 2 → 3 → 4 → … → 16 (RIP)*. The network remains in a sub-optimal state for many seconds.
- **Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1 (routing control plane).

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

*Show the iterative table for routers A, B, C with destination A, B, C. The cost to *themselves* is 0. Initially:*

| Node | To A | To B | To C |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| B | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| C | 2 | 1 | 0 |

*At t = 1, link A—B dies. B sets $D_B(A) = 16$ (unreachable). C still believes $D_C(A) = 2$.*

| Iteration | $D_C(A)$ via B | $D_B(A)$ via C | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| t0 | 2 | 1 | Initial |
| t1 | 2 | 16 | Link A—B dies |
| t2 | 16 | 17 → clipped to 16 (RIP) | B hears C's stale 2, computes 1+2 = 3, advertises 3 |
| t3 | 16 (stable) | 16 (stable) | C hears 3 from B, computes 1+3 = 4 — but using poison reverse, C never advertised to B in the first place, so this stabilises at 16 |

*(Without poison reverse the value would have crept up: 2 → 3 → 4 → 5 … → 16 — the count-to-infinity.)*

**Mark split for (b):** Initial table (2) + iteration trace showing creep (3) + identification of count-to-infinity and the role of poison reverse (2).

---

**Q.B2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]` (Internal Choice to Q.B1)**
**(a)** Explain the **Link-State Routing** algorithm and Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm with a suitable example of at least 5 nodes. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Apply Dijkstra's algorithm to the graph below and show the shortest path tree from node $S$. Populate the routing table at $S$. *(7 marks)*

*Graph: S—A=7, S—B=2, S—C=3, A—B=3, A—D=4, B—D=4, B—C=1, C—D=1, D—E=5, C—E=8.*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Link-State routing uses a global, replicated topology database at every node and computes shortest paths locally using Dijkstra's algorithm.
- **Components (2 marks):**
  1. Each router discovers its neighbours and link costs (via HELLO packets, e.g., OSPF).
  2. It constructs a **Link-State Packet (LSP)** containing its own link costs and floods it to *all* routers using reliable flooding.
  3. Every router builds the identical **LSDB** (Link-State Database).
  4. Each router runs **Dijkstra's algorithm** locally to compute its shortest-path tree.
- **Dijkstra Algorithm (2 marks):** Initialise $D(S) = 0$, all others = $\infty$. Repeatedly pick the unvisited node $w$ with smallest $D(w)$, mark it visited, and relax neighbours: $D(v) = \min\{D(v), D(w) + c(w, v)\}$.
- **Comparison with DV (1 mark):** LS converges fast, has no count-to-infinity, but requires more memory and CPU per router; the size of LSPs and flooding traffic is a concern in very large networks.
- **Diagram (1 mark):** LSDB + Dijkstra block (refer Section 4.2).

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

*Trace Dijkstra from S.*

| Step | Visited $N'$ | $D(S)$ | $D(A)$ | $D(B)$ | $D(C)$ | $D(D)$ | $D(E)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $\{S\}$ | 0 | 7 | 2 | 3 | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| 1 | $\{S, B\}$ | 0 | 7 | 2 | $\min(3, 2+1) = 3$ | $\min(\infty, 2+4) = 6$ | $\infty$ |
| 2 | $\{S, B, C\}$ | 0 | 7 | 2 | 3 | $\min(6, 3+1) = 4$ | $\min(\infty, 3+8) = 11$ |
| 3 | $\{S, B, C, D\}$ | 0 | $\min(7, 4+4) = 7$ | 2 | 3 | 4 | $\min(11, 4+5) = 9$ |
| 4 | $\{S, B, C, D, A\}$ | 0 | 7 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 9 |
| 5 | All | 0 | 7 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 9 |

*Final shortest path tree from S:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
S \rightarrow B &: \text{cost } 2, \text{ path } S \text{—} B \\
S \rightarrow C &: \text{cost } 3, \text{ path } S \text{—} B \text{—} C \\
S \rightarrow D &: \text{cost } 4, \text{ path } S \text{—} B \text{—} C \text{—} D \\
S \rightarrow A &: \text{cost } 7, \text{ path } S \text{—} A \\
S \rightarrow E &: \text{cost } 9, \text{ path } S \text{—} B \text{—} C \text{—} D \text{—} E
\end{aligned}
$$

*Routing table at S:*

| Destination | Next Hop | Cost |
|---|---|---|
| A | A | 7 |
| B | B | 2 |
| C | B | 3 |
| D | B | 4 |
| E | B | 9 |

**Mark split for (b):** Initialisation (1) + step-by-step table (4) + final routing table with next-hops (2).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "next hop" with "full path"** — the routing table stores only the next hop, not the complete path. Examiners will deduct 1 mark if you list the full path inside the table.
> 2. **Forgetting to set $D(\text{self}) = 0$** in Dijkstra initialisation — this is a routine ½-mark cut.
> 3. **Not marking "visited" nodes in Dijkstra** — leading to repeated updates. Always show the $N'$ set evolution.
> 4. **Wrong application of poison reverse** — students often say "split horizon fixes count-to-infinity" — it only *limits* it; poison reverse + triggered updates together are the standard RIP fix.
> 5. **Mixing intra-AS and inter-AS** — write **OSPF/RIP** for intra-AS and **BGP** for inter-AS. Examiners check this terminology strictly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Routing = control-plane (slow, network-wide); Forwarding = data-plane (fast, per-packet).**
- The **Bellman-Ford equation** $D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)}\{c(x,v) + D_v(y)\}$ is the mathematical heart of **Distance-Vector** routing (RIP).
- **Dijkstra's algorithm** is the heart of **Link-State** routing (OSPF); complexity $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ naive, $O(\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ with a heap.
- **Count-to-infinity** is unique to DV; mitigations: split horizon, poison reverse, triggered updates, max hop count (RIP uses 16).
- **Hierarchical routing** via Autonomous Systems (Intra-AS = OSPF/RIP, Inter-AS = BGP) makes the Internet's $\sim 900$k-prefix table scalable.
- A **routing table** lists *destination → next hop + cost*; a **forwarding table (FIB)** is its hardware-optimised, TCAM-cached form.
- For KTU numericals: always show the **visited set $N'$** evolving in Dijkstra and the **iteration index $t$** in DV.
- Watch for the **KTU buzzwords**: "control plane", "data plane", "Bellman-Ford", "longest-prefix match", "FIB", "RIB", "LSDB", "AS", "BGP".
- **Convergence guarantee** (Bellman-Ford): in at most $\vert V \vert - 1$ iterations in a static network with no negative cycles.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static Routing and Dynamic Routing
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT414 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Static Routing and Dynamic Routing

# Module 3: Static Routing and Dynamic Routing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Routing?

**Routing** is the process of selecting a path for traffic across one or more networks. It is the network-layer function performed by routers (Layer-3 devices) that determines the optimal route for an IP packet from its source to destination based on a maintained **Routing Table**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Routing is the act of moving information across an internetwork from a source to a destination, involving two primary activities: (1) Determination of optimal routes (using routing algorithms), and (2) Transport of information packets (forwarding/switching) through the internetwork.

### 1.2 Classification of Routing

Routing in IP networks is broadly classified into two fundamental categories:

| Category | Trigger for Route Updates | Network Size Suitability |
|----------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| Static Routing | Manually configured by administrator | Small, stable networks |
| Dynamic Routing | Automatically learned via routing protocols | Large, frequently changing networks |

---

### 1.3 Static Routing — Definition & Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Static Routing is a routing mechanism in which routes are manually entered into the routing table by the network administrator and remain unchanged until manually modified. The router does not exchange routing information with neighboring routers and cannot automatically discover new paths.

#### Conceptual Analogy: The Train Conductor with a Fixed Timetable
Imagine a railway station where the **conductor has a printed, paper timetable** pinned to his desk. He does not call other stations to ask about delays or track blocks. He simply refers to his static paper sheet and directs every train according to pre-decided fixed tracks. If a track is closed due to a landslide, the train **cannot reroute itself** — it just stops until a human supervisor rewrites the timetable. Static routing works the same way: the administrator writes the routes, and the router blindly follows them forever.

**Characteristics of Static Routing:**
- Routes are **manually configured** by the network administrator.
- Router does **not exchange routing updates** with other routers.
- Suitable for **small networks** (typically fewer than 10–15 routers).
- **No CPU/memory overhead** on the router for running routing algorithms.
- **High administrative overhead** when the network topology changes (every change must be manually updated).
- Provides **maximum security** because no routing information is advertised.
- Used for defining **Default Routes (0.0.0.0/0)** and for stub networks.

---

### 1.4 Dynamic Routing — Definition & Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Dynamic Routing is a routing mechanism in which routers automatically build and maintain their routing tables by exchanging topology information with neighboring routers using specialized routing protocols (such as RIP, OSPF, EIGRP, BGP). When a topology change occurs, the routing protocol automatically recalculates paths and updates the routing tables.

#### Conceptual Analogy: The GPS-Based Taxi Dispatcher
Consider a modern **taxi dispatcher** in a big city. The dispatcher has a **live GPS feed** of every taxi. If traffic jams on the main road (link failure), the system **automatically recalculates** every taxi's path in real-time and dispatches rerouted instructions. The dispatcher communicates continuously with drivers (neighboring routers) sharing road conditions. Dynamic routing protocols behave identically — routers gossip with each other, detect failures, and find alternative paths **without human intervention**.

**Characteristics of Dynamic Routing:**
- Routes are **automatically learned** using routing protocols.
- Routers **continuously exchange routing information** with neighbors.
- Suitable for **large, complex networks** with frequent topology changes.
- Uses **router CPU and memory** to run routing algorithms.
- **Low administrative overhead** after initial configuration.
- Adapts to **link failures, congestion, and new paths** automatically.
- Slight **security risk** as routing updates can be spoofed (hence authenticated protocols like OSPF with MD5/SHA authentication exist).

---

### 1.5 KTU Highlight: Default Routing
> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Default Route** (`0.0.0.0/0` in IPv4 or `::/0` in IPv6) is a special static route used when no specific route matches the destination IP. It is essentially the "gateway of last resort" — if the router does not know where to send the packet, it sends it to the default gateway. KTU frequently tests default routing in numerical problems.

---

### 1.6 Static vs Dynamic Routing — Quick Comparison Table

| Parameter | Static Routing | Dynamic Routing |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| Configuration | Manual | Automatic |
| Adaptability | None | High |
| CPU/Memory Usage | Negligible | Moderate to High |
| Administrative Overhead | High (in large nets) | Low |
| Scalability | Poor | Excellent |
| Security | High (no advertisements) | Lower (updates can be spoofed) |
| Convergence | Instant (manually set) | Seconds to Minutes |
| Bandwidth Usage | Zero | Uses link BW for updates |
| Best Use Case | Stub networks, default routes | Enterprise / ISP backbones |
| Examples | Default route, Floating static route | RIP, OSPF, EIGRP, BGP |
