7. **Step 6 — Unicast Response:** Host B constructs an **ARP Reply** packet containing its MAC address (`BB:BB:BB:BB:BB:BB`) and unicasts it back to Host A's MAC (`AA:AA:AA:AA:AA:AA`).
8. **Step 7 — Cache Update:** Host A receives the reply, stores the new mapping in its ARP cache, and proceeds to send the original IP datagram.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Broadcasting:** ARP requests are sent to the **Ethernet broadcast address** because the sender does not yet know the recipient's MAC — a classic **chicken-and-egg problem**. The Ethernet hardware in every NIC is pre-programmed to accept frames addressed to `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`.

---

## 2.2 The Four Operational Cases of ARP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** This is the **most asked** ARP sub-topic in University Exams.

| Case | Sender IP | Sender MAC | Target IP | Target MAC | Packet Type |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **ARP Request** | Known (self) | Known (self) | Known (target) | **Unknown** (`00:00:00:00:00:00`) | **Broadcast** |
| **ARP Reply** | Known (responder) | Known (responder) | Known (original requester) | Known (original requester) | **Unicast** |
| **RARP Request** | Unknown (`0.0.0.0`) | Known (self) | **Ignored / 0.0.0.0** | Known (self) | **Broadcast** |
| **RARP Reply** | Known (server) | Known (server) | Known (requester) | Known (requester) | **Unicast** |

---

## 2.3 ARP Packet Format (RFC 826)

The ARP packet is **28 bytes** long (excluding Ethernet header) and has the following field structure:

| Field | Size (bytes) | Description |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Hardware Type** | 2 | `1` for Ethernet (10 Mbps), `6` for IEEE 802.3 |
| **Protocol Type** | 2 | `0x0800` for IPv4 |
| **Hardware Address Length** | 1 | `6` for MAC addresses (in bytes) |
| **Protocol Address Length** | 1 | `4` for IPv4 addresses (in bytes) |
| **Operation Code (Opcode)** | 2 | `1` = ARP Request, `2` = ARP Reply, `3` = RARP Request, `4` = RARP Reply |
| **Sender Hardware Address (SHA)** | 6 | MAC address of the sender |
| **Sender Protocol Address (SPA)** | 4 | IP address of the sender |
| **Target Hardware Address (THA)** | 6 | MAC address of the intended receiver (`00:00:00:00:00:00` if unknown) |
| **Target Protocol Address (TPA)** | 4 | IP address of the intended receiver |

> **Total ARP Payload = 2 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 6 + 4 + 6 + 4 = 28 bytes**
> **Total Ethernet Frame Payload (with ARP) = 14 (Ethernet header) + 28 (ARP) = 42 bytes** (excluding the 4-byte Ethernet FCS trailer)

---

## 2.4 RARP — Operational Logic

1. **Boot-Time Trigger:** A diskless workstation powers on with no IP address stored locally.
2. **RARP Request Broadcast:** The workstation broadcasts a RARP Request containing its own MAC address and the unknown IP field set to `0.0.0.0`.
3. **RARP Server Lookup:** One or more RARP servers on the LAN receive the request and consult their **static configuration file** (mapping MAC → IP).
4. **RARP Reply (Unicast):** The server unicasts a RARP Reply back to the workstation's MAC with the assigned IP.
5. **Configuration Continues:** The workstation now has an IP and continues boot-up (typically via TFTP in older diskless systems).

### Limitations of RARP

- **Link-Layer Limitation:** RARP cannot cross routers (uses Layer 2 broadcast only).
- **Manual Configuration:** Requires static MAC-to-IP entries on the RARP server — no dynamic allocation.
- **Single Purpose:** Only provides IP address; cannot supply subnet mask, default gateway, or DNS servers.
- **Replaced by BOOTP and DHCP:** BOOTP added subnet mask and gateway info, while DHCP provides full dynamic configuration with leases.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Quick Reference:** Memorize the byte sizes and encapsulation overheads below.

| Concept | Formula / Value | Unit | Notes |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| ARP packet size (excluding Ethernet header) | $2+2+1+1+2+6+4+6+4 = 28$ | bytes | RFC 826 standard |
| Minimum Ethernet frame size | $64$ | bytes | Excludes preamble/SFD |
| Maximum Ethernet payload (ARP + IP) | $1500$ | bytes | Standard MTU |
| Ethernet broadcast MAC | $\text{FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF}$ | — | All 1s in binary |
| Unknown MAC placeholder | $\text{00:00:00:00:00:00}$ | — | Used in ARP Request THA |
| ARP cache entry timeout (typical) | $20$ | minutes | RFC default; OS-dependent |
| Opcode for ARP Request | $1$ | — | Decimal value |
| Opcode for ARP Reply | $2$ | — | Decimal value |
| Opcode for RARP Request | $3$ | — | Decimal value |
| Opcode for RARP Reply | $4$ | — | Decimal value |
| Protocol Type (IPv4) | $0\text{x}0800$ | — | Hexadecimal |
| Hardware Type (Ethernet) | $1$ | — | Decimal value |
| Hardware Type (IEEE 802.3) | $6$ | — | Decimal value |

---

## 2.6 Encapsulation Hierarchy (Layering)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** ARP packets are **NOT** encapsulated inside IP datagrams. They sit **directly inside the Ethernet frame** (Layer 2), with the Ethernet Type field set to `0x0806` (for ARP) or `0x8035` (for RARP).

$$
\boxed{\text{ARP Packet} \;\subset\; \text{Ethernet Frame (Type = 0x0806)}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{RARP Packet} \;\subset\; \text{Ethernet Frame (Type = 0x8035)}}
$$

Compare this to a normal IP datagram:
$$
\boxed{\text{IP Datagram} \;\subset\; \text{Ethernet Frame (Type = 0x0800)}}
$$

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Network Troubleshooting:** The `arp -a` command (Windows/Linux) is the **first diagnostic tool** used by network engineers to detect **IP conflicts**, **duplicate MACs**, or **stale cache entries**.
- **ARP Spoofing / Poisoning:** A security attack where a malicious host sends forged ARP Replies to associate its MAC with the default gateway's IP, enabling **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** attacks. Defenses include **Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI)** on managed switches.
- **Gratuitous ARP in Virtualization:** VMware and VirtualBox use gratuitous ARP to inform the physical switch of the virtual machine's new MAC-IP binding after VM migration, preventing traffic blackholing.
- **DHCP Handshake Foundation:** Although DHCP uses its own layer-2 broadcast mechanism, the **DHCP Discover packet** often relies on ARP cache state to prevent IP conflicts before the lease is granted.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Size of an ARP-Encapsulated Ethernet Frame

> **Problem Statement:** Compute the total size in bytes of a complete Ethernet frame carrying an ARP Request, given the standard field widths defined in RFC 826.

### Given Information

- Ethernet **Preamble + SFD** = `8 bytes` (not counted in frame size on wire)
- Ethernet **Header** = `14 bytes` (6-byte dest MAC + 6-byte source MAC + 2-byte EtherType)
- ARP Packet = `28 bytes` (per RFC 826)
- Ethernet **FCS (Frame Check Sequence)** = `4 bytes` (CRC-32 trailer)

### Step-by-Step Deduction

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Frame Size (on wire)} &= \text{Ethernet Header} + \text{ARP Payload} + \text{FCS} \\
&= 14 \text{ bytes} + 28 \text{ bytes} + 4 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 46 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The Ethernet header contains the source/destination MAC addresses and the EtherType field (`0x0806` for ARP). The ARP payload itself contains 9 fields whose lengths sum to 28 bytes as derived in Section 2.3. The 4-byte FCS is appended by the NIC hardware and is not visible to upper layers.

### Validation Against IEEE 802.3 Minimum

The IEEE 802.3 standard mandates a **minimum frame size of 64 bytes** (excluding preamble). Since our ARP frame is only **46 bytes**, the NIC automatically appends **18 bytes of padding**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Padding Required} &= 64 \text{ bytes (min frame size)} - 46 \text{ bytes (computed frame size)} \\
&= 18 \text{ bytes of zero-padding}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The on-wire Ethernet frame for a standard ARP Request occupies the **64-byte minimum** (with padding). This padding is required for **collision detection (CSMA/CD)** to function correctly on legacy shared-media Ethernet.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: ARP Cache Memory Footprint

> **Problem Statement:** Estimate the maximum memory consumed by an ARP cache on a Linux workstation that maintains up to `1024` entries.

### Given Information

- Each ARP cache entry contains: **IP address (4 bytes)** + **MAC address (6 bytes)** + **Interface index (4 bytes)** + **State flags (4 bytes)** + **Timestamp (8 bytes)** ≈ `26 bytes` of essential data, plus **linked-list pointers and padding** ≈ `~32 bytes` total per entry (OS-dependent, often rounded to 64 bytes with alignment).

### Step-by-Step Deduction

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Memory per Entry} &\approx 32 \text{ to } 64 \text{ bytes (OS-dependent)} \\
\text{Maximum Entries} &= 1024 \\
\text{Total Cache Footprint} &\approx 32 \times 1024 = 32768 \text{ bytes} = 32 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The kernel allocates a hash table for O(1) lookup. The total memory required is negligible by modern standards, which is why ARP caches are aggressively populated and rarely flushed entirely.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use ARP vs RARP vs BOOTP vs DHCP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Comparison Table:** Examiners frequently ask students to **contrast** these address-resolution / configuration protocols. Memorize the distinctions below.

| Parameter | ARP | RARP | BOOTP | DHCP |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **RFC Number** | RFC 826 | RFC 903 | RFC 951 | RFC 2131 / 2132 |
| **Function** | IP → MAC | MAC → IP | MAC → IP + boot file | Full dynamic config |
| **Transport** | Direct on Layer 2 | Direct on Layer 2 | UDP (ports 67/68) | UDP (ports 67/68) |
| **Provides Subnet Mask?** | No | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Provides Gateway?** | No | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Provides DNS?** | No | No | No | Yes |
| **Routable (cross-subnet)?** | No (broadcast only) | No (broadcast only) | Yes (via relay agent) | Yes (via relay agent) |
| **Dynamic Allocation?** | N/A (only resolves) | No (static table) | No (static table) | Yes (lease-based) |
| **Modern Usage** | Universal | Obsolete | Rare | Standard |

---

## 3.4 Detailed Walkthrough: The Four Cases of ARP Packet Fields

> **Problem Statement:** Fill in all fields of an ARP Request and the corresponding ARP Reply for the scenario: Host A (`IP = 10.0.0.5`, `MAC = AA-AA-AA-AA-AA-AA`) wishes to communicate with Host B (`IP = 10.0.0.9`, `MAC = BB-BB-BB-BB-BB-BB`).

### Case 1: ARP Request (Broadcast from A)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hardware Type} &= 1 \quad \text{(Ethernet)} \\
\text{Protocol Type} &= 0\text{x}0800 \quad \text{(IPv4)} \\
\text{HW Addr Len} &= 6 \\
\text{Proto Addr Len} &= 4 \\
\text{Opcode} &= 1 \quad \text{(ARP Request)} \\
\text{SHA} &= \text{AA-AA-AA-AA-AA-AA} \\
\text{SPA} &= 10.0.0.5 \\
\text{THA} &= 00\text{-}00\text{-}00\text{-}00\text{-}00\text{-}00 \quad \text{(unknown, all zeros)} \\
\text{TPA} &= 10.0.0.9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Host A fills its own MAC and IP into the sender fields. The target MAC is set to all zeros because A does not yet know B's MAC — this is the placeholder convention. The packet is wrapped in an Ethernet frame with destination MAC = `FF-FF-FF-FF-FF-FF` and EtherType = `0x0806`.

### Case 2: ARP Reply (Unicast from B to A)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hardware Type} &= 1 \\
\text{Protocol Type} &= 0\text{x}0800 \\
\text{HW Addr Len} &= 6 \\
\text{Proto Addr Len} &= 4 \\
\text{Opcode} &= 2 \quad \text{(ARP Reply)} \\
\text{SHA} &= \text{BB-BB-BB-BB-BB-BB} \\
\text{SPA} &= 10.0.0.9 \\
\text{THA} &= \text{AA-AA-AA-AA-AA-AA} \\
\text{TPA} &= 10.0.0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Notice how the **sender and target fields swap roles**. B (the responder) places its own MAC/IP in the sender positions, while echoing A's MAC/IP in the target positions. The reply is sent as a **unicast** Ethernet frame directly to A's MAC.

### Case 3: RARP Request (Broadcast from diskless client)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hardware Type} &= 1 \\
\text{Protocol Type} &= 0\text{x}0800 \\
\text{Opcode} &= 3 \quad \text{(RARP Request)} \\
\text{SHA} &= \text{CC-CC-CC-CC-CC-CC} \quad \text{(client's own MAC)} \\
\text{SPA} &= 0.0.0.0 \quad \text{(unknown IP)} \\
\text{THA} &= \text{CC-CC-CC-CC-CC-CC} \\
\text{TPA} &= 0.0.0.0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Case 4: RARP Reply (Unicast from RARP server)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hardware Type} &= 1 \\
\text{Protocol Type} &= 0\text{x}0800 \\
\text{Opcode} &= 4 \quad \text{(RARP Reply)} \\
\text{SHA} &= \text{DD-DD-DD-DD-DD-DD} \quad \text{(server's MAC)} \\
\text{SPA} &= 10.0.0.42 \quad \text{(assigned IP)} \\
\text{THA} &= \text{CC-CC-CC-CC-CC-CC} \\
\text{TPA} &= 0.0.0.0
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Key Observation:** In RARP, the client **does not know** its own IP, so it sets SPA and TPA to `0.0.0.0`. The RARP server recognizes the client by its MAC (SHA) and supplies the assigned IP in the SPA field of the reply.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example: ARP Cache Eviction Time

> **Problem Statement:** A host's ARP cache has 500 entries. If each entry is valid for **20 minutes** and the host adds an average of **5 new entries per minute**, calculate the steady-state cache size.

### Solution

In steady state, the rate of cache additions equals the rate of cache evictions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Eviction Rate} &= \frac{\text{Cache Size}}{\text{Timeout Duration}} = \frac{N}{20} \text{ entries/min} \\
\text{Steady State:} \quad \frac{N}{20} &= 5 \\
N &= 5 \times 20 = 100 \text{ entries}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Since each entry expires after 20 minutes, the fraction of the cache that expires per minute is $1/20$. To sustain an addition rate of 5/min, the cache must hold enough entries that 5/min expire. This yields a steady-state size of 100 entries. Any additional growth (e.g., from a network scan) will be naturally pruned back by the timeout.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ARP Request-Reply Sequence (Functional Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Host A wants to send IP packet to Host B] --> B{Check ARP Cache\nfor B's MAC?}
    B -- Cache Hit --> C[Use cached MAC\nencapsulate frame\nunicast to B]
    B -- Cache Miss --> D[Construct ARP Request\nSender MAC = A, Sender IP = A\nTarget MAC = 00...00, Target IP = B]
    D --> E[Wrap in Ethernet Frame\nDest MAC = FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF\nEtherType = 0x0806]
    E --> F[Broadcast to all hosts\non the LAN segment]
    F --> G[All hosts receive frame\ncheck Target IP field]
    G --> H{Is Target IP\nmy own IP?}
    H -- No --> I[Discard ARP Request]
    H -- Yes --> J[Host B constructs ARP Reply\nSender MAC = B, Sender IP = B\nTarget MAC = A, Target IP = A\nOpcode = 2]
    J --> K[Unicast ARP Reply\ndirectly to A's MAC]
    K --> L[Host A receives Reply\nupdates ARP Cache\nwith B's IP-MAC binding]
    L --> C
```

## 4.2 RARP Request-Reply Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Diskless Workstation powers on] --> Q[Workstation knows only\nits own MAC address\nIP = 0.0.0.0]
    Q --> R[Constructs RARP Request\nOpcode = 3\nSender MAC = self, Sender IP = 0.0.0.0]
    R --> S[Wrap in Ethernet Frame\nDest MAC = FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF\nEtherType = 0x8035]
    S --> T[Broadcast to all hosts]
    T --> U{RARP Server\npresent on LAN?}
    U -- No --> V[No reply — boot fails]
    U -- Yes --> W[RARP Server consults\nstatic MAC-to-IP table]
    W --> X[Server constructs RARP Reply\nOpcode = 4\nTarget MAC = client, Target IP = assigned IP]
    X --> Y[Unicast to client's MAC]
    Y --> Z[Workstation receives IP\nresumes boot process\nvia TFTP or next stage]
```

## 4.3 ARP Encapsulation Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L3["Network Layer (Layer 3)"]
        IP["IP Datagram Header\n(20+ bytes)"]
    end
    subgraph L2LINK["Data Link Layer (Layer 2)"]
        ETH["Ethernet Frame\nDestination MAC (6B)\nSource MAC (6B)\nEtherType (2B)"]
        ARP["ARP Packet (28 bytes)\nHardware Type, Protocol Type\nOpcode, SHA, SPA, THA, TPA"]
    end
    subgraph L1["Physical Layer (Layer 1)"]
        PHY["Bits on the wire\nPreamble + SFD + Frame + FCS"]
    end
    IP -.-> ETH
    ARP -.-> ETH
    ETH --> PHY
    style L3 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style L2LINK fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style L1 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
```

## 4.4 Decision Flow: Why RARP Was Replaced

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Diskless Client] --> B{RARP\nRFC 903}
    B -- Limited to LAN --> C[BOOTP\nRFC 951]
    C -- Static config --> D[DHCP\nRFC 2131]
    D -- Dynamic leases\nfull config --> E[Modern Networks]
    B -.->|Obsolete| F[Not used today]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#ffe1e1
    style C fill:#fff4e1
    style D fill:#e1ffe1
    style E fill:#d4edda
    style F fill:#f8d7da
```

## 4.5 ARP Cache Lookup State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CacheEmpty
    CacheEmpty --> Resolving: Packet to send,\nMAC unknown
    Resolving --> BroadcastingARP: Cache miss detected
    BroadcastingARP --> WaitingReply: ARP Request sent
    WaitingReply --> CachePopulated: ARP Reply received
    WaitingReply --> TimeoutRetry: No reply in 3 seconds
    TimeoutRetry --> BroadcastingARP: Retry up to 3 times
    TimeoutRetry --> HostUnreachable: Max retries exceeded
    CachePopulated --> ForwardingFrame: Use cached MAC
    ForwardingFrame --> [*]
    CachePopulated --> EntryAging: After 20 minutes idle
    EntryAging --> CacheEmpty: Entry purged
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
> **Q: Differentiate between ARP and RARP. List the four operational cases of ARP with their opcode values.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | ARP | RARP |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Direction** | IP → MAC (logical to physical) | MAC → IP (physical to logical) |
| **RFC** | RFC 826 | RFC 903 |
| **EtherType** | `0x0806` | `0x8035` |
| **User** | Any IP host | Diskless workstations at boot |
| **Server** | Not required (self-learning cache) | Requires dedicated RARP server |
| **Modern status** | Universal | Obsolete (replaced by BOOTP/DHCP) |

**Four Operational Cases of ARP:**

1. **ARP Request** — Opcode `1` (Broadcast: "Who has IP X?")
2. **ARP Reply** — Opcode `2` (Unicast: "I have IP X, my MAC is Y")
3. **RARP Request** — Opcode `3` (Broadcast: "My MAC is Z, what is my IP?")
4. **RARP Reply** — Opcode `4` (Unicast: "Your IP is W")

---

### Question A2

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
> **Q: Explain the concept of "ARP Cache" and "Gratuitous ARP". Why is ARP said to operate at the boundary between Layer 2 and Layer 3?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**ARP Cache:** A local memory-resident lookup table maintained by every IP host, mapping recently resolved IP addresses to their corresponding MAC addresses. The cache enables **O(1) lookup** and avoids repeated broadcast storms. Entries typically expire after **20 minutes** to handle topology changes.

**Gratuitous ARP:** A host broadcasts an ARP Request for its **own IP address** without being prompted. Purposes:
1. **Duplicate IP Detection** — If another host replies, an IP conflict is detected.
2. **Cache Update** — All other hosts on the LAN update their ARP cache with the sender's (possibly new) MAC.
3. **VRRP/HSRP Failover** — Backup routers use gratuitous ARP to announce takeover of the virtual gateway IP.

**ARP at Layer 2/3 Boundary:** ARP packets are encapsulated **directly inside Ethernet frames** (not inside IP), so the operation is logically **Layer 2** in transmission. However, ARP resolves an IP address (a **Layer 3** logical identifier) to a MAC address (a **Layer 2** physical identifier). Hence, ARP **bridges** Layer 2 and Layer 3, serving as a **glue protocol** between the two layers.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B from each module.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
> **Q: (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) in detail with a neat diagram. Describe all four cases of ARP and the encapsulation of an ARP packet in an Ethernet frame.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the limitations of RARP and explain how BOOTP and DHCP overcome these limitations with suitable comparisons.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
ARP is a link-layer protocol defined in RFC 826 that dynamically maps a 32-bit IP address to a 48-bit MAC address within a local broadcast domain.

**Operational Steps (3 Marks):**
1. Sender checks its ARP cache for the target IP.
2. If **cache miss**, sender broadcasts ARP Request to `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`.
3. Target host recognizes its IP and unicasts ARP Reply with its MAC.
4. Sender updates cache and forwards the original IP packet.

**ARP Packet Format (2 Marks):**
The 28-byte ARP packet contains nine fields:
- Hardware Type (2 bytes) = `1` for Ethernet
- Protocol Type (2 bytes) = `0x0800` for IPv4
- HW Addr Len (1 byte) = `6`
- Proto Addr Len (1 byte) = `4`
- Opcode (2 bytes) = `1` (request) or `2` (reply)
- SHA (6 bytes), SPA (4 bytes), THA (6 bytes), TPA (4 bytes)

**Encapsulation (1 Mark):**
ARP packet is encapsulated directly inside the Ethernet frame. The Ethernet header contains source/destination MAC addresses and the EtherType field set to `0x0806` for ARP. The total frame is 14 (header) + 28 (ARP) + 4 (FCS) = 46 bytes, padded to the 64-byte minimum.

**Four Cases:** As detailed in Section 2.2 above.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Limitations of RARP (3 Marks):**
1. **No subnet mask or gateway information** — RARP only provides the IP address.
2. **Link-layer broadcast limitation** — RARP requests cannot cross routers, restricting usage to a single LAN segment.
3. **Static configuration** — RARP server requires manually maintained MAC-to-IP tables, with no dynamic allocation.
4. **Single-purpose protocol** — Cannot supply DNS, default gateway, or other boot-time parameters.

**BOOTP Improvements (2 Marks):**
BOOTP (RFC 951) uses UDP (ports 67/68) and can traverse routers via **BOOTP relay agents**. It provides the **IP address, subnet mask, default gateway, and boot file name** in a single transaction. However, BOOTP still uses **static tables** on the server.

**DHCP Improvements (2 Marks):**
DHCP (RFC 2131) extends BOOTP with **dynamic lease-based allocation**, automatic reuse of unused addresses, and full configuration parameters (DNS, WINS, lease time, etc.). DHCP is **backward compatible** with BOOTP clients and is the **de facto standard** for automatic IP configuration in modern networks.

**Comparison Table:** As detailed in Section 3.3 above.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**
> **Q: (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the working of ARP. Show the complete ARP Request and ARP Reply packet structure with field values for a scenario where Host `10.0.0.5` (MAC `AA-AA-AA-AA-AA-AA`) resolves the MAC of Host `10.0.0.9`.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** What is Proxy ARP? Explain with a diagram how a router uses Proxy ARP to enable communication between hosts on different subnets. List the advantages and disadvantages of Proxy ARP.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Working of ARP with Diagram (4 Marks):** See the flowchart in Section 4.1 above. The diagram should show:
- Host A checking its ARP cache
- Broadcasting the ARP Request to `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`
- Host B unicast reply
- Cache update and subsequent frame forwarding

**ARP Request Packet (1.5 Marks):**

| Field | Value |
|:---|:---|
| Hardware Type | `1` (Ethernet) |
| Protocol Type | `0x0800` (IPv4) |
| HW Addr Len | `6` |
| Proto Addr Len | `4` |
| Opcode | `1` (Request) |
| SHA | `AA-AA-AA-AA-AA-AA` |
| SPA | `10.0.0.5` |
| THA | `00-00-00-00-00-00` |
| TPA | `10.0.0.9` |

**ARP Reply Packet (1.5 Marks):**

| Field | Value |
|:---|:---|
| Hardware Type | `1` |
| Protocol Type | `0x0800` |
| HW Addr Len | `6` |
| Proto Addr Len | `4` |
| Opcode | `2` (Reply) |
| SHA | `BB-BB-BB-BB-BB-BB` |
| SPA | `10.0.0.9` |
| THA | `AA-AA-AA-AA-AA-AA` |
| TPA | `10.0.0.5` |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Definition of Proxy ARP (1 Mark):**
Proxy ARP is a technique in which a **router answers ARP Requests on behalf of a host** that resides on a **different subnet**, making the remote host appear to be on the same local network as the requester.

**Working with Diagram (3 Marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HA["Host A\n10.0.0.5/24\nAA-AA-AA-AA-AA-AA"] -->|ARP Request:\nWho has 10.0.0.9?| R["Router\nInterface 1: 10.0.0.1\nInterface 2: 10.0.1.1"]
    R -->|Proxy ARP Reply:\n10.0.0.9 is at\nRouter's MAC| HA
    R -->|Forward packet\nto Host B| HB["Host B\n10.0.1.9/24\nBB-BB-BB-BB-BB-BB"]
```

**Scenario:** Host A (subnet `10.0.0.0/24`) wants to reach Host B (subnet `10.0.1.0/24`). Host A broadcasts an ARP Request for `10.0.0.9` (the IP of Host B, even though B is on a different subnet). The **router**, knowing that `10.0.0.9` is reachable via its other interface, replies with **its own MAC address**. Host A then sends the IP packet to the router's MAC, and the router forwards it to Host B.

**Advantages (1.5 Marks):**
1. Hosts can be added to a network **without modifying their routing tables or default gateway configuration**.
2. Simplifies migration between subnets — hosts do not need to be reconfigured.
3. Useful in networks with legacy devices that cannot handle subnetting.

**Disadvantages (1.5 Marks):**
1. **Scalability problem** — One ARP entry per remote host creates a large ARP cache on the router.
2. **Security risk** — Proxy ARP can be exploited for **MITM attacks** if misconfigured.
3. **Inefficient** — Increases broadcast traffic and is considered a **workaround** rather than a proper routing solution.
4. Modern networks disable Proxy ARP by default on most routers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing EtherType values:** Students often write `0x0800` (IP) for ARP packets. The correct EtherType for ARP is **`0x0806`** and for RARP is **`0x8035`**. This is a **2-mark deduction** in Part A.
>
> 2. **Writing "RARP is the reverse of ARP" without elaboration:** Examiners expect explicit comparison on **at least 4 parameters** (direction, RFC, EtherType, server requirement, modern usage).
>
> 3. **Forgetting the THA field in ARP Request:** Students frequently write the target MAC as the **destination host's MAC**. The correct value is **`00-00-00-00-00-00`** (all zeros) because the sender does not yet know it.
>
> 4. **Misstating the encapsulation:** ARP is **NOT** inside IP. It is encapsulated **directly in the Ethernet frame**. Writing "ARP packet inside IP datagram" loses **2-3 marks**.
>
> 5. **Confusing cache timeouts:** Students may write the timeout as "1 hour" or "indefinite". The correct standard is **20 minutes** (RFC-defined, OS-dependent).
>
> 6. **Omitting Gratuitous ARP:** When the question asks about "types of ARP", students list only Request and Reply. **Always mention Gratuitous ARP and Proxy ARP** for full marks.
>
> 7. **Not mentioning DHCP:** When comparing RARP with modern protocols, students stop at BOOTP. Examiners expect the **BOOTP → DHCP evolution** explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam!**

### 🔑 Core Concepts

- ✅ **ARP** = IP → MAC (RFC 826, EtherType `0x0806`)
- ✅ **RARP** = MAC → IP (RFC 903, EtherType `0x8035`)
- ✅ ARP packet is **28 bytes**; encapsulated **directly in Ethernet frame** (NOT inside IP)
- ✅ ARP Request = **Broadcast** to `FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF`
- ✅ ARP Reply = **Unicast** back to requester
- ✅ **Four Cases:** Request (opcode 1), Reply (opcode 2), RARP Request (opcode 3), RARP Reply (opcode 4)
- ✅ **ARP Cache timeout** = **20 minutes** (standard)
- ✅ **Minimum Ethernet frame size** = 64 bytes (ARP frame padded to 64 with 18 bytes of zeros)
- ✅ Total on-wire frame = 14 (header) + 28 (ARP) + 4 (FCS) = **46 bytes** (padded to 64)

### 🔑 Special Variants

- ✅ **Gratuitous ARP** — Host broadcasts ARP for its own IP (used for duplicate detection, VRRP/HSRP failover, VM migration)
- ✅ **Proxy ARP** — Router answers ARP on behalf of a host on a different subnet
- ✅ **Reverse ARP (RARP)** — Used by diskless workstations at boot (now obsolete)
- ✅ **Inverse ARP (InARP)** — Maps Frame Relay DLCI to IP (used in NBMA networks)

### 🔑 Modern Replacements for RARP

- ✅ **BOOTP (RFC 951)** — UDP-based, routable via relay agents, supplies IP + subnet mask + gateway + boot file
- ✅ **DHCP (RFC 2131/2132)** — Dynamic lease-based allocation, full parameter set, backward compatible with BOOTP

### 🔑 Common Exam Quick-Fire Facts

- ✅ Hardware Type for Ethernet = **1**
- ✅ Protocol Type for IPv4 = **`0x0800`**
- ✅ Unknown MAC placeholder = **`00-00-00-00-00-00`**
- ✅ IPv4 address size = **4 bytes**; MAC address size = **6 bytes**
- ✅ ARP sits at the **boundary of Layer 2 and Layer 3**
- ✅ **ARP Spoofing** is the basis for MITM attacks on switched LANs (mitigated by DAI)

### 🔑 Mnemonic for Four ARP Opcodes

> **"O.R.R.B." → 1, 2, 3, 4**
> **O**ne = ARP Request
> **R**eply = ARP Reply
> **R**equest (RARP) = RARP Request
> **B**ootstrap (RARP Reply) = RARP Reply

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routing Algorithms: Distance Vector Routing Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT414 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Routing Algorithms: Distance Vector Routing Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: ROUTING ALGORITHMS — DISTANCE VECTOR ROUTING ALGORITHM

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Distance Vector Routing Algorithm (DVR)** is a *distributed, iterative, and asynchronous* routing algorithm used in computer networks wherein each router maintains a **distance vector** — a table of estimated *minimum costs* (typically hop counts or delay metrics) to every reachable destination in the network. The algorithm is fundamentally grounded in the **Bellman-Ford equation** for shortest-path computation. Each router shares its entire distance vector *only with its directly connected neighbors*, and updates its own table iteratively until the network reaches a **steady-state convergence** (known as the *self-stabilizing equilibrium*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The DVR algorithm is most famously implemented in **RIP (Routing Information Protocol — RFC 1058 / RFC 2453)** and **IGRP (Interior Gateway Routing Protocol)**, which are explicitly listed in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus under routing protocols for intra-domain (interior) routing.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Hiking Group Compass Method"**
>
> Imagine a group of **7 friends** scattered across a hilly region, each holding a compass and a small notebook. Every friend can only shout to the friends *immediately next to them* (not across the valley). Periodically, each friend calls out to their adjacent friends: *"To reach the mountain peak X, it takes me 4 kilometers, and my friend Bob says it takes 5 km from him."* Each friend then updates their own notebook with the *shortest indirect path* they have heard about.
>
> After several rounds of shouting back-and-forth, every friend eventually knows the absolute minimum distance to the peak — *not because they computed it themselves globally*, but because the local gossip propagated through the network. This is **exactly** how DVR works: **decentralized, iterative, neighbor-only communication**.

### 1.3 Standard Terminology & Metrics (KTU Board Definitions)

| Terminology | KTU Definition |
|---|---|
| **Distance Vector** | A one-dimensional array maintained by each router containing the estimated cost to all destinations in the network. |
| **Hop Count** | The default distance metric in RIP; each link costs **1 hop**. |
| **Bellman-Ford Equation** | The mathematical foundation: $D_x(y) = \min_v \{C(x,v) + D_v(y)\}$. |
| **Convergence** | The state in which no router's distance vector changes in an update cycle. |
| **Count-to-Infinity Problem** | A pathological slow-convergence failure where bad-news propagates at **1 hop per iteration**, potentially taking up to **N iterations** to stabilize. |
| **Split Horizon** | A loop-avoidance rule: a router must *not advertise* a route back to the neighbor from which it learned it. |
| **Poison Reverse** | A stronger rule: a router advertises a route back to the source with an *infinite metric* (16 in RIP). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Anchor:** The KTU 2024 module categorizes DVR alongside **Link-State Routing (LSR)**. The contrast — *global vs. local information exchange* — is a frequently tested concept in the University ESE.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Characteristics of DVR (The "Five Pillars")

The DVR algorithm exhibits **five defining characteristics**, all of which are board-favorite theory questions:

1. **Distributed** — Each router independently runs the algorithm; no centralized controller exists.
2. **Iterative** — Updates continue across *multiple rounds* until convergence is reached.
3. **Asynchronous** — Routers do *not* need to update in lockstep; updates can occur at any time.
4. **Self-Terminating** — The algorithm halts on its own once convergence is achieved (no changes in any distance vector).
5. **Self-Stabilizing** — If a link cost changes, the algorithm automatically re-converges to a new steady state.

## 2.2 The Bellman-Ford Equation — Heart of DVR

For every router $x$ and every destination $y$, the minimum cost $D_x(y)$ is computed as:

$$
D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \{ C(x,v) + D_v(y) \}
$$

Where:
- $D_x(y)$ = estimated minimum cost from router $x$ to destination $y$
- $N(x)$ = the set of *all neighbors* of router $x$ (i.e., routers directly connected to $x$)
- $C(x,v)$ = the cost of the direct link from $x$ to neighbor $v$
- $D_v(y)$ = the *advertised* distance from neighbor $v$ to destination $y$ (read from $v$'s distance vector)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The '+' symbol is NOT addition in the abstract — it is path cost accumulation.** Each term $C(x,v) + D_v(y)$ represents the total cost of routing from $x$ through intermediate neighbor $v$ to reach $y$. The `min` operator then picks the best (cheapest) intermediate hop.

## 2.3 Update Rule (How a Router Modifies Its Table)

When router $x$ receives a distance vector from neighbor $v$, $x$ recomputes the cost to every destination $y$ using:

$$
D_x^{\text{new}}(y) = \min\{ D_x^{\text{old}}(y),\ \min_{v \in N(x)} [C(x,v) + D_v(y)] \}
$$

In RIP, this update occurs every **30 seconds** (periodic updates). If no update is heard for **180 seconds**, the route is declared invalid and the metric is set to **16 (infinity)**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Concept | Formula / Value | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Bellman-Ford Core | $D_x(y) = \min_v \{C(x,v) + D_v(y)\}$ | Used for **every** DVR table update |
| Iteration Update | $D_x^{\text{new}}(y) = \min\{D_x^{\text{old}}(y),\ C(x,v) + D_v(y)\}$ | Computed for *each* neighbor $v$ |
| RIP Infinity Metric | $\infty = 16$ hops | Hop count **16 = unreachable** |
| RIP Update Period | $T_{\text{update}} = 30$ seconds | RFC 2453 standard |
| RIP Timeout | $T_{\text{timeout}} = 180$ seconds | After 6 missed updates |
| RIP Hold-Down | $T_{\text{hold}} = 180$ seconds | Suppresses updates during instability |
| Count-to-Infinity Upper Bound | $N$ iterations | Where $N$ = number of hops in the failed link |
| Split Horizon Rule | Do not advertise route $r$ back to neighbor $v$ that gave route $r$ | Prevents 2-node loops |
| Poison Reverse Rule | Advertise route $r$ with metric **16** to neighbor $v$ | Stronger loop prevention |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where DVR is Used in Industry Today:**
> - **Small-to-medium enterprise LANs** still frequently use RIP (RIPv2) due to its simplicity.
> - **Cisco's IGRP and EIGRP** — EIGRP uses a *hybrid* approach (DUAL algorithm) that retains the distance vector concept while adding DUAL's loop-free guarantees.
> - **BGP (Border Gateway Protocol)** — the *backbone of the Internet* — is a **path vector** protocol, which is a *descendant* of DVR augmented with AS-path information.
> - **Sensor networks & IoT** — lightweight routing in ad-hoc WSNs often uses DVR-like flooding mechanisms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Constructing a DVR Table via Iterative Updates

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Expectation:** Students are expected to *manually iterate* a DVR table across multiple rounds, showing the Bellman-Ford update at each step. The example below is the **canonical KTU board pattern**.

### 3.1.1 Network Topology

Consider a 4-router network with the following link costs (all costs = hop count = 1):

**Network Diagram (textual representation):**

- Router **A** is directly connected to **B** (cost 1) and **C** (cost 1).
- Router **B** is directly connected to **A** (cost 1) and **D** (cost 1).
- Router **C** is directly connected to **A** (cost 1) and **D** (cost 1).
- Router **D** is directly connected to **B** (cost 1) and **C** (cost 1).

So the topology is a **4-node square (cycle)**: A–B–D–C–A.

### 3.1.2 Initial Distance Vectors (Iteration 0)

Each router initially knows:
- Cost to itself = **0**
- Cost to direct neighbors = **1**
- Cost to all other nodes = **infinity (∞)**

| From \ To | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 1 | 1 | ∞ |
| **B** | 1 | 0 | ∞ | 1 |
| **C** | 1 | ∞ | 0 | 1 |
| **D** | ∞ | 1 | 1 | 0 |

### 3.1.3 Step-by-Step Bellman-Ford Update — Router A, Iteration 1

Router A receives distance vectors from its two neighbors, **B** and **C**.

**Step 1: List what A receives.**
- From B: $D_B(A)=0$, $D_B(B)=0$, $D_B(C)=\infty$, $D_B(D)=1$
- From C: $D_C(A)=0$, $D_C(B)=\infty$, $D_C(C)=0$, $D_C(D)=1$

**Step 2: Apply Bellman-Ford for destination D via each neighbor.**

$$
D_A^{\text{new}}(D) = \min\{ C(A,B) + D_B(D),\ C(A,C) + D_C(D) \}
$$

$$
D_A^{\text{new}}(D) = \min\{ 1 + 1,\ 1 + 1 \} = \min\{ 2, 2 \} = 2
$$

**Step 3: Apply Bellman-Ford for destination B via each neighbor.**

$$
D_A^{\text{new}}(B) = \min\{ C(A,B) + D_B(B),\ C(A,C) + D_C(B) \}
$$

$$
D_A^{\text{new}}(B) = \min\{ 1 + 0,\ 1 + \infty \} = \min\{ 1, \infty \} = 1
$$

(No change — A already had the direct path to B.)

**Step 4: Apply Bellman-Ford for destination C via each neighbor.**

$$
D_A^{\text{new}}(C) = \min\{ C(A,B) + D_B(C),\ C(A,C) + D_C(C) \}
$$

$$
D_A^{\text{new}}(C) = \min\{ 1 + \infty,\ 1 + 0 \} = \min\{ \infty, 1 \} = 1
$$

(No change.)

**Step 5: Updated distance vector for A after Iteration 1.**

| From \ To | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 1 | 1 | **2** |

### 3.1.4 Updated Table After Iteration 1 (All Routers)

Applying the same logic symmetrically to all routers:

| From \ To | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| **B** | 1 | 0 | 2 | 1 |
| **C** | 1 | 2 | 0 | 1 |
| **D** | 2 | 1 | 1 | 0 |

### 3.1.5 Iteration 2 — Verifying Convergence

At Iteration 2, applying the Bellman-Ford update again yields **identical** values to Iteration 1. This means the algorithm has **converged** at Iteration 2 (i.e., 2 rounds of updates were sufficient for this 4-router topology).

## 3.2 The Count-to-Infinity Problem — Detailed Walkthrough

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's High-Yield Topic:** Count-to-infinity is a **favourite 7-mark question**. Students lose marks for not showing the *iteration-by-iteration* table.

### 3.2.1 Setup

Consider a 3-router line topology: **A — B — C**. Initially, all links cost 1. Router A is the destination. We track B's distance to A.

**Initial state (before failure):**

| From \ To | A | B | C |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| **B** | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| **C** | 2 | 1 | 0 |

**Failure event:** The direct link **A–B goes down** at $t = 0$.

### 3.2.2 Iteration-by-Iteration Walkthrough

**Iteration 1 (t = 30s):**
- B does not yet know the link is down. But B's table still says $D_B(A) = 1$ via A.
- C's table still says $D_C(A) = 2$ via B.
- C's update to B: $D_C(A) = 2$. B recomputes: $D_B^{\text{new}}(A) = \min\{\infty, 1 + 2\} = 3$.

**Iteration 2 (t = 60s):**
- B now advertises $D_B(A) = 3$ to C.
- C recomputes: $D_C^{\text{new}}(A) = \min\{1 + 3, 2\} = 4$ (originally 2 via B, but B's update has worsened).

**Iteration 3 (t = 90s):**
- C advertises $D_C(A) = 4$ to B.
- B recomputes: $D_B^{\text{new}}(A) = \min\{3, 1 + 4\} = 5$.

**Iteration k:** $D_B(A)$ increments by **1 each iteration** until it hits **16 (infinity in RIP)**.

| Iteration | $D_B(A)$ | $D_C(A)$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 (before failure) | 1 | 2 |
| 1 | 3 | 2 |
| 2 | 3 | 4 |
| 3 | 5 | 4 |
| 4 | 5 | 6 |
| ... | ... | ... |
| 15 | 15 | 16 |
| 16 | **16 (∞)** | **16 (∞)** |

**Convergence time** = up to **16 iterations** in RIP, each lasting 30 seconds = up to **8 minutes** of incorrect routing — a critical real-world failure mode.

## 3.3 Solution Mechanisms — Comparative Matrix

| Mechanism | How It Works | Limits Count-to-Infinity? |
|---|---|---|
| **Split Horizon** | Don't advertise a route back to the neighbor from which it was learned. | Partially (2-node loops) |
| **Poison Reverse** | Advertise the route back to the source with metric 16. | Partially (still fails for 3+ node loops) |
| **Hold-Down Timers** | After learning an unreachable route, ignore further updates for 180s. | Slows but doesn't eliminate |
| **Triggered Updates** | Send update *immediately* upon detecting change (no wait for 30s). | Speeds convergence |
| **Path Vector (BGP)** | Include the *full AS-path* in the advertisement; reject routes containing your own AS. | **Yes** — completely eliminates |

## 3.4 Detailed Bellman-Ford Algebraic Derivation (Single-Source Form)

Starting from the general dynamic programming principle: the shortest path from $x$ to $y$ with at most $h$ hops satisfies:

$$
D_x^{(h)}(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \{ C(x,v) + D_v^{(h-1)}(y) \}
$$

**Base case:** $D_x^{(0)}(x) = 0$ and $D_x^{(0)}(y) = \infty$ for $y \neq x$.

**Inductive step:** Assume $D_v^{(h-1)}(y)$ is the minimum cost from $v$ to $y$ using at most $h-1$ hops. Then to reach $y$ from $x$ in at most $h$ hops, we must first travel *one hop* to some neighbor $v$ (cost $C(x,v)$), then continue via the optimal $h-1$ hop path from $v$. Taking the minimum over all $v$ yields the recursion.

For $h = 1$ (one hop away): $D_x^{(1)}(y) = C(x,y)$ for direct neighbors, $\infty$ otherwise.

For $h = 2$ (two hops): $D_x^{(2)}(y) = \min_v \{C(x,v) + C(v,y)\}$, which corresponds to the first DVR table update.

> [!NOTE]
> **Connection to Dijkstra's Algorithm:** Link-State Routing (LSR) is mathematically equivalent to DVR at convergence but achieves it using *global* topology information and Dijkstra's algorithm, while DVR achieves it using *only neighbor-to-neighbor* exchanges.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Distance Vector Router

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **internal functional blocks** of a single router running the DVR algorithm. It depicts how a routing update event flows from the network interface, through the Bellman-Ford computation engine, into the routing table, and back out to neighbors.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PktIn[Incoming Routing Update Packet] --> Parser[Update Parser Block]
    Parser --> DVA[Distance Vector Array Buffer]
    DVA --> BFEq[Bellman Ford Equation Engine]
    TopTbl[Local Distance Vector Table] --> BFEq
    BFEq --> Compare[Minimum Cost Comparator]
    Compare --> Decision{New Cost Smaller than Existing}
    Decision -- Yes --> Updt[Update Routing Table Entry]
    Decision -- No --> Keep[Retain Old Entry]
    Updt --> Trigger[Trigger Update Generator]
    Keep --> Idle[No Update Sent]
    Trigger --> Sched[Update Scheduler 30 sec timer]
    Idle --> Sched
    Sched --> PktOut[Outgoing Routing Update Packet to Neighbors]
    Sched --> ChkConv{Convergence Check}
    ChkConv -- Stable --> Steady[Steady State No Further Updates]
    ChkConv -- Changed --> Repeat[Loop Continue Iterating]
```

## 4.2 Iterative Update Cycle Across Multiple Routers

The diagram below illustrates how a *single distance vector update* ripples through the network across **multiple iterations**, leading to convergence. The blocks represent routers; the arrows represent the direction of distance vector advertisement.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Iter0[Iteration 0 Initial State] --> Iter1[Iteration 1 First Update]
    Iter1 --> Iter2[Iteration 2 Second Update]
    Iter2 --> Iter3[Iteration 3 Third Update]
    Iter3 --> Conv[Convergence Reached Steady State]
    Iter0A[Router A knows self and direct neighbors] -.-> Iter0
    Iter1A[Router A learns distances to D via B and C] -.-> Iter1
    Iter2A[All routers have updated their tables] -.-> Iter2
    ConvNote[No further changes occur in any DV] -.-> Conv
```

## 4.3 Count-to-Infinity State Propagation Diagram

This Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the *bad news* about a failed link propagates hop-by-hop, incrementing the distance metric at each router in the path. Each block represents one iteration of the algorithm.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Fail[Link A B Fails at time t 0] --> I1[Iteration 1 B sets D B A to 3 via C]
    I1 --> I2[Iteration 2 C sets D C A to 4 via B]
    I2 --> I3[Iteration 3 B sets D B A to 5 via C]
    I3 --> I4[Iteration k D B A increments by 1]
    I4 --> I16[Iteration 15 B sets D B A to 16 infinity in RIP]
    I16 --> Stable[Network declares A unreachable]
    Stable --> Mit[Apply Split Horizon or Poison Reverse to Mitigate]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid:** The above diagrams are *block-level functional schematics* rather than literal network drawings. Each block represents a state or functional component of the algorithm. KTU examiners reward diagrams that *clarify the flow of computation* in addition to textual explanations.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**Q:** Define *Distance Vector Routing*. Mention any two characteristics of the algorithm.

**Model Answer:**

**Distance Vector Routing (DVR)** is a distributed, iterative, and asynchronous routing algorithm in which each router maintains a table (a "distance vector") giving the estimated minimum cost to every possible destination. The router updates this table based *only* on information received from its directly connected neighbors using the **Bellman-Ford equation**:

$$
D_x(y) = \min_v \{ C(x,v) + D_v(y) \}
$$

**Two characteristics:**
1. **Distributed and asynchronous** — no central controller, no synchronization required between routers.
2. **Iterative and self-terminating** — updates continue across multiple rounds and halt automatically upon convergence.

*(1 + 1 + 1 = 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**Q:** What is the *count-to-infinity problem* in DVR? Briefly explain how *split horizon* helps to mitigate it.

**Model Answer:**

The **count-to-infinity problem** is a slow-convergence failure in DVR where, upon a link failure, routers continue to advertise increasing distance metrics to the unreachable destination, one hop at a time, until the metric reaches the protocol-defined infinity (16 in RIP). This can take up to *N* iterations, where *N* is the number of hops in the loop.

**Split horizon** mitigates this by enforcing the rule that a router *must not advertise a route back to the neighbor from which it learned the route*. This breaks the immediate 2-node feedback loop that drives the count-to-infinity behavior.

*(1.5 + 1.5 = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting units and infinity notation** — always state "infinity = 16 hops in RIP."
> - **Skipping iterations in the count-to-infinity table** — show *at least 4-5 iterations* in the table; do not jump from initial to final.
> - **Confusing DVR with LSR** — DVR uses *only neighbor information*; LSR uses *global topology* (Dijkstra's algorithm). Examiners deduct 2 marks for confusing the two.
> - **Omitting the Bellman-Ford equation** — always write the equation *before* the numeric evaluation. A bare number with no formula is worth 0.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**Q (a)** Explain the **Distance Vector Routing Algorithm** in detail. State and explain the **Bellman-Ford equation** used for updating the routing table. (7 marks)

**Q (b)** Consider a network with 4 nodes A, B, C, D. The link costs are: A–B = 1, B–C = 2, C–D = 3, A–D = 7, A–C = 4. Using DVR, construct the initial distance vector tables for all nodes. Show **Iteration 1** updates for all nodes using the Bellman-Ford equation. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Distance Vector Routing Algorithm — Detailed Explanation (7 marks):**

The **Distance Vector Routing (DVR) algorithm** is one of the two classical dynamic routing algorithms used in packet-switched networks (the other being Link-State Routing). It is *distributed* — no router has global topology knowledge — and is based on the **Bellman-Ford shortest-path algorithm**.

**Core Idea:** Each router $x$ maintains a distance vector $D_x = [D_x(y_1), D_x(y_2), \ldots, D_x(y_N)]$ for all destinations $y_i$ in the network. Initially, $x$ knows only the cost to its direct neighbors. Periodically (e.g., every 30s in RIP), each router sends its entire distance vector to *all directly connected neighbors*. Upon receiving a vector from neighbor $v$, router $x$ updates its own entries using:

$$
D_x(y) = \min_{v \in N(x)} \{ C(x,v) + D_v(y) \}
$$

The algorithm is **iterative** (multiple rounds of updates), **asynchronous** (updates happen independently), and **self-terminating** (stops when no changes occur in any table). It is also **self-stabilizing** — it automatically re-converges after topology changes.

**Five properties (board-favorite):**
1. Distributed
2. Iterative
3. Asynchronous
4. Self-terminating
5. Self-stabilizing

The protocol most associated with DVR is **RIP**, which uses hop count as the metric and treats 16 hops as infinity.

*(Breakdown: definition = 2 marks, Bellman-Ford equation = 2 marks, properties = 2 marks, protocol example = 1 mark = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Worked Example (7 marks):**

**Network:** Nodes A, B, C, D with link costs: A–B=1, B–C=2, C–D=3, A–D=7, A–C=4.

**Initial Distance Vectors (Iteration 0):**

| From \ To | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 1 | 4 | 7 |
| **B** | 1 | 0 | 2 | ∞ |
| **C** | 4 | 2 | 0 | 3 |
| **D** | 7 | ∞ | 3 | 0 |

**Iteration 1 — Update for Node A:**

A's neighbors: B, C, D.

For destination **D**:
$$
D_A^{\text{new}}(D) = \min\{1 + \infty,\ 4 + 3,\ 7 + 0\} = \min\{\infty, 7, 7\} = 7
$$
(No change.)

For destination **B**:
$$
D_A^{\text{new}}(B) = \min\{1 + 0,\ 4 + 2,\ 7 + \infty\} = \min\{1, 6, \infty\} = 1
$$
(No change.)

For destination **C**:
$$
D_A^{\text{new}}(C) = \min\{1 + 2,\ 4 + 0,\ 7 + 3\} = \min\{3, 4, 10\} = 3
$$
(**Improvement!** Old value was 4 via direct link; new value is 3 via B.)

**Updated A:** A = [0, 1, **3**, 7].

**Iteration 1 — Update for Node B:**

B's neighbors: A, C.

For destination **C**:
$$
D_B^{\text{new}}(C) = \min\{1 + 4,\ 2 + 0\} = \min\{5, 2\} = 2
$$
(No change.)

For destination **D**:
$$
D_B^{\text{new}}(D) = \min\{1 + 7,\ 2 + 3\} = \min\{8, 5\} = 5
$$
(**Improvement!** Old value was ∞; new value is 5 via C.)

For destination **A**:
$$
D_B^{\text{new}}(A) = \min\{1 + 0,\ 2 + 4\} = \min\{1, 6\} = 1
$$
(No change.)

**Updated B:** B = [1, 0, 2, **5**].

**Iteration 1 — Update for Node C:**

C's neighbors: A, B, D.

For destination **A**:
$$
D_C^{\text{new}}(A) = \min\{4 + 0,\ 2 + 1,\ 3 + 7\} = \min\{4, 3, 10\} = 3
$$
(**Improvement!** Old = 4; new = 3 via B.)

For destination **B**:
$$
D_C^{\text{new}}(B) = \min\{4 + 1,\ 2 + 0,\ 3 + \infty\} = \min\{5, 2, \infty\} = 2
$$
(No change.)

For destination **D**:
$$
D_C^{\text{new}}(D) = \min\{4 + 7,\ 2 + \infty,\ 3 + 0\} = \min\{11, \infty, 3\} = 3
$$
(No change.)

**Updated C:** C = [**3**, 2, 0, 3].

**Iteration 1 — Update for Node D:**

D's neighbors: A, C.

For destination **A**:
$$
D_D^{\text{new}}(A) = \min\{7 + 0,\ 3 + 4\} = \min\{7, 7\} = 7
$$
(No change.)

For destination **B**:
$$
D_D^{\text{new}}(B) = \min\{7 + 1,\ 3 + 2\} = \min\{8, 5\} = 5
$$
(**Improvement!** Old = ∞; new = 5 via C.)

For destination **C**:
$$
D_D^{\text{new}}(C) = \min\{7 + 4,\ 3 + 0\} = \min\{11, 3\} = 3
$$
(No change.)

**Updated D:** D = [7, **5**, 3, 0].

**Final Iteration 1 Tables:**

| From \ To | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 1 | 3 | 7 |
| **B** | 1 | 0 | 2 | 5 |
| **C** | 3 | 2 | 0 | 3 |
| **D** | 7 | 5 | 3 | 0 |

*(Breakdown: initial tables = 1.5 marks, four Bellman-Ford applications = 4 marks, final iteration-1 tables = 1.5 marks = 7 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme — Alternative]`

**Q (a)** Compare **Distance Vector Routing** and **Link State Routing** with respect to information scope, convergence speed, message complexity, and loop behavior. (7 marks)

**Q (b)** With a neat example, explain the **count-to-infinity problem** in Distance Vector Routing. Show the iteration-by-iteration metric values for a 3-node line topology where the middle link fails. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) DVR vs LSR — Comparative Analysis (7 marks):**

| Criterion | Distance Vector Routing | Link State Routing |
|---|---|---|
| **Information Scope** | Each router knows *only its neighbors' distances* (local information) | Each router has *complete topology* of the network (global information) |
| **Algorithm Used** | Bellman-Ford equation | Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm |
| **Convergence Speed** | **Slow** — can take many iterations; count-to-infinity may occur | **Fast** — typically O(N) iterations using Dijkstra |
| **Message Complexity** | Low — O(E) per update period (only neighbor-to-neighbor) | High — O(N·E) initial flood (LSAs to all routers) |
| **Loop Behavior** | Prone to routing loops (count-to-infinity) | Loop-free after convergence due to synchronized LSDB |
| **Storage Requirement** | Small — one table per destination | Large — full topology + LSDB must be stored |
| **CPU Usage** | Low | High (Dijkstra computation on every change) |
| **Bandwidth (steady state)** | Periodic broadcasts (e.g., 30s in RIP) | Event-triggered LSAs only |
| **Example Protocols** | RIP, IGRP | OSPF, IS-IS |
| **Scalability** | Poor for large networks (slow convergence) | Better — designed for large ASes |

*(Breakdown: 7 criteria × 1 mark each ≈ 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Count-to-Infinity — Detailed Walkthrough (7 marks):**

**Topology:** A line network **A — B — C**. Initially all links cost 1, so:
- $D_B(A) = 1$ (direct via A)
- $D_C(A) = 2$ (via B)
- $D_B(C) = 1$ (direct via C)
- $D_C(B) = 1$ (direct via B)

**Failure event:** Link A–B fails at $t = 0$.

**Initial Tables (Before Failure):**

| From \ To | A | B | C |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| **B** | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| **C** | 2 | 1 | 0 |

**Iteration 1 (B detects failure):**
- B's direct link to A is down. However, B still holds stale information: $D_B(C) = 1$ and $D_C(A) = 2$ (from C's last advertisement).
- B recomputes: $D_B^{\text{new}}(A) = C(B,C) + D_C(A) = 1 + 2 = 3$.
- B advertises $D_B(A) = 3$ to C.

| From \ To | A | B | C |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| **B** | **3** | 0 | 1 |
| **C** | 2 | 1 | 0 |

**Iteration 2 (C updates using B's new value):**
- C recomputes: $D_C^{\text{new}}(A) = C(C,B) + D_B(A) = 1 + 3 = 4$.
- C advertises $D_C(A) = 4$ to B.

| From \ To | A | B | C |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| **B** | 3 | 0 | 1 |
| **C** | **4** | 1 | 0 |

**Iteration 3 (B updates using C's new value):**
- B recomputes: $D_B^{\text{new}}(A) = C(B,C) + D_C(A) = 1 + 4 = 5$.

**Iteration k:** The metric continues to increment by 1 each round. After 15 iterations, $D_B(A) = 16$, which RIP interprets as **infinity** (unreachable). The algorithm *converges* with A marked as unreachable, but it has taken up to **15 iterations × 30s = 7.5 minutes** of incorrect routing — a serious real-world issue.

**Why it happens:** The routers have no way to *immediately* distinguish a *genuine* "A is still reachable via the long path" advertisement from a *stale* one based on outdated information. The metric is increased by mutual reinforcement.

**Mitigation Strategies:**
- **Split Horizon** (prevents 2-node loops)
- **Poison Reverse** (advertises metric 16 back to source)
- **Hold-Down Timers** (180s suppression)
- **Triggered Updates** (immediate update on change)
- **Path Vector protocols like BGP** (eliminates entirely)

*(Breakdown: setup = 1 mark, iterations 1-3 table = 2 marks, explanation of cause = 2 marks, mitigation list = 2 marks = 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Board 2024 DVR Module:**

- **DVR definition:** Distributed, iterative, asynchronous, self-terminating, self-stabilizing. *(All five "self" / distributed properties are high-yield.)*
- **Bellman-Ford equation:** $D_x(y) = \min_v \{C(x,v) + D_v(y)\}$ — **must be written before any numerical evaluation** in the answer sheet.
- **Protocol association:** DVR ⇒ **RIP (RIPv1, RIPv2)**, IGRP; LSR ⇒ **OSPF**, IS-IS.
- **RIP default values:** update = 30s, timeout = 180s, hold-down = 180s, **infinity = 16 hops**.
- **Count-to-infinity:** Always show **at least 4 iterations** of the table; the metric increases by 1 per iteration.
- **Split horizon:** Don't advertise a route back to the neighbor from which it was learned.
- **Poison reverse:** Advertise metric **16 (infinity)** back to the source neighbor.
- **DVR uses local information only;** LSR uses global topology — this is the **single most important distinction** in KTU module 3 theory.
- **Convergence** in DVR is reached when **no router's distance vector changes** in a complete update round.
- **Diagrams fetch 1-2 extra marks** — always include a topology diagram for routing algorithm questions.
- **BGP is a path vector** protocol (descendant of DVR) — a common trick question asks to classify it correctly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** When asked "explain DVR with example," the expected structure is: (1) one-line definition, (2) Bellman-Ford equation, (3) topology diagram, (4) initial table, (5) iteration 1 update, (6) iteration 2 update, (7) convergence statement, (8) mention of count-to-infinity with one mitigation. This **8-step structure** consistently scores 13-14/14 in the University ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routing Protocols: Routing Information Protocol (RIP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEVT414 - COMPUTER NETWORKS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Routing Protocols: Routing Information Protocol (RIP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Routing Information Protocol (RIP)** is one of the oldest **Distance-Vector Interior Gateway Protocols (IGP)** standardized for use inside an autonomous system. Defined originally in **RFC 1058 (RIPv1, 1988)** and updated in **RFC 2453 (RIPv2, 1998)** and **RFC 2080 (RIPng for IPv6, 1997)**, RIP enables each router to build and maintain a routing table by exchanging reachability information only with its *directly connected neighbors*.

RIP is fundamentally classified under the following taxonomy:

| Property | Classification |
|---|---|
| Algorithm Class | Distance-Vector (Bellman-Ford) |
| Administrative Distance | **120** |
| Metric | **Hop Count** |
| Maximum Hop Count | **15** (16 = "infinity" / unreachable) |
| Update Mechanism | Periodic full-table broadcast/multicast |
| Update Interval | **30 seconds** |
| Transport | UDP Port **520** (RIPv1/v2), UDP Port **521** (RIPng) |
| Scope | Interior Gateway Protocol (IGP) |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of RIP as a "Village Gossip Network" 🗣️**

Imagine a chain of villages connected by single-lane roads. Each village has a *postmaster* (the router) who maintains a personal ledger of which village is how many roads away. Every **30 seconds**, each postmaster walks to his immediate neighboring villages and **shouts his entire ledger** aloud. After listening to the neighbors, the postmaster updates his ledger using the simple rule: *"If my neighbor tells me he can reach village X in N hops, and I'm 1 hop from him, then I can reach village X in N+1 hops."*

**Critical Real-World Limits Embedded in the Analogy:**
- If any village is **more than 15 roads away**, the postmasters will forever declare it "unreachable" because the rumor gets a stamp of "16" which means *infinity*.
- News travels **slowly** because each village only tells its immediate neighbors, who then tell *their* neighbors — this is the famous **slow convergence** problem.
- If a road gets washed out, the rumor of its destruction also takes time to propagate — this can lead to the **count-to-infinity problem**.

## 1.3 Core Operational Metrics

- **Hop Count**: The number of routers a packet must pass through to reach a destination. RIP treats each link as cost 1, ignoring bandwidth, delay, or load.
- **Administrative Distance (AD)**: A trustworthiness score used by routers to choose between routes learned from different protocols. RIP's AD of **120** is intentionally higher (less preferred) than OSPF (110) or EIGRP (90) so that it acts as a fallback.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *"RIP keeps it small — 15 hops, 30 seconds, 120 AD, 520 UDP."*
> Memorize **15, 30, 120, 520** — these four numbers answer roughly 60% of KTU numerical/short-answer questions on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Distance-Vector Algorithm Foundation

RIP is mathematically built on the **Bellman-Ford Equation** for shortest paths:

$$
D_x(y) = \min_v \{ C(x,v) + D_v(y) \}
$$

Where:
- $D_x(y)$ = The shortest distance from node $x$ to destination $y$
- $v$ = Any direct neighbor of $x$
- $C(x,v)$ = The cost of the link from $x$ to $v$ (always **1** in RIP)
- $D_v(y)$ = The currently known distance from $v$ to $y$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU:** Every routing-table update in RIP is essentially a *distributed, iterative application* of the Bellman-Ford equation. When you compute the new distance, you are literally minimizing over all neighbors.

## 2.2 The Four RIP Timers (Highly Testable ⚡)

| Timer | Default Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Update Timer** | **30 s** | Periodic interval between full routing-table advertisements. |
| **Invalid Timer** | **180 s** | If no update refreshes a route within 180 s, the hop count is set to **16** (unreachable). |
| **Hold-Down Timer** | **180 s** | After learning a route is down, ignore updates about that route for 180 s to prevent flapping. |
| **Flush Timer** | **240 s** | After 240 s of being invalid, the route is finally purged from the routing table. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Relation Check:** Flush Timer = Invalid Timer + Hold-Down Timer (some implementations). KTU examiners love to ask: *"What is the hold-down time? Why is it 180 s?"*

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The following sequence occurs on **every RIP-enabled router** continuously:

1. **Initialization**: On boot, each router discovers its directly connected networks and seeds its routing table with entries of metric **0** for its own interfaces and metric **1** (or the configured interface cost) for neighbors.
2. **Listening State**: The router opens UDP port 520 and waits for incoming RIP messages from neighbors.
3. **Periodic Trigger**: Every 30 seconds (drifted slightly by a small random offset of $0$ to $5$ seconds to avoid synchronization storms), the router **sends its entire routing table** to:
   - **RIPv1**: Broadcast address `255.255.255.255`
   - **RIPv2**: Multicast address `224.0.0.9`
4. **Receive & Process**: On receiving a RIP update, the router validates the source, then for every route entry, computes a new metric:
   $$\text{New Metric} = \text{Reported Metric from Neighbor} + 1$$
5. **Bellman-Ford Comparison**: If this computed value is **strictly less than** the currently installed route, the router updates its table (this is called a **route improvement**). If it is **equal**, it may install for tie-breaking. If it is **greater**, the route is kept (unless it is the existing successor and has become worse — then it is flagged for triggered updates).
6. **Triggered Updates**: If the metric to a network changes (e.g., link failure causes a route to go to 16), the router **immediately** sends an update without waiting for the next 30 s cycle.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Print this table. It covers 90% of KTU numerical answers.**

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Numerical Default |
|---|---|---|
| Hop Count Limit | $\text{Max Usable Hops} = 15$ | Infinity $= 16$ |
| Convergence Delay (worst case) | $T_{conv} \le (\text{Net Diameter}) \times 30\,s$ | 15 hops $\Rightarrow \le 7.5$ min |
| New Metric Computation | $D_{new}(x) = D_{neighbor}(x) + 1$ | Always add 1 |
| Update Trigger Interval | $T_{update} = 30 \pm \delta$ s | $\delta \in [0, 5]$ s jitter |
| Route Expiry | $T_{invalid} = 180$ s | Route marked unreachable |
| Route Hold-Down | $T_{hold} = 180$ s | Suppress updates |
| Route Purge | $T_{flush} = 240$ s | Removed from table |
| UDP Port | Port 520 (IPv4), Port 521 (IPv6) | Layer-4 transport |
| RIP Packet Size | $\le 512$ bytes | Max 25 route entries |
| Administrative Distance | $AD_{RIP} = 120$ | Lower = more trusted |

## 2.5 RIP Versions — Critical KTU Comparison

| Feature | RIPv1 | RIPv2 | RIPng |
|---|---|---|---|
| RFC | 1058 | 2453 | 2080 |
| Address Class | Classful | Classless (VLSM/CIDR) | IPv6 only |
| Subnet Mask in Update | ❌ Not carried | ✅ 32-bit mask field | ✅ Prefix length |
| Next-Hop Field | ❌ | ✅ (eliminates extra hop) | ✅ |
| Authentication | ❌ | ✅ Plaintext or MD5 | ✅ IPv6 native |
| Update Destination | Broadcast | Multicast (224.0.0.9) | Multicast (FF02::9) |
| Multicast Group | N/A | `224.0.0.9` | `FF02::9` |

## 2.6 The Count-to-Infinity Problem

This is the **single most important conceptual question** KTU asks about RIP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scenario:** Link between A–B fails. A used B as its successor. A advertises the network as unreachable (metric 16). B, *before* it has learned A is down, advertises the network as 1 hop. A hears this, thinks *"Oh, B can reach it in 1 hop via some other path"* and installs metric **2**. B then hears A's metric 2, installs metric **3**, and so on. The metric creeps upward: 3, 4, 5, …, 16 — only then is it declared unreachable. This **slow poisoning** is called **count-to-infinity**.

$$\lim_{k \to \infty} D_A^{(k)}(N) = 16 \quad \text{(in RIP, capped at 16)}$$

Where $D_A^{(k)}$ is the metric at iteration $k$. The metric increases by 1 per iteration cycle (every 30 s), so for a network at distance $d$, full convergence requires:

$$T_{worst} = d \times 30\,s + \text{processing overhead}$$

## 2.7 Loop-Prevention Mechanisms

| Mechanism | Mechanism Description | Effectiveness |
|---|---|---|
| **Maximum Hop Count = 15** | Hard cap on how far rumors travel. Stops infinity from truly being infinity. | Bounds the loop. |
| **Split Horizon** | *"Never advertise a route back to the router from which you learned it."* | Prevents 2-node loops. |
| **Poison Reverse** | *"When advertising a route back to its source, advertise it as metric 16 (poison)."* | Stronger than split horizon. |
| **Hold-Down Timers** | After learning a route is down, ignore better metrics for 180 s. | Prevents premature acceptance of bad info. |
| **Triggered Updates** | Send updates *immediately* on topology change, not at next 30 s cycle. | Speeds up convergence. |
| **Route Poisoning** | Mark a failed route as metric 16 immediately. | Propagates failure fast. |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

RIP is rarely deployed in modern enterprise networks (replaced by **OSPF** and **EIGRP**) but remains academically dominant and still appears in:
- **Small branch-office networks** with simple topologies.
- **Network simulations** (Cisco Packet Tracer, GNS3 default labs).
- **IoT and embedded routers** where simplicity and tiny code footprint matter.
- **Foundational CCNA / KTU networking curriculum** to teach *why* more sophisticated protocols (OSPF link-state) were designed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative #1 — RIP Update Metric Calculation

> **Problem Statement:** Router **R3** receives the following RIP entry from its neighbor **R2**: *"Network 192.168.10.0 is reachable with metric 4."* What metric does R3 install for this network?

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the incoming RIP message parameters.**

Let $M_{reported}$ denote the metric advertised by the neighbor (R2):
$$M_{reported} = 4$$

**Step 2 — Recall the RIP cost model.**

RIP assigns a uniform cost of **1 hop** to every directly connected link between adjacent routers:
$$C(R3, R2) = 1$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Bellman-Ford-style update rule.**

The new metric is the sum of the link cost and the reported metric:
$$
D_{R3}(192.168.10.0) = C(R3, R2) + M_{reported}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
D_{R3}(192.168.10.0) = 1 + 4 = 5 \text{ hops}
$$

**Step 5 — Validity check against the infinity threshold.**

Since $D_{R3} = 5 < 15$, the route is valid. If the calculation yielded $\geq 16$, RIP would mark the destination as **unreachable**.

$$
\boxed{D_{R3}(192.168.10.0) = 5 \text{ hops, route is REACHABLE}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivative #2 — Count-to-Infinity Numerical Trace

> **Problem Statement:** Three routers A, B, C are connected linearly: **A — B — C**. Network N is attached to C with hop count 1 (from C). The link **B–C fails**. Assuming all routers had previously converged with B's route to N = 1 hop and A's route to N = 2 hops. Using RIP defaults and **no triggered updates**, trace the hop count to N as seen by A over the first 5 update cycles.

### Step-by-Step Trace

**Step 0 — Steady state before failure.**
$$
D_B(N) = 1, \quad D_A(N) = 2 \quad \text{(via B)}
$$

**Step 1 — Link B–C fails.** B detects the link down and sets its metric to N as **16** (infinity).

**Step 2 — A's next update cycle.** A sends its routing table to B. A still thinks N is 2 hops away (it has not been told otherwise). B receives A's advertisement:
$$D_B(N) = \min\{16,\ D_A(N) + 1\} = \min\{16,\ 2+1\} = 3$$

**Step 3 — B sends its updated table to A.** A receives metric = 3 from B for N:
$$D_A(N) = D_B(N) + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4$$

**Step 4 — Next cycle (B↔A continue oscillating).**
$$D_B(N) = D_A(N) + 1 = 4 + 1 = 5$$
$$D_A(N) = D_B(N) + 1 = 5 + 1 = 6$$

**Step 5 — Continue iteration k.**
$$
D_B^{(k)}(N) = D_A^{(k-1)}(N) + 1
$$
$$
D_A^{(k)}(N) = D_B^{(k)}(N) + 1
$$

**Step 6 — Observe the pattern.** With each iteration, the metric increases by exactly **1** at each hop. The closed-form solution:
$$
D_A^{(k)}(N) = 2 + (k-1) \cdot 1 = k + 1 \quad \text{(for } k \ge 1 \text{ after failure)}
$$

**Step 7 — Tabulate the result for 5 cycles:**

| Iteration $k$ | $D_B(N)$ | $D_A(N)$ | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 (failure) | 16 | 2 | B poisoned |
| 1 | 3 | 4 | Loop begins |
| 2 | 5 | 6 | Metric creeping |
| 3 | 7 | 8 | Still looping |
| 4 | 9 | 10 | Still looping |
| 5 | 11 | 12 | Still looping |
| ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ |
| 14 | 16 | — | **Reaches infinity** |

**Step 8 — Convergence time.** From $k=1$ to $k=14$ requires 14 iterations. Each iteration takes up to 30 seconds (the update interval). Therefore:
$$
T_{convergence} = (k_{final} - k_{initial}) \times T_{update} = 14 \times 30\,s = 420\,s = 7 \text{ minutes}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often answer "infinity" instead of giving a numerical bound. Always state: *"In RIP, count-to-infinity converges in a finite time because the metric is capped at 16, which takes approximately $14 \times 30\,s = 7$ min in the worst case."*

---

## 3.3 Derivative #3 — Maximum Network Diameter

> **Problem Statement:** What is the maximum number of routers that can lie on a single RIP path between a source and destination?

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the constraint.** RIP imposes:
$$\text{Max Hops} = 15$$

**Step 2 — Count the routers on a 15-hop path.**

A path of $h$ hops passes through $h+1$ routers. For $h = 15$:
$$
N_{routers} = h + 1 = 15 + 1 = 16 \text{ routers}
$$

**Step 3 — Tabulate hop counts and their meaning:**

| Hop Count | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| 0 | Directly connected network |
| 1–14 | Reachable, valid |
| **15** | Reachable but at the limit |
| **16** | **Unreachable** (infinity) |

$$
\boxed{N_{max\,routers\,per\,path} = 16}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Hint:** If a question shows a network diagram with 17 routers in a line, the destination is **automatically unreachable** via RIP — state this explicitly in your answer.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: RIP vs OSPF vs EIGRP

| Parameter | RIP (v2) | OSPF | EIGRP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Algorithm Type | Distance-Vector | Link-State | Hybrid (DUAL) |
| Metric | Hop Count | Cost (bandwidth-based) | Composite (BW + Delay) |
| Max Hop Count | 15 | Unlimited | 224 (default 100) |
| Convergence Speed | Slow | Fast | Very Fast |
| Updates | Periodic (30 s) | Event-driven (LSA flood) | Event-driven (DUAL) |
| Administrative Distance | 120 | 110 | 90 (internal) |
| CPU/Memory Use | Low | High | Medium |
| VLSM/CIDR Support | ✅ (v2) | ✅ | ✅ |
| Hierarchical Design | ❌ Flat | ✅ Areas | ✅ Stub/Autonomous |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RIP Operational Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Router Boot - Initialize Routing Table] --> B[Start RIP Process on Interfaces]
    B --> C[Listen on UDP Port 520]
    C --> D{Periodic Timer 30s Triggered?}
    D -- No --> C
    D -- Yes --> E[Build RIP Packet from Routing Table]
    E --> F[Encapsulate in UDP Port 520]
    F --> G[Send to 224.0.0.9 Multicast - RIPv2]
    G --> H[Receive RIP Update from Neighbor]
    H --> I[Validate Source and Authentication]
    I --> J[For Each Route Entry Compute New Metric]
    J --> K{Metric less than current route?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Update Routing Table - Bellman-Ford]
    K -- No --> M[Keep Existing Route]
    L --> N[Send Triggered Update if Metric Changed]
    M --> D
    N --> D
```

## 4.2 RIP Message Format (RIPv2) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[RIP Header - 4 bytes] --> P2[Command Field 1 byte]
    P2 --> P3[Version Field 1 byte]
    P3 --> P4[Routing Domain 2 bytes unused]
    P4 --> P5[Route Entry 1 - 20 bytes]
    P5 --> P6[Route Entry 2 - 20 bytes]
    P6 --> P7[Dot Dot Dot]
    P7 --> P8[Route Entry N - 20 bytes]
    P8 --> P9[Maximum 25 Entries - 512 bytes cap]
```

**Field Description of each 20-byte Route Entry (RIPv2):**

| Field | Size | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Address Family Identifier (AFI) | 2 bytes | 2 = IP, 0xFFFF = Authentication |
| Route Tag | 2 bytes | External route info (used with BGP) |
| IP Address | 4 bytes | Destination network |
| Subnet Mask | 4 bytes | **RIPv2 addition** (enables VLSM) |
| Next Hop | 4 bytes | **RIPv2 addition** (avoid extra hop) |
| Metric | 4 bytes | Hop count, 1–16 |

## 4.3 Count-to-Infinity Visualization

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Router A
    participant B as Router B
    participant C as Router C
    Note over C: Network N attached, D_C = 1
    Note over B: D_B to N = 1
    Note over A: D_A to N = 2
    Note over B,C: Link B-C FAILS
    B->>B: Set D_B to N = 16
    A->>B: I can reach N in 2 hops
    B->>B: Update D_B to N = 3
    B->>A: I can reach N in 3 hops
    A->>A: Update D_A to N = 4
    B->>A: I can reach N in 5 hops
    A->>A: Update D_A to N = 6
    Note over A,B: Metric climbs 4,5,6,7... until 16
```

## 4.4 RIP Convergence Loop Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Topology Change Detected]) --> Trigger[Triggered Update Generated]
    Trigger --> SH{Split Horizon Check}
    SH -- Route from this neighbor --> Block[Block Advertisement - Loop Prevent]
    SH -- Route from elsewhere --> Poison[Poison Reverse - Metric 16]
    Block --> HD[Hold-Down Timer 180s]
    Poison --> HD
    HD --> Update[Routing Table Updated]
    Update --> Send[Periodic Update Every 30s]
    Send --> Stable([Network Stable])
    Stable --> Start
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define RIP. State its administrative distance and the maximum hop count it supports. Mention the transport layer protocol and port number used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**RIP (Routing Information Protocol)** is a **distance-vector interior gateway protocol** that uses **hop count** as its routing metric. It is defined in **RFC 2453** for RIPv2 and operates at the application layer, using **UDP as its transport layer protocol on port 520**.

Key parameters:
- **Administrative Distance: 120**
- **Maximum Hop Count: 15** (16 = infinity / unreachable)
- **Update Interval: 30 seconds**
- **Algorithm: Bellman-Ford**

> **Marking Note:** Full 3 marks require all four parameters. Omitting the port number or AD usually costs 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the count-to-infinity problem in distance-vector routing? How does RIP bound it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **count-to-infinity problem** occurs in distance-vector protocols when routers have inconsistent routing information about a failed network. The metric keeps increasing by 1 each update cycle as routers advertise stale routes back and forth to each other, slowly counting upward.

**RIP bounds this problem** by imposing a **maximum hop count of 15**, with **hop 16 treated as infinity (unreachable)**. As soon as any router's metric reaches 16, the route is declared invalid and purged, preventing the metric from growing infinitely. The bound ensures convergence in at most $15 \times 30\,s = 7.5$ minutes in the worst case.

> **Marking Note:** Definition (1.5 marks) + RIP's bound mechanism (1.5 marks). Naming the 7.5-minute bound adds bonus credit.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

---

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question]`
