## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]
**Q: List any six limitations of firewalls.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Insider threats:** Firewalls cannot protect against attacks originating from authenticated internal users or compromised internal hosts.
2. **Encrypted traffic blindness:** Payloads encrypted via TLS/SSL cannot be inspected by traditional firewalls without expensive SSL-decryption mechanisms.
3. **Application-layer attacks:** Basic firewalls operating at OSI Layers 3–4 cannot detect Layer 7 threats such as SQL injection, XSS, or DNS tunneling.
4. **Zero-day vulnerabilities:** Signature-based firewall rules are ineffective against newly disclosed exploits until vendor signatures are deployed.
5. **Bypass via alternate paths:** Rogue wireless access points, mobile hotspots, dial-up modems, and IPv6 tunnels allow attackers to circumvent the firewall entirely.
6. **Misconfiguration vulnerability:** A single overly permissive ACL rule can render the entire firewall useless; industry studies suggest misconfiguration causes the majority of firewall-related breaches.

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]
**Q: Why is a firewall alone insufficient to protect a corporate network? Explain with respect to the principle of Defense-in-Depth.**

**Model Answer:**
A firewall provides **perimeter security only** and operates on **rule-based logic**. It fails against insider threats, encrypted malware, application-layer exploits, zero-day attacks, and bypass channels. Therefore, relying solely on a firewall violates the **Defense-in-Depth** principle, which mandates that security be implemented across **multiple independent layers**—perimeter firewall, IDS/IPS, internal segmentation, host-based firewalls, endpoint detection and response (EDR), and centralized SIEM monitoring. Each layer compensates for the weaknesses of others, ensuring that a single point of failure does not compromise the entire network. Hence, firewalls must be combined with complementary security mechanisms for robust protection.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A — Choice 1 [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the various limitations of firewalls. Why is Defense-in-Depth architecture necessary in modern enterprise networks?**

**Part (a) — Limitations of Firewalls (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The limitations of firewalls can be categorized as follows:

**(i) Cannot protect against insider attacks (2 marks):**
Firewalls enforce security only at the network perimeter. Once a user is authenticated inside the trusted network, the firewall does not monitor their internal activities. A malicious or compromised insider can exfiltrate confidential data over allowed ports (e.g., HTTPS on port 443) without triggering any firewall alert.

**(ii) Cannot inspect encrypted traffic (2 marks):**
A significant portion of modern internet traffic (over 80% as of 2024, per Google Transparency Reports) is encrypted via TLS/SSL. Standard firewalls cannot decrypt this traffic without performing SSL interception, which is computationally expensive, breaks end-to-end trust models, and raises privacy concerns. Malware embedded in encrypted streams passes through undetected.

**(iii) Cannot stop application-layer attacks (1.5 marks):**
Basic packet-filter and stateful firewalls operate at Layers 3 and 4 of the OSI model. They cannot parse HTTP, DNS, or SMTP payloads to detect SQL injection, cross-site scripting (XSS), command injection, or DNS tunneling attacks.

**(iv) Vulnerable to misconfiguration and zero-day exploits (1.5 marks):**
A misconfigured rule (`ANY → ANY → ALLOW`) neutralizes the firewall entirely. Furthermore, zero-day exploits—those without known signatures—bypass all signature-based detection logic until patches and rule updates are deployed.

**Part (b) — Defense-in-Depth Architecture (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Defense-in-Depth (DiD) is a security strategy that deploys **multiple layers of controls** so that the failure of any single layer does not result in a complete security breach. The architecture includes:

| Layer | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| L1 | Perimeter Firewall | Filters unauthorized inbound traffic |
| L2 | IDS/IPS | Detects and prevents known attack signatures |
| L3 | Internal Segmentation Firewall | Isolates VLANs and restricts lateral movement |
| L4 | Web Application Firewall (WAF) | Filters Layer 7 HTTP attacks |
| L5 | Host-Based Firewall | Protects individual endpoints |
| L6 | EDR / Anti-Malware | Detects runtime malicious behavior |
| L7 | SIEM / SOC | Correlates logs and orchestrates response |

**Why DiD is necessary (4 marks):**
- **Redundancy:** Each layer compensates for the failure of another.
- **Diverse defense vectors:** A combination of signature, behavior, and heuristic-based detection addresses zero-day and advanced persistent threats (APTs).
- **Compliance:** Regulatory frameworks (NIST 800-53, ISO 27001, PCI-DSS) mandate layered controls.
- **Insider threat mitigation:** Internal segmentation firewalls and EDR detect threats that perimeter firewalls miss.
- **Resilience:** Network remains functional even when one layer is breached.

**(Conclude with a diagram—refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the Defense-in-Depth block diagram.)**

---

#### Question B — Choice 2 [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**Q: Explain in detail the types of firewall bypass attacks. How can organizations mitigate these bypass techniques while supplementing firewalls with other security mechanisms?**

**Part (a) — Types of Firewall Bypass Attacks (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Firewall bypass attacks exploit **alternate communication channels** or **logical vulnerabilities** in firewall rule sets. The major categories are:

**(i) Backdoors and rogue access points (2 marks):**
Attackers or careless employees install unauthorized wireless access points or USB-based cellular modems. These devices create direct internet links from the internal network, completely bypassing the corporate firewall.

**(ii) Exploiting open ports and allowed services (2 marks):**
Firewalls commonly allow outbound traffic on ports 80 (HTTP), 443 (HTTPS), and 53 (DNS). Attackers tunnel malicious traffic through these allowed channels using techniques like:
- **DNS tunneling:** Exfiltrating data via encoded DNS queries.
- **HTTPS reverse shell:** Establishing command-and-control channels over port 443.
- **HTTP-based C2:** Using HTTP POST requests to send stolen data to attacker servers.

**(iii) IP address spoofing and source routing (1.5 marks):**
Attackers craft packets with spoofed source IP addresses belonging to the trusted internal subnet. Stateless firewalls that trust internal IP ranges may allow such packets. Source routing allows attackers to dictate the packet's path, bypassing firewall inspection points.

**(iv) ICMP and protocol tunneling (1.5 marks):**
ICMP tunneling (e.g., `ping` and `traceroute` payloads), IPv6-over-IPv4 tunnels, and SSH tunneling can carry arbitrary data through firewalls that allow these protocols for diagnostic purposes.

**Part (b) — Mitigation Strategies and Supplementary Security (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Mitigation | Description | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| **Network Access Control (NAC)** | Authenticates and authorizes every device before granting network access; blocks rogue APs. | 1.5 |
| **Egress Filtering** | Restricts outbound traffic to known-good destinations; prevents C2 beaconing. | 1.5 |
| **Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) / NGFW** | Inspects Layer 7 content even on allowed ports; detects tunneling anomalies. | 1.5 |
| **IDS/IPS Deployment** | Detects attack signatures and anomalous behavior in real time. | 1.0 |
| **Regular Rule Audits and Penetration Testing** | Identifies misconfigurations and unmonitored backdoors. | 1.0 |
| **Endpoint Security (EDR / Anti-Malware)** | Detects malicious activity at the host level, complementing network firewalls. | 0.5 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Listing limitations without explanation** — Examiners deduct marks if you only list 5–6 limitations without briefly explaining each. Always pair the limitation with its mechanism.
> 2. **Confusing firewall TYPES with firewall LIMITATIONS** — A common mistake is writing about packet filters vs. stateful firewalls when the question asks for limitations. Stay focused on **what firewalls cannot do**.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention misconfiguration** — Many students omit this, but it is statistically the **most common cause of firewall failure** and earns easy marks.
> 4. **Not drawing a diagram in 14-mark answers** — A labeled diagram (even a simplified one) typically carries **2–3 marks**. Always include the Defense-in-Depth block diagram.
> 5. **Ignoring the "why" in Defense-in-Depth** — Simply naming the layers is not enough; explain **why each layer compensates for a specific firewall limitation**.
> 6. **Spelling "Defense-in-Depth" incorrectly** — Use the American spelling "Defense" (not "Defence") consistently throughout your answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Limitations of Firewalls**

- [ ] Firewalls are **perimeter-only** devices and cannot protect against **insider threats**.
- [ ] **Encrypted traffic (TLS/SSL)** renders traditional firewalls blind to payload content.
- [ ] Firewalls operating at **OSI Layers 3–4** cannot detect **Layer 7 application attacks** (SQLi, XSS, DNS tunneling).
- [ ] **Zero-day exploits** have no signatures and pass through rule-based firewalls.
- [ ] **Bypass channels** (rogue APs, USB modems, IPv6 tunnels, reverse shells) circumvent firewall perimeters.
- [ ] **Misconfiguration** is the leading cause of firewall-related breaches — not design flaws.
- [ ] Firewalls do **not protect against** social engineering, physical theft, or compromised endpoints.
- [ ] **Defense-in-Depth** is mandatory: combine firewalls with IDS/IPS, WAF, EDR, NAC, SIEM, and SOC.
- [ ] **NGFWs** with DPI and sandboxing partially address encrypted traffic and zero-day issues but are **not complete solutions**.
- [ ] Always include a **labeled diagram** in 14-mark answers on this topic.
- [ ] Use the **rule order principle**: ACL rules are evaluated **top-down, first-match-wins** — explains why misconfiguration is catastrophic.
- [ ] Recall key metrics: $P_{FN}$, $L_f$, $E_r$ (encrypted traffic ratio), $A$ (availability) for analytical questions.
- [ ] The phrase **"no single security mechanism is sufficient"** must appear in your conclusion for full marks.
